comment: Avoid using string interpolation with `%` inside logging calls. It eagerly formats the message even when the log level is disabled, and it also bypasses structured logging patterns used elsewhere in Airflow. Prefer parameterized logging: `self.log.info("dropping module %s due to presence in mod_overrides", mod_key)`. Also consider lowering this to `debug` to avoid noisy logs during DAG parsing/reloading.

This test calls a non-existent helper: `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_system_inner` is not defined anywhere in this hunk, while the helper you added above is named `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_external_inner`. This will raise `AttributeError` and make the suite fail. Likely you meant to call `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_external_inner(DependencyPosition.AFTER)` here (or rename the helper consistently).

Avoid adding a broad `# pylint: disable=no-member` here unless it's strictly necessary. In Alembic migrations, `op.get_bind()` is a well-known API, and if pylint is complaining it may be better addressed by importing/typing `op` correctly (or adding a targeted disable at the file level with a short justification). If you keep the inline disable, consider adding a brief comment explaining why pylint is a false positive to prevent this from becoming a pattern of suppressing real issues.

Disabling `pylint` on this line suggests `op` is not being recognized as an Alembic `Operations` proxy. Prefer fixing the import/typing so pylint can infer the type (e.g., `from alembic import op` is present and, if needed, add a local type hint like `from alembic.operations import Operations; op: Operations` or use `cast(Operations, op)`), rather than suppressing `no-member` in migrations. If you keep the suppression, consider scoping it to the file header with a brief rationale to avoid repeating it in both upgrade/downgrade.

Same concern as in `upgrade()`: the `# pylint: disable=no-member` is likely masking a typing/import issue with Alembic's `op` proxy. Consider addressing the root cause (type hinting/casting) or moving a single, documented suppression to the module level to avoid duplication and reduce the chance of hiding real `no-member` problems later.

Consider making this alter operation more explicit/robust for Alembic by specifying `existing_type`, `existing_nullable`, and (if applicable) `existing_server_default`. Without these, Alembic may emit incomplete DDL on some MySQL variants or fail when the column has constraints/defaults. Also, `mysql.MEDIUMTEXT` is a type class; prefer `mysql.MEDIUMTEXT()` for consistency with SQLAlchemy type instantiation (and to avoid edge cases where Alembic expects an instance).

Switching from `UnicodeText` to generic `Text` doesn’t guarantee `MEDIUMTEXT` on MySQL; SQLAlchemy’s `Text` typically maps to `TEXT` (still ~64KB). If the goal is specifically `MEDIUMTEXT` (16MB), consider using a MySQL-specific type (e.g., `sqlalchemy.dialects.mysql.MEDIUMTEXT`) or `Text().with_variant(MEDIUMTEXT(), 'mysql')` to keep other DBs unchanged. Also ensure there’s an accompanying Alembic migration altering the column type, otherwise existing installations won’t be updated.

Importing Airflow at module import time to read `__version__` can have side effects (and can be relatively expensive) when this rule module is imported, and it also makes the rule behavior depend on the runtime environment rather than the target codebase being checked. Consider using a safer/version-only source (e.g., `importlib.metadata.version("apache-airflow")`) and/or deferring this version check to rule execution time (e.g., inside `check()`/`_check_file`) so importing the upgrade rules doesn't require importing `airflow` itself.

This changelog entry updates the example env var from `AIRFLOW__CORE__...` to `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__...`. Since this is a user-facing behavior change, please ensure the changelog also clarifies backward-compatibility/transition behavior (e.g., whether `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN_CMD` is still honored with a deprecation warning, or no longer works). If both are supported, consider mentioning both env vars here to reduce upgrade confusion.

This paragraph is a bit hard to parse and has a few grammar issues. Consider rewording for clarity and consistency with other UPDATING entries, e.g. "When reading the new options, Airflow will fall back to the old `[core]` options if set. If an old option is used, a `DeprecationWarning` is emitted." Also consider explicitly stating the deprecation/removal timeline (e.g., "will be removed in Airflow X") if known, since this is a user-facing migration note.

This switches the lookup to the new [database] section, but it may break on upgrades where users still have `sql_alchemy_conn` configured under [core]. Consider adding a backward-compatible fallback: first try `conf.get('database', 'SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN', fallback=None)` and if missing/None then read from `conf.get('core', 'SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN', fallback='NOT AVAILABLE')` (or rely on Airflow's deprecation/mapping layer if one exists, but this call site currently hard-codes the section). Also ensure the option name casing matches how config keys are defined/normalized (most code uses `sql_alchemy_conn`), to avoid surprises if normalization behavior changes.

This change assumes `sql_alchemy_conn` is always available under the new `[database]` section. For backward compatibility (users still on `[core]`), consider falling back to `conf.get('core', 'sql_alchemy_conn')` when the new key/section is missing, or use a helper that resolves renamed config options. Otherwise `airflow standalone` may break for existing configs that haven’t been migrated yet.

The default value appears to have a stray trailing character (`"utf-8"2`), which will both change the intended default and may break YAML parsing / config generation. This should be `default: "utf-8"` (no trailing `2`). Also consider keeping the original `version_added` (was 1.10.1 in `[core]`) rather than bumping to 2.3.0 when merely moving sections.

Otherwise, tests that still call `conf.get('core', 'sql_alchemy_conn')` may start failing. If backward-compat is expected, consider leaving a commented note/alias in `[core]` or ensure the migration logic is covered by tests.

This switches the sqlite detection to read from the new `[database]` section, which is correct for the migration. However, `_validate_config_dependencies()` is part of early config validation and should remain robust during upgrades: if `[database] sql_alchemy_conn` is missing (older configs, partial upgrades, or custom parsers), `self.get('database', 'sql_alchemy_conn')` may raise and prevent startup. Consider using the migration/compat layer here (e.g., a helper that resolves renamed options) or providing a fallback to the old location (core/sql_alchemy_conn) so validation still works when only the legacy key is present. Also, the check `"sqlite" in conn` is a bit loose (could match in username/password); if you touch this code, consider parsing the URL scheme instead.

This condition changes semantics in a way that can silently ignore task-level `end_date`s whenever *any* task lacks an `end_date` (common case). That fixes the reported bug, but it also means tasks with an `end_date` will no longer constrain scheduling at all, which may be too broad. Consider computing `latest` as the DAG's own `end_date` only (if set), and otherwise leave `latest=None` regardless of task end_dates, or explicitly document/encode the intended behavior. Also, `len(end_dates) == len(self.tasks)` is a brittle proxy for "all tasks have end_date"; it will treat an empty DAG as true and the comment is unclear. Prefer `all(t.end_date for t in self.tasks)` (and handle empty tasks explicitly) and rename the comment to something like `# Only apply task end_dates if all tasks define one` if that is truly intended.

Operator precedence here is subtle and likely not what you intend. In Python, `and` binds tighter than `or`, so this currently evaluates as `self.is_backfill or (task.start_date <= execution_date and end_date_ok)`. That means for backfills you will still create TIs even when `execution_date` is after `task.end_date`, which seems inconsistent with the goal of honoring task-level `end_date`. Consider adding explicit parentheses for clarity and correctness, e.g. `return task.task_id not in task_ids and (self.is_backfill or (task.start_date <= self.execution_date and (task.end_date is None or self.execution_date <= task.end_date)))`, or if backfills should also respect `end_date`, move the end_date check outside the `or`.

This assertion compares `TimeRestriction` objects directly. If `TimeRestriction` is a dataclass this is fine, but if equality semantics change (or timezone-aware vs naive datetimes differ), this can become brittle. Consider asserting individual fields (`earliest`, `latest`, `catchup`) to make the test intent clearer and failures more actionable. Also, since this is a private attribute (`_time_restriction`), it may be worth asserting via a public API if one exists, or at least add a short comment explaining why testing the private attribute is necessary for this regression.

Bumping the expected TI count for backfill from 2 to 3 makes sense only if the new `with_end_date` task is expected to be created for backfill runs. However, since `with_end_date` is set to `DEFAULT_DATE - 1 day`, it should be out of range for the run’s logical date and likely *not* create a TI (or be marked removed) depending on the intended behavior. Please double-check the assertion logic later in this test: if the goal is to ensure tasks with specific start/end dates are only created for backfill runs, consider making the `end_date` fall *within* the backfill window (or add explicit assertions about which task_ids are present) rather than only changing the count, which can be brittle and may mask regressions.

Using `end_date=DEFAULT_DATE - timedelta(1)` makes this task immediately out-of-range for any run at/after `DEFAULT_DATE`, so it may never create a TI in either manual or backfill scenarios. If the intent is to validate that task-level `end_date` does not stop the whole DAG (and that only this task is skipped/removed), it would be clearer to set `end_date` relative to the DagRun’s logical date (e.g., `DEFAULT_DATE + timedelta(1)` and then create a run after that) and assert explicitly that other tasks still get TIs while this one does not. As written, the test may pass without actually exercising the bug fix.

Using `timezone.utcnow()` in tests can introduce flakiness (time moves during the test run) and makes the test non-deterministic. Prefer a fixed, timezone-aware datetime (e.g., `DEFAULT_DATE` if it's aware) or freeze time (e.g., `freezegun`/Airflow time freezer) and compute `end_date` relative to that frozen "now". If the intent is to ensure `end_date` is after `execution_date`, consider setting `execution_date` to a fixed value and `end_date = execution_date + timedelta(days=10)` to keep the test stable.

Same concern as above: `timezone.utcnow()` makes this test time-dependent and potentially flaky. Use a deterministic base time (fixed `execution_date`/`DEFAULT_DATE`) and derive `end_date` from it, or freeze time for the duration of the test to avoid intermittent failures around boundary conditions.

Consider avoiding `timezone.utcnow()` here to keep the test deterministic. A fixed `end_date` (or one derived from a fixed `execution_date`) is usually preferable in unit tests; otherwise failures can occur if other parts of the test compare against "now" or if the test suite runs across time boundaries.

Passing `self.extra_args` into `Object.load()` fixes SSE-C/RequestPayer use cases, but `extra_args` is also used for other operations (e.g., upload/download) and may contain keys that are not valid for `HeadObject`/`load()`. Consider filtering/whitelisting to only the parameters supported by `head_object` (e.g., SSECustomerAlgorithm/SSECustomerKey/RequestPayer/ExpectedBucketOwner/VersionId, etc.) or catching `ParamValidationError` to raise a clearer message. Also ensure `self.extra_args` is always a dict (default `{}`) to avoid `TypeError` when it is `None`, and add a regression test covering SSE-C download/head behavior.

Converting any aware datetime to UTC fixes the reported failure, but this also changes the contract of `DateTimeTrigger`: previously it rejected non-Pendulum UTC tzinfo, now it silently normalizes. Please ensure this is intentional and add/adjust tests to cover: (1) aware non-UTC datetimes (e.g. America/Chicago) are accepted and converted, (2) naive datetimes still raise, and (3) already-UTC datetimes remain unchanged. Also consider whether `timezone.convert_to_utc()` preserves the expected type/serialization (Pendulum vs stdlib datetime) and whether `serialize()`/`deserialize` consumers assume Pendulum UTC specifically; if so, you may want to explicitly coerce to Pendulum UTC or document the relaxed behavior.

Changing the condition from `if access_control:` to `if access_control is not None:` alters semantics: an explicitly empty dict (`{}`) will now trigger `_sync_dag_view_permissions`, which is good for the proposed “authoritative when explicitly set” behavior, but only if `_sync_dag_view_permissions` actually *revokes* any existing DAG-level perms not present in `access_control`. Please ensure `_sync_dag_view_permissions` handles the empty-dict case by removing all non-Admin DAG permissions for this resource (and add a regression test for the reported UI-created permission disappearing / and for `{}` clearing). Otherwise this change could become a no-op for `{}` and leave stale permissions behind.

This assertion hard-codes an expected UTC time (01:00) for a 09:00 naive input, but the test never sets a non-UTC DAG timezone—`DEFAULT_TIMEZONE` may be UTC in many test environments, making this either incorrect or flaky depending on global config. To make the test deterministic and actually validate “use DAG timezone”, explicitly set the DAG timezone to a known non-UTC zone (e.g. `timezone=pendulum.timezone("Europe/Berlin")` or `tz="Europe/Berlin"` on `start_date`) and assert the expected conversion based on that zone (including DST considerations). Also consider asserting the full datetime (date + time) rather than just `.time()` to avoid ambiguity around day boundaries.

Adding a `queued()` helper is useful, but please double-check that introducing a new public method on `BaseExecutor` is necessary and consistent with the rest of the API. Most state helpers here map to terminal states (success/failed/etc.); `QUEUED` is non-terminal and may have different semantics across executors. If this is only needed for adoption logic, consider whether it should be private/internal (or documented clearly as part of the stable executor interface) and ensure all call sites expect `QUEUED` events to be emitted via the event buffer. Also consider adding/adjusting unit tests to cover that `queued()` results in the expected event buffer entry and doesn’t break executors that don’t use it.

This lookup is O(n) per described task and can also raise IndexError if the ECS response contains a task ARN that isn't present in `tis` (or if there are duplicates). Consider building a dict once (e.g., `ti_by_arn = {ti.external_executor_id: ti for ti in tis if ti.external_executor_id}`) and then using `ti = ti_by_arn.get(task.task_arn)` with a guard/log/skip when missing. This will be more efficient and safer, and avoids the `[0]` indexing pattern.

Docstring update to “ECS Task” is good, but please ensure the rest of the class (and any callers) no longer assumes Fargate-specific semantics. If this DTO is now used for both EC2 and Fargate launch types, consider clarifying in the docstring what fields are expected to be present across both (e.g., whether `started_at`/`stopped_reason` are always available) to avoid misleading future readers.

Adding `external_executor_id` is likely needed for adoption, but this is a public DTO used across the executor. Please verify all instantiation sites are updated (including any `from_aws_response`/factory helpers, tests, and type-checking) and consider documenting what format/value is expected (e.g., Airflow scheduler/executor identifier) and whether it can be derived from ECS task tags/overrides. Also consider whether this should be a required field for adopted tasks vs. newly launched tasks to prevent silent `None` propagation.

This assertion is a bit indirect and can be flaky if `active_workers` already contains entries from fixture setup or prior test state. It also doesn't verify *which* tasks were adopted. Consider asserting on the exact adopted keys/ARNs (e.g., that `active_workers` contains entries for external_executor_id `001` and `002` and not for `None`), and/or assert `len(mock_executor.active_workers) == 2` after ensuring the executor starts with an empty `active_workers`.

Calling the listener hook here uses the pre-existing `dag_run` ORM object, but `set_dag_run_state_to_success(..., commit=True)` likely updates state in DB and may return/refresh a different instance. Consider refreshing `dag_run` from the session (or using the object returned by the state-change helper, if available) before emitting the event so listeners see the correct final state/timestamps. Also, passing `msg=""` is a bit odd—if the hook expects an optional message, prefer `None` or a meaningful message (e.g. "Manually marked as success via API") to avoid downstream consumers treating empty string as a real message.

Using an `autouse=True` fixture at this scope will affect every test in the class/module, which can hide cross-test interactions and make failures harder to diagnose. Consider scoping this fixture more narrowly (e.g., `scope="function"` explicitly and/or only applying it to the new listener-notification test via a normal fixture argument) to avoid unintentionally clearing listeners for unrelated tests that might rely on global listener state.

Clearing the global listener manager before/after is good for isolation, but `get_listener_manager()` is called twice; consider storing it in a local variable to avoid repeated global lookups and to make intent clearer. Also, if `clear()` can raise, you may want to ensure cleanup still runs (e.g., `try/finally`) to prevent leaking listeners into subsequent tests.

Importing `ClassBasedListener` inside the test can be fine to avoid import-time side effects, but it can also obscure dependencies and make static analysis harder. If there are no side effects, prefer a top-level import; if there are, add a short comment explaining why this import is intentionally local.

The expected `listener_state` for the `queued` transition is an empty list, while `success/failed` expect a `DagRunState` value. This asymmetry may make the assertion less robust/clear. If the listener is supposed to be notified for all state changes, consider asserting the exact expected state for `queued` as well (or explicitly document that queued transitions should not emit listener events).

Switching the class-based listener definition to `if AIRFLOW_V_3_0_PLUS` changes behavior for Airflow 2.10–2.11: they will now use the legacy signature branch. Please double-check that the hook signature changes you’re targeting actually start in 3.0 (not 2.10), otherwise this will silently stop testing the new-style hooks on 2.10+ and could mask regressions. If the new hook API is available in 2.10+, consider keeping `if AIRFLOW_V_2_10_PLUS` (or using a more precise feature flag) and only branching where signatures truly differ.

Including `task_sdk/` in the Docker build context can significantly increase context size and slow down CI builds if it contains build artifacts (e.g., `dist/`, `.venv/`, `.pytest_cache/`, `__pycache__/`). Consider also adding explicit ignore patterns for common generated files under `task_sdk/` (or ensure they’re covered elsewhere) to keep the context lean while still making sources available.

This new label rule only matches `task_sdk/**/*`. If the new project is intended to be the `airflow-sdk` dist (per issue) and/or lives under a different top-level directory (e.g. `airflow_sdk/`, `airflow/sdk/`, `airflow-sdk/`), this won’t auto-label PRs correctly. Consider aligning the path pattern with the actual package root(s) and include related build/test files (e.g. `pyproject.toml`, `task_sdk/pyproject.toml`, `task_sdk/tests/**`) if they live outside this glob.

This new output is only useful if `steps.selective-checks` actually sets `run-task-sdk-tests` and downstream jobs consume it. Please ensure the selective-checks step defines this output (and defaults it to `false` when not applicable) to avoid empty/undefined output values causing unexpected job conditions. Also verify naming consistency with the rest of the workflow (e.g., `task-sdk` vs `airflow-sdk`) so the flag is discoverable and used correctly.

`inputs.run-task-sdk-tests` is referenced in the job-level `if`, but this input is not declared under `on.workflow_call.inputs`. This will cause workflow validation/runtime failures (undefined input) and the job may never run. Either add a `run-task-sdk-tests` input (type: string/boolean with a default) or remove/replace this condition with an existing input (e.g., `canary-run`) or a repository/dispatch condition.

This creates `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/` but then touches `./task_sdk/src/airflow/__init__.py`. That mismatch is a bit confusing and may not achieve the intended goal (ensuring the editable install produces a .pth entry for the right package). If the intent is to make `airflow.sdk` importable, consider touching `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/__init__.py` (and/or ensuring `airflow` is a namespace package per your packaging approach). Also, since `airflow` already exists in the main repo, adding a concrete `airflow/__init__.py` in task_sdk could accidentally turn the namespace into a regular package and cause import/packaging conflicts depending on how the SDK is built/installed.

This creates `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/` but the touched file is `./task_sdk/src/airflow/__init__.py`. If the goal is to ensure the editable install includes the `airflow.sdk` namespace/package, you likely want to `touch ./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/__init__.py` (and possibly also `touch ./task_sdk/src/airflow/__init__.py` depending on whether `airflow` is intended to be a namespace package). As written, the created directory may still be excluded from the editable install if it contains no files.

The glob pattern for task-sdk looks too broad and may match unintended artifacts (e.g. any file starting with `apache-airflow-task-sdk` regardless of version/extension), and it’s also inconsistent with the airflow/providers patterns that include a version prefix. Consider tightening it to include the version segment and/or expected extensions, e.g. `apache[_-]airflow[_-]task[_-]sdk-[0-9]` (and let the existing logic append format-specific suffixes), to avoid picking up stale/renamed files in `dist/`.

This new command group is defined but (in this hunk) not wired into whatever top-level list/registry aggregates release-management command groups (e.g. a list of sections shown in help). Please ensure `RELEASE_AIRFLOW_TASK_SDK_COMMANDS` is included wherever `RELEASE_AIRFLOW_COMMANDS` / `RELEASE_OTHER_COMMANDS` are collected, otherwise it may never appear in the CLI help/menu even though the command exists.

Make sure the command key exactly matches the actual CLI command name/registration (including hyphenation) and that the handler supports these options. If `--package-format` is intended to be constrained (e.g. sdist/wheel), consider documenting allowed values here or using a dedicated option group name to avoid ambiguity with other package commands.

This assumes `task_sdk/tests` always exists and is readable; `Path.iterdir()` will raise if the directory is missing (e.g., partial checkouts, packaging-only contexts, or early stages of the skeleton). Consider guarding with `if not path.exists(): return []` (or `is_dir()`), and possibly filtering only Python packages (e.g., `candidate.is_dir() and (candidate / "__init__.py").exists()`) to avoid picking up non-package directories like `__pycache__`, `.pytest_cache`, or `__fixtures__`.

Please double-check the naming/path consistency with the PR/issue intent. The issue/PR talks about an `airflow-sdk` (or task SDK) dist, but this mounts `task_sdk` into `/opt/airflow/task_sdk`. If the new top-level package directory is intended to be `airflow_sdk` (or `airflow/sdk`), consider aligning the host folder name and container mount path to avoid confusion and mismatches with packaging/import paths. Also ensure this mount is actually needed for all Breeze commands (and doesn’t shadow an installed package in the image); if it’s only for certain workflows, it may be better to gate it behind a feature flag or specific command to reduce unintended side effects.

Please double-check the path and naming here. The issue/PR text refers to an `airflow-sdk` dist and a top-level `airflow_sdk`/SDK package, but this entry points to `task_sdk/tests`. If the new project lives under a different directory (e.g., `airflow_sdk/tests` or `airflow-sdk/tests`), Breeze will silently not exclude the intended tests. Also consider whether this should be `task_sdk/tests` vs `task_sdk/tests/` and whether the group name should match existing conventions (e.g., `Task SDK` vs `TaskSDK`) to avoid confusion in CLI output/help.

The regex is hard-coded to `task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/.*\.py$`, which assumes the Task SDK code will always live under `airflow/sdk`. If the intent is to treat the whole `task_sdk/src` tree as TaskSDK-related (including non-`airflow/sdk` modules, package metadata, or future layout changes), consider broadening this to `^task_sdk/src/.*` (and similarly for tests) or at least adding patterns for non-`.py` files that should trigger TaskSDK checks (e.g. `pyproject.toml`, `uv.lock/poetry.lock`, `README`, etc.). Otherwise, changes to packaging/config files won’t trigger TaskSDK CI selectively, which can lead to missed failures.

Adding `TaskSDK` to the expected test-type list is fine, but this test is asserting an exact space-delimited string. That makes it brittle if the canonical ordering of test types changes elsewhere (or if `TaskSDK` is conditionally included). Consider deriving the expected value from the same source of truth used by Breeze (e.g., a constant/list of all test types) and comparing as sets or normalized/sorted tokens, or at least ensure `TaskSDK` is added consistently in the central list so ordering stays stable across all expectations.

Adding `task_sdk.tests` to the `testing` isort section is fine, but ensure this matches the actual import namespace used in the new project. If the package is intended to be `airflow.sdk` (or similar) rather than `task_sdk`, this will misclassify imports and cause churn in formatting. Consider aligning this entry with the real top-level module name used by the SDK tests (and add any other test namespaces if they differ).

If `apache-airflow-task-sdk` is a workspace member, adding it as a dev-dependency can be redundant and may create confusing resolution behavior (editable/workspace vs. dependency). Verify whether you actually need it in `dev-dependencies` (e.g., for non-workspace installs/CI jobs) or if workspace membership alone is sufficient. Also double-check the distribution name is consistent with the issue/PR wording (`airflow-sdk` vs `apache-airflow-task-sdk`) to avoid later renames and packaging metadata drift.

Make sure the workspace source key `apache-airflow-task-sdk` exactly matches the `[project].name` in `task_sdk/pyproject.toml` (uv requires the names to match). If the SDK project ends up being named differently (e.g., `airflow-sdk`), this mapping will break workspace resolution.

Adding `task_sdk` as a workspace member is good, but ensure the folder name here matches the actual workspace directory and that it contains a valid `pyproject.toml`. If the intended top-level folder is `airflow_sdk` or `airflow-sdk`, this will fail workspace discovery.

Binding the repo folder into the container is fine for local dev, but please double-check the naming/target path matches the intended distribution name and import namespace. The issue/PR text refers to an `airflow-sdk` dist, while this mounts `task_sdk` to `/opt/airflow/task_sdk`. If the package is meant to be `airflow_sdk` (or similar) as a top-level namespace package, consider aligning the directory name and mount target to avoid confusion and import/path mismatches in CI/local runs. Also ensure this mount doesn’t unintentionally shadow an installed package inside the image (editable vs installed), and that any tooling (breeze/pytest) expects the same path.

Adding the new editable install is fine, but please ensure the path/name matches the actual project layout and intended distribution name. The issue/PR text mentions `airflow-sdk` while this points to `./task_sdk`; if the folder is renamed later, this will silently break CI. Also consider whether this should be installed with extras (e.g. `-e ./task_sdk[devel]`) to ensure any test/CI-only dependencies for the SDK are present in the k8s image, otherwise CI may pass here but fail when SDK tests are added.

This creates `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/` but the placeholder file is touched at `./task_sdk/src/airflow/__init__.py`. If the goal is to ensure the editable install includes the `airflow.sdk` namespace/package, consider touching `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/__init__.py` (and/or ensuring `airflow` is a namespace package per packaging config). As written, you might still end up with an empty `airflow/sdk` not being recognized as a package depending on how `task_sdk` is packaged.

This README currently contains only the ASF license header inside an HTML comment. For a new top-level project skeleton, it would be more useful to include at least a minimal visible README (purpose of `task_sdk`/`airflow-sdk`, how to build/install, and how to run tests). Also consider whether the license header needs to be commented out here—most Airflow markdown files keep the header as plain text at the top; hiding it in an HTML comment can make it easy to miss and may be inconsistent with repository conventions.

Pointing the wheel build at `src/airflow` will package the `airflow` top-level namespace from this project, which is risky in a monorepo where the main Airflow distribution also provides `airflow`. If the intent is a namespace package, ensure this is explicitly configured (PEP 420) and that the package layout avoids collisions (e.g., `airflow/sdk/...` only) and includes the necessary `__init__.py`/namespace markers. Also consider using Hatch's `packages = ["src"]` with an include filter (or `only-include`) to avoid accidentally shipping other `airflow.*` modules in the future. Finally, verify that the sdist includes the same package set; otherwise installs from sdist may differ from wheel.

Having a `hello()` function in the package `__init__` looks like placeholder/demo code and will become part of the public API surface of `airflow.sdk`. For a skeleton project, consider removing this entirely (or moving it under tests/examples) and keeping `__init__.py` minimal (e.g., just `__all__`, version metadata, or namespace-package setup). If you need a smoke-test entrypoint, prefer a dedicated module (e.g., `airflow.sdk._internal` or `airflow.sdk.cli`) to avoid committing to a stable API accidentally.

Per PEP 561, `py.typed` should be an empty marker file (or at most contain whitespace). Adding comments/license text can cause some tooling to not recognize it as the marker file. Suggest making this file empty and moving the license header to a nearby documented file (e.g., `LICENSE`/`NOTICE`) or rely on repository-level licensing.

This file is currently empty aside from the license header. If `task_sdk/tests` is intended to be a package, consider adding a short module docstring explaining its purpose (even as a placeholder). If it’s only a test collection directory for pytest, you can also omit `__init__.py` entirely to avoid turning it into a package (which can affect import resolution and test discovery in some setups).

Setting `config.inicfg["airflow_deprecations_ignore"] = ()` unconditionally can override any value provided by the user's `pyproject.toml`/`pytest.ini` and may also fail if `inicfg` is not mutable in some pytest versions. Consider using `setdefault` (or only setting when the key is absent) to avoid clobbering configuration, e.g. `config.inicfg.setdefault("airflow_deprecations_ignore", ())`, or use `config.addinivalue_line`/`config.getini` depending on how this option is defined in the plugin.

This import assumes the new distribution exposes a top-level `airflow.sdk` package at test time. If the intent is to test the `task_sdk` project in isolation, consider importing from the local package (e.g. `task_sdk` / `airflow_sdk`) or ensure the test environment installs the built dist so `airflow.sdk` is resolvable. Otherwise this can become brittle in CI (tests passing only when run from the monorepo with `airflow` on `PYTHONPATH`) and may mask packaging/namespace issues.

Adding `end_date` is useful, but please ensure it is consistently timezone-aware. Airflow typically uses timezone-aware datetimes (UTC) and can be strict about naive values. Consider using Airflow's `DateTime`/timezone-aware type (or at least documenting/enforcing UTC) and clarify whether this field is always set for terminal states. Also verify serialization expectations across the comms boundary (e.g., ISO format) and whether `None` is acceptable/handled by the supervisor for all terminal states.

Hard-coding a 20s default (and making it `init=False`) makes this behavior non-configurable and potentially surprising in production/tests. Since the comment says this should come from `[core] task_success_overtime`, consider wiring this value from config (or injecting it via `__init__`/factory) and defaulting to `None`/disabled when unset. Also, the PR description says overtime now applies to all states, but this implementation only triggers on `SUCCESS`; either update the logic or adjust naming/docs to match the intended behavior.

This computes overtime as 0 when `_task_end_datetime` is missing, which can silently disable the kill path if the TaskState message is never received (or arrives late). Consider explicitly guarding for `None` (e.g., return early until end_date is known, or fall back to a different timestamp like process exit time) and/or logging when SUCCESS is set without an end_date to avoid masking protocol issues.

`_terminal_state` is typed as `str | None`, but here it's assigned `msg.state` (likely an enum such as `TerminalTIState`). This can lead to subtle comparison/serialization issues (e.g., comparing enum to string in `_handle_task_overtime_if_needed`). Consider tightening the type to the actual enum type (or `TerminalTIState | IntermediateTIState | None`) and ensuring `TaskState.state` uses the same type as other state assignments (e.g., `IntermediateTIState.DEFERRED`).

This sets `end_date` only for the SUCCESS path, but other terminal outcomes (e.g. SKIPPED below, and any future terminal states) will not report an `end_date` to the supervisor. If the supervisor relies on `end_date` for duration/overtime/metrics consistency, consider always populating `end_date` for any terminal `TaskState` message (or have the supervisor set it when missing). Also, ensure the timezone-aware `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` matches the expected serialization format for the comms/datamodels.

This assertion hard-codes the exact `kill()` call signature (`SIGTERM, force=True`). That makes the test brittle if the supervisor changes its termination strategy (e.g., different signal, no `force` kwarg, or escalation path). Consider asserting more semantically: that `kill` was invoked (and optionally that the first arg is a termination signal) rather than the full signature, or split into two tests (one for “overtime triggers termination” and another specifically for “uses SIGTERM+force”). Also, since `mock_kill` is patched on the class, ensure the call is actually coming from this instance’s `wait()` path and not from other code paths executed during `wait()`; using `assert_called_once()` plus checking `mock_kill.call_args[0][0]` may be more resilient.

This assertion hard-codes an exact `end_date` equality to `instant`, which can be brittle if `run()` sets `end_date` with timezone-awareness differences, microseconds, or uses `timezone.utcnow()`/`timezone.now()` internally (even with `tick=False`, some code paths may still add microseconds). Consider asserting on the message type/state and that `end_date` is close to `instant` (or equals after normalizing tzinfo), e.g. inspect `call_args.kwargs["msg"]` and compare `msg.state == TerminalTIState.SUCCESS` and `msg.end_date == instant` after `timezone.coerce_datetime`, or allow a small delta. Also, if `TaskState` is a dataclass, `assert_called_once_with` will require exact equality; using `assert_called_once()` + manual field assertions is usually more robust.

This change removes the pre-check for an existing asset-triggered DagRun with the same (dag_id, run_after). As written, the scheduler will now always attempt to create a new DagRun each loop for the same `triggered_date` until the queue entry is deleted/committed, which can lead to duplicate DagRuns (or IntegrityError if there is a unique constraint) and potential session rollback issues. Consider restoring an existence check (either bulk like before, or per-dag `select exists`) or handling `IntegrityError` around `create_dagrun` with a safe rollback and skipping creation. Also ensure the `AssetDagRunQueue` delete happens only after successful DagRun creation/flush to avoid losing the trigger if creation fails.

For consistency with other provider integrations, consider pointing `external-doc-url` to the official Google Calendar API documentation (e.g. https://developers.google.com/calendar/api/v3/reference) rather than the end-user web UI (calendar.google.com). This is especially relevant since the PR adds an API hook and users will look for API reference/auth scopes/quotas.

The Calendar API expects RFC3339 timestamp strings for `timeMax`/`timeMin` (and `updatedMin`), but this hook passes `datetime` objects through directly. Depending on the googleapiclient version, this may not serialize as expected and can lead to request errors or incorrect filtering. Consider converting datetimes to RFC3339 strings (e.g., `time_max.isoformat()` with timezone handling) before calling `.list()`, and validate/normalize naive datetimes (either reject them or assume a timezone). Also note that `timeMin` is documented as inclusive for start time (not “exclusive for end time” as in the docstring).

Adding a new binary asset is fine, but please ensure it follows the repo’s documentation asset conventions: (1) confirm the filename matches the naming scheme used in `docs/integration-logos/gcp/` (case, hyphens, etc.) and is referenced from the provider docs; (2) verify the image is optimized (PNG size/compression) to avoid bloating the repo; (3) ensure licensing/attribution is compliant for the Google Calendar logo (Google brand assets often have usage restrictions) and that any required attribution is documented where other integration logos are documented.

Setting `dag_ids` to `missing=None` makes the field optional, but it also means the endpoint can be called with no DAG filter and potentially return *all* dag runs across the instance. For a batch/list endpoint this can be very expensive and may be unintended. Consider either (a) requiring `dag_ids` (or at least enforcing a non-empty list when provided), or (b) using `missing=list` (empty list) and explicitly handling empty vs. omitted in the handler with a clear default/limit. Also, if you keep it optional, it would be good to document/validate that `page_limit` is capped server-side to prevent unbounded queries.

`_create_dag_runs` builds `dag_runs` but never returns it anymore, and the `create_session()` context only calls `add_all` without an explicit `commit()`. This makes the helper's behavior ambiguous and can lead to flaky tests depending on whether `create_session()` auto-commits on exit. Suggest: (1) `return dag_runs` (even if callers currently ignore it) for consistency with the previous implementation and other helpers, and (2) explicitly `session.commit()` (or use `session.flush()` if you only need PKs) to ensure the data is persisted before the API call.

Calling `process.exit(1)` will force termination, but it can also bypass pending async cleanup (e.g. flushing stdout/stderr, releasing resources, finishing telemetry) and can truncate output in some environments. If the goal is to ensure the process actually exits on error, consider first ensuring the top-level async IIFE is properly awaited/handled (e.g. return/await the promise and set `process.exitCode = 1`), and only fall back to `process.exit(1)` if the event loop is kept alive unexpectedly. If you keep `process.exit(1)`, it may be safer to write to stderr synchronously or explicitly flush/close streams before exiting to avoid losing the error message.

The synthesized-constructor detection relies on the temp variable name starting with `_this`. This is a heuristic and could be brittle across TS versions/minifiers (e.g. different temp names or no `_this` assignment form). Consider loosening this check to focus on the initializer shape (the `(_super...apply(this, ...)) || this` pattern) rather than the variable name, or at least document why `_this` is safe for the ESM5 host inputs and add a regression test covering alternative emit forms (e.g. `var _a = ...` or `var _this_1 = ...`).

The synthesized file header for the inline helper uses `var __spread = (this && this.__spread) || ...`, but in CommonJS modules `this` at the top-level is typically `exports`/`module.exports` (or `undefined` in strict mode), whereas TS’s emitted helper usually uses a global object pattern (e.g. `var __spread = (this && this.__spread) || function...` in non-module scripts) or `tslib.__spread` when imported. Since these tests are specifically about matching constructor patterns, consider making the helper stub closer to actual TS downlevel output for CommonJS (or at least add a comment explaining the intentional simplification). Otherwise the test may be brittle/misleading if the host behavior ever depends on how the helper is referenced/resolved.

The inlined helper stub uses rest parameters (`function (...args)`) which is not ES5 syntax. Since this spec is explicitly about ESM5/ES5 output patterns, using ES2015 syntax in the generated test file could make the test less representative (and potentially brittle if parsing/transform assumptions change). Consider rewriting the stub to ES5-compatible syntax (e.g. `function () { var args = []; for (var i = 0; i < arguments.length; i++) args[i] = arguments[i]; /* ... */ }`) or even simpler: avoid defining a function body at all and just declare `var __spread = function() {};` if the host only needs the identifier to exist.

There are trailing spaces after the opening backtick in this template literal (`` `            ``). This can make the generated UMD header differ subtly from the other cases and is easy to miss in reviews. Please remove the extra whitespace (or align with the other `fileHeaderWithUmd` assignments) to keep the fixture deterministic and avoid brittle snapshot/string-based parsing issues in tests.

This looks like a doc comment formatting fix, but please ensure the fenced code block is still correctly nested under the list item (if it’s intended to be a bullet). Previously it was `*  * ````, which is a common pattern for a code fence inside a bullet list in TSDoc/Markdown. Changing it to `* ``` ` may alter how the docs render (e.g., losing the bullet indentation or breaking the list). If the goal is to remove the list, consider also removing the preceding list marker text ("*  *") consistently across the whole comment, or keep the extra `*` to preserve list formatting.

The deprecated `flags?: InjectFlags` overload still returns `T | null` unconditionally, which preserves the original typing issue: `null` should only be possible when `InjectFlags.Optional` is set. If the goal is to fix invalid typing (and allow TS narrowing), consider splitting this deprecated overload into more specific overloads (e.g., one for `InjectFlags.Optional` returning `T | null` and one for other flags returning `T`), or otherwise constrain the return type based on the presence of `Optional`. As-is, callers using the deprecated signature will still be forced to handle `null` even when not optional.

Exporting `InjectOptions` from the public `@angular/core/di` index changes the public API surface. Please ensure this is intentional and that `InjectOptions` is marked/typed as a stable public API (or prefixed/annotated appropriately if it’s meant to be internal). Also verify the symbol is actually exported from `./injector_compatibility` and included in the public API golden files / API extractor baselines, and consider whether it should be re-exported from the top-level `@angular/core` entrypoint as well for discoverability/consistency with `inject()`.

The overload `options: InjectOptions & {optional?: false}` won’t narrow the return type when callers pass a plain `InjectOptions` variable (since `optional?: boolean` is still compatible). Consider using a discriminated union / literal-typed overloads to enable better narrowing, e.g. `options?: Omit<InjectOptions, 'optional'> & {optional?: false}` for the non-optional case and `options: InjectOptions & {optional: true}` for the nullable case. Also consider adding an overload for `options?: InjectOptions` (optional parameter) to preserve the common `inject(token)` / `inject(token, undefined)` ergonomics without forcing the deprecated flags signature.

Since this deprecation is specifically about the *public* `inject()` API moving from bit flags to an options object, consider tightening the JSDoc to avoid implying `InjectFlags` is generally deprecated for all DI usage. E.g. clarify that `InjectFlags` is deprecated *as an argument to `inject()`* (and point to the new `InjectOptions` type / signature), and ensure the deprecation tag matches the actual exported API name (`inject`, not `ɵɵinject`) to reduce confusion for users and tooling.

This import list is extremely long and the change adds `ViewEncapsulation` solely for a single test below. Consider either (a) moving the new options-object `inject()` tests into a smaller dedicated spec file with minimal imports, or (b) switching to a namespace import (`import * as core from '@angular/core'`) / grouped imports to keep diffs readable and reduce merge conflicts. Also double-check that `ViewEncapsulation` is actually required for the host-injection test; if it’s not essential to the behavior under test, removing it would avoid pulling in another symbol here.

Using `Intl.DateTimeFormat('fr', {timeZone, timeZoneName: 'short'})` as a workaround is fragile: `timeZoneName: 'short'` is not specified to always return an `UTC±hh[:mm]` style string across engines/locales, and relying on a specific locale (`fr`) is an implicit contract that may break with ICU/CLDR updates or platform differences. Also, `Intl.DateTimeFormat` can throw a `RangeError` for unknown/unsupported IANA zones, and `shortOffset!` will crash if `formatToParts()` doesn't include a `timeZoneName` part. Consider (a) guarding with try/catch and falling back to the existing offset parsing path, (b) validating the extracted value matches an expected `^UTC[+-]` pattern before `replace('UTC','')`, and (c) documenting/centralizing this hack with a clear fallback behavior. If possible, prefer `timeZoneName: 'shortOffset'` when available (feature-detect) and only use this locale hack as a fallback.

Doc update is good, but please ensure the wording matches actual supported behavior and platform constraints. IANA time zone support depends on `Intl.DateTimeFormat` timeZone support (and may vary across environments / Node builds), so consider clarifying that this requires an environment with IANA/Intl time zone data and that invalid/unsupported IANA IDs will fall back or throw (whichever the implementation does). Also, the phrase “An IANA time zone name” should be consistent with other docs (e.g., `formatDate`/`DATE_PIPE_DEFAULT_OPTIONS`) and ideally mention that offsets/abbreviations are still accepted for backward compatibility.

This assertion hard-codes the en-US `short` date/time output (`11/7/22, 8:00 AM`). `formatDate(..., ɵDEFAULT_LOCALE_ID, ...)` can vary depending on what `ɵDEFAULT_LOCALE_ID` resolves to in the test environment (and potentially across CLDR/ICU/Node versions). To make the test more robust, consider explicitly passing a fixed locale (e.g. `'en-US'`) or asserting on a less locale-sensitive format (e.g. a custom pattern like `y-MM-dd HH:mm`), so the test validates the timezone/DST behavior rather than locale formatting quirks.

These expectations hard-code locale-specific output (e.g. `11/7/22, 8:00 AM`) and can be flaky across environments/ICU versions (Node vs browser, full-ICU vs small-ICU, or locale data changes). Since the feature under test is IANA TZ handling (incl. DST), consider asserting on more stable parts: e.g. use a fixed locale explicitly in the pipe setup (or in the test module) and/or assert via `formatToParts`-like parsing (hour/minute) rather than the full formatted string. Also consider adding a guard/skip if `Intl` time zone data is unavailable in the test runtime, otherwise CI may fail on minimal-ICU builds.

Using `nativeListener.call(signal, 'abort', ...)` assumes `AbortSignal` implements `addEventListener` and that `nativeListener` is compatible with it. In this patch, `nativeListener` is the (patched) target's native `addEventListener`, not necessarily `AbortSignal.prototype.addEventListener`. This can break when the event target being patched is not the same interface as `AbortSignal` (e.g., patching `Window`/`HTMLElement` but calling its native listener with `this=signal`). Consider calling `signal.addEventListener('abort', ...)` (or capturing `AbortSignal.prototype.addEventListener` if you need the unpatched version) instead of reusing `nativeListener` from the patched target.

Changing the default from `null` to `{}` and then mutating `options.signal` can unintentionally change behavior for callers that pass a non-plain object (e.g. `Request` as the second arg) or rely on the original options object identity. `fetch(input, request)` is valid and `Request` instances have a read-only `signal` in many implementations, so `options.signal = ...` may throw. Consider guarding with `if (args.length > 1 && options && typeof options === 'object' && !(options instanceof Request))` (or feature-detect `Request`) and otherwise create a new init object (e.g. `const init = {...options, signal: fetchSignal}`) rather than mutating the provided value.

This change switches the test from explicitly calling `removeEventListener` to relying on `AbortController.abort()` to remove the listener. That’s good for covering the new `signal` behavior, but it also changes what’s being asserted: you’re no longer verifying that `removeEventListener('click', listener1)` works for a listener registered with `{signal}`. Consider keeping the explicit `removeEventListener` assertion in a dedicated test (or asserting both paths) to avoid losing coverage for manual removal semantics, especially since the bug report involves handler coalescing and could affect both abort-based and manual removal.

The YAML indentation here looks incorrect for a changelog fragment. `bugfixes:` should be a top-level key (no leading spaces), and the list item should be indented under it. As written, the leading spaces may cause the fragment to be parsed as a nested mapping or fail linting. Suggest:

```yaml
bugfixes:
  - "import_tasks - raise an error when invalid params are specified (https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/64935)."
```

Hard-coding a long list of “valid Task-level keywords” in module docs is likely to drift from the actual parser/TaskInclude validation over time (and may vary by Ansible version). Since this PR is about raising errors for invalid attributes like `notify`/`register`, consider either (a) documenting the *invalid* keywords explicitly (e.g., “notify/register are not supported; use include_tasks”), or (b) referencing the authoritative validation source (TaskInclude/Task keyword docs) rather than enumerating a list here. If you keep the list, please ensure it is complete/accurate (e.g., common task keywords like `loop`, `loop_control`, `until`, `retries`, `delay`, `failed_when`, `changed_when`, `any_errors_fatal`, `throttle`, `timeout`, etc. are notably absent) and consider formatting it as a YAML list for readability/maintainability.

This diff calculation will flag *all* keys not in VALID_IMPORT_KEYWORDS, including core task fields that are expected to exist in `ds` (e.g., `action` itself, `file`/`_raw_params`, `apply`, `vars`, etc.). That can cause false positives and raise/warn even for valid import_tasks usage. Consider computing the diff against the full set of allowed keys for import_tasks, e.g. `VALID_ARGS ∪ VALID_IMPORT_KEYWORDS` (and likely also include `action`), or explicitly subtract known internal keys before diffing. Also, `ds['action']` can KeyError if action is missing; safer to use `ds.get('action')`.

The `elif` here makes `always_child_state` mutually exclusive with `rescue_child_state`. If a state can have both (or if `always` should be cleared regardless of whether `rescue` exists), this will leave errors uncleared in the `always` branch when `rescue_child_state` is present. Consider using a separate `if` for `always_child_state` (like `tasks_child_state` and `rescue_child_state`) to ensure all child states are traversed and cleared.

This refactor centralizes error clearing, which is good, but please double-check that `clear_host_errors()` preserves the previous semantics of clearing *both* the active state's `fail_state` and the base host state. The removed code explicitly reset `fail_state` on `get_active_state(get_state_for_host(...))` and on `get_state_for_host(...)`; if `clear_host_errors()` only touches one of these (or behaves differently during handler/flush handler states), hosts may remain marked failed/unreachable in some iterator states. Consider adding/adjusting a unit/integration test that reproduces the original bug (handlers + strategy free/serial) and asserts host failure state is cleared across all relevant iterator states after this path runs.

Moving `now` earlier may address the reported zipfile header error, but please avoid leaving commented-out code and unclear notes. Instead, remove the old assignment entirely and add a short, actionable comment explaining *why* `now` must be computed before zip creation (e.g., ZipInfo timestamp range/consistency across Python versions) and ideally link to the issue/PR. Also consider whether `now` should be computed only when the cache file is being created (inside the `if not os.path.exists(...)` block) to avoid unnecessary work and to keep semantics tight.

Using the PEP 604 union operator (`|`) requires Python 3.10+. If Ansible still supports older Python versions, this will break at runtime even though the annotation is quoted. Consider using `t.Optional["FinalQueue"]` (and `import typing as t`) or `"FinalQueue"` with `None` via `t.Optional` to keep compatibility. Also, if `FinalQueue` is only needed for typing, you can avoid importing it entirely by using a forward reference string without the TYPE_CHECKING import, unless you need it for static checkers to resolve the symbol.

The fragment content appears to include prefixed line numbers ("1:") as literal YAML, which will break the changelog fragment format. Changelog fragments should start directly with `---` (or omit it) and then the sections (e.g., `bugfixes:`). Please remove the `1:`, `2:`, `3:` prefixes so this is valid YAML and can be consumed by the release tooling.

Using a dynamic key in set_fact is fine, but quoting the entire Jinja expression as a YAML string key (`'{{ ... }}'`) is unusual and can be brittle/readability-hostile in docs. Prefer an unquoted key expression (`{{ columns[item|int] }}`) or, even clearer, build a dict and combine it (e.g., `set_fact: bgp_facts: "{{ bgp_facts|default({}) | combine({columns[item|int]: csvline}) }}"`). Also note this example sets one fact per loop iteration; consider showing the final structure (or using a single dict fact) to avoid confusion.

The fragment content appears to include prefixed line numbers/indentation ("           1:---", etc.), which will make the YAML invalid for Ansible’s changelog tooling. This file should be plain YAML starting with `---` on the first line (no leading spaces, no `1:`/`2:` prefixes). Please regenerate/clean the fragment to:

```yaml
---
bugfixes:
  - facts - gather pagesize and calculate respective values depending upon architecture (https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/84773).
```

Casting `self.sysctl['hw.pagesize']` directly to `int` can raise `ValueError` if the sysctl value is missing/empty or formatted unexpectedly (e.g., includes whitespace/newlines). Consider using `self.sysctl.get('hw.pagesize')` and guarding the conversion with `try/except` (falling back to 4096) or normalizing via `.strip()` before `int(...)` to keep fact collection resilient.

This fixture hard-codes `hw.pagesize: 4096`, which is correct for Intel macOS but differs from Apple Silicon (often 16384). Since the PR is about using `hw.pagesize` dynamically, ensure the unit tests explicitly cover both architectures by using this new Intel fixture *and* an Apple Silicon fixture with `hw.pagesize: 16384`, and that the tests select the appropriate fixture based on the simulated platform/arch. Otherwise, the tests may still be self-referential and miss regressions where the collector falls back to 4096.

This hunk only renames the fixture file. Please ensure all unit tests and any code that references the old path (`fixtures/sysctl_darwin.txt`) are updated accordingly (e.g., test parametrization/fixtures list). Also consider whether you still need an Intel/4K pagesize fixture alongside this new Apple Silicon one; if so, keep the original name for the 4K fixture and add this as an additional fixture rather than a rename, to avoid losing coverage for non-Apple-Silicon macOS.

This invalid-character validation is now guarded by `#ifdef _WIN32`, but the comment says "Only on Windows" while the code still includes `/` and `\\` in `invalid_chars` and no longer checks for `\\` at all. On Windows, backslash is a path separator, so allowing it here is likely intentional for full paths, but then it shouldn't be listed as invalid. Also, `:` is valid in drive prefixes (e.g. `C:\foo\bar.png`), so rejecting any `:` will still prevent selecting files by full path on Windows. Suggestion: (1) make the check consistent with the intent: if you want to allow full/relative paths, validate only truly illegal characters for Windows filenames *excluding* path components (e.g. check each path segment, or allow `:` only as `^[A-Za-z]:` at the beginning, and allow `\\`/`/` as separators). (2) Update `invalid_chars` to match the actual set being rejected, and consider building it conditionally to avoid misleading users.

This assertion hard-codes the expected HEADERS frame payload length (55), which is brittle and can break with unrelated changes (e.g., different default response headers, HPACK dynamic table behavior, date formatting, or encoder changes). Consider asserting on the decoded headers/content instead of the raw frame length, or compute the expected length from the actual frame received (e.g., just assert frame type/flags/stream id and then decode). If you want to keep a length check, add a comment explaining why 55 is stable in this scenario and what would legitimately change it.

This test helper now depends on `_serviceContext.ServerOptions` being initialized and having a stable `Latin1RequestHeaders` value. If any tests construct `Http2TestBase` without fully populating `ServerOptions`, this could introduce a null/ordering dependency. Consider capturing the option into a local (e.g., `_useLatin1RequestHeaders`) during setup/constructor and using that here, or ensure `_serviceContext`/`ServerOptions` are always non-null in this test base. Also, since the issue scope mentions different control-character rejection behavior under Latin1, it would be good to add/adjust tests that explicitly cover both `Latin1RequestHeaders = true/false` paths for header decoding in HTTP/2.

Project name/path contain a typo: "Comopnents" should be "Components". As-is, this will create an incorrectly named project in the solution and likely break builds/packaging that expect the correct assembly/package naming. Please fix both the displayed project name and the referenced .csproj path to use "Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.HttpHandler" (and corresponding folder/file name).

The XML doc for `NoCache` is identical to `Default`/`ForceCache` (“looks for a matching request in its HTTP cache”), which is misleading. In the Fetch API, `no-cache` means the browser may use a cached response but must revalidate with the origin (i.e., conditional request) before using it. Consider updating the summary to reflect revalidation semantics (and similarly ensure `ForceCache`/`Default` descriptions match their actual Fetch behaviors).

Consider explicitly assigning underlying values (e.g., Omit = 0, SameOrigin = 1, Include = 2) to lock the public API contract. Since this is a ref-assembly/stop-gap package, consumers may persist/compare these values and future reordering would be a breaking change.

Consider whether exposing `Navigate` is appropriate for this API surface. In the Fetch spec, `navigate` is generally reserved for browser navigation requests and is not a valid/meaningful mode for programmatic `fetch()` calls; some environments may ignore it or behave unexpectedly. If this enum is meant to map to `RequestMode`, it may be safer to omit `Navigate` (or keep it internal) unless the handler explicitly supports it and tests cover the behavior. If you keep it, please document the limitations/expected behavior when used from `HttpClient`.

Consider avoiding a raw string key in `HttpRequestMessage.Properties` here. Using a hard-coded key ("WebAssemblyReadResponseAsStream") risks collisions with other libraries and makes refactoring/error detection harder. Prefer a `private const string` (shared with the handler) or, ideally, a unique key type (e.g., a private static object) and/or `HttpRequestOptions` (in newer TFMs) to store per-request options. Also, since the XML docs mention a `streamingEnabled` parameter but the method has none, either add an overload that accepts a bool or fix the docs to match the API.

The referenced project path/name appears to contain a typo: `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Comopnents.WebAssembly.HttpHandler.csproj` ("Comopnents" vs "Components"). If this is not intentional, it will break the build/restore on case-sensitive filesystems and is easy to miss in CI depending on environment. Please verify and correct the filename/path to match the actual project.

Setting `Private="true"` on a `ProjectReference` is unusual and may not have the intended effect (it controls CopyLocal for assembly references; with `ReferenceOutputAssembly="false"` there may be nothing to copy). If the goal is to prevent the referenced project's outputs from flowing transitively to consumers, consider using `PrivateAssets="all"` (and/or `ExcludeAssets`) instead, which is the standard way to stop transitive asset propagation in SDK-style projects. Also double-check `IncludeAssets="commit"`—that value is not a typical MSBuild asset category and may be a typo for `compile`/`contentfiles`/etc.

Renaming the assembly to `System.Net.Http.WebAssemblyHttpHandler` is a breaking change for consumers that may have referenced the previous assembly name (`WebAssembly.Net.Http`) directly (e.g., binding redirects, analyzer rules, or reflection-based loads). Please double-check this matches the intended final assembly identity in the runtime and that the package/nuspec (and any docs/samples) are updated accordingly. Also consider whether we need a compatibility shim/forwarders or at least a clear release note since this is a ref-assembly-only package and assembly identity is what consumers compile against.

Placing this type in the BCL namespace (`System.Net.Http`) is a high-risk choice: it can create type/namespace collisions, confuse consumers (it looks like a framework type), and complicate binding/forwarding when the runtime eventually ships its own handler. If the goal is a stop-gap ref assembly, consider keeping the type in a product namespace (e.g., `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.Http`) and using type-forwarding later, or ensure this is strictly an implementation detail not exposed to app code. Also, the file/package name contains a typo (`Comopnents`) which will leak into assembly/package identity and should be corrected before shipping.

This is a ref-assembly stub, but inheriting from `HttpMessageHandler` in `System.Net.Http` while also fully-qualifying `System.Net.Http.HttpMessageHandler` is redundant and suggests namespace confusion. Prefer `: HttpMessageHandler` inside this namespace for clarity. More importantly, verify the public surface matches the intended runtime type name: the diff removes `WasmHttpMessageHandler` and related option enums/properties; if any existing consumers depend on those, this is a breaking change. If the rename is intentional, consider adding type-forwarders/obsolete shims to preserve compatibility (even in a stop-gap package).

In a reference assembly, `throw null;` is typical, but ensure this is actually produced as a ref assembly (so the method body is never executed). If this file can be compiled into a runtime assembly by mistake, `throw null;` will cause a `NullReferenceException` instead of a clearer `NotSupportedException`. Consider using `throw new NotSupportedException();` (or `throw null;` only in ref builds guarded by build configuration) to make accidental runtime use fail with a more actionable error.

The file/package naming here looks inconsistent with the PR intent and may be a breaking change for consumers. This nuspec is for `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Comopnents.WebAssembly.HttpHandler` (note the apparent typo in `Comopnents`), but it now ships `System.Net.Http.WebAssemblyHttpHandler.dll` under `lib/netstandard2.1`. Please verify (1) the output assembly name is actually `System.Net.Http.WebAssemblyHttpHandler.dll` in this project/build, (2) the package id/assembly name alignment is intentional, and (3) any existing references to `WebAssembly.Net.Http.dll` are updated. If this is meant to be a stop-gap ref-assembly package, consider adding/keeping a compatibility shim (or at least documenting the rename) to avoid breaking existing consumers.

Adding a global `@using` for `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.Http` in `_Imports.razor` makes the namespace available to every component in the test asset, which can hide missing `using` directives and potentially introduce type-name ambiguities (e.g., if other packages define similarly named types). If only a few components need these types (e.g., `WebAssemblyHttpMessageHandler`/options), consider scoping the `@using` to those components/pages instead. If the intent is to validate the new ref-assembly package is referenced and usable broadly, a short comment in the file/PR explaining why it’s global would help future maintainers.

This creates a new HttpClient with a custom DelegatingHandler to force credentials, but it no longer disposes the handler/client (previously DI created the HttpClient instance directly). Consider registering via IHttpClientFactory (AddHttpClient) or at least ensure the handler chain is disposed when the host shuts down. Also, if the intent is only to include credentials for cross-origin requests, verify this is the minimal scope: setting BrowserRequestCredentials.Include on every request may have side effects for same-origin calls and could mask tests that expect default behavior.

This change removes the AzureAD sample solution folder/project entries. If the intent is to fully replace these samples with Identity.Web-based equivalents, ensure the new sample projects (or updated ones) are added back into the solution (and nested correctly) so developers can still F5/run and validate the templates. Also double-check there are no remaining references to the removed project GUIDs elsewhere in the .sln (e.g., SolutionItems, build configurations, or nested projects) to avoid a broken solution load in VS.

Adding Microsoft.Identity.Web.UI to LatestPackageReference will make it flow through dependency update tooling, but ensure this is the correct package for all templates (RazorPages/MVC/WebApi). WebApi templates typically depend on Microsoft.Identity.Web (not the .UI package). If only some templates need the UI helpers, consider adding Microsoft.Identity.Web as well (or instead) to avoid pulling UI dependencies into non-UI scenarios and to keep package graph minimal.

Microsoft.Graph is a large dependency with frequent breaking changes across major versions. Before tracking it as a 'latest' package, confirm the templates actually require Graph (vs. optional sample code) and that the repo's dependency update policy can tolerate major-version bumps. If templates only need Graph in optional scenarios, consider pinning to a specific major version elsewhere or avoiding 'LatestPackageReference' to prevent unintended upgrades.

These are preview package versions. Please confirm this is intentional for the templates and that the repo’s dependency policy allows preview packages here. If so, consider adding a brief comment or aligning with a centrally-managed prerelease label (if one exists) to make future upgrades easier. Also verify whether Microsoft.Identity.Web and Microsoft.Identity.Web.UI should be kept in lockstep (same version) and whether MicrosoftGraph should be pinned to a compatible version range with Identity.Web to avoid transitive conflicts.

Marking the controller as `[Obsolete]` is fine, but consider whether this should be a compile-time error to prevent new usage (e.g., `Obsolete(..., error: true)`), especially since the message says it will be removed. Also, since this is an MVC controller that may be referenced by templates/consumers, verify the attribute is applied at the right surface area (controller vs. package/type) to avoid noisy warnings for existing apps that still rely on it. If the intent is to guide template users only, a doc/link in templates might be less disruptive than obsoleting the runtime type.

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is a good signal, but consider whether this should be an error to prevent new usage (e.g., `Obsolete(..., error: true)`) or at least ensure the message is consistent with the rest of the deprecation strategy in this package. Also consider specifying the ObsoleteAttribute 'error' flag (e.g., Obsolete(message, error: false)) explicitly to document intent, and ensure the message/URL is consistent with the recommended replacement APIs (WebApp vs WebApi) since this class contains both patterns.

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is fine, but consider whether this should be an error-level obsoletion to actively steer template consumers away (e.g., `Obsolete(..., error: true)`), or at least ensure the message is consistent with the rest of the AzureAD.UI deprecation surface. Also, if this assembly is used by external consumers, verify that obsoleting an `internal` type provides the intended guidance (it may not show up for most users); you may want to place the attribute on a more user-facing API as well.

Consider specifying the Obsolete diagnostic behavior explicitly. If this type is intended to be phased out but still usable for now, use `Obsolete(message, error: false)` (or omit `error` but be intentional). If you want to prevent new usage immediately, set `error: true`. Also verify the message/URL is consistent with other deprecation annotations in this package and that applying `[Obsolete]` to an `internal` type is sufficient to guide consumers (it will mainly affect this repo/assembly usage).

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is good for steering users to Microsoft.Identity.Web, but consider whether this should be an error in a future release (e.g., `Obsolete(..., error: true)`) and/or include a more actionable message (package name + migration doc link). Also, since this is an `internal` type, verify this attribute is actually surfaced to the intended consumers; if the goal is to warn app developers, the attribute may need to be placed on the public API surface they reference (or the containing assembly/package) rather than an internal implementation class.

Marking the entire options type as obsolete is a breaking change for consumers who treat warnings as errors. Consider using a softer deprecation path (e.g., add `error: false` explicitly and/or use `ObsoleteAttribute` with a diagnostic ID via `DiagnosticId`/`UrlFormat` if available in your target framework) and ensure the message is consistent with other deprecations in this package. Also verify that the `using System;` addition is placed correctly (the file header currently appears to have `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` appended to the license line), otherwise the build may still fail or style analyzers will complain.

Marking the type as obsolete is good for steering users to Microsoft.Identity.Web, but consider whether this should be a warning-only deprecation (default) or an error in a future major version. If the intent is to discourage new usage without breaking existing builds, keep it as-is; if you want to prevent usage in new templates only, you might prefer applying [Obsolete] at the public API surface (or adding a diagnostic/analyzer) rather than on an internal type. Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is good, but consider whether this should be a compile-time error to drive migration (e.g., `Obsolete(..., error: true)`), or at least ensure the message is consistent with the rest of the package deprecation strategy. For the `AddAzureADB2C` (OpenIdConnect UI) path, the recommended replacement in Microsoft.Identity.Web is typically `AddMicrosoftIdentityWebApp` (or equivalent web app helper), not the Web API helper. Marking the class as `[Obsolete]` is a good signal, but consider whether this should be `ObsoleteAttribute` with `error: false/true` explicitly to control build behavior (e.g., keep as warning now, potentially flip to error closer to removal). If the goal is to guide template consumers, you may also want to add the same guidance at the public API surface (e.g., extension methods/options) or in package docs/release notes so downstream users actually see it.

Adding `[Obsolete]` is a good step, but consider specifying `error: false` explicitly (or `true` if you intend to block new usage) to make the deprecation policy unambiguous. Suppressing CS0618 at the project level will hide all obsolete-API usage in these tests, which can mask new deprecations/regressions. Consider scoping the suppression more narrowly (e.g., only for specific files via <NoWarn> in an ItemGroup, or using #pragma warning disable/restore around the known obsolete calls) and add a tracking link/condition (e.g., only when building templates or until the package is removed) so this doesn’t become a permanent blanket suppression.

Suppressing CS0618 at the project level can hide new/accidental uses of obsolete APIs across the entire test project. If the intent is to keep a specific deprecated test compiling until it’s removed, consider scoping the suppression more narrowly (e.g., `#pragma warning disable CS0618` around the specific usage, or `NoWarn` on the specific `Compile` item) and add a tracking issue/cleanup note so this doesn’t become permanent. Also, ensure `$(NoWarn)` is defined in all build contexts; if not, you may want to guard against an empty value or use a separate property to avoid unexpected MSBuild concatenation behavior.

Adding new GeneratedContentProperties entries is fine, but ensure these MSBuild properties are actually defined for all target branches/TFMs (e.g., in Versions.props/eng/Versions.props) and have consistent naming with existing version properties. If any of these are missing, template generation will fail with an undefined property or produce empty versions in generated content.

Same concern as above: verify MicrosoftIdentityWebUIPackageVersion is defined and kept in sync with MicrosoftIdentityWebPackageVersion where required (Identity.Web and Identity.Web.UI often need compatible versions). Consider whether a single shared version property would reduce drift if the packages are expected to move together.

Introducing MicrosoftGraphPackageVersion implies templates will reference Microsoft.Graph. Confirm this dependency is required for all auth-enabled templates (and not only some scenarios), since it increases template surface area and restore time. Also ensure the property is defined centrally and that the chosen Graph SDK major version aligns with the target framework and Identity.Web guidance to avoid breaking changes in generated projects.

The Graph SDK package is being added unconditionally based only on `GenerateGraph`, but Graph calls typically require auth (OrganizationalAuth or B2C) and the corresponding Identity.Web configuration. Consider tightening the condition to also require `'$(OrganizationalAuth)' == 'True' OR '$(IndividualB2CAuth)' == 'True'` (or whatever auth modes the generated Graph code supports) to avoid pulling in Microsoft.Graph for templates that don't configure any AAD/B2C auth. Also, minor style: align spacing with other `Condition=` attributes (there’s an extra space before the closing `/>`).

The Graph package reference is conditioned only on `GenerateGraph`, but Graph usage typically also requires an AAD/B2C auth setup (and the new Identity.Web packages). As written, a template option combination could set `GenerateGraph=True` while neither `OrganizationalAuth` nor `IndividualB2CAuth` is true, producing a project that restores `Microsoft.Graph` without the corresponding auth/config scaffolding. Consider tightening the condition to also require the relevant auth option(s), e.g. `Condition="'$(GenerateGraph)' == 'True' AND ('$(IndividualB2CAuth)' == 'True' OR '$(OrganizationalAuth)' == 'True')"`, or ensure the template logic prevents invalid option combinations.

This adds Microsoft.Identity.Web unconditionally inside an ItemGroup that is guarded by the template directive `<!--#if (OrganizationalAuth || IndividualB2CAuth) -->`. That’s fine as long as the directive is preserved by the templating engine, but it’s easy to accidentally break/strip these comments in future edits. Consider also adding an explicit MSBuild `Condition` on the PackageReference (e.g., `Condition="'$(OrganizationalAuth)' == 'True' OR '$(IndividualB2CAuth)' == 'True'"`) to make the intent resilient even if the template preprocessor changes, and to avoid pulling Identity.Web into non-auth projects if the directive is ever removed.
