The Condition attribute has leading/trailing spaces inside the quotes (`Condition=" '$(GenerateGraph)' == 'True' "`). While MSBuild will usually tolerate this, it’s inconsistent with the rest of the file and can be error-prone in template processing. Recommend normalizing to `Condition="'$(GenerateGraph)' == 'True'"`.

Disabling CS0618 here suggests the template is still using obsolete AzureAD extensions (AddAzureAD/AddAzureADB2C). Since the issue/PR goal is to move templates to Microsoft.Identity.Web, consider replacing these calls with AddMicrosoftIdentityWebApp/AddMicrosoftIdentityWebApi (and related configuration) instead of suppressing the warning. If this suppression is only a temporary bridge, add a comment explaining why it’s needed and keep the pragma scope as tight as possible (ideally only around the specific obsolete call).

The pragma disable/restore placement around Configure<OpenIdConnectOptions>(...) looks off: the disable is before the call, but the restore is inserted before the lambda body, meaning the obsolete warning may still be emitted for the Configure call itself (and the restore may not be paired as intended). Consider moving the restore to after the full statement (after the closing ");") or wrapping the entire Configure call in the pragma block to ensure the suppression actually covers the obsolete API usage and doesn’t accidentally affect unrelated code.

Same concern as the OrganizationalAuth block: suppressing CS0618 indicates AddAzureADB2C is obsolete. Given the stated intent to incorporate Microsoft.Identity.Web templates, it would be better to migrate this template path to Microsoft.Identity.Web B2C support rather than suppressing warnings. If migration isn’t possible in this PR, document the rationale and ensure the pragma scope is minimal.

Suppressing CS0618 here suggests the template is still using obsolete AzureAD auth extensions. Since the issue/PR goal is to incorporate Microsoft.Identity.Web templates, consider replacing `AddAzureADBearer`/`AddAzureADB2CBearer` with the Identity.Web equivalents (e.g., `AddMicrosoftIdentityWebApi(...)` / `AddMicrosoftIdentityWebApiAuthentication(...)`) rather than adding a pragma. If this suppression is only a temporary bridge, add a TODO with a tracking issue and keep the suppression scope as tight as possible (ideally around the specific obsolete call) to avoid masking other obsoletions in this block.

Avoid setting an empty string for `shortName`. In dotnet template host config, `shortName` is expected to be a valid alias; an empty value can lead to confusing/help output or unexpected parsing behavior. If you don't want a short option, omit the `shortName` property entirely (or set it to `null` if supported by the schema) rather than `""`.
Same concern as above: `shortName` should not be an empty string. Either provide a meaningful short alias (e.g., `cau`/`cas`) or remove the property to indicate there is no short form.

Consider removing the empty `shortName` here as well. Also, since `CallsMicrosoftGraph` already has a longName `calls-graph`, you may want to align naming with other templates/parameters (e.g., `--calls-microsoft-graph` vs `--calls-graph`) for discoverability and consistency.

The computed expression for `GenerateApi` hard-codes the Graph default URL and default scope string. This is brittle: if the template’s defaults change (or if `CalledApiUrl`/`CalledApiScopes` are unset/empty), the condition may incorrectly flip and include/exclude API-related files. Consider basing this on explicit user intent (e.g., a dedicated `CallsDownstreamApi` boolean) or at least normalize/guard for empty/null values and whitespace/casing in `CalledApiScopes` (since scopes are often space-delimited lists). Also, `CalledApiScopes` has no `defaultValue` in this hunk, so ensure it’s always defined to avoid unexpected comparisons.

Rendering `ViewData["ApiResult"]` directly can lead to confusing output (e.g., null/empty) and potentially unsafe rendering if the value contains HTML. Razor will HTML-encode by default, but if `ApiResult` is ever set to `HtmlString`/pre-encoded content, it could bypass encoding. Consider strongly typing the page model (e.g., `IndexModel.ApiResult`) and rendering it in a `<pre>`/`<code>` block with a null/empty fallback, or explicitly ensure it’s treated as plain text (e.g., `@Html.Encode(...)`) to avoid accidental raw HTML output.

The attribute is only compiled when `GenerateApiOrGraph` is true, but it references a hard-coded configuration section name ("CalledApi:CalledApiScopes"). For the Graph template, this section name may not exist or may differ, which can cause runtime failures when the attribute tries to resolve scopes. Consider using a Graph-specific scope key section when `GenerateGraph` is enabled (e.g., `Graph:Scopes`), or ensure the template always emits the matching configuration section for both API and Graph variants. Also verify the section name matches the rest of the template's appsettings/README to avoid drift.

This injects `IOptions<MicrosoftIdentityOptions>` but the variable name `AzureADB2COptions` is now misleading (and the rest of the file still refers to B2C-specific properties like `EditProfilePolicyId`). Consider renaming the injected variable to something like `MicrosoftIdentityOptions`/`IdentityOptions` for clarity, and verify that `MicrosoftIdentityOptions` actually exposes the B2C policy properties used below (or switch to the correct B2C options type if needed). Otherwise this will either not compile or will silently drop B2C-specific behavior in the template.

Using `DefaultRequestHeaders.Add` on a reused `HttpClient` will accumulate multiple `Authorization` headers across calls and can throw if the header already exists. Prefer setting the per-request header (e.g., create an `HttpRequestMessage` and set `request.Headers.Authorization = new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", accessToken)`), or at least assign via `DefaultRequestHeaders.Authorization = ...` after clearing/removing any existing value. Also note the scheme should be `Bearer` (case-insensitive, but conventional) and using the typed header avoids formatting issues.

Typo in local variable name (`tokenAquisitionService` -> `tokenAcquisitionService`). More importantly, `GetService<ITokenAcquisition>()` can return null and will lead to a null reference when constructing `TokenAcquisitionCredentialProvider`. Prefer `GetRequiredService<ITokenAcquisition>()` (and consider validating `initialScopes` is non-null/non-empty) so template failures are immediate and easier to diagnose.

Consider using the typed header API instead of manually adding the Authorization header string. `request.Headers.Add("Authorization", ...)` can lead to duplicate headers if called multiple times and bypasses validation/parsing. Prefer `request.Headers.Authorization = new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", await _tokenAcquisition.GetAccessTokenForUserAsync(_initialScopes));` (requires `using System.Net.Http.Headers;`). This is the standard pattern for Graph auth providers and avoids subtle formatting issues.

This `.AddMvcOptions(options => {})` is a no-op and adds noise to the template. If it’s only here to force the MVC builder type for chaining, consider removing it (the chain should work without it) or replace it with a comment/real option configuration. Keeping an empty lambda can confuse users into thinking they need to customize something here.

The sample SignedOutCallbackPath value includes a policy id in the URL ("/signout/MySignUpSignInPolicyId"). In Azure AD B2C, the sign-out callback path is typically a fixed route (e.g., "/signout-callback-oidc"), while the policy is usually conveyed via query parameters/authority rather than being embedded in the path. Consider updating this comment to a more standard/default callback path (or add a brief note explaining why the policy id is part of the path) to avoid users copying an incorrect configuration.

Avoid setting an empty string for `shortName`. In dotnet template host config, `shortName` is expected to be a valid alias; an empty value can lead to confusing/help output or parsing issues. If you don't want a short alias, omit the `shortName` property entirely (or provide a meaningful one like `cau`/`cas`). Apply the same change to the other new parameters (`CalledApiScopes`, `CallsMicrosoftGraph`).

The computed expression for `GenerateApi` is tightly coupled to the *default* Graph URL and scope values. This means a user can set `CallsMicrosoftGraph=true` (expecting Graph support) but still end up with `GenerateApi=true` (and `GenerateGraph=false`) if they also change `CalledApiUrl`/`CalledApiScopes`, which may generate the wrong service files. Consider basing `GenerateApi` on an explicit flag (e.g., `CallsDownstreamApi`) or at least excluding the Graph case (e.g., `... && !CallsMicrosoftGraph`) and/or using `GenerateGraph` as the primary switch when Graph is selected. Also, comparing strings to hard-coded defaults is brittle (case/whitespace/format differences) and can lead to unexpected generation behavior.

This call to `CallWebApi()` is missing the required parameters in Microsoft.Identity.Web. `IDownstreamWebApi.CallWebApi` typically requires at least the configuration section name (e.g., "CalledApi") and optionally a relative path/scopes. As written, this likely won’t compile against current Identity.Web APIs. Consider using the overload that specifies the downstream API name/section and endpoint, e.g. `await _downstreamWebApi.CallWebApiForUserAsync("CalledApi", options => options.RelativePath = "me")` (or the appropriate overload for the version you’re targeting), and ensure the template’s appsettings keys match the `ScopeKeySection`/downstream API section used here.

Using `DefaultRequestHeaders.Add` on a reused `HttpClient` will accumulate multiple `Authorization` values across calls and can throw on subsequent invocations. Prefer setting the header per-request (e.g., create an `HttpRequestMessage` and set `request.Headers.Authorization = new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", accessToken)`), or at least assign via `DefaultRequestHeaders.Authorization = ...` after removing any existing value. Also note the scheme should be `Bearer` (case-insensitive, but conventional).

Typo in variable name (`tokenAquisitionService` -> `tokenAcquisitionService`) and consider using `GetRequiredService<ITokenAcquisition>()` instead of `GetService` to fail fast if the dependency isn't registered. As written, `tokenAquisitionService` can be null and will cause a later null-ref inside `TokenAcquisitionCredentialProvider`, which is harder to diagnose.

Consider using the strongly-typed header API instead of adding a raw string header. `Headers.Add("Authorization", ...)` can allow multiple Authorization values and bypasses validation/formatting. Prefer `request.Headers.Authorization = new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", token)` (requires `using System.Net.Http.Headers;`). Also, `GetAccessTokenForUserAsync` can throw; if this is used by Graph SDK, you may want to let exceptions bubble but ensure the header isn't partially set.

The Graph scopes are being read from the "CalledApi:CalledApiScopes" config key, which appears to be intended for the downstream Web API scenario. For Graph templates, this should likely use a Graph-specific scopes key (e.g., "Graph:Scopes" / "DownstreamApi:Scopes" depending on the template conventions) to avoid coupling Graph auth to the generic CalledApi settings. Otherwise, Graph calls may request the wrong scopes or fail when only Graph is enabled. Consider aligning the configuration section name with the one emitted in appsettings for the Graph template and/or using a dedicated key for Graph scopes.

Rendering `@ViewData["ApiResult"]` directly can lead to confusing output (e.g., object type name) and, depending on what gets stored there, could risk unintended HTML rendering if someone later switches to `Html.Raw`. Consider using a strongly-typed view model (preferred) or at least cast/format explicitly (e.g., `@((string?)ViewData["ApiResult"])`) and ensure the controller always sets a safe, user-friendly string (or JSON formatted) value. Also, if this is meant to show Graph/API JSON, wrapping in `<pre>` can improve readability.

This inject switches from `IOptionsMonitor<AzureADB2COptions>` to `IOptions<MicrosoftIdentityOptions>`, but the variable name `AzureADB2COptions` is now misleading and suggests B2C-specific behavior. Consider renaming the injected variable to something like `MicrosoftIdentityOptions`/`IdentityOptions` to avoid confusion in the template. Also, if this template still needs to support per-scheme/per-tenant configuration (previously done via `Get(AzureADB2CDefaults.AuthenticationScheme)`), `IOptions` may be insufficient—verify that `Value` is correct for all template scenarios or use `IOptionsMonitor<MicrosoftIdentityOptions>` if named options are required.

The example value for `SignedOutCallbackPath` looks like it includes the policy id in the path (`/signout/MySignUpSignInPolicyId`). In Microsoft.Identity.Web / OpenIdConnect, `SignedOutCallbackPath` is typically a fixed route (e.g., `/signout-callback-oidc`) and not policy-specific; the policy is usually handled via authority/policy configuration rather than the callback path. Consider updating this comment to a more standard default (or clarify that this is specific to B2C and why the policy id is embedded), otherwise users may copy/paste an incorrect path and sign-out callbacks won’t be hit.

These new CLI options are being added with empty `shortName` values. In dotnet template host files, it’s usually better to omit `shortName` entirely when you don’t want one (or provide a real short alias) to avoid ambiguity/host behavior differences across SDK versions. Also consider whether these should have `description`/`helpText` (or be documented via `usageExamples`) so `dotnet new ... -h` is self-explanatory, especially since these are new Identity.Web-specific parameters.

The computed expression for `GenerateApi` hard-codes the default Graph URL and default scope string. This is brittle (e.g., casing/whitespace differences, trailing slashes, or future default changes) and can cause unexpected inclusion/exclusion of `DownstreamWebApi.cs`. Consider basing this on `CallsMicrosoftGraph`/`GenerateGraph` and/or checking for non-empty/changed parameters more robustly (e.g., compare against the `defaultValue` via a symbol, or use a separate boolean parameter like `CallsDownstreamApi`). Also note that `CalledApiScopes` has no `defaultValue` but is compared to "user.read" here; if the user doesn't pass it, the comparison may not behave as intended depending on templating defaults.

This call is missing the required parameters for IDownstreamWebApi in Microsoft.Identity.Web. In current Identity.Web, CallWebApi typically requires at least a configured downstream API name (and often a relative path and options), e.g. `CallWebApiForUserAsync("DownstreamApi", options => options.RelativePath = "me")`. As written, `CallWebApi()` is unlikely to compile against the intended package version and also doesn't specify which downstream API configuration to use. Consider updating to the correct API surface and passing the configured downstream API name/relative path/scopes so the template is functional out of the box.

Using `DefaultRequestHeaders.Add` on a reused `HttpClient` will accumulate duplicate Authorization headers across calls and can throw on subsequent invocations. Prefer setting the per-request header (e.g., create an `HttpRequestMessage` and set `request.Headers.Authorization = new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", accessToken)`), or at least assign via `DefaultRequestHeaders.Authorization = ...` after clearing/replacing. Also note the scheme should be `Bearer` (capital B) per convention.

Consider using `GetRequiredService<ITokenAcquisition>()` (or at least null-check) instead of `GetService`, since `TokenAcquisitionCredentialProvider` will fail later with a less clear NRE if the service isn't registered. Also, the local variable is misspelled (`tokenAquisitionService` -> `tokenAcquisitionService`), which can propagate into templates and user code.

Consider using the typed `Authorization` header instead of `Headers.Add("Authorization", ...)`. `Add` can create duplicate headers if called multiple times and bypasses validation/formatting. Prefer `request.Headers.Authorization = new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", await _tokenAcquisition.GetAccessTokenForUserAsync(_initialScopes));` (requires `using System.Net.Http.Headers;`). This is the standard pattern for Graph auth providers and avoids subtle header issues.

The Graph registration is pulling scopes from `CalledApi:CalledApiScopes` and URL from `CalledApi:CalledApiUrl`, which looks like the generic downstream API settings rather than Graph-specific configuration. This can lead to misconfigured Graph clients (wrong base URL/scopes) when both downstream API and Graph options exist. Consider using Graph-specific keys (e.g., `Graph:Scopes` / `Graph:BaseUrl`) or at least `MicrosoftGraph:Scopes` per Identity.Web conventions, and validate the split result (trim/remove empty entries) to avoid passing empty scopes when the config value is null/whitespace.

The sample scope value here is likely incorrect for Microsoft Graph. Graph scopes are typically fully qualified (e.g., "User.Read") and case-sensitive in docs/samples. Using "user.read" may confuse users and lead to auth failures or mismatched expectations. Consider changing to "User.Read" (or a more explicit example like "User.Read Mail.Read") and ensure the comment matches the intended called API (Graph vs custom API).

Using `Guid.NewGuid().ToString().Substring(0, 10)` is a bit brittle and allocates more than needed. While it works today, it relies on the default GUID string format and manual substringing. Consider using a safer/clearer approach like `Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N").Substring(0, 10)` (no hyphens) or a helper that generates a short unique suffix without substringing. Also, if the goal is uniqueness across parallel test runs, ensure the resulting name stays within any filesystem/path length constraints and is deterministic enough for debugging (e.g., include the test name prefix).

Using `Guid.NewGuid().ToString().Substring(0, 10)` is a bit brittle and allocates more than needed. Consider using a safer/clearer approach (e.g., `Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N").Substring(0, 10)` or `Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N")[..10]`) to avoid relying on the default GUID format and to remove the extra `ToLower()` call ("N" is already lowercase hex). Also, if the goal is uniqueness across parallel test runs, it may be worth using the full "N" string or a longer prefix to reduce collision risk.

Using `Guid.NewGuid().ToString().Substring(0, 10)` is a bit brittle and allocates more than needed. Consider using a format specifier to avoid hyphens and substring (e.g., `Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N").Substring(0, 10)`) or a helper that generates a short, filesystem-safe suffix. Also, if the goal is uniqueness across parallel test runs, it may be worth adding a separator (e.g., `"-"`) for readability and to avoid accidental name collisions with the base name.

The previous entry for Testing had a malformed line prefix in the slnf ("- 119         :"), and this change fixes the formatting and adds a trailing comma. Please ensure the JSON remains valid for all consumers (VS/CLI) by also verifying the file ends with a newline (the diff still shows "No newline at end of file"). Some tooling is sensitive to missing final newlines and it can cause noisy diffs.

This allocates a new JsonSerializerOptions instance on every call, which can be relatively expensive and may offset some of the gains from switching to UTF-8 bytes. Consider caching a static readonly JsonSerializerOptions (or using a source-generated JsonSerializerContext if available) and reusing it here. Also verify that the SignalR hub method binder will treat `byte[]` as raw binary; if it base64-encodes in JSON, the payload size/CPU may not improve as expected.

Throwing on any unknown property makes the reader brittle to forward-compatible payload changes (e.g., browsers/framework adding fields) and can break apps on minor updates. Consider ignoring unknown properties (or gating this behind a strict/debug option) to match typical JSON deserialization behavior. If you do want strictness, include more context in the exception (e.g., the event type / path) and consider using a consistent message format across readers.

Throwing on any unknown property makes the reader brittle to forward-compatible payload changes (e.g., new fields added by the browser/JS side or future framework versions). For event args JSON, it’s usually safer to ignore unrecognized properties (or gate this behind a debug/strict mode) to avoid breaking older servers/clients when new properties appear. If strictness is desired, consider including more context in the exception (event type, expected properties) and ensure all known properties for ClipboardEventArgs (e.g., `clipboardData`) are handled before enforcing this.

Throwing on unknown properties makes the reader brittle to forward-compatible payload changes (e.g., browsers adding new fields or Blazor adding fields in a later version). Since this code is on the hot path for event dispatch, a JsonException here can break apps at runtime. Consider ignoring unknown properties (or gating strictness behind a debug/feature flag) and only throwing when required properties are missing/invalid. If you do keep the exception, include context (e.g., event type / path) and consider using NameEquals against known UTF8 constants to avoid allocating property.Name in the message unless actually throwing.

Throwing on any unknown property makes the reader very strict and can break forward/backward compatibility if the browser/event payload adds fields (or if different runtimes include extra metadata). Consider either ignoring unknown properties (default System.Text.Json behavior) or gating this behind a debug/validation flag. If you do want strictness, include more context in the exception (e.g., event type / full path) and consider allowing known-but-ignored properties to avoid regressions when payloads evolve.

Throwing on any unknown JSON property makes the reader brittle to forward-compatible payload changes (e.g., browsers/framework adding new fields). For event args, it’s usually safer to ignore unknown properties (or gate this behind a debug/strict mode) to avoid breaking apps when new properties appear. If you do want strictness, consider including more context in the exception (event type, expected properties) and/or using a case-insensitive comparison strategy consistently so benign casing differences don’t start throwing.

Throwing on any unknown JSON property makes the reader brittle and can break forward/backward compatibility if the browser/framework adds new fields to the event payload (or if different runtimes send slightly different shapes). For event args deserialization it’s usually safer to ignore unknown properties (like System.Text.Json’s default behavior) unless you explicitly want strict validation. If strictness is desired, consider gating this behind a debug/feature flag, or at least include more context in the exception (e.g., event type / list of known properties) and ensure this won’t surface as a user-facing runtime failure for benign payload changes.

Throwing on any unknown JSON property makes the event payload contract brittle and can break forward/backward compatibility (e.g., browsers/framework adding new fields, or server/client version skew). For event args readers it’s usually safer to ignore unrecognized properties (or gate this behind a debug/strict mode) rather than failing the entire dispatch. If you want validation, consider only throwing when the property is known-but-invalid (wrong type), or add an opt-in strict flag used by tests.

This fallback delegates all unknown properties to MouseEventArgsReader, which is good for avoiding a full pre-pass, but it also means any unexpected/typo'd PointerEvent property names will be silently treated as mouse properties (or ignored) without any signal. Consider either (a) explicitly handling the known mouse property names in this reader (or via a shared helper) to keep the contract strict, or (b) adding a debug-only/assert/log path when MouseEventArgsReader doesn't recognize the property, so regressions in the JSON payload shape are easier to detect. Also ensure MouseEventArgsReader.ReadProperty is resilient to being called for non-mouse properties (no exceptions on unexpected names).

Throwing on any unknown property makes the reader brittle to forward-compatible payload changes (e.g., browsers/framework adding new fields to ProgressEvent). This can break event dispatch for otherwise valid events. Consider ignoring unknown properties (or gating this behind a strict/debug mode), and if you keep the exception, include more context (e.g., event type / JSON path) and use a consistent message format across all *ArgReader implementations.

Throwing on unknown properties makes the reader brittle to forward-compatible payload changes (e.g., browsers/framework adding new fields). For event args, it’s usually safer to ignore unknown properties to preserve compatibility. If strictness is desired, consider gating this behind a debug/feature flag, or at least include more context in the exception (e.g., reader type + JSON path) and ensure this behavior is consistent across all *EventArgsReader implementations.

Same concern here: failing on unknown touch point properties can break when new touch fields are introduced. Prefer ignoring unknown properties (or make strict mode opt-in). If you keep the exception, consider quoting the property name and adding context (e.g., "TouchPoint"), since this will surface to app code and can be hard to diagnose from just the property name.

Throwing on unknown properties makes the reader strict and can break forward/backward compatibility if the browser/client starts sending additional fields (or if payloads include extra metadata). If strictness is intended, consider including more context in the exception (e.g., which object is being read and the raw JSON/value kind) to aid diagnostics. Otherwise, consider ignoring unknown properties (or gating strict mode behind an option) to be resilient to schema evolution.

Routing all non-wheel properties through MouseEventArgsReader.ReadProperty(eventArgs, property) is a good way to avoid a second enumeration, but please ensure ReadProperty is resilient to unknown/extra properties (i.e., it should no-op rather than throw) since this `else` will now receive *every* non-Delta* property in the payload. Also consider whether wheel-specific properties should be handled case-insensitively or with a shared constant set to avoid subtle mismatches with the JS payload naming.

Switching from `_fields.Equals(...)` to `!=` changes semantics: for flag enums this is fine, but if `LoggingFields` is a non-primitive (or can be null) this becomes reference/nullable comparison and may not match the intended value-based equality. Consider using `!EqualityComparer<...>.Default.Equals(_fields, loggerOptions.LoggingFields)` (or keep `.Equals` with a null-safe pattern) to ensure correct value comparison and avoid potential NREs if `_fields` can be null.

Using DateTimeOffset.Now makes this test time-dependent and potentially flaky (local time zone/DST changes, and it can still cross midnight between computing fileName1/fileName2 and writing). Consider using a fixed DateTimeOffset (or injecting a controllable clock into FileLoggerProcessor and using that in tests) so the expected filenames are deterministic and the test doesn't need to bail out based on the current hour.

`_listener` can be null if `OnChange` was never called (or if multiple listeners are expected). Calling `Invoke` unconditionally will throw. Consider using the null-conditional operator (`_listener?.Invoke(CurrentValue, Options.DefaultName)`), and/or supporting multiple registrations by using `+=` in `OnChange` and returning an `IDisposable` that unsubscribes. Also, passing `null` for the name is inconsistent with `IOptionsMonitor` usage; prefer `Options.DefaultName` (or the actual name) to better match production behavior in tests.

This test is still using `ConsumesAttribute` with `IsOptional`, but the issue/PR description indicates the new API should flow required/optional via `IAcceptsMetadata.IsRequired` (and `ConsumesAttribute.IsRequired`). If the implementation has moved to `IsRequired`, this test should be updated accordingly (e.g., `IsRequired = false` instead of `IsOptional = true`) and ideally add coverage for the new `Accepts(..., isRequired: false, ...)` metadata path as well. Otherwise the test may be validating an obsolete API surface and miss regressions in the new required/optional behavior.

This rename + assertion change flips the expected behavior from “body parameter is represented as a Body ApiParameterDescription” to “no parameter descriptions at all”. That seems like a behavioral regression for ApiExplorer: even if request body is modeled separately (e.g., via SupportedRequestFormats / RequestBody metadata), ApiDescription typically still needs to surface a body parameter (or at least some representation) for OpenAPI generators to infer schema/requiredness. Given the issue/PR goal is to add required/optional support for Accepts/Consumes metadata, consider updating the test to assert the body is represented via the new IAcceptsMetadata/Consumes IsRequired flow (e.g., ApiDescription.SupportedRequestFormats + a body parameter with IsRequired reflecting metadata), rather than asserting the body disappears entirely. If the intent really is to remove body parameters from ParameterDescriptions, please add/adjust companion assertions that validate where the body schema/requiredness is now exposed so downstream tooling doesn’t lose it.

Add a period to the summary (done), but also consider clarifying the behavior/meaning of this flag in the XML doc. For example: does `IsRequired = false` mean the request body may be omitted entirely (e.g., empty body) while still matching the content types, and how does it interact with `RequestType` being set? Also, if this property is intended to flow into `IAcceptsMetadata.IsRequired`/ApiExplorer/OpenAPI, it would be helpful to mention that in the doc comment so consumers understand its effect.

Nit: good to add the period for consistency. Consider also aligning the XML doc wording with the public API proposal/IAcceptsMetadata docs (e.g., "Gets a value indicating whether the request body is required.") and, if applicable, mention the default behavior (true) somewhere in the type/constructor docs so consumers understand what happens when not explicitly set.

This overload takes a non-nullable `EntityTagHeaderValue entityTag`, but the proposed/typical file APIs allow ETag to be optional (`EntityTagHeaderValue?`). As written, callers can’t omit the ETag without passing a dummy value, and it’s inconsistent with the `lastModified` parameter being nullable. Consider changing the signature to `EntityTagHeaderValue? entityTag` (and updating the other overload at line 1760 similarly) to match existing MVC file overload patterns and the minimal API proposal.

Registering the executor makes sense, but please ensure this is the correct lifetime and registration pattern for the new result. Most IActionResultExecutor<T> implementations are stateless and use Singleton, but if PushFileStreamResultExecutor depends on scoped services (e.g., HttpContext/Response features, ILoggerFactory is fine), Singleton will cause captive dependency issues. If it has any scoped dependencies, switch to TryAddScoped or refactor the executor to only depend on singleton-safe services. Also confirm the executor type is in the same assembly/package so it’s always available when MvcCore is added (and that it’s covered by tests verifying the executor is resolved).

Consider flushing the response after the callback completes (e.g., `await context.HttpContext.Response.Body.FlushAsync(context.HttpContext.RequestAborted)`), or at least document that the callback is responsible for flushing. With a push-to-response-stream API, buffering behavior can vary by server/middleware, and without an explicit flush the client may not observe data promptly (especially for streaming scenarios). Also consider passing `RequestAborted` into the callback (or ensuring the callback can observe cancellation) so long-running writes can stop when the client disconnects.

This new overload exposes `entityTag` as non-nullable (`EntityTagHeaderValue!`) while `lastModified` is nullable. That makes it impossible to call this overload with only `lastModified` (or with neither) without passing a dummy ETag, and it’s inconsistent with typical file result APIs where ETag is optional. Consider making `entityTag` nullable (`EntityTagHeaderValue?`) in the public surface (and corresponding implementation) to avoid forcing callers to provide an ETag and to align with other file-related overloads.

Consider making the callback immutable (e.g., `public Func<Stream, Task> StreamWriterCallback { get; }`) and set it only via the constructor. As a `FileResult`, this is typically treated as a single-use, fully-initialized value object; allowing the callback to be replaced after construction can lead to surprising behavior (e.g., mutation between filters/execution) and makes nullability harder to reason about. If you need settable for model binding, at least make the setter `init` and/or guard against null assignments.

The override signature uses non-nullable `EntityTagHeaderValue entityTag = null`, which is inconsistent and will produce nullable warnings (and is misleading to callers). Consider changing this to `EntityTagHeaderValue? entityTag = null` (and similarly ensure the base method signature matches) to accurately represent that the parameter is optional and may be null.

The signature uses non-nullable `EntityTagHeaderValue entityTag = null`, which will produce nullable warnings and is inconsistent with the test usage (sometimes passing null is valid). Consider changing this to `EntityTagHeaderValue? entityTag = null` (and similarly consider `string? contentType` if null is allowed by the API) to accurately model optional parameters and avoid NRT issues.

Switching `ConfigureWebDefaults` to `builder.UseKestrel(ConfigureKestrel)` while `ConfigureWebDefaultsCore` uses `UseKestrelSlim` changes the default behavior depending on which entry point is used. Please double-check that this split is intentional and that all call sites that should get the new “pay-for-play” behavior are routed through `ConfigureWebDefaultsCore` (or vice versa). Otherwise, apps using `ConfigureWebDefaults` may still pull in TLS/QUIC dependencies and not see the intended size reduction. If the intent is that *all* web defaults should be slim by default (or only in certain hosting models), consider documenting that here or aligning both paths to the same Kestrel builder method to avoid surprising differences.

Consider using `localhost` instead of hard-coding `127.0.0.1` for consistency with other template/test infrastructure and to avoid edge cases where binding to loopback IP behaves differently than hostname resolution (e.g., IPv6 preference, proxy tooling, or platform-specific loopback handling). If the intent is specifically to avoid IPv6, a short comment explaining why `127.0.0.1` is required here would help future maintainers.

This change makes the test always opt out of HTTPS registration, which may unintentionally reduce coverage for the default API template (where HTTPS is typically expected to work). If the intent is to validate only the slim/minimal scenario, consider gating `noHttps: true` behind the relevant template/args (e.g., only when `UseKestrelSlim`/minimal hosting is selected) or adding a separate test case for the slim scenario while keeping the existing HTTPS-enabled path for the standard template. Also, the comment mentions "minimal/slim/core" but the method name is `ApiTemplateCore`; please ensure the terminology matches the scenario being tested to avoid confusion.

Consider making the guidance in this error message more explicit/accurate for users: include the fully-qualified extension method (e.g., `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.WebHostBuilderKestrelExtensions.UseHttpsConfiguration`) and/or mention the `UseKestrelSlim` scenario so it’s clear why HTTPS isn’t being auto-enabled. Also verify the method name matches the final public API (including any overloads) to avoid shipping a stale instruction string.

The comment says this explicit interface implementation is to avoid accidentally calling it within this class, but the property is still accessible via `((IHttpsConfigurationService)this).IsInitialized` and the semantics are a bit surprising: it returns `true` when an initializer exists even if `_isInitialized` is still `false`. That can mislead callers into thinking TLS is ready when it will still throw until `EnsureInitialized()` runs. Consider renaming the concept (e.g., `CanInitialize`/`IsInitializationAvailable`) or adjusting the property to reflect actual initialization state, and expose a separate capability flag if needed. Also fix the typo in the comment (“implentation” -> “implementation”).

Consider whether this should be a method (e.g., `HasServerCertificateOrSelector()`) or a more descriptive property name. As written, it’s a computed property that reads like state but actually re-evaluates each time; that’s fine, but the XML doc “Convenient shorthand for a common check” is a bit vague—calling out the exact semantics (non-null `ServerCertificate` OR `ServerCertificateSelector`) would make it clearer. Also, if this is intended to be used across assemblies for the new pay-for-play behavior, `internal` may be too restrictive; if it’s only for Core internals, `internal` is appropriate but please ensure it’s not needed by other Kestrel packages.

Doc comment has a grammar/clarity issue: "will *always* be registered by only be" reads like a typo and is confusing. Consider rewording to something like: "In normal usage, it will always be registered, but it will only be initialized (see IsInitialized) if the consumer explicitly opts into HTTPS/TLS support." Also, the comment references `CreateSlimBuilder`, but the PR description/API mentions `UseKestrelSlim`; aligning terminology here will help avoid confusion and keep docs accurate if the public name changes.

This changes behavior from an in-place mutating call (`options.UseHttps()`) to a delegate that returns `ListenOptions`, but the return value is ignored. If the provided `useHttps` implementation returns a new/wrapped `ListenOptions` (or otherwise expects the caller to use the returned instance), TLS may not actually be applied and subsequent `BindAsync` will run on the original `options`. Either (a) change the delegate type to `Action<ListenOptions>` if mutation is the contract, or (b) assign the result back (e.g., `options = _useHttps(options);`) and ensure the rest of the method uses the updated instance. Also consider a null-check/guard for `_useHttps` to avoid a late NRE when https URLs are used in slim mode; throwing a targeted exception here would produce a clearer bind-time error.

This call now centralizes all HTTP/3 TLS feature population behind IHttpsConfigurationService, which is good for trimming, but it also changes behavior: previously, missing HttpsOptions/HttpsCallbackOptions simply resulted in no TlsConnectionCallbackOptions being set (allowing some in-memory/non-TLS scenarios to proceed). If PopulateMultiplexedTransportFeatures throws unconditionally when HTTPS configuration isn’t enabled, it may break QUIC/HTTP3 tests or scenarios that intentionally don’t configure TLS (or that rely on the transport to detect missing TLS options). Consider ensuring this method only throws when the selected multiplexed transport actually requires TLS/HTTP3 (e.g., when binding to a QUIC endpoint/transport), and otherwise becomes a no-op. Also, please verify the thrown exception message is actionable and includes the intended one-liner guidance (UseHttpsConfiguration/UseQuic) since this is now the bind-time failure point.

Consider adding a null-check for the injected IHttpsConfigurationService (e.g., ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(httpsConfigurationService)) before assigning it. This is a new required ctor dependency; failing fast with a clear exception will make misconfigured DI (especially in slim scenarios) easier to diagnose and avoids a later NullReferenceException when passing the delegate into AddressBinder/TransportManager.

Switching to `fallbackHttpsOptions.HasServerCertificateOrSelector` changes the condition semantics from checking only `fallbackHttpsOptions.ServerCertificate` + `_fallbackServerCertificateSelector` to whatever `HasServerCertificateOrSelector` considers a valid cert/selector. Please double-check that `HasServerCertificateOrSelector` includes the same sources as before (including the `_fallbackServerCertificateSelector` field) and doesn’t treat unrelated values (e.g., a client cert mode, a cert chain without leaf, etc.) as satisfying the requirement. If `HasServerCertificateOrSelector` only inspects `fallbackHttpsOptions` and not `_fallbackServerCertificateSelector`, this could incorrectly skip the exception and later fail with a less actionable error.

The constructor removed the null-guards but still assigns reference-type parameters directly. If this type is ever constructed outside DI (or if a misconfigured container passes null), this will now fail later with less actionable NREs. Consider restoring explicit `?? throw new ArgumentNullException(...)` checks (at least for `options`, `configuration`, and `httpsConfigurationService`) or documenting/enforcing non-null via `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(...)` to keep failure mode consistent with previous behavior.

Throwing NotImplementedException here is risky because it turns a configuration/usage mistake into a hard crash with an unhelpful message, and it’s easy for future refactors to start calling this method (e.g., when binding endpoints from config) without realizing this “simple” implementation can’t handle it. If this method truly must never be called for this code path, consider either (1) implementing it by delegating to the existing HttpsConfigurationService worker logic (preferred for functional completeness), or (2) throwing a more specific exception with a clear actionable message (e.g., InvalidOperationException explaining that HTTPS configuration support isn’t enabled and pointing to UseHttpsConfiguration/UseKestrel vs UseKestrelSlim). Also consider adding a debug assert or comment explaining the invariant that prevents this from being invoked.

Relying on a Debug.Assert here means this becomes a no-op in Release builds, but the comment indicates correctness depends on HTTPS configuration being initialized (to avoid incorrectly preferring the dev cert over a user-specified one). If this invariant can be violated in production (e.g., via UseKestrelSlim + some path that still calls ApplyDefaultCertificate), we should fail deterministically with a clear exception (or ensure ApplyDefaultCertificate is never reachable until EnableHttpsConfiguration has been called). Consider replacing/augmenting the assert with a runtime check that throws an InvalidOperationException with guidance (e.g., call UseHttpsConfiguration), or moving the initialization/guard earlier so this method cannot be invoked in an uninitialized state.

Enabling HTTPS configuration here makes sense as an explicit user-intent signal, but it also changes behavior for callers that invoke `UseHttps(...)` with a fully-specified certificate and previously didn’t need any of the config/default-certificate plumbing. Please confirm `EnableHttpsConfiguration()` is idempotent and does not eagerly load/resolve TLS-related services (or throw) in scenarios where the user is providing `ServerCertificate`/`ServerCertificateSelector` directly. If it can throw or pull in extra dependencies at runtime, consider deferring the enablement until it’s actually needed (e.g., only when applying defaults / default cert), or document the new requirement/behavior clearly.

Switching to `HasServerCertificateOrSelector` improves readability, but please ensure the property is a pure/null-check equivalent of the previous condition. In particular: (1) it should not have side effects (e.g., invoking the selector), (2) it should not change semantics around “empty”/invalid certificates (e.g., a non-null but unusable cert), and (3) it should be safe under trimming/AOT (no hidden reflection). If the property does anything beyond `ServerCertificate != null || ServerCertificateSelector != null`, consider keeping the explicit check here or add a brief comment clarifying the exact semantics to avoid future regressions.

Loading the dev cert via `new X509Certificate2(path, password)` can keep the private key in a user key store and may behave differently across platforms/containers. Consider specifying `X509KeyStorageFlags` (e.g., `EphemeralKeySet` and/or `MachineKeySet` depending on intended behavior) to avoid persisting keys and to improve reliability in trimmed/container scenarios. Also consider whether you want to catch broader exceptions (e.g., `IOException`, `UnauthorizedAccessException`) to log a clearer failure when the file exists but can't be read.

Consider making this delegate `static` (or a local `static` in each test) to avoid capturing `this` and allocating a new closure per test class instance. E.g. `private static readonly Func<ListenOptions, ListenOptions> NoopUseHttps = static l => l;`. Also, naming it `NoopUseHttps` (PascalCase) would better match typical field naming conventions for readonly/static helpers.

This test now registers a mock `IHttpsConfigurationService`, which may mask the new “pay-for-play” behavior where HTTPS configuration is intentionally absent/throws unless explicitly enabled. If the goal of this test is unrelated to HTTPS, consider using the new default registration path (e.g., whatever internal helper is always present but throws until enabled) or explicitly asserting that HTTPS isn’t required here. Otherwise, this mock could make the test pass even if production DI would fail/throw when `UseKestrelSlim` is used. At minimum, add a comment explaining why HTTPS configuration is required for this test, or prefer registering the real implementation/enabler to better reflect runtime behavior.

This test helper is now manually constructing and initializing a concrete `HttpsConfigurationService`, which risks diverging from the production DI wiring and can make tests brittle (e.g., if initialization requirements change). Consider resolving `IHttpsConfigurationService` from `options.ApplicationServices` when available (or using a test double) instead of `new HttpsConfigurationService()`, and only calling `Initialize` when the service is the real implementation. Also, if `options?.ApplicationServices` is null, this leaves the service uninitialized—ensure that’s an intended/covered scenario for the tests that use this helper.

Adding a new public extension method is a significant API surface change. Please double-check the naming and discoverability: `UseHttpsConfiguration` may be confused with existing `UseHttps(...)`/endpoint HTTPS configuration APIs and could be interpreted as enabling HTTPS itself rather than just registering the helper/services. Consider whether the name should more explicitly convey "enable HTTPS configuration services" (or similar), and ensure XML docs clearly state what it does/does not do (e.g., does not automatically make `https://` URLs work unless Kestrel is configured accordingly). Also confirm this is in the intended assembly/namespace for hosting extensions and that the API is consistent with the `UseKestrelSlim` story (e.g., whether `UseQuic` should imply this).

Consider using a TryAdd* registration (e.g., TryAddSingleton) or guarding against duplicate registrations. As written, every call to UseHttpsConfiguration() will unconditionally add another singleton mapping for the same service type, which can lead to multiple IInitializer instances being created and potentially multiple initializations running (depending on how IHttpsConfigurationService consumes IEnumerable<IInitializer>). If multiple registrations are intended, it would be good to document that explicitly; otherwise, prefer idempotent registration to avoid surprising behavior when extensions are called multiple times or by multiple packages.

This test loads a PFX via `new X509Certificate2(path, password)` which can be flaky on some platforms/CI configurations (e.g., key storage permissions, ephemeral key set behavior) and can also leave key material persisted depending on defaults. Consider using the existing test certificate helpers used elsewhere in the repo (or specify `X509KeyStorageFlags.EphemeralKeySet` / `MachineKeySet` as appropriate) to make the test more reliable and avoid side effects. Also, if the intent is only to provide a cert for Kestrel, prefer a shared helper that abstracts the file path and flags so the test doesn't depend on relative paths like `shared/TestCertificates`.

These tests are now manually registering the concrete TLS configuration services. That can make the test brittle vs. production wiring (especially with the new pay-for-play behavior) and may hide failures where the services are intentionally absent. Consider using the same registration path as the real host builder (e.g., whatever extension method/configure method adds these services) or, if the intent is to test the "enabled" path explicitly, add a comment explaining why the test opts into HTTPS configuration here. Also verify the lifetime is correct: if production uses TryAdd/Singleton/Scoped differently, mirroring it here avoids subtle behavior differences.

Registering both `IHttpsConfigurationService` and the concrete `HttpsConfigurationService.IInitializer` here may be more than this test helper needs and could unintentionally force TLS-related types into all test server setups (working against the pay-for-play goal). If `KestrelServerImpl` only requires `IHttpsConfigurationService`, consider registering just that (or a lightweight test stub) and only add the initializer in tests that explicitly exercise HTTPS configuration. Also verify the intended lifetime: `AddSingleton` is fine if the service is stateless, but if it captures per-server state, scoped/transient might be safer for isolation across tests.

This test now wires up `IHttpsConfigurationService` directly, but it only registers the concrete `HttpsConfigurationService` and its initializer. If `HttpsConfigurationService` has additional DI requirements (e.g., `IHostEnvironment`, logging, options/config, etc.), consider using the same service-registration helper used by production code (or a dedicated test helper) to avoid brittle tests that break when the service’s constructor changes. Also, since `CreateServerOptions()` is now responsible for providing the environment and HTTPS config services, it may be worth asserting that `options.ApplicationServices.GetRequiredService<IHostEnvironment>()` is present (or documenting why it’s required) to make the dependency explicit.

Using Moq here adds a new test dependency and makes the test setup heavier than necessary. Since the comment indicates this service is only needed to satisfy the `ApplyDefaultCertificate` check, consider replacing the mock with a minimal stub implementation (e.g., a private sealed `TestHttpsConfigurationService : IHttpsConfigurationService` that returns `IsInitialized = true`). That keeps the test self-contained, avoids relying on Moq behavior, and reduces the chance of future interface changes breaking the setup silently.

This test server is now explicitly registering the concrete TLS configuration services. That may undermine the goal of making TLS pay-for-play in tests by always pulling in the HTTPS configuration implementation (and its dependencies) even when a test doesn’t need it. If the intent is to keep the in-memory test host “slim” by default, consider registering the new throwing/disabled helper (or a lightweight test stub) unless a given test opts into HTTPS via the new `UseHttpsConfiguration` path. At minimum, add a comment explaining why the full `HttpsConfigurationService` is required here (e.g., KestrelServerImpl now requires it unconditionally).

Using `host.Run` inside `Assert.Throws` is risky in tests because `Run()` is a blocking call and can hang if the failure mode changes (e.g., exception thrown asynchronously during Start/Bind). Prefer asserting on `host.StartAsync()` (or `await Assert.ThrowsAsync<InvalidOperationException>(() => host.StartAsync())`) so the test remains non-blocking and more deterministic. If you need to validate the exact bind-time error, consider also asserting on the exception message to ensure the failure is for missing QUIC/HTTPS configuration rather than some unrelated startup issue.

Adding a first-class `RefreshToken` to `AuthenticationProperties` is a broad API surface change that will flow through many handlers/middleware. A couple concerns to address here:

1) The XML doc is ambiguous: “the token must be valid for sign in to continue” doesn’t specify *which* token (refresh token vs access token) or *who* validates it. Consider clarifying that this is an opaque refresh token used by the BearerTokenHandler (and Identity integration) during a refresh/sign-in flow.

2) Consider whether this should be `[JsonIgnore]` (as proposed in the issue) to avoid accidental serialization when `AuthenticationProperties` is used in JSON contexts. Even if it’s not commonly serialized, the risk of leaking refresh tokens is high.

3) Naming/keying: `internal const string RefreshTokenKey = ".refreshToken";` is fine, but please ensure it won’t conflict with existing `Items` keys used by apps. If this is intended to be public API, consider whether the key should be exposed similarly to other well-known keys or documented as reserved.

4) Storage choice: you’re using `Parameters` via `GetParameter/SetParameter`. If the refresh token needs to round-trip through cookie/session stores, confirm `Parameters` is persisted consistently across all `IAuthenticationProperties` serializers (some historically only persisted `Items`). If not, this may need to use `Items` or ensure serializers include `Parameters`.

Net: please tighten docs + add `JsonIgnore` (or justify why not) and verify persistence/round-tripping behavior for `Parameters` across auth property serialization.

Since this adds a new public API surface (AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken), ensure the corresponding implementation is also annotated as proposed (e.g., [JsonIgnore]) to avoid accidentally serializing the refresh token when AuthenticationProperties is logged/serialized. Also double-check naming consistency with the rest of the bearer token feature (e.g., "refresh_token" in JSON responses vs. RefreshToken property) and that the API is added to the correct assembly/package for the intended layering.

Consider adding basic validation/constraints for the refresh token payload. As-is, `required` only enforces presence during deserialization, but it can still be an empty/whitespace string. If the endpoint expects a non-empty token, add validation (e.g., `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` check in the endpoint) or annotate with something like `[Required, MinLength(1)]` (and ensure the pipeline enforces data annotations). Also confirm the JSON contract matches the intended API name (`refreshToken` vs `refresh_token`) and whether you want to support both via `[JsonPropertyName]` for compatibility.

This creates a ClaimsIdentity with only an authentication type, but no Name/NameIdentifier claim. The comment says “sign in will fail without an identity name”, yet nothing here ensures a name is present. If BearerTokenHandler/OnSigningIn expects an authenticated principal (or uses NameIdentifier to reload the user), this endpoint may always fail or behave inconsistently. Consider either (a) constructing the principal with the minimal required claim(s) (e.g., NameIdentifier) if the refresh token format allows extracting it, or (b) using an empty principal and relying on the handler to fully validate/replace it (and update the comment accordingly), or (c) explicitly set a placeholder identity name only if the handler requires it. Also, double-check the scheme: you accept `scheme` in the login endpoint, but hardcode `IdentityConstants.BearerScheme` here—if the API supports custom schemes, refresh should likely use the same scheme as login.

Setting `bearerOptions.Events.OnSigningIn` unconditionally here can clobber a user-provided `OnSigningIn` callback passed via `configureOptions`, depending on ordering expectations. Today you set the Identity handler first and then call `configureOptions`, which means user code can overwrite Identity’s security-stamp validation (and likely break refresh behavior) without realizing it. Consider composing the callbacks instead (e.g., capture `var prior = bearerOptions.Events.OnSigningIn; bearerOptions.Events.OnSigningIn = async ctx => { await HandleSigningIn<TUser>(ctx); await prior(ctx); };` or vice-versa), or at least document/guard against overriding (e.g., only set if it’s the default no-op). This is especially important since the whole point of `AddIdentityBearerToken` is to ensure refresh integrates with Identity.

Typo in the XML doc comment: "ans" should be "and". Also consider whether the cref should point to the new Identity-specific bearer token extension (e.g., AddIdentityBearerToken) rather than the generic BearerTokenExtensions.AddBearerToken, since the issue/PR description emphasizes Identity wiring (security stamp validation / principal reload) during refresh.

This composite scheme is registered as the default scheme (AddAuthentication(IdentityConstants.BearerAndApplicationScheme)) and derives from SignInAuthenticationHandler, so SignInAsync/SignOutAsync can be invoked (e.g., by minimal API SignIn/SignOut results, or other middleware/components). Throwing NotImplementedException here will turn those calls into 500s at runtime. Either implement these by forwarding to the appropriate underlying scheme(s) (likely ApplicationScheme for sign-in/sign-out, and possibly also BearerScheme depending on desired semantics), or change the handler base type to one that doesn't advertise sign-in/sign-out if you don't intend to support it. At minimum, consider returning a clear failure (AuthenticateResult/Forbid) rather than throwing, and add tests/coverage for SignIn/SignOut paths when the composite scheme is default.

This change replaces the previously listed public type `IdentityApiEndpointsIdentityBuilderExtensions` with `IdentityAuthenticationBuilderExtensions`. If the former type was renamed/moved (as described in the issue), ensure the old type is either still present (e.g., as a forwarding/obsolete shim) or explicitly removed as part of a breaking-change decision. Also verify that all other renamed extension types (`IdentityApiEndpointsServiceCollectionExtensions` -> `IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions`, `IdentityApiEndpointsIdentityBuilderExtensions` -> `IdentityBuilderExtensions`) are consistently reflected in this file; otherwise the public API baseline will be incomplete and CI will fail or consumers will see unexpected breaks.

Adding a test project to the Identity solution filter can be useful for local dev, but it also changes the default load set for anyone opening this slnf (potentially increasing restore/build time and pulling in extra test-only dependencies). Is this required for the Identity work in this PR, or should it be limited to a test-focused slnf (or a separate change) to avoid broadening the Identity slnf scope? If it is required, consider adding a brief note in the PR description/commit message explaining why Identity.slnf needs Authentication.Test.

This test reads the access token from the *login* response after calling /refresh, so it never validates that the refreshed access token works. It also looks like a copy/paste mistake since you already have `refreshContent` available. Consider asserting `refreshResponse.IsSuccessStatusCode` and then using `refreshContent.GetProperty("access_token")` (or at least `refreshContent` rather than `loginContent`) to ensure the refresh endpoint actually returns a usable new access token.

Renaming the virtual from `MessageReceived` to `MessageReceivedAsync` is a breaking change for anyone deriving from `BearerTokenEvents` and overriding the method. If this is intended, it should be called out explicitly in API review/compat notes; otherwise consider keeping the existing `MessageReceived(MessageReceivedContext)` virtual (possibly `[Obsolete]`) and forwarding it to the new `MessageReceivedAsync` to preserve overrides. Also ensure the handler calls the correct method so existing overrides still run.

Refresh token generation should use the post-callback principal/properties, not the pre-callback `user`. `OnSigningIn` is explicitly meant to allow security-stamp validation and principal reloading during refresh; if it updates `signingInContext.Principal`, the refresh token should be minted from that updated principal (and potentially updated properties). As written, a refresh flow that replaces the principal will still issue a refresh token bound to the old principal, which can lead to inconsistent claims between access/refresh tokens and potentially reintroduce stale identities. Consider changing to `CreateRefreshTicket(signingInContext.Principal!, utcNow)` (and/or pass the context) and ensure the refresh ticket uses the same final principal that the access token uses.

Renaming `BearerTokenProtector` to `TokenProtector` is a breaking change for anyone configuring BearerTokenOptions directly. If this is intentional, please ensure there is a clear migration path: consider keeping `BearerTokenProtector` as an `[Obsolete]` shim that forwards to `TokenProtector` (at least for one release), and update all internal references/docs accordingly. Also verify the default protector creation uses distinct purposes (or otherwise prevents token-type confusion) now that the same protector is used for both access and refresh tokens.

Consider whether these sign-in logs should be `LogLevel.Debug` (or at least `LogLevel.Information` only when explicitly enabled) to avoid high-volume noise in production. Sign-in can be frequent (especially with refresh flows), and these messages don’t appear to add actionable diagnostics beyond what existing auth logs provide. Also ensure EventId `10` doesn’t conflict with other BearerTokenHandler event IDs in this assembly/package (it’s easy to collide when adding new logging).

This entry shows a rename from `MessageReceived(...)` to `MessageReceivedAsync(...)`. If the intent is to *rename* the virtual method, make sure the old signature is also listed as removed in this file (or in `PublicAPI.Shipped.txt` if it was already shipped) so API compat tooling correctly tracks the breaking change. If the old method was never public/shipped, consider whether the rename is necessary; otherwise, you may want to keep the old method as an `[Obsolete]` forwarding shim to avoid breaking derived `BearerTokenEvents` implementations.

Doc comment has a couple of issues: (1) typo/grammar: "written the to" -> "written to"; (2) missing period at the end of the last sentence; (3) consider clarifying that this refresh token is returned in the response body and is intended for the /refresh endpoint (or equivalent) to obtain a new access token. These comments are part of the public API surface, so tightening wording helps avoid confusion.

Renaming the JSON DTO property from `ExpiresInTotalSeconds` to `ExpiresInSeconds` is a breaking change for any internal callers constructing this DTO (even though the JSON name remains `expires_in`). Please ensure all usages are updated in this PR, and consider whether the rename is worth the churn vs. keeping the old CLR name (or adding an `[Obsolete]` shim property if this type ever leaks across assemblies). Also, the remarks mention serialization as `"{ExpiresInSeconds}"` (string), but the JSON value is numeric; consider adjusting the wording to avoid implying a string.

Removing `RefreshTokenKey` and the `RefreshToken` property is a breaking change for any consumers already using `AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken` (even if it was preview-only). If the intent is to replace it with a new `[JsonIgnore] public string? RefreshToken { get; set; }` (as described in the issue), that new property is not present in this hunk—please ensure it’s added back in this file (and that it still flows through the auth pipeline). If the new design no longer stores the value in `Items`/`Parameters`, consider how the refresh token will be persisted across redirects/serialization and whether existing code that relied on `Items[".refreshToken"]` needs a compatibility shim or migration path.

This hunk removes the newly-added `AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken` API from `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt`. If the PR still introduces `AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken` (as described in the issue/PR), it must remain listed here until it ships; otherwise the API will be missing from the unshipped contract and can break API validation. If the property was intentionally reverted/renamed (e.g., moved to a derived properties type), ensure the corresponding new API is added to `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt` instead and that any shipped/unshipped API files are updated consistently.

Throwing an ArgumentException here will turn a server misconfiguration (RefreshTokenProtector not wired) into a 500 with an exception message, and it also makes the endpoint behavior inconsistent with the rest of the method which returns auth results. Consider handling this as a configuration error more explicitly: either (a) fail fast at startup when AddIdentityBearerToken/AddBearerToken is configured (preferred), or (b) return a Problem/500 with a generic message (or Challenge/Unauthorized if you want to avoid leaking details). Also, if the new BearerTokenOptions API is intended to use TokenProtector for both access/refresh, double-check that RefreshTokenProtector is the correct option name and that it’s always set for IdentityConstants.BearerScheme.

This change removes the Identity-specific `OnSigningIn` hook that validated the security stamp and reloaded the user principal during refresh. If `AddIdentityBearerToken<TUser>` is intended to be the recommended way to wire up bearer tokens with Identity (per the issue/PR description), it should still attach an `OnSigningIn` handler (or otherwise ensure equivalent behavior) before/around invoking `configureOptions`. As-is, callers who use `AddIdentityBearerToken` will no longer get security stamp validation / principal rehydration on `/refresh`, which is a functional regression and undermines the main purpose of this extension method. Consider restoring the handler (and composing with any user-provided `OnSigningIn` rather than overwriting it), e.g., capture existing delegate and invoke both in order.

This change switches from setting `options.TimeProvider = clock` to registering `TimeProvider` in DI. That’s fine if the handler/options now resolve `TimeProvider` from DI, but the test should ensure it’s actually using the same instance (and not falling back to `TimeProvider.System`). Consider adding an assertion or a small sanity check (e.g., advance the fake clock and verify token expiry behavior changes accordingly) to prevent regressions if the options stop honoring DI-provided `TimeProvider`.

IConfigureNamedOptions<T> requires implementing Configure(TOptions) for the default (unnamed) case. Throwing NotImplementedException here can break option initialization paths that call the non-named overload (e.g., when configuring default options or when consumers resolve IOptions<BearerTokenOptions> without a scheme name). Consider either delegating to the named overload with Options.DefaultName (or similar) or providing a no-op implementation. If the intent is to only configure named schemes, implement Configure(BearerTokenOptions options) as an empty method with a comment explaining why, rather than throwing at runtime.

This change removes the `OnSigningIn` event and `SigningInAsync` virtual method from `BearerTokenEvents`, but the issue/PR description explicitly relies on `BearerTokenOptions.Events.OnSigningIn` to support refresh-token flows (security stamp validation + principal reloading). If this removal is intentional (e.g., the hook moved to a different type like `BearerTokenOptions` directly, or renamed), please ensure the replacement is present and wired up by `BearerTokenHandler` before merging. Otherwise, this is a breaking change that will prevent Identity integration and any custom token/principal logic during sign-in/refresh. At minimum, update docs/usage examples and add an obsoleted shim or keep the event here for compatibility.

Registering a global `IConfigureOptions<BearerTokenOptions>` here will apply to *all* BearerToken schemes and can also override/compete with per-scheme configuration passed via `AddBearerToken(authenticationScheme, configure)`. If `BearerTokenConfigureOptions` is intended to be scheme-specific (common for auth options), consider using `IConfigureNamedOptions<BearerTokenOptions>` and ensure it only applies when `name == authenticationScheme` (or register via `TryAddEnumerable` of a named configure that respects the scheme name). Also verify ordering/precedence: `IConfigureOptions` runs before `IPostConfigureOptions`, and multiple `IConfigureOptions` run in registration order—make sure this doesn’t unintentionally change defaults for existing consumers calling `AddBearerToken` without expecting extra configuration.

This now assumes `Options.BearerTokenProtector` is always non-null, but the handler no longer creates a default protector (the `IDataProtectionProvider` dependency and fallback `TicketDataFormat` were removed). Unless `BearerTokenOptions` guarantees initialization of `BearerTokenProtector` for all registrations, this can throw `NullReferenceException` at runtime during authentication. Consider restoring a safe fallback (e.g., create a default `TicketDataFormat` via DI) or add an explicit null-check with a clear exception message so misconfiguration is diagnosed early.

Throwing from the getter makes this option effectively required, but the XML docs say "If not provided, one will be created...". As written, any code path that reads BearerTokenProtector before the handler initializes it will fail with an InvalidOperationException. Consider either (a) keeping these as nullable auto-properties and letting the handler populate defaults, or (b) implementing lazy initialization in the getter (e.g., create a TicketDataFormat via IDataProtectionProvider) or at least throwing a message that clearly indicates a misconfiguration/initialization order issue. Also ensure the handler sets both protectors during post-configure so consumers never hit this exception in normal usage.

Changing the event id from 10 to 1 is a breaking change for log filtering/telemetry dashboards that key off EventId. Unless there’s a strong reason to renumber, consider keeping the original id (10) and just remove/obsolete the refresh-token-specific log message. If the intent is to consolidate logs, also ensure the removed "SignedInWithRefreshToken" scenario is still distinguishable (e.g., via a structured property like {IsRefresh} or a different EventName) without reusing/renumbering ids unexpectedly.

This line re-introduces `BearerTokenOptions.BearerTokenProtector` in the public API list, but the issue/PR description indicates the property was renamed to `TokenProtector` (and also mentions adding refresh token support). The current PublicAPI hunk also adds `RefreshTokenProtector`, which suggests the implementation may now have *two* protectors. Please verify the actual `BearerTokenOptions` surface area and ensure `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt` matches it exactly (names + nullability). If the intended API is `TokenProtector` (or `AccessTokenProtector`) rather than `BearerTokenProtector`, update this entry accordingly; otherwise update the proposal/docs to avoid a breaking/incorrect API contract and mismatched analyzers.

Consider broadening this check to include derived types (e.g., `error is NavigationException` already matches derived, so OK) but also ensure this is the *only* path where NavigationException can be intercepted. In particular, calling `HandleException(error)` here will route through the renderer's global exception handling—verify that this ultimately results in the intended redirect behavior in all hosting models (WASM/Server/WebApp) and doesn't get logged/treated as an unhandled error. If `HandleException` reports/logs exceptions, you may want a dedicated path (or a flag) to avoid noisy logging for NavigationException while still allowing the framework to process the redirect.

This assertion depends on the exact URL encoding produced by the runtime/browser (e.g., emoji encoding and potential normalization of query strings). To reduce flakiness across environments, consider asserting on the path and key query parts separately (e.g., `new Uri(Browser.Url).AbsolutePath` ends with `/subdir/nav/scroll-to-hash` and `QueryHelpers.ParseQuery(uri.Query)["foo"] == "🙂"`), or at least assert the presence of `foo=` rather than the full percent-encoded value.

This line appears to include a UTF-8 BOM/zero-width character before `@page` (shown as `﻿`). That can cause noisy diffs and occasional parsing/tooling issues. Please remove the BOM and ensure the file is saved as UTF-8 without BOM (or at least ensure the first character is `@`).

This link is intended to validate the new behavior (NavigationException should bypass error boundaries). Consider adding a brief note in the link text or nearby markup indicating that the target page performs a redirect from within an <ErrorBoundary>, so the purpose is clear when running the test asset. Also, if this is meant to be exercised by automated tests, ensure there is a corresponding test case that navigates to this URL and asserts the redirect occurs (otherwise this can become a manual-only scenario that regresses silently).

This null-guard fixes the NRE when FormFeature is constructed with an IFormCollection (where _request is intentionally null). Consider simplifying back to a single-line early return (`if (_request is null) return false;`) to match existing style unless a block is required for debugging/logging. Also, please add a small unit test covering the IFormCollection ctor + Form getter path to prevent regressions (ensuring ResolveHasInvalidAntiforgeryValidationFeature() is safe when _request is null).

Using reflection on every parameter to detect `[Required]` may be relatively expensive and also misses some common MVC metadata paths. Consider using `ApiParameterDescription.ModelMetadata` / `ParameterDescriptor.BindingInfo` / `ModelMetadata.IsRequired` (or `ValidationMetadata.IsRequired`) when available, and only fall back to reflection when metadata isn’t present. Also, `GetCustomAttributes(inherit: true)` can pick up attributes from base/interface declarations; verify that’s intended for parameters (inheritance semantics for parameter attributes can be surprising). If you keep reflection, consider caching per `ParameterInfo` to avoid repeated scans during document generation.

This modifier is a good approach, but it currently drops required-attribute handling when `SerializerOptions.TypeInfoResolver` is null. Consider defaulting to `new DefaultJsonTypeInfoResolver()` (or otherwise ensuring a resolver exists) so `[Required]` is honored even when no resolver is configured. Also, `if (jsonTypeInfo.Type is Type type)` is redundant (and `type` is unused); you can simplify to `if (jsonTypeInfo.Kind != JsonTypeInfoKind.Object) return;` to reduce noise.

This test only asserts `parameter.Required` but doesn’t verify the parameter `In`/`Name`/`Schema.Nullable` behavior for `[Required]` across different binding sources. In particular, `[Required] int? age` should likely also result in a non-nullable schema (or at least be explicitly validated), otherwise the test could pass while still generating a nullable schema that contradicts the intent of `[Required]`. Consider asserting `parameter.In` (query/header), `parameter.Name`, and `parameter.Schema.Nullable == false` (and/or `parameter.Schema.Type`) for each mapped endpoint to ensure the feature is functionally complete and not just flipping the `Required` flag.

This assertion is order-dependent. `schema.Required` is typically a set/collection where ordering may not be stable across runtimes/implementations, which can make the test flaky. Consider asserting set equality instead (e.g., `Assert.Equal(new[] { "title", "completed" }.OrderBy(x => x), schema.Required.OrderBy(x => x))` or `Assert.True(new HashSet<string>(schema.Required).SetEquals(["title","completed"]))`).

This assertion assumes a deterministic ordering of `schema.Required` (title then completed). OpenAPI `required` is an unordered set semantically, and the generator may not guarantee stable ordering across runtimes/reflection order changes. To avoid flaky tests, consider asserting set-equivalence instead (e.g., `Assert.NotNull(schema.Required); Assert.Equal(2, schema.Required.Count); Assert.Contains("title", schema.Required); Assert.Contains("completed", schema.Required);` or sort before `Assert.Equal`). Also consider adding an explicit `Assert.NotNull(schema.Required)` to make failures clearer if the list is missing.

Applying [Required] to a non-nullable value type like bool is redundant and may be misleading in tests: value types are always present in the CLR model, and in OpenAPI the more relevant distinction is usually "required" vs "nullable" for reference/nullable types. If the intent is to validate RequiredAttribute handling, consider using a nullable value type (e.g., bool?) or a reference type without a default initializer so the test clearly exercises the required-property behavior rather than a type-system guarantee.

Serializing the default value via `SerializeToNode(defaultValue, jsonTypeInfo)` assumes `jsonTypeInfo` matches the runtime type of `defaultValue`. With `[DefaultValue]`, the attribute value is often a `string` (or other convertible type) even when the target property/parameter type is e.g. `int`, `enum`, `DateTime`, etc. In that case this will either serialize the wrong JSON type (e.g., string instead of number) or potentially throw. Consider converting/coercing `defaultValue` to the target type represented by `jsonTypeInfo.Type` (including enum parsing and nullable handling) before serializing, and/or falling back to `JsonSerializer.SerializeToNode(defaultValue, defaultValue.GetType(), options)` when types don’t align. Also consider guarding against `JsonSerializer.SerializeToNode` returning null (e.g., unsupported types) to avoid writing a null default into the schema unintentionally.

Using `GetCustomAttributes(...).LastOrDefault()` makes the chosen `DefaultValueAttribute` dependent on reflection order, which is not guaranteed and can be non-deterministic across runtimes/builds.
