Consider switching this to `MockClientWriter<EchoRequest>` (and similarly ensure the reader/writer types match the expected request/response directions) so the test better reflects real usage and catches type-related breakages.

Since this file is under `tools/run_tests/generated/`, it is typically auto-generated. Please ensure the source-of-truth generator (and any checked-in template) is updated to emit the `mock_stream_test` entry, otherwise this change may be overwritten the next time tests.json is regenerated. If the project expects manual edits here, consider adding a brief comment in the PR description or updating the generation docs to avoid future churn.

Using `\s*?` is redundant here (it behaves the same as `\s*` since `*` already allows zero or more). Consider simplifying to `/\}\s*\);[^}\w]*$/` for readability. Also, if the intent is to allow only spaces/tabs (not newlines) between `}` and `)`, use `[ \t]*` instead of `\s*` to avoid matching across line breaks.

Same note as above: `\s*?` is unnecessary; `\s*` is equivalent and clearer. Additionally, this pattern is fairly broad (`[^\}]+`) and could be sensitive to edge cases; if you only need to tolerate whitespace between `}` and `)`, tightening just that part (and keeping the rest unchanged) is good, but consider adding/adjusting tests to cover both `});` and `} );` (and possibly `}\n);` if `\s` is kept).

Since this PR drops EdgeHTML support, updating the support comment makes sense. However, please double-check the actual behavior this try/catch is guarding: if any non-IE engines still throw on malformed `a.href` access, the comment would become misleading. If the exception is truly IE-only now, consider tightening the wording to the specific IE versions still relevant (e.g., IE <=11) and/or linking it to the exact condition/bug being worked around so future cleanups can safely remove it when IE support is dropped.

The comment says we want `-1` for href-less anchors, but the condition still checks `rclickable.test(...) && elem.href` and returns `0` when true. That means href-less anchors (where `elem.href` is falsy) will *not* hit this branch and will fall through to the `-1` behavior, which matches the intent—but the comment is a bit confusing because it reads like the condition is about href-less anchors. Consider rewording to clarify that this branch is for anchors *with* `href` (return `0`), while href-less anchors should fall through to `-1`. Also, `elem.href` is a resolved absolute URL property in many browsers; if the intent is to check for presence of the attribute, `elem.getAttribute("href")` may be a more precise check (depending on desired semantics).

This now unconditionally returns `elem.textContent` for element/document/documentFragment nodes. That’s fine if 4.0 truly drops all environments where `textContent` might be missing, but it changes behavior from the previous fallback traversal (which would still produce text even if `textContent` were absent/undefined). If we still intend to support any non-DOM or very old DOM implementations (e.g., some embedded/legacy WebViews), consider keeping a minimal fallback like `return elem.textContent || "";` or retaining the child traversal to avoid returning `undefined` and breaking callers expecting a string.

This change drops the `getAttribute` fallback and the special handling for `nonce` being reset when connected. That may be OK for dropping EdgeHTML, but it also removes support for the documented `jQuery.globalEval` use case where `node` can be a plain object “faking” attributes (i.e., no DOM `getAttribute`). With the new code, only direct properties are copied, so callers providing `{ nonce: "..." }` still work, but any other attribute that was previously provided via `getAttribute` (or via a DOM node where the property is empty but attribute exists) will no longer be preserved. Also, switching from `setAttribute` to property assignment changes semantics for attributes like `type`/`noModule`/`nonce` (property vs attribute reflection differs across browsers). Consider keeping `script.setAttribute(i, node[i])` (or a guarded `getAttribute` fallback) for correctness, or at least add a comment/tests confirming that only property-based copying is intended and that `globalEval` object-faking remains supported.

`elem.getRootNode( composed )` assumes `elem` is a Node and that `getRootNode` exists on it. While the outer `if ( !documentElement.getRootNode )` guards the *document* API, it doesn't strictly guarantee `elem.getRootNode` is present (e.g., if `elem` is a window/document or a non-Node value passed in). Consider hardening this by checking `elem && elem.getRootNode` (or `elem.nodeType`) before calling, or documenting that `isAttached` only accepts DOM nodes. This avoids potential TypeErrors in edge cases and keeps the fallback behavior consistent.

This change makes the table-row fallback unconditional for IE 10–11, but previously it was gated by `!support.reliableTrDimensions()` (and applied to IE/Edge). If IE 11 (or IE 10) actually reports reliable `tr` dimensions in some modes, this could cause unnecessary offset* reads (layout thrash) and potentially change behavior. Consider either (a) keeping a targeted feature-detect for IE (e.g. reintroduce a minimal `reliableTrDimensions` check without Edge), or (b) add a comment explaining why the bug is assumed to always exist in IE 10–11 so the unconditional `nodeName(elem, "tr")` is safe.

Since this change is part of dropping EdgeHTML support, consider whether the comment should still mention the full IE range. The `-ms-` prefix normalization is primarily for old IE/legacy MS implementations; if jQuery 4 is also dropping IE<=9 (or other IE versions) elsewhere, this comment may become inaccurate. Suggest aligning this support note with the project’s actual supported IE versions for this branch/release (and/or clarifying that the workaround is for legacy MS vendor prefix behavior rather than specific browser versions).

Since this comment is now IE-only, consider tightening the version range to match what the code actually needs. If the overflow shorthand inference issue applies to all IE versions you still support (including IE11), you can simplify to "Support: IE" or "Support: IE 11" (depending on your support matrix). If it’s truly only for older IEs, ensure the surrounding logic is gated accordingly; otherwise the comment may mislead future maintainers about why we store all three overflow properties.

Dropping the `getAttribute("nonce")` fallback may break CSP nonce propagation in browsers/environments where `HTMLScriptElement#nonce` is not supported or not reflected as a property (e.g., older engines, some embedded WebViews, or when the property is not implemented but the attribute exists). Even if Edge Legacy is dropped, consider keeping a safe fallback (`node.nonce || node.getAttribute("nonce")`) or at least gating the removal behind a documented support assumption and adding a test to ensure `_evalUrl` receives the nonce when set via attribute.

This changes the condition from a feature-detect (`!support.scope`) to a browser-detect (`isIE`). That assumes all non-IE browsers support `:scope` in the specific QSA usage here, which may not hold in all supported environments (e.g., older Safari/WebKit quirks, embedded WebViews). If the goal is to drop EdgeHTML but keep correctness, consider keeping a capability check (e.g., `supportsScope` local test) and only special-case IE where needed, rather than relying solely on UA/engine detection. At minimum, please confirm `isIE` is the only remaining browser without `:scope` support in the project’s support matrix and add a comment explaining why feature detection is no longer necessary.

This changes `rbuggyQSA` from an array built via feature-detection to a static IE-only regex. That assumes the `[name='']` QSA bug exists in all supported IE versions (incl. IE9/10) and that no other engines need these workarounds. Previously, the `[name='']` part was guarded by an actual `querySelectorAll` check, and the comment noted IE10 & older *didn't* seem to have the issue. Please either (a) keep the feature-detection for the `[name='']` selector, or (b) gate this pattern to IE11 specifically (e.g., via a more precise IE version check) to avoid unnecessary regex bloat/false positives in older IE. Also ensure downstream code handles `rbuggyQSA` being `false` when not IE (it used to be `[]` then `false` after `length && ...`, but now it’s directly `false`/RegExp).

Since this PR drops EdgeHTML support, updating the support note makes sense. However, the comment now claims this is IE-only; please double-check that the underlying behavior ("Permission denied" on strict document comparisons) is truly limited to IE and not also reproducible in other legacy/nonstandard contexts (e.g., old WebView/embedded IE modes). If it can still happen outside IE, consider wording it more generally (or referencing the specific IE bug) so future maintainers don’t incorrectly remove the loose-equality workaround.

This comment is now IE-specific, but the fallback is still used for any environment lacking `Array#flat` (e.g., older Safari/Android WebView, embedded runtimes, or unusual JS engines). Consider keeping the wording generic ("Provide fallback for browsers without Array#flat") or explicitly stating that IE is the primary target but not the only possible consumer, to avoid misleading future maintainers about when this branch can execute.

Since Edge Legacy support is being dropped, this comment update makes sense, but please double-check the actual guard condition matches the intent. The code currently checks `if ( config.esmodules && QUnit )` and then builds a string containing `import(...)` to avoid parse-time SyntaxError in non-supporting browsers. If this path can still execute in IE 11, ensure the string-based construction is still necessary and that no other parts of this block (or surrounding code) reference Edge-specific behavior. Also consider clarifying that the crash is a *parse-time* SyntaxError (not runtime) to document why the import is embedded in a string.

Dropping the Edge Legacy exclusion changes the condition to skip this test for any UA containing "safari". That will also skip the test in Chromium-based Edge if its UA includes "Safari" (as Chrome/Edge UAs typically do), which seems opposite of the comment above about Chrome/Safari behavior and may reduce coverage on modern browsers. Consider switching to a more robust check (e.g., detect real Safari vs Chromium via /chrome|chromium|edg\//i) or update the surrounding comment to clarify the intended browser matrix for running/skipping this test.

This changes the skip condition from feature-detection (`Array.prototype.flat`) to UA-based detection (`QUnit.isIE`). That makes the test less robust: any non-IE environment lacking `Array#flat` (older Safari/Android WebView, embedded runtimes, etc.) would now run the test and potentially fail. Consider keeping feature detection (or combining both, e.g. `if ( Array.prototype.flat && !QUnit.isIE )`) and update the comment/message accordingly. If the intent is specifically to avoid IE due to missing `flat`, feature detection alone already expresses that without relying on `QUnit.isIE` being present/accurate.

This UA sniffing got broadened significantly. Previously it tried to approximate “WebKit/Blink but not Firefox/Edge” (even if imperfect); now it matches any UA containing “safari”, which includes Chrome/Chromium/Edge (all include `Safari/…`) and may change which branch runs and what the test asserts. If the intent is to target Safari/WebKit-specific behavior, consider tightening the detection (e.g., `isSafari = /safari/i && !/chrome|chromium|crios|android/i`), or better, avoid UA sniffing and feature-detect the behavior under test. Also, if Edge Legacy is the only thing being removed, dropping the `edge` exclusion is fine, but you likely still need to exclude Chromium-based browsers if they shouldn’t be treated as Safari.

These comments explained why the JSON string uses escaped double quotes (JSHint + JSON parsing). If the underlying linting rule/tooling changed (e.g., JSHint removed/disabled or rule updated), consider replacing this with a shorter, current explanation (or a link to the relevant lint config) so future contributors don’t “simplify” the string and accidentally break parsing. If no longer needed, it’d be good to confirm there’s no remaining linter that would flag single quotes here.

Switching from a feature-detect-based skip (`document.body.getRootNode`) to `QUnit.testUnlessIE` changes the intent of the test: it will now run in any non-IE environment even if Shadow DOM APIs are missing/partial (e.g., older/embedded WebViews). If the test truly requires Shadow DOM, consider keeping a capability check (e.g., `getRootNode`/`attachShadow`) in addition to the IE exclusion, or update the comment/name to reflect that this is now an IE-only skip rather than a Shadow DOM support gate.

Switching to `QUnit.testUnlessIE` makes sense after dropping EdgeHTML, but please double-check that this helper is available in all test runners/environments we support (including any external QUnit harnesses). If `testUnlessIE` is defined only in jQuery’s custom QUnit setup, consider keeping the explicit `QUnit[ QUnit.isIE ? "skip" : "test" ]` pattern for portability/clarity, or add a brief note pointing to where `testUnlessIE` is defined.

Switching to `QUnit.testUnlessIE` matches the updated support note, but it changes the skip condition from `(QUnit.isIE || /edge\//i.test(navigator.userAgent))` to IE-only. If this PR is truly dropping EdgeHTML support, that's fine; however, please ensure there are no remaining EdgeHTML runs in CI (or downstream) where this test would now execute and fail due to missing `:scope`. Also, relying on UA sniffing was removed here—good—but confirm `QUnit.testUnlessIE` is available in all test environments and behaves identically (i.e., skips only IE, not other legacy engines).

The new condition `QUnit.jQuerySelectors && !QUnit.isIE` is broader than the previous `QUnit.jQuerySelectors && document.body.getRootNode` check. This will now run the test in any non-IE environment even if `getRootNode`/Shadow DOM APIs are missing or partially implemented, which could cause false failures in non-IE but non-Shadow-DOM-capable browsers/environments. Consider keeping a feature-detect (e.g., `document.body.getRootNode` and possibly `Element.prototype.attachShadow`) in addition to `!QUnit.isIE`, or update the comment to justify why the feature-detect is no longer needed.

The comment is now misleading: `/chrome/i` will not match Chromium-based Edge UA strings (they contain `Edg/`, not `Chrome` in a way you can rely on for detection here), so this branch does *not* necessarily "catch Edge". If the intent is to treat Chromium Edge like Chrome in this test, consider explicitly checking for `/(?:edg|edga|edgios)\//i` (or `\bedg\//i`) before/alongside the Chrome check, or update the comment to avoid claiming Edge is covered.

Hard-coding the new "<ticker>.<custom-type>" string here makes the regression test brittle (renames/refactors of the type name, or changes to the symbol formatting, will break it). Prefer using the actual Symbol instance returned by AddData<Bitcoin>("BTC") (store it in a field) and pass that to Order, or build the string via the same helper used to create the symbol (e.g., Symbol.Create("BTC", SecurityType.Base, Market.USA, typeof(Bitcoin)) if available). This also avoids relying on the SymbolCache backward-compat shim behavior for string tickers.

Hard-coding the new custom-data symbol string here makes the regression test brittle and forces users to know the internal "<ticker>.<type>" formatting. Prefer using the Symbol returned by AddData (store it, e.g. `self.quandl = self.AddData(...).Symbol`) and then index `self.Securities[self.quandl]` / pass that Symbol into History. If you must keep string-based access for backwards-compat coverage, consider adding a small helper/constant in this algorithm to avoid repeating the literal and to make future format changes localized.

Using `symbol.ID.Symbol` as the alias is the right direction for disambiguating custom data, but please ensure this is the *only* cache key you want to register here. If you intend to preserve backwards compatibility for lookups by the raw `ticker` (e.g. `"BTC"`), consider also setting `SymbolCache.Set(ticker, symbol)` when it’s safe (no existing mapping / no ambiguity), or explicitly document that Python `AddData` will no longer populate the cache under the raw ticker. Otherwise, existing code paths that rely on `SymbolCache.GetSymbol("TWX")` may behave differently depending on whether the symbol was created via C# vs Python.

Passing `typeof(T)` into `Symbol.Create(...)` is intended to disambiguate `SecurityType.Base` symbols, but here `securityType` is a parameter and may be non-Base. If `Symbol.Create` ignores the type for non-Base, this is harmless but confusing; if it doesn’t, it could unintentionally change symbol identity for other security types. Consider guarding this so the type is only provided when `securityType == SecurityType.Base` (or when `T : BaseData` and `securityType` is Base), and add/ensure tests for `AddUniverse<T>` with non-Base security types to confirm no SID changes.

Same concern as above: `typeof(T)` is being applied to every selected symbol regardless of `securityType`. If `selector` returns tickers for equities/forex/etc, we should not be appending/encoding the universe data type into those symbols. Recommend conditionally passing the type only for `SecurityType.Base`, otherwise call the 3-arg overload (or pass `null` for the type) to avoid unexpected symbol/SID changes and potential cache misses.

Passing `null` for the custom data type defeats the goal of disambiguating `SecurityType.Base` symbols by type (and may reintroduce collisions for user-defined universes). If this universe can represent custom data, consider threading the actual `Type` through `CreateSymbol` (e.g., add a `Type customDataType = null` parameter) and pass it here. If `UserDefinedUniverse` is intentionally type-agnostic, add a comment explaining why `null` is correct and confirm `GenerateBase(null, ...)` still produces stable/unique IDs in this context.

This change makes `Future` use `symbol.ID.Symbol` instead of `symbol.Value`. Please double-check this is intended: for futures, `Value` can include the mapped/alias representation while `ID.Symbol` is the canonical SID symbol (often including expiry). Using `ID.Symbol` may change the market-hours DB lookup key and could break lookups if the database keys are based on the ticker/root (or mapped symbol) rather than the full SID symbol. If the goal is only to fix custom data (`SecurityType.Base`) collisions, consider limiting this branch to `SecurityType.Base` only, or add a comment/test demonstrating why futures must also use `ID.Symbol` here.

Appending `dataType.Name` can still collide for types with the same simple name in different namespaces/assemblies (e.g., `Foo.Bar.Bitcoin` vs `Baz.Qux.Bitcoin`). Consider using a more stable/unique discriminator such as `dataType.FullName` (possibly sanitized/shortened) or an explicit attribute/alias for custom data types. Also, `ToUpperInvariant()` here is unconditional while `GenerateBase(..., forceSymbolToUpper: false)` disables uppercasing later; this means base symbols will still be uppercased at least for the ticker portion. If the intent is to preserve user-provided casing for Base symbols, avoid uppercasing here (or make it conditional/consistent with `forceSymbolToUpper`).

Passing a potentially null `baseDataType` into `SecurityIdentifier.GenerateBase` could reintroduce collisions or throw depending on that implementation. Consider guarding here: if `baseDataType == null`, either fall back to the legacy `GenerateBase(ticker, market)` behavior (for backward compatibility) or throw an `ArgumentNullException` when `securityType == SecurityType.Base` to force callers to disambiguate. Also update the XML doc to clarify the null behavior explicitly.

This fallback does a full scan over the entire symbol cache (`_cache.Symbols.Where(...)`) on every miss, which can become expensive as the cache grows and is also not atomic with respect to concurrent updates (you may get inconsistent results between enumeration and subsequent use). Consider avoiding `ToList()` + `Count` by using a single-pass approach (track first match and whether there are multiple), and/or maintaining an auxiliary index for custom-data prefixes (e.g., map from upper ticker to list/count of matching custom symbols) to keep lookups O(1). Also, `StartsWith(search)` should specify `StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase` (even though the dictionary is case-insensitive) to make the intent explicit and avoid culture-related surprises.

This assertion hard-codes the expected SID symbol format as "<TICKER>.<TypeName>". Since the canonical behavior is already validated on the next line via `SecurityIdentifier.GenerateBaseSymbol(typeof(Quandl), ticker)`, this line is redundant and makes the test brittle if the formatting rules change (e.g., delimiter, casing, or inclusion of additional qualifiers). Consider removing this assertion and relying solely on `GenerateBaseSymbol(...)`, or at least derive the expected string from the same helper to keep the test aligned with production logic.

Passing `null` for the new `Type` parameter makes this test exercise the legacy/ambiguous Base SID path, which may be fine for backwards-compat coverage but no longer reflects the intended uniqueness fix (type-disambiguated custom symbols). Consider updating the test to pass an explicit custom data type (e.g., `typeof(MyCustomData)`) so it validates the new behavior and avoids future regressions where `null` could reintroduce collisions. If the goal is specifically to test the legacy behavior, add a brief comment explaining why `null` is intentional.

This test couples Common unit tests to an Algorithm project type (`LiveTradingFeaturesAlgorithm.Bitcoin`). That creates an unnecessary dependency and can make the test brittle if the algorithm/test fixture is moved/renamed. Prefer defining a minimal local `class Bitcoin : BaseData { }` (or similar) inside this test file/namespace and using `typeof(Bitcoin)` so the test only validates `GenerateBase` behavior without relying on unrelated algorithm code.

Passing `null` for the new `GenerateBase` type parameter makes this test symbol use the legacy/non-disambiguated SID format. That’s fine if the intent is to keep CASH as a generic base symbol, but it also means this test won’t exercise the new collision-avoidance behavior (type-suffixed base symbols). Consider using an explicit type (e.g., `typeof(Cash)`/a dummy custom data type) if the test should validate the new uniqueness semantics, or add a brief comment explaining why `null` is required here to preserve backwards compatibility. Also ensure `GenerateBase` is null-safe and won’t throw if it tries to access `type.Name`.

This test is still constructing a custom `Symbol` with ticker-only value ("BTC") while the PR changes custom data symbols to include the custom data type name for uniqueness (e.g., "BTC.Bitcoin"). Passing `null` for the new `GenerateBase` type parameter also defeats the collision fix and may not reflect real usage (`AddData<T>` will pass the type). Consider updating the test to pass the custom data type (e.g., `typeof(LiveTradingFeaturesAlgorithm.Bitcoin)`) into `GenerateBase` (or use `Symbol.Create("BTC", SecurityType.Base, Market.USA, typeof(...))` if available) and adjust subsequent expectations/market-hours entry accordingly so the test validates the new behavior rather than the legacy one.

Test name says `InvalidOperation` but the assertion expects `InvalidOperationException`. Consider renaming to `GetSymbol_ThrowsInvalidOperationException_WhenTooManyCustomDataSymbolMatches` (or similar) to match the actual behavior and keep naming consistent with `GetSymbol_ThrowsInvalidOperationException_WhenNoMatches` above. This helps readability and avoids confusion when scanning test failures.

This test now calls the new `GenerateBase(Type, string, string)` overload with a `null` type. If the production change relies on appending the custom data type name to avoid collisions, passing `null` here may bypass the new behavior and reduce coverage (and could even reintroduce collisions if other tests use the same ticker). Consider passing an explicit custom data `Type` (e.g., `typeof(YourCustomData)`) or at least add a comment/assertion clarifying that this test intentionally exercises the legacy/no-type path. Also verify `GenerateBase` handles `null` consistently (no NRE, deterministic SID).

This changes the expected-statistics value from a raw numeric string ("0.001") to a currency-formatted string ("$2600"). Please confirm the AUM Capacity statistic is consistently formatted across result handlers/regression expectations (e.g., always currency with `$` and no decimals, or with thousands separators). If the underlying statistic is numeric, consider keeping `ExpectedStatistics` in a culture-invariant numeric form and applying formatting only at display time to avoid brittle regression tests (locale/currency symbol/rounding differences).

This changes the expected statistic from a raw numeric string ("0.004") to a currency-formatted string ("$200"). Please confirm the new AUM Capacity statistic is always emitted as a formatted currency string (including symbol, rounding, and culture invariance) across result handlers/backtest vs live. If formatting can vary (e.g., "$200.00", "200", different currency symbols), consider asserting a numeric value or using a consistent invariant formatting helper in both production code and regression expectations to avoid brittle tests.

This expected statistic value changed from an unformatted decimal ("33790.197") to a currency-formatted string ("$174393400"). Please ensure the AUM Capacity statistic is consistently formatted across the codebase/regressions (e.g., always currency with separators like "$174,393,400" or always a raw numeric string). If the statistic is now emitted as currency, consider updating any parsing/validation logic and other regression ExpectedStatistics entries accordingly to avoid brittle string comparisons due to formatting differences (culture/number formatting). Also double-check the magnitude jump is intended (capacity algorithm change) and not a units mismatch (e.g., dollars vs. thousands).

Removing the hard-coded expected "AUM Capacity" value will make this regression less strict, but it also risks masking regressions in the new capacity calculation. If the capacity manager is now producing this stat for this algorithm, consider keeping the entry and updating it to the new deterministic expected value (or, if it’s inherently non-deterministic due to volume/price data variability, add a tolerance-based assertion or document why this algorithm should not assert AUM Capacity). Otherwise, ensure the regression test framework won’t fail due to an unexpected extra statistic key being present.

This expected-statistics value changed from a plain numeric string to a currency-formatted string with a `$` prefix and no decimals. Please ensure the AUM Capacity statistic is consistently formatted across the codebase (and matches how other monetary stats like "Total Fees" are emitted). If the statistic is produced as a numeric value and formatted later, consider keeping the expected value numeric (or using the same formatting helper used by the result handler) to avoid brittle regression tests due to culture/formatting changes (currency symbol, separators, rounding).

This expected statistic changed from a plain numeric string ("1956.742") to a currency-formatted string ("$6847400"). Please ensure the AUM Capacity statistic is consistently formatted across the codebase (e.g., always currency with separators like "$6,847,400" or always a raw number) and matches whatever formatting `StatisticsBuilder`/result handlers apply. Mixing formats can cause brittle regression tests and parsing issues in tooling that expects numeric values. If the new capacity is intended to be a dollar amount, consider adding thousands separators and/or fixed decimals for readability and consistency with other currency stats (e.g., Total Fees).

The expected statistic value format changed from a plain number ("3003.653") to a currency string ("$18228000"). Please ensure this matches the formatting produced by the new AUM capacity statistic (e.g., whether it uses currency formatting with/without commas, decimals, and culture-invariant behavior). If the result handler formats using `C`/`ToString("C")`, this expectation may be locale-dependent; consider using an invariant/explicit format (e.g., "$18,228,000" or "$18228000.00") consistent with the engine output to avoid regression test flakiness across environments.

This expected-statistics value changes format and scale (from an unformatted decimal to a currency string). Please ensure the AUM Capacity statistic is consistently formatted across result handlers/regression expectations (e.g., always currency with/without decimals) and that the underlying calculation is deterministic for this regression (no dependence on live volume data, time zone/day boundaries, or brokerage model differences). If the statistic is emitted as a numeric internally, consider keeping ExpectedStatistics as a numeric string (or standardizing via a shared formatter) to avoid brittle string comparisons in regressions.

This changes the expected statistic format from a plain numeric string ("2953.758") to a currency-formatted string ("$15241900"). Please ensure the AUM Capacity statistic is consistently formatted across all regression algorithms and result handlers (e.g., always currency with symbol, thousands separators, and fixed decimals, or always raw numeric). If the formatting is culture-dependent, consider using an invariant format to avoid regression failures on non-US locales. Also, verify the magnitude/unit change is intended (it looks like a shift from ~2.9k to ~$15.2M), and add a brief comment or reference to the new capacity calculation to make the expectation clear.

`System.Diagnostics.Eventing.Reader` looks unused in this file and is also a Windows-specific API surface that can cause portability/build issues on non-Windows targets. Please remove this using directive unless it's required elsewhere in the file.

Removing `MaximumTradeableVolume` (and its accessor) is fine if capacity logic has been fully migrated to `AssetsUnderManagementCapacityManager`, but please verify there are no remaining references (including reflection/config usage) to `PortfolioStatistics.GetMaximumTradeableVolume()` or the constant. If other components still need this value, consider relocating it to a more appropriate shared location (e.g., the new capacity manager or a config setting) rather than deleting outright, to avoid breaking downstream consumers and to keep the default (2.5%) discoverable.

This formatting is brittle and likely incorrect for many values: dividing by 100, rounding, then appending "00" effectively forces the last two digits to zero and can materially change the displayed capacity (e.g., 150 -> "$20000"). It also drops thousands separators and ignores culture/formatting conventions. Consider formatting as currency directly (e.g., aumCapacity.ToString("C0", CultureInfo.InvariantCulture) or "$" + aumCapacity.ToStringInvariant("0")) depending on desired rounding, and document/ensure the unit of `aumCapacity` (dollars vs cents) instead of encoding scaling logic in the string.

Using `?? 0m` avoids a potential NRE, but defaulting to `0` may be misleading in stats (it can look like the strategy has zero capacity rather than “not computed/unsupported”). Consider either (a) ensuring `AssetsUnderManagementCapacityManager` is always initialized for result handlers so this value is always meaningful, or (b) propagating a nullable/flag to `StatisticsBuilder.Generate` so it can omit the stat or display "N/A" when the manager is not available. If `0` is the intended sentinel, please add a brief comment explaining that semantics.

This changes `size` from a raw volume-based scaling (`volume * 1_000_000`) to `volume / price * 1_000_000`, which implicitly assumes `volume` is a *dollar volume* (or notional) and converts it to shares using `price`. However, the rest of the tests/comments read like `volume` is already in *shares* (e.g., "Volume of 45mi"), and the capacity formulas above use `50_000_000` etc. as if they were share volumes. This mismatch can silently skew all expected capacities by a factor of `1/price` and makes the test semantics unclear.

Suggestion: clarify the unit of the `volume` parameter (rename to `dollarVolumeMillions` or `shareVolumeMillions`), and adjust the conversion accordingly. If `volume` is intended to be share volume in millions, keep `volume * 1_000_000`. If it’s intended to be dollar volume in millions, update the test comments/expected values to reflect that and consider using the actual order fill price instead of a hard-coded `price = 100` to avoid coupling the test to an arbitrary constant.

This help text change makes `source-meta-dir` sound optional for SmartInsiderConverter, but the option name is still `source-meta-dir`, which is confusing if it’s now being used to point to the *processed files* directory (per issue/PR description). Consider introducing a dedicated option name (e.g., `processed-dir` / `processed-source-dir`) or at least updating the description to explicitly state what directory SmartInsider expects here. Also ensure the parser/validation logic actually treats it as optional for SmartInsider (and required for IVolatility) so the CLI help matches runtime behavior.

The new parameter name `source-meta-dir` is a bit ambiguous given the issue/PR description about selecting the processed files directory. Consider renaming to something clearer/consistent with existing options (e.g., `processed-dir` / `processed-source-dir` / `meta-dir`) and ensure it’s documented in the ToolBox config/help output. Also, the default path uses `Globals.DataFolder/...`; if `destination-dir` is intended to be the output location, double-check that defaulting this to the global data folder won’t surprise users running the tool from a custom data root—ideally the default should be derived from `destination-dir` or `source-dir` if that’s where processed/meta files typically live.

This builds the processed file path using `destinationDirectory.Name` as an intermediate folder. That assumes the processed-files directory is organized exactly like the destination directory structure (and that `destinationDirectory.Name` is the correct subfolder key). If the intent is to let users choose an arbitrary processed-files root, consider making the subfolder explicit (e.g., pass in the relative subdirectory name, or use a well-known constant), and ensure the directory exists before reading. Also, if `_processedFilesDirectory` can be null/missing, add validation in the constructor to fail fast with a clear message.

Defaulting `processedFileDirectory` to `Globals.DataFolder/...` is reasonable, but this method currently doesn't validate that the directory exists (or create it) before constructing `DirectoryInfo` and passing it to the converter. If the converter expects the processed files to already be present, consider adding an explicit `Directory.Exists` check with a clear exception message (or create the directory if that's the intended behavior). Also consider trimming whitespace (`string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace`) to avoid treating "   " as a valid path.

Switching the condition to `fill.IsAssignment` matches the intent (raise `OptionPositionAssigned` only for assignments), but please ensure `IsAssignment` is reliably set for backtesting fills. In particular, confirm that option exercises do not set `IsAssignment=true`, and that assignment events do set it even if the originating order type isn’t `OptionExercise` (e.g., assignment generated without an explicit order). If `IsAssignment` can be false/undefined in some assignment paths, consider guarding with additional context (order type / direction) or adding a unit test covering both assignment vs exercise to prevent regressions.

Removing the `fill.IsAssignment` guard here will cause `_algorithm.OnAssignmentOrderEvent(fill)` to be invoked for *all* fills routed through `HandlePositionAssigned`, including option exercises (the bug described in #4800). If the intent is to only raise assignment events, keep the `fill.IsAssignment` check here (or ensure the caller only invokes `HandlePositionAssigned` for assignments). Otherwise, this change appears to reintroduce/retain the incorrect behavior where exercises trigger assignment callbacks.

Renaming the public API from `Delta(...)` to `D(...)` is a breaking change and also creates ambiguity with other common indicator shorthands (e.g., DEMA, DonchianChannel `DCH`, etc.). The PR goal is to *add* an Option Delta indicator, so it would be safer to keep `Delta(...)` as the primary method name and (optionally) add `D(...)`/`Δ(...)` as aliases. If you intended to introduce a shorthand, consider keeping `Delta` and adding `D` as a wrapper to preserve backward compatibility and discoverability.

Using a non-ASCII method name (`Δ`) can cause tooling/IDE/searchability issues and may not be supported consistently across all environments (source control diffs, some editors, code generators, documentation tooling). It also makes the API harder to type and discover. Recommend avoiding Unicode identifiers in the public API; if you want a shorthand, prefer an ASCII alias (e.g., `Delta`/`D`) and keep `Δ` out of the public surface (or at least ensure it’s purely additive and well-documented).

Using `algorithm.D(_symbol)` here is ambiguous and likely inconsistent with the intended public API for an Option Delta indicator. `D` is commonly used for other indicators (e.g., derivative) and is not self-descriptive; it also risks name collisions/overload confusion. Since this test is specifically for Delta, it should call the explicit helper (e.g., `algorithm.Delta(_symbol)`) unless the PR intentionally introduced `D` as the canonical alias for Delta. If `D` is meant to be an alias, consider keeping `Delta` as the primary method and optionally adding `D` as a convenience, and update tests/docs accordingly to avoid breaking existing user code and to improve readability.

Indicator name formatting: most QCAlgorithm indicator helpers use a consistent naming convention (often without parentheses or with a standardized pattern). Using $"ZLEMA({period})" may be fine, but please confirm it matches existing indicator naming patterns to avoid breaking expectations in tests/logging/chart series names. If other indicators use e.g. $"ZLEMA" + period or $"ZLEMA" with period as a separate suffix, consider aligning for consistency.

Returning 0 while warming up is inconsistent with most Lean indicators and can leak a misleading value into downstream indicators/plots. Prefer returning the previous value (e.g., `Current.Value`) or `input.Value` until ready, and rely on `IsReady`/`WarmUpPeriod` to signal readiness. Also consider still updating `_ema` with the best available de-lagged value (or at least updating with `input.Value`) instead of early-returning, so the EMA state progresses during warmup.

This commented-out assertion suggests the test originally intended to verify that the internal EMA state is reset too, but it’s currently not being checked. Either remove this dead/commented code to keep the test clean, or (preferably) add a proper assertion that validates all internal state is reset without relying on private field access (e.g., expose/reset via public properties, or validate behaviorally by feeding a known sequence after Reset and asserting expected outputs/IsReady transitions). Leaving this commented line can hide a real regression where Reset() doesn’t fully reset internal components.

Consider using the same item type used elsewhere in the test project for data files (often `Content` with `CopyToOutputDirectory`) instead of `None Update`, unless there’s a specific reason. `None` items can behave differently across tooling (e.g., not showing as content in some IDE views) and may not be included as expected if the file isn’t already part of the project via globbing. If the intent is to always ship this CSV with test outputs, `Content Include="TestData\spy_with_zlema.csv"` (or ensuring the file is covered by an existing wildcard) is typically clearer and more robust.

This CSV appears to include prefixed line numbers/indentation (e.g., leading spaces and `1:`) as part of the file content. Most LEAN test data CSVs are plain `Header\nrow...` without embedded line numbers, and parsers typically expect the first column to be `Date` (not `1:Date`). If this is accidental (copied from a diff viewer), tests that read this file will fail to parse dates/columns. Please regenerate/commit the raw CSV without the `N:` prefixes and leading whitespace, and ensure the header matches the expected schema (e.g., `Date,Close,ZLEMA5`).

Creating a mute unconditionally can raise on duplicate dismissals (e.g., user taps twice, retries, concurrent requests) if there is/should be a uniqueness constraint on [account, announcement]. Consider making this idempotent by using `find_or_create_by!` (or `create_or_find_by!` on newer Rails) and/or rescuing `ActiveRecord::RecordNotUnique` to return `render_empty` anyway. Also consider whether dismissing an announcement should be allowed when `current_account` is nil (it’s guarded by `require_user!` for `dismiss`, so OK) and whether you want to validate that the announcement is local/published at time of dismissal (you already scope to `published`).

This SVG asset is stored as a single minified line and still lacks a trailing newline (the previous version explicitly had `\ No newline at end of file`). Please add a final newline to avoid noisy diffs and potential tooling issues (POSIX text file expectations, some linters/formatters). Also consider running it through the repo’s standard SVG optimization/formatting step (e.g., SVGO) so future changes are stable and reviewable.

This POST is fire-and-forget: the UI state is updated optimistically, but failures (network/auth/5xx) are ignored and there’s no rollback or error handling. Consider returning the promise and adding `.catch(...)` to either re-dispatch a revert action / refetch announcements, or at least surface an error. Also consider awaiting the request before dispatching dismiss if you want strict consistency.

This assumes `announcement.content` is always a non-null string. If the API can return `null`/missing content (e.g., draft/scheduled/invalid data), `emojify` may throw or produce unexpected output. Consider guarding/coercing (e.g., `normalAnnouncement.content || ''`) and/or aligning the field naming with other normalizers (many use `contentHtml` but ensure consumers expect this exact key for announcements). Also, if announcements support mentions/hashtags/links like statuses, this only emojifies; you may need to ensure the server already provides sanitized HTML or add the same HTML handling pipeline used for statuses to avoid inconsistent rendering.

Parsing `data.payload` without guarding can throw and potentially break the streaming message handler for all subsequent events if the payload is malformed or empty. Consider wrapping `JSON.parse` in a try/catch (and logging/reporting) or ensuring the streaming client already provides a parsed object. Also, if `updateAnnouncements` expects an array vs a single announcement, confirm the payload shape matches; otherwise normalize here (e.g., `const payload = safeParse(...); if (payload) dispatch(updateAnnouncements(payload));`).

The second <FormattedDate> passes `second={skipTime ? undefined : ''}`. `FormattedDate` expects a valid option value (e.g., '2-digit'/'numeric') or the prop to be omitted; an empty string is not a valid Intl.DateTimeFormat option and may cause warnings or inconsistent behavior across browsers. Suggest changing to `second={skipTime ? undefined : undefined}` (i.e., remove the prop entirely when skipping) or use a valid value like `'2-digit'` when you want seconds displayed.

Consider providing a safe default (e.g., an empty List) in case the announcements reducer hasn't been mounted/initialized yet. As written, `state.getIn(['announcements', 'items'])` can return `undefined`, which may break `Announcements` if it assumes an iterable. Using `state.getIn(['announcements', 'items'], Immutable.List())` (or whatever default the component expects) makes the container more robust during initial load and reducer changes.

Switching to the aliased import path is fine, but please ensure the bundler/webpack alias for `mastodon/*` is consistently configured for all environments (tests, storybook, production builds) and that other files in this area use the same convention. If the alias isn’t guaranteed, this change can break builds that rely on relative paths.

Prepending the announcements unconditionally means the container will mount for every home timeline render (including when there are no announcements), which can add extra API/streaming subscriptions and re-render cost. Consider guarding this with a selector/prop (e.g., only render when there are active announcements or when the user hasn’t dismissed them), or ensure `AnnouncementsContainer` is internally a no-op without network work when empty. Also verify `alwaysPrepend` is necessary—if it forces the prepend to stay visible even when scrolled, it may be undesirable UX for pinned/scroll states.

Removing the `onSwitching` handler may change swipe/drag behavior (e.g., updating UI state during the gesture, preloading, or preventing scroll/zoom issues). Please confirm `handleSwitching` is no longer needed and that it’s removed/unused elsewhere in this component; otherwise keep the prop or delete the now-dead method/bindings to avoid regressions and lint warnings.

Sorting by `starts_at` after `unshift` may not behave as intended if `starts_at` can be `null`/missing (e.g., immediate announcements) or if it is a string that isn't consistently parseable. `Immutable.sortBy` will also place `null` values first, which could reorder items unexpectedly. Consider normalizing the sort key (e.g., `Date.parse(starts_at) || 0` / `Infinity` depending on desired ordering) and explicitly choosing ascending/descending (e.g., `sortBy(...).reverse()` if newest/most recent should be on top). Also note this re-sorts the entire list on every update; if updates are frequent, it may be worth inserting in order or sorting once at fetch time.

Adding the `announcements` reducer here is correct, but please ensure the key name matches what the rest of the app expects (selectors, streaming event handlers, and any persisted state). If other reducers use a naming convention (e.g., snake_case vs camelCase) or are grouped/ordered consistently, consider placing `announcements` in the appropriate position to keep the reducer map stable and predictable. Also verify `./announcements` has a default export; otherwise this import will break at runtime.

`word-wrap: break-word;` is a legacy alias and can behave inconsistently. Consider using `overflow-wrap: break-word;` (and optionally `word-break: break-word;`/`break-all` depending on desired behavior) to ensure long URLs/strings in announcements reliably wrap across browsers.

Adding `:id` to the select list changes the shape of the cached record. If this cache key is shared across deployments/versions, older cached entries may not have `id` loaded, which can lead to unexpected `nil`/missing attribute behavior when code starts relying on it. Consider either (a) versioning the cache key (e.g., include a schema/version component in `to_key`) to avoid mixed cached payloads, or (b) ensuring callers don’t depend on `id` being present unless it’s guaranteed. Also, if the intent is to use `id` later, verify `find_remote` doesn’t override the select (e.g., via `reselect`) and that `id` is actually needed here (otherwise it’s extra data in cache).

Adding a new serializer mapping is fine, but please ensure `REST::AnnouncementSerializer` is loaded in all code paths where `InlineRenderer` is used. If this file relies on autoloading, it should be OK in Rails, but in eager-load/production or non-Rails contexts (e.g., background jobs/streaming), missing autoload paths can surface as `NameError`. Consider adding/confirming an explicit require or ensuring the serializer is in an eager-load path, and add a small spec covering `InlineRenderer` rendering `:announcement` to catch regressions.

This line is doing a lot of work in a single expression (scan/split/uniq/cache lookup), which hurts readability and makes it harder to reason about edge cases. Consider extracting into a few named steps (e.g., `pairs = text.scan(...).map...`, `pairs.uniq!`, then `pairs.map { ... }`) and/or moving the mention parsing into a dedicated helper/service shared with other text parsing code.

Also, `split('@', 2)` will yield `[username]` when no domain is present; `domain` will be `nil`, so please confirm `EntityCache.instance.mention(username, nil)` is supported and won’t create ambiguous cache keys. If the intent is to only resolve local mentions when domain is missing, it may be safer to normalize `domain` (e.g., `domain&.downcase`) and/or explicitly handle the no-domain case.

Finally, `uniq` here is applied before cache lookup, which is good, but it’s based on raw `username/domain` strings; if mention matching is case-insensitive, you may want to normalize case before `uniq` to avoid duplicate lookups.

This scope builds a SQL fragment via string interpolation of `account.id`, which is brittle and can become an injection vector if the value is ever not strictly an integer (or if this pattern gets copied elsewhere). Prefer parameterized joins/where or an Arel-based join. For example: `left_outer_joins(:announcement_mutes).where(announcement_mutes: { id: nil, account_id: account.id })` (or join with `sanitize_sql_array`). Also consider handling `account` being nil to avoid raising on `account.id`.

Consider enforcing this uniqueness at the database level as well (unique composite index on [:account_id, :announcement_id]). The model-level validation alone is race-condition prone and can allow duplicates under concurrent inserts; a DB constraint will guarantee integrity and let you handle RecordNotUnique cleanly.

Consider specifying `class_name` (and `inverse_of`) for clarity/consistency with other associations, since `announcement_mutes` implies a non-standard model name and Rails will constantize to `AnnouncementMute` by default. If the actual model is named differently (e.g., `AnnouncementMute` vs `AnnouncementMuteRelationship`), this will break at runtime. Also consider whether `dependent: :destroy` is desired here (callbacks) vs `:delete_all` for a pure join/mute table to avoid N+1 deletes when removing an account.

Changing the signature to `domain = nil` makes the argument optional, but the method likely uses `domain` to scope emoji lookup (local vs remote). Please ensure the implementation below handles `nil` explicitly (e.g., defaulting to local emojis only, or inferring domain from context) rather than accidentally broadening the query to all domains or returning incorrect matches. Also consider whether callers that previously always passed `domain` should be updated for clarity, and add/adjust tests for `from_text(text)` behavior to avoid regressions.

`has_many :mentions` is missing an explicit serializer. Since this file defines an inner `AccountSerializer`, it looks like mentions are intended to serialize accounts with a limited field set, but without `serializer: AccountSerializer` it will fall back to the default serializer for the mention objects (or accounts), which may be incorrect/inconsistent and could expose extra fields. Consider `has_many :mentions, serializer: AccountSerializer` (or a dedicated Mention serializer) to ensure stable API output.

Using `redis.exists` here is both a performance and correctness concern: it adds an extra round-trip per account (N+1 Redis calls) and `EXISTS` is not atomic with `PUBLISH` (a subscriber could connect/disconnect between calls). Consider publishing unconditionally (Redis `PUBLISH` is cheap and returns subscriber count), or pipeline/batch the checks, or use a Redis set of subscribed timelines to iterate over instead of scanning all active accounts. Also note that `exists` is deprecated in newer redis-rb in favor of `exists?` (or `exists` returning integer), so this may break depending on the client version.

The expiration predicate looks inverted: to unpublish expired announcements, you likely want those with `ends_at <= now` (already ended), not `ends_at >= now` (still active). As written, `unpublish_expired_announcements!` would unpublish announcements whose end time is in the future. Consider changing to `ends_at <= ?` (or `< ?`) and ensure the scope matches the intended semantics/timezone handling.

Using a member POST for `dismiss` implies dismissing a single announcement by ID. The issue/PR description suggests a global "mark all as read"/read-state concept; if the intended behavior is per-user per-announcement dismissal, this is fine, but then ensure the API naming matches Mastodon conventions (often `dismiss` is used for global dismissals) and that idempotency is handled (repeated calls should be safe). If the intent is to dismiss all announcements at once, consider making this a collection route (e.g., `collection { post :dismiss }`) or using a more explicit action name like `mark_read`/`mark_all_read` to avoid ambiguity.

Using `default: ''` on a `text` column can be problematic across DBs (e.g., MySQL historically disallows defaults on TEXT/BLOB, and even where allowed it can cause schema/load issues). Since `null: false` already enforces presence at the DB level, consider removing the default and handling empty text validation at the model layer (or use a `string` with a length limit if you truly need a default).

Disabling the index on `account` here is a bit surprising. You add a composite unique index later, which is good for enforcing uniqueness, but queries like `AnnouncementMute.where(account_id: ...)` (e.g., fetching all mutes for a user) won’t be able to use a simple `account_id` index. If the app needs to list mutes by account, consider keeping the default index (`index: true`) or adding a separate non-unique index on `account_id` in addition to the composite one. If the intent is to avoid redundant indexes, please add a short comment explaining why `index: false` is safe for expected query patterns.

Schema version bump is expected, but please ensure the corresponding migration(s) are present and ordered correctly (especially since this PR adds new tables). Also note the formatting change (removal of the leading spaces before `ActiveRecord::Schema.define`)—if this is unintentional, it will create noisy diffs; consider keeping the original indentation/style consistent with the rest of the file.

Using `Faker::Lorem.paragraph(sentence_count: 2)` can make specs flaky/noisy (random length/content) and can also introduce unexpected characters. Prefer a deterministic default (e.g., a fixed string) or seed Faker in the spec suite so failures are reproducible. If you want variability, consider `sequence(:text)` with a predictable pattern.

The file appears to have unintended leading indentation before `Fabricator(...)`. This can trip style checks (Rubocop) and is inconsistent with other fabricators. Please remove the leading whitespace so the definition starts at column 0 (and ensure the file ends with a newline).

This spec file is currently empty (no examples), so it doesn't validate any behavior and may be flagged by CI/linting as a useless spec. Consider adding at least basic model specs (e.g., associations/validations/uniqueness constraints) or removing the file until there are meaningful tests. Also, the leading `1:` prefix shown in the diff looks like an artifact—ensure the actual file content is just `require 'rails_helper'` without the line-number prefix.

This spec file is effectively empty and also appears to include stray line-number prefixes/indentation in the source. At minimum, remove the prefixed numbering/leading spaces so the file is valid Ruby, and add basic model specs (e.g., validations, scopes for time range/scheduling, local-only visibility, and reaction associations if applicable). An empty spec won’t exercise the new feature and may indicate the file was generated incorrectly.

Requiring a raw `url` param is fine, but it should be validated/normalized before use. As written, any string (including non-HTTP schemes like `javascript:`, `data:`, or malformed URIs) will be accepted and later redirected to (line 16) with `allow_other_host: true`, which can create an open-redirect and potentially a script-injection vector depending on browser handling. Consider parsing with `Addressable::URI`/`URI`, enforcing an allowlist of schemes (http/https), and rejecting/raising on invalid URIs. Also consider length limits to avoid abuse (very long params) and ensuring the param is properly URL-decoded/encoded consistently.

Building the redirect URL via string interpolation is fine, but consider using `new URL('/redirect', window.location.origin)` + `searchParams.set('url', link.href)` to avoid edge cases with existing query strings, encoding, and to keep the code more robust/readable. Also, if `link.href` can be non-http(s) (e.g., `mailto:`, `tel:`, `javascript:`), routing it through `/redirect` may be unnecessary or could create unexpected behavior/security concerns—worth explicitly whitelisting schemes before rewriting the href.

Rewriting every non-hashtag/non-mention link to `/redirect?url=...` is risky: it will also affect ordinary external links in the profile header (website, etc.), changing expected behavior and potentially breaking non-HTTP(S) schemes (mailto:, xmpp:, magnet:, etc.). Consider narrowing this to only links that are known/suspected to be Mastodon/fediverse status/profile URLs (e.g., based on `rel="tag"`/known classes, hostname patterns, or a `data-*` marker from the renderer), and/or explicitly allowlist `http:`/`https:` before rewriting. Also, using `link.href` reads the fully-resolved absolute URL; if the original `href` was relative, you may want to preserve intent (or at least be sure the redirect endpoint expects absolute URLs). Finally, if the redirect route is same-origin, ensure it’s protected against open-redirect/SSRF and that the client-side rewrite doesn’t bypass any existing `target`/`rel` expectations.

Building the redirect URL via string interpolation is fine, but consider using a helper (e.g., a shared `redirectUrl(url)` util or `new URL('/redirect', window.location.origin)` + `searchParams.set('url', ...)`) to avoid duplicating this pattern across components and to ensure correct base path handling when Mastodon is served from a subdirectory. Also, if `card.get('url')` can ever be null/undefined, `encodeURIComponent` will stringify it to "undefined"; it may be worth guarding/falling back to the original URL or omitting the redirect in that case.

Ignoring this warning effectively silences an open-redirect finding (`redirect_to(params.require(:url), allow_other_host: true)`). Given this PR introduces a redirect route, it would be safer to address the root cause instead of suppressing it: validate/normalize the target URL (e.g., only allow http/https), consider restricting hosts to known/expected domains (local instance + resolved ActivityPub hosts), and/or sign the redirect target (Rails `signed_id`/`MessageVerifier`) so the controller only redirects to URLs generated by the app. If you must keep the ignore, add a non-empty `note` explaining why the redirect is safe and what mitigations exist, so future reviewers don’t miss a security regression.

Consider naming this route (e.g., `as: :redirect`) and/or scoping it under a clearer namespace to avoid a very generic `/redirect` path that could conflict with other features or be ambiguous. Also, since this endpoint will likely accept a target URL, ensure the controller enforces strict allowlisting/validation to prevent open-redirect abuse; adding constraints at the routing level (e.g., required params) can help document intent and reduce accidental misuse.

The comment above says the search route is excluded because it is not strict JSON, but `/indexes/doggo/search` is still included in `routes`. This makes the test intent inconsistent and may cause false failures if search accepts other content-types (or behaves differently). Either remove this route from the list or adjust the comment/test to reflect the expected behavior for `/search` (e.g., separate test for non-strict JSON routes).

This proxies to a VFS method named `umount`, but the existing MicroPython API is typically `uos.umount(path)` (or `vfs.umount()` depending on port) and unmounting is usually path-based. Here you hardcode 0 args, which means the underlying VFS implementation must expose a zero-arg `umount` method and know what to unmount (likely the current/only mount). That’s fragile if multiple mount points are supported or if the VFS expects a mount-point argument. Consider aligning with the common signature (accept a mount point/path argument and pass it through), and ensure `vfs_proxy_call` handles the case where no VFS is mounted (should raise a Python exception rather than crash/reboot). Also verify `MP_QSTR_umount` exists/gets generated for this port; otherwise this will fail to build.

Removing `STATIC` makes `fatfs_umount` globally visible, which increases the exported symbol surface and can cause name collisions across ports/objects (and may affect LTO/size). If the goal is to call this from another C module, consider keeping it `STATIC` and instead exposing it via a header with an explicit API (e.g., `mp_vfs_umount(...)`) or by adding a dedicated wrapper function with a namespaced name. At minimum, ensure there is a corresponding prototype in a header (or `extern` declaration) and that no other translation unit defines a conflicting `fatfs_umount`.

This new API is declared as returning `mp_obj_t`, but unmount is typically a side-effecting operation that either returns `None` or raises on error. Please ensure the return type/contract is consistent with how it will be exposed to Python (e.g., `uos.umount()` / `Vfs.umount()`), and consider documenting it in the header comment or aligning it with existing patterns (many helpers here are `void`/`int`-like internally). Also, since this is a public header used across ports, make sure the corresponding definition is added in a C file and that any ports including this header won’t get an unresolved symbol when `MICROPY_VFS_FAT` (or similar) is enabled/disabled—guard the declaration with the same feature macros as the implementation if needed.

This cast/field access looks unsafe and likely incorrect for a VfsFat instance. `vfs_in` is an `mp_obj_t` (typically a pointer to the VFS object), but you're casting it directly to `fs_user_mount_t *` and then indexing `readblocks[1]` to obtain a mount pointer. That relies on internal layout assumptions and can easily crash (and may be the root cause of the esp8266 reboot). Please use the proper VFS/FAT object type (e.g. the struct used by `VfsFat` in this file) and store the `fs_user_mount_t *` (or whatever `fatfs_umount` expects) explicitly in the VFS object, then pass that field here. Also add type checking (e.g. `MP_OBJ_IS_TYPE(vfs_in, &mp_fat_vfs_type)`) and guard against double-unmount / NULL mount pointers to avoid calling `fatfs_umount` with an invalid pointer.

Switching from `dockerversion.IAmStatic == "true"` to `linkmode.IsStatic` is a good cleanup, but please double-check that `linkmode.IsStatic` is correctly set for all build modes (including cross builds) and matches the previous semantics. The old check relied on a string value (likely injected via ldflags); if `IsStatic` is derived differently (e.g., build tags), it could change behavior and the error message selection. Consider adding/ensuring a unit test or a small comment indicating how `IsStatic` is determined to avoid regressions in static vs dynamic detection.

The file appears to have leading indentation before the build tags (and line numbers in the diff suggest whitespace before `//go:build`). Build constraints must start at the beginning of the file with no preceding whitespace, otherwise they may be ignored by `go` tooling. Please ensure `//go:build !static_build` and the legacy `// +build` line are the very first bytes in the file (no spaces/tabs, no BOM), followed by a blank line before the `package` clause.

The file appears to have leading indentation before the build tags (likely introduced by the patch formatting). `//go:build` and `// +build` lines must start at column 1 to be recognized by the Go toolchain; otherwise this file may be included unconditionally or the tags ignored, breaking the intended linkmode selection. Please ensure there are no leading spaces/tabs before these comment lines.

Renaming the helper is fine, but please ensure the new name matches the actual behavior and is updated consistently across the package. The previous name (`ensureHomeIfIAmStatic`) implied a check on the current binary; `ensureHomeIfStatic` is a bit ambiguous (static what?). If the function is meant to detect a statically-linked daemon binary and set HOME to avoid `os/user.Current()` segfaults, consider a clearer name like `ensureHomeIfStaticBinary` and verify all call sites/tests were updated. Also confirm the function signature/implementation was renamed accordingly to avoid build breakage.

Switching from the string comparison `dockerversion.IAmStatic == "true"` to the boolean `linkmode.IsStatic` is cleaner, but please double-check that `linkmode.IsStatic` is guaranteed to reflect the same condition in all build modes (especially for non-sandboxed/dev builds and cross builds). If `IsStatic` can be false/unknown in some static configurations, this guard would stop setting HOME and could reintroduce the segfault described in #29344. Consider adding a brief comment (or a small unit/integration check) documenting how `IsStatic` is set and why it’s safe here.

Renaming the function changes the package API surface. Please ensure all references (including other build-tagged files in this package, e.g., linux/windows variants) were updated to call `ensureHomeIfStatic()`; otherwise this will fail to compile on non-target platforms due to an undefined symbol. Also consider whether the old name was intentionally descriptive ("IfIAmStatic")—if the intent is "when running as a static binary", a clearer name like `ensureHomeIfStaticBinary` may improve readability.

Removing `IAmStatic` changes the public API surface of the `dockerversion` package. Please verify there are no remaining references in the repo (including build scripts that may inject/override this var via `-ldflags -X`) and that downstream consumers won’t break. If this is intentional as part of removing static/cross-build logic, consider adding a brief comment in the PR/commit message or providing a compatibility alias (deprecated) for one release cycle if external users are likely.

Removing the default `IAMSTATIC='true'` changes the build mode for all invocations that don’t explicitly set it, which can affect binary portability (e.g., static vs dynamic linking) and may break expectations in downstream scripts/packaging. If the intent is to deprecate/replace this behavior as part of the cross-build refactor, consider either (a) keeping a backward-compatible default (e.g., `: "${IAMSTATIC:=true}"`) with a clear comment, or (b) ensuring all call sites (Makefile, Dockerfile stages, CI) explicitly set `IAMSTATIC` where required and adding a note in docs/changelog. Also verify that Windows/cross builds don’t rely on this default implicitly.

This change drops the ldflag that sets `dockerversion.IAmStatic` (defaulting to true). If the `IAmStatic` variable still exists in `github.com/docker/docker/dockerversion`, removing this will leave it at the Go zero value (false) and may change runtime/version output semantics (or any logic that checks it). Please confirm the variable/feature was removed or is no longer used; otherwise keep setting it (or set it conditionally based on the new build mode) to avoid behavior regressions in `docker version`/diagnostics.

This script no longer exports `IAMSTATIC='false'`. Please double-check that downstream scripts (e.g., `hack/make/.go-autogen`, `hack/make/binary`, or any packaging logic) don’t still branch on `IAMSTATIC` being explicitly set. If any code uses `[ -n "$IAMSTATIC" ]` or expects the variable to exist (even when false), removing the export could change behavior. If the intent is to deprecate `IAMSTATIC` entirely, consider also removing/adjusting any remaining references, or add a brief comment here clarifying that dynamic builds are now inferred from `LDFLAGS_STATIC_DOCKER`/build tags instead of `IAMSTATIC`.

Removing `IAMSTATIC='false'` changes the contract for downstream scripts that may rely on this flag to distinguish static vs dynamic builds. If `IAMSTATIC` is still referenced anywhere (e.g., in `hack/make/.go-autogen`, `hack/make/binary`, or packaging logic), this could silently flip behavior depending on the default/empty value. Please verify all call sites no longer use `IAMSTATIC`, or explicitly set a safe default (e.g., `: "${IAMSTATIC:=false}"` or keep exporting `IAMSTATIC=false`) to avoid regressions in dynamic proxy builds.

The helper is named `isNotFound`, but it currently treats `errcode.ErrorCodeDenied` as "not found". That can mask real auth/permission problems and trigger the tag fallback in cases where the user is simply unauthorized, potentially changing behavior and making debugging harder. Consider limiting this to true 404-style conditions (e.g., `v2.ErrorCodeManifestUnknown`, `v2.ErrorCodeNameUnknown`, and possibly `errcode.ErrorCodeUnknown`/`errcode.ErrorCodeManifestUnknown` depending on which package defines them), or rename the function to reflect broader semantics (e.g., `isNotFoundOrDenied`) and ensure callers intentionally include denied. Also consider unwrapping (`errors.As`) so wrapped errors are detected reliably.

The fallback fetch uses `ms.Get(ctx, "", distribution.WithTag(tagged.Tag()))`. Passing an empty digest string is a bit opaque and may rely on implementation quirks; consider using the explicit tag-based API if available (e.g., `ms.Get(ctx, digest.Digest(""), ...)` or a dedicated `GetByTag`/`Get` overload) and add a short comment explaining why the digest argument is intentionally empty. Also, ensure the returned error is only wrapped when non-nil; currently `errors.Wrap(nil, ...)` returns nil, but being explicit (wrap only if err != nil) improves readability.

Consider using a shared helper/constant for this BuildKit-required error message (also used in dispatchCopy and dispatchRun) to avoid duplication and keep the docs URL consistent. Also, if possible, include the Dockerfile line/command context in the error (some other parse/dispatch errors include location), which would make it easier for users to find the offending instruction in larger Dockerfiles.

Same as in dispatchAdd: this error string is duplicated across multiple dispatchers. Prefer factoring into a helper (e.g., buildkitRequiredError("chmod")) so future changes (URL, wording) don't require touching multiple call sites. Also consider whether COPY/ADD should mention the full flag name ("--chmod") consistently with RUN's formatting ("--%s").

Failing on the first flag is fine, but consider reporting all unsupported flags found (or at least de-duplicating) to reduce iterative fix cycles for users (e.g., RUN --mount ... --network=...). Also, ensure FlagsUsed is normalized (no leading "--") so the message doesn't end up as "----mount"; if normalization isn't guaranteed by the parser, sanitize here before formatting.

Using fmt.Sprintf to build the expected error string makes the test brittle and duplicates the exact wording/URL in multiple places. Consider asserting on a stable substring (e.g., `assert.ErrorContains(t, err, "requires BuildKit")` and `assert.ErrorContains(t, err, "--"+f)`), or centralize the expected message/URL in a const/helper to avoid future doc URL/message tweaks breaking tests. Also note that including "any-flag" here implies even unknown flags at the instruction level should produce the BuildKit-required error; if the intended behavior is to keep "unknown flag" errors for truly unknown flags, this test may be over-constraining.

For the create event, setting `Attributes["name"]` to the imageID is a bit misleading/inconsistent with other image events, where `name` typically contains a human-friendly reference (repo:tag) and the ID is already in `Actor.ID`. For untagged images, consider omitting `name` entirely, or using a more explicit key (e.g., `"image"`/`"id"`) if consumers rely on it. If you keep `name`, it may confuse event subscribers that interpret `name` as a tag/reference.

Passing the whole daemon (`d`) as `ImageExportedCallback` increases coupling and makes it less obvious which method(s) are required. Consider passing a narrower interface (e.g., `type ImageExportedCallback interface { OnImageExported(ctx context.Context, id digest.Digest, ... ) }`) or an explicit function to avoid accidental dependency on other daemon methods and to simplify testing/mocking. Also ensure the callback is safe to call from BuildKit goroutines (non-blocking, no long locks) and that it won’t emit duplicate events for multi-platform exports or repeated exports within a single build.

Consider using the existing event/action constants (if available) instead of the raw string "create" to avoid typos and keep event semantics consistent across the daemon. Also, this callback currently ignores `ctx` and `desc`; if they’re intentionally unused, add a brief comment or use `_ = ctx` / `_ = desc` to make that explicit, and consider whether `desc` should be used to validate/normalize the image ID (e.g., ensure it’s a digest) before logging the event. Finally, check whether `LogImageEvent` can return an error; if so, propagate it instead of always returning nil so failures to emit events are observable.

Emitting the create event here is good for untagged builds, but consider whether this is the right point in the lifecycle: at this line the image may not yet be unpacked (and could still fail at `unpackImage`). If `unpackImage` fails, subscribers will have seen an `image create` event for an image that ultimately isn't usable/fully created. If the intent is “image record created in metadata”, add a clarifying comment; otherwise consider moving the event emission after `unpackImage` succeeds (or emitting a corresponding failure/delete event on error) to avoid misleading event consumers. Also double-check what `img.Name` is for untagged builds (may be empty) and whether `logImageEvent` handles empty names gracefully.

Emitting the create event here is good for untagged builds, but consider whether this can produce duplicate `image create` events for other code-paths that also call `CommitImage` (e.g., `docker commit`, import/load, or other internal commits). If those already emit create/tag events elsewhere, we may end up with double notifications. Also, `LogImageEvent` takes both an ID and a name; passing `id.String()` for both may be misleading for consumers expecting `name` to be a repo tag (or empty). If the intent is “created image with no tag”, consider passing an empty name (or a more explicit value) and/or adding a comment explaining why both fields are the digest. Finally, ensure the event is only emitted after the image is fully persisted/marked built locally if consumers rely on the image being immediately inspectable when they receive the event.

Nit: grammar/readability in the comment. It should be “a DOCKER_BUILDKIT environment variable” (not “an”), and consider clarifying that this affects the *CLI* build behavior (enables/disables BuildKit) rather than “make the build use buildkit (or not)”. Also, style-wise, Docker uses “BuildKit” capitalization.

This timeout looks very tight for `docker events` to return reliably on slower/loaded CI (especially with BuildKit enabled). Since the build happens before starting `docker events`, you’re relying on the daemon to have persisted the event and for the CLI to fetch it within 300ms. Consider increasing the timeout (e.g., a few seconds) and/or adding a small retry/poll loop around the `events` call to reduce flakiness. Also consider asserting `cmd` succeeded (or at least didn’t time out) before checking stdout, so failures are easier to diagnose.

This assertion hard-codes the total number of events (now 4), which can make the test flaky if additional image-related events are emitted by the daemon/build backend (e.g., changes in BuildKit/classic builder behavior, extra tag/untag events, or future event additions). Consider asserting on the presence/count of specific expected events (e.g., one `image create` and the two `image tag` events) rather than the exact total length, or filter the collected events by `type=image`/`action=` and validate those counts. That would keep the test focused on the regression being fixed (emitting `image create` for builds) while being more robust.

The test subscribes to *all* daemon events with no filters and ignores the error return from `Events()`. This can make the test flaky (unrelated image create events from other tests/daemon activity) and can also silently pass/fail if the events stream fails to start. Consider adding filters (e.g., `type=image` and ideally `event=create`, and/or narrowing by `scope=local` if applicable) and assert the error is nil. Also consider starting the events subscription before the build (as you do) but draining any initial backlog if the API can return buffered events.

Using a fixed `time.After(3.5s)` timeout can be flaky on slow CI or under load, and it allocates a new timer each loop iteration. Prefer a single `context.WithTimeout` for the whole wait, or create one `timer := time.NewTimer(...)` outside the loop (and `defer timer.Stop()`), and/or use `select` on `ctx.Done()` to avoid hanging if the parent context is canceled. This will make the test more deterministic and resource-friendly.

The port-length validation looks off by one and may accept invalid ports or reject valid ones. `port_end - host_end` includes the ':' character, so the max length check should be based on the number of digits (`port_end - (host_end + 1)`) and ideally also validate the numeric range (1..65535). Also, on this error path you `return 0` (success) and leak `*aclk_hostname` allocated just above; consider returning non-zero and freeing any partially allocated outputs.

Style/const-correctness: this function likely doesn't modify the enum value, so consider taking it by value (`ACLK_PROXY_TYPE type`) or as a `const ACLK_PROXY_TYPE *type` to avoid implying mutation and to allow passing pointers to const.
