Also, if this is a header-only declaration used across translation units, ensure the signature matches the definition exactly (including whitespace is fine, but pointer/value/const must match) to avoid compiler warnings or ODR/link issues.

For HTTP proxies, `lws_set_proxy()` typically expects a host:port (and optionally credentials) rather than a full URL with scheme. In this function you pass `proxy` directly, but for SOCKS you strip the `socks5://` prefix via `aclk_wss_set_socks()`. If `proxy` here is configured as `http://HOST:PORT` (as described in the PR), this may fail or behave inconsistently depending on libwebsockets version. Consider normalizing the HTTP proxy string similarly (strip `http://` / `https://` and validate non-empty host:port) before calling `lws_set_proxy()`, and add a clear error message when the format is invalid.

Opening "/dev/null" with O_RDWR and mode 0 works, but the third argument is ignored unless O_CREAT is set. Consider using `open("/dev/null", O_RDWR | O_CLOEXEC)` (or set FD_CLOEXEC via fcntl) to avoid leaking the fd across any future exec paths. Also, if the intent of this PR is to move stderr back to error.log (per PR description), this change contradicts that—either restore opening the error.log file here or update the PR description accordingly.

Potential functional bug: when `frameLengthInt != -1` (i.e., we previously computed the frame length but didn't have enough bytes yet), `frameLength` remains `0` and is then passed into `failOnFrameLengthLessThanInitialBytesToStrip(...)`. This changes behavior vs the old code (which used the actual computed `frameLength`) and can produce incorrect error messages/logic. Consider persisting the computed `frameLength` (long) as an instance field as well, or recompute it when needed, or at least set `frameLength = frameLengthInt` before this check so the failure path uses the correct value.

Catching a generic Exception here is likely masking real layout/logic bugs (e.g., NPE from getThumb(), width==0 before layout, or invalid LayoutParams). Consider guarding explicitly instead: check that seekbarPreviewContainer/playbackWindowRoot have been laid out (width > 0), that playbackSeekBar.getThumb() != null, and that the current LayoutParams are MarginLayoutParams before casting/creating new params. If you still need a fallback, catch specific exceptions and avoid logging on every progress tick to prevent log spam/perf issues while dragging.

Unconditionally recycling `srcBitmap` here is risky: the bitmap may be (a) still referenced elsewhere (e.g., cached/held by the holder), (b) the same instance currently set on the `ImageView` if scaling fails or if future changes skip scaling, or (c) already recycled. Recycling a bitmap that is still in use can lead to `Canvas: trying to use a recycled bitmap` crashes. Prefer letting GC handle it, or only recycle bitmaps you *own* and are sure are not used anymore (e.g., recycle the *previous* ImageView bitmap after replacing it, or recycle only intermediate bitmaps you created). If you keep this, at least guard with `if (!srcBitmap.isRecycled())` and ensure the ownership contract is clear (document that the passed bitmap will be consumed).

This creates a new Bitmap on every supplier invocation, which can be very frequent while scrubbing and may cause heavy allocations/GC churn and even OOM. Consider caching the cropped frame (e.g., LruCache keyed by position) or pre-splitting per page into smaller bitmaps once, and make sure to recycle/allow GC of the large `srcBitMap` when no longer needed. Also validate `bounds` before use: `frameset.getFrameBoundsAt(currentPosMs)` might return coordinates that exceed `srcBitMap` dimensions (or be null/short), which would throw `IllegalArgumentException` in `Bitmap.createBitmap`; guard with bounds checks and return null on invalid bounds.

The exception message concatenation is missing a separator, producing messages like `Failed to download imageIO_ERROR`. Consider adding a colon/space and including more context (e.g., the imageUri) to make debugging easier. Also, throwing a generic RuntimeException here makes it harder for callers to handle; a more specific exception (e.g., IOException/IllegalStateException) or a custom exception type would be clearer.

This constant currently just clones `BASE_DISPLAY_IMAGE_OPTIONS` without any seekbar-preview-specific behavior. If the preview thumbnails are fetched frequently while scrubbing, consider explicitly setting options here to avoid unnecessary memory churn (e.g., `cacheInMemory(false)` if these are transient, or a dedicated `bitmapConfig(Bitmap.Config.RGB_565)` to reduce memory). Also consider setting placeholders/fail images appropriate for the seekbar preview (or explicitly disabling them) so UI behavior is intentional rather than inherited implicitly from the base options.

Calling `hideControls(0, 0)` with magic-number arguments makes the intent unclear and may be brittle if the method signature/semantics change. Consider using named constants (e.g., `HIDE_CONTROLS_DELAY_MS`, `HIDE_CONTROLS_ANIMATION_MS`) or a dedicated helper like `hideControlsImmediately()`/`hideControlsAfterDefaultDelay()` to document why this should be immediate when resuming via SPACE. Also double-check whether this should run only in fullscreen: the reported issue is about external resume (media keys/Bluetooth), which can happen outside fullscreen too, so limiting it to `isFullscreen` might leave the bug partially unfixed.

Calling `hideControls(0, 0)` unconditionally on every media-session `play()` may hide UI even when playback doesn't actually start (e.g., player not prepared, error state, already playing, or play request ignored). Consider guarding this with a state check (e.g., only if transitioning from paused to playing / `!player.isPlaying()` before `play()` and `isPlaying()` after), or move the control-hiding logic to a single place that reacts to the actual playback state change (e.g., onPlayerStateChanged/playing event) to avoid UI desync and duplicated logic across input sources.

Removing `f32` from the list changes the documented API surface. If `f32` is no longer supported, consider explicitly stating that it’s unsupported/removed (and why) to avoid confusing users who may have seen llama.cpp docs or older discussions. If `f32` is still accepted by the underlying runner, this doc change would be inaccurate—please confirm support and either keep it listed or add a note that `f16` is the highest supported precision in Ollama.

The KV size calculation now uses float math and drops the previous `2 *` factor. This risks both precision loss (for large contexts) and a potential regression if the old `2` was accounting for K+V buffers (or another duplication) that is no longer represented. Prefer keeping the computation in integer arithmetic (e.g., compute total elements as `context*blockCount*(embeddingHeadsK+embeddingHeadsV)*headsKV` and multiply by an integer bytes-per-element), and ensure the factor for K/V is correctly represented (either via `(embeddingHeadsK+embeddingHeadsV)` or an explicit `*2`, but not accidentally removed). Also consider validating/normalizing `kvCacheType` (or defaulting to f16) before calling `kvCacheBytesPerElement` to avoid silently returning 0/incorrect sizes.

This refactor drops the previous guard that forced non-flash-attn backends to use f16/f32 only. `estimateKvCacheSize` now always applies the requested `cacheType` multiplier, which can under-estimate KV memory on backends that don't actually support quantized KV (or where it is disabled), potentially causing the scheduler to overcommit VRAM/RAM. Consider reintroducing a capability check here (or ensure callers pass an already-validated/effective cache type) so the estimate matches what will actually be allocated.

Direct float equality on computed values can make this test brittle if kvCacheBytesPerElement ever changes to derive values via arithmetic (or returns float32/float64 conversions). Consider using an epsilon comparison (e.g., math.Abs(got-tt.want) < 1e-9) or change the function/test to use an integer/ratio type (bytes as int, or numerator/denominator) since these are discrete sizes (2, 1, 0.5). This will avoid flaky failures across platforms/implementations.

This uses `discover.GetGPUInfo()` instead of the `gpus []discover.GpuInfo` argument passed into `EstimateGPULayers`. That can lead to incorrect behavior in multi-GPU scenarios (or when the scheduler is evaluating a specific GPU set) because it queries global/current GPU info rather than the candidate GPU(s) being estimated. Consider deriving flash-attention support from the `gpus` slice (e.g., any/all selected GPUs support it) or passing the relevant GPU info into this check, to keep estimation consistent with the rest of the function.

Setting `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE` to an empty string relies on downstream code treating "" as "use default". If the implementation uses `os.LookupEnv` (presence-based) rather than `os.Getenv` (value-based), this will still be considered "set" and may not exercise the true default path. Consider `t.Setenv("OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE", "f16")` to be explicit, or `os.Unsetenv("OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE")` (with `t.Cleanup`) if you specifically want to test the unset/default behavior.

KV cache quantization is currently gated behind `if fa { ... }`, which means users cannot set `--kv-cache-type` unless flash attention is enabled. In llama.cpp these are independent features (KV cache quantization can be used without flash-attn), and the PR description suggests KV quantization should be generally available. Consider moving the kv-cache-type handling outside the `fa` block (or at least allowing it when FA is disabled), and only gate it on model/backend support. Also consider skipping the warning when `kvct` is empty/default to avoid noisy logs.

Adding the env var to the CLI help/list is good, but please ensure it’s also wired end-to-end: (1) documented in the same place as other env vars (FAQ/config docs), (2) validated/normalized to the supported set (e.g., f16/q8_0/q4_0) with a clear error message on invalid values, and (3) consistent naming with any existing config/flag (if there’s also a CLI flag, consider surfacing it there too). Otherwise users may see it advertised but it won’t actually affect runtime behavior or may fail silently.

The comment is misleading/contradictory with the implementation. It says "check if it does NOT support flash attention", but the function returns true only if *all* GPUs support flash attention (returns false on the first unsupported GPU). Please reword to match behavior (e.g., "Returns true if all GPUs support flash attention") or adjust logic/name if the intent is different.

Good improvement to add a language hint for syntax highlighting. However, the diff shows the old line had an extra leading `:` before the fenced block (likely a formatting artifact from the markdown renderer or a previous indentation style). Please double-check the rendered FAQ to ensure the code fence is correctly aligned/indented and that there isn’t still a stray `:` on the previous line that could break the fence. If the `:` was intentional for a specific markdown processor, consider keeping consistency across the doc or removing similar artifacts elsewhere.

This states KV cache quantization is available only when Flash Attention is enabled. Is that strictly true in the implementation (i.e., quantized KV is ignored/unsupported without FA), or is it just recommended? If it’s a hard requirement, consider making it explicit (e.g., “only supported when…”) and add a brief note about what happens otherwise (fallback to f16? error? warning). If it’s not a hard requirement, reword to avoid misleading users.

Consider documenting accepted values/casing and behavior on invalid values for `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE` (e.g., does it error, warn and fall back to `f16`, etc.). Also, earlier text says “Key cache type” in the issue, but here it’s “K/V cache” (both). If the implementation quantizes both K and V, this is fine; if it only affects K (as llama.cpp allows separate types), the docs should match the actual behavior and ideally mention whether K and V are quantized together or independently.

The GQA statement looks potentially inverted/unclear: models with *more* GQA (more grouped heads, fewer KV heads) generally have a smaller KV cache footprint already, so the memory benefit from KV quantization may be smaller; the quality impact claim also needs a citation or clearer rationale. Consider rephrasing to something more defensible/less specific (e.g., “impact varies by model architecture and context length”) or add a reference/benchmark note.

Consider validating/normalizing this env var at the config boundary rather than exposing it as a raw string. As-is, any value (including typos/case differences like "Q8_0" vs "q8_0") will flow downstream and likely fail later in llama.cpp init with a less actionable error. Suggest: (1) document the supported set (e.g., f16/q8_0/q4_0) in the help text, (2) normalize to lowercase/trim, and (3) optionally provide a typed accessor (enum) or a ParseKvCacheType() that returns a default (f16) and a clear error for unsupported values.

Using `slog.Warn` here introduces a new logging dependency and will emit warnings during normal operation if callers pass values that are valid in llama.cpp but not mapped here (or if future ggml types are added). Consider (a) validating `kvCacheType` earlier and returning an error instead of silently defaulting, or (b) wiring this through the project’s existing logging/config mechanism so it’s consistent and testable. Also, since you already normalize with `strings.ToLower`, the extra checks for `"f16"`/`"fp16"` in the default branch are redundant; you can simplify by warning only when `s != ""` and not in the supported set.

Using an empty string as the default while the help text says "default: f16" is a bit ambiguous and pushes defaulting/validation into lower layers. Consider defaulting the flag to "f16" (or whatever llama.cpp expects, e.g. "f16"/"F16") and validating allowed values here (e.g. f16, q8_0, q4_0) so users get an immediate, clear error instead of a runtime failure inside llama.NewContextParams/NewContextWithModel. Also consider documenting whether this applies to K only vs K/V and whether it’s GPU/CPU backend dependent.

Using `0.5` bytes/element for `q4_0` is likely too optimistic and can under-estimate KV cache memory. GGML quantized types are stored in blocks with per-block scale/metadata (and alignment), so the effective bytes/element is > 0.5 in practice. This impacts the scheduler’s VRAM estimates and could lead to overcommit/OOM. Consider computing size using ggml’s actual type size (e.g., `ggml_type_size`/`ggml_row_size` equivalents via cgo) or at least incorporate block overhead (q4_0 is typically ~0.56–0.62 bytes/elem depending on layout) and document that this is an approximation.

This test sets up `tt.gpus` but never uses it; `kVCacheQuantization` appears to depend on GPU capabilities (e.g., flash-attn support) yet the test doesn't inject/override the GPU list, so the result may depend on global state and can be flaky or not actually testing the intended branches. Consider refactoring `kVCacheQuantization` to accept `discover.GpuInfoList` (or a small interface) as an explicit parameter, or set the global GPU state used by the implementation within the test. Otherwise, remove `gpus` from the table to avoid misleading coverage.

Potential divide-by-zero/panic: `ggml.KV().BlockCount()` may be 0 for some models/metadata failures (similar to the earlier `blk.0` missing warning). Since this value is used as a divisor, please guard it (e.g., if 0 then warn and skip/assume 1) to avoid crashing during estimation. Also consider whether KV cache should be scaled by block count here when `kv` is already computed for the full context/model; if `estimateKvCacheSize` returns total KV bytes, dividing by block count may undercount unless it intentionally returns per-layer bytes.

Setting `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE` to an empty string is a bit ambiguous: depending on how the production code reads env vars, an explicitly-empty value may be treated differently than “unset” (e.g., `LookupEnv` returns ok=true). If the intent is to ensure the default behavior, consider either not setting it at all, or explicitly setting it to the default (e.g. `f16`) to make the test’s assumptions clearer and avoid behavior changes if env parsing logic changes.

Bug: `checkFlashAttentionSupport` creates a new empty `discover.GpuInfoList{}` and then calls `gpus.FlashAttentionSupported()`, so this will never reflect the actual GPUs passed into `NewLlamaServer` (and may always return false depending on implementation). This likely disables flash attention even when supported. Pass the real `gpus` into `checkFlashAttentionSupport(gpus, ggml)` (or make it a method) and use that list for the support check.

This `owners` collection is computed once during `Tick()` and then captured by `ownerDropdown.OnClick`. If the player list or valid owners can change after this point (e.g., map settings/players updated, actor selection changes, or `Tick()` runs before `OnClick`), the dropdown may show stale/incorrect options. Consider moving the owner filtering back inside `OnClick` (or recomputing via a `Func<IEnumerable<PlayerReference>>`) so it always reflects current state. Also, `Dictionary<...>.ValueCollection` is an awkward type here; prefer `IEnumerable<PlayerReference>`/`IReadOnlyCollection<PlayerReference>` to avoid leaking implementation details and to make recomputation/laziness easier.

Enabling nullable here for just this attribute type is fine, but consider scoping it more explicitly to avoid accidental bleed if code is inserted between the directives later. Either wrap the region with comments (e.g. `// NRT: DescAttribute`) or move `#nullable enable` to the top of the file (if the file is being migrated) and remove the paired disable. Also, with NRT enabled, you may want to validate whether `lines` can be null (callers could pass null explicitly) and annotate/guard accordingly (e.g. `string[]?` or throw on null) to avoid warnings/incorrect assumptions.

This removes the generic Range<T> struct from this file. Please double-check that there are no remaining references to Graphics.Range<T> (or unqualified Range<T> usages that previously resolved to this type) elsewhere in the project. If this was unused, consider adding a note in the PR/commit message or ensuring any call sites were migrated to an alternative (e.g., System.Range for index ranges, or a project-specific Range type in a more appropriate namespace) to avoid breaking changes or subtle type-resolution differences.

Enabling nullable for only a single-line interface and immediately disabling it again adds noise and can be misleading (it suggests the rest of the file is not nullable-aware even though this interface is). Consider either (a) leaving nullable enabled for the remainder of the file if feasible, or (b) moving `IGlobalModData` into its own file (or a dedicated `#nullable enable` region) so the directive scope is clearer. If the intent is to annotate this interface for downstream consumers, also consider whether it needs any nullable annotations at all (it currently has no members), in which case the directive may be unnecessary here.

This null-forgiving cast assumes `item1OrArray` is non-null and actually an `object?[]` whenever `item2OrSentinel == Sentinel`. That invariant is established in the ctor, but it’s implicit and easy to break with future edits (e.g., new ctor paths, default(struct) usage, or refactoring). Consider making the invariant explicit by (a) using a dedicated `object?[]? array` field instead of overloading `item1OrArray`, or (b) adding a debug assertion/guard before the cast (e.g., `Debug.Assert(item1OrArray is object?[])`) to fail fast if the struct is misused. Also note that `default(Values)` would leave `item2OrSentinel` null (not Sentinel) and route to the switch path, so ensure callers never rely on default initialization for sizes > 0.

With nullable reference types enabled, `Type.GetConstructor(...)` can return null if the type has no public parameterless ctor (or if `type` is an open generic, etc.). Calling `.Invoke(...)` will then throw a `NullReferenceException`. Consider explicitly handling this by using the null-forgiving operator with a clear justification, or (preferably) throwing a more descriptive exception, e.g. `var ctor = type.GetConstructor(Type.EmptyTypes) ?? throw new InvalidOperationException($"No public parameterless constructor for {type}"); return ctor.Invoke(Array.Empty<object>());`. Also consider whether you want `BindingFlags` to include non-public ctors if that’s expected in this code path.

Using an empty lambda as a sentinel avoids nullability warnings, but it also allocates a new delegate each call and may subtly change semantics if `DelayedAction` ever invokes the action (even accidentally) during comparisons/debugging. Consider refactoring `Index`/search to not require constructing a dummy `DelayedAction` at all (e.g., add an overload that binary-searches by `desiredTime`), or at least use a cached static no-op action (e.g., `static readonly Action NoOp = () => { };`) to avoid repeated allocations.

With `#nullable enable`, the `Stream stream` parameter is non-nullable, but callers from non-nullable-aware code can still pass null at runtime. Removing the explicit null guard changes behavior from a clear `ArgumentNullException` to a later `NullReferenceException` (e.g., on `stream.CanRead`). Consider restoring the null check (or using `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(stream);`) to preserve API contract and provide a better exception message.

With `#nullable enable` in this file, removing the explicit null-check changes the runtime behavior: passing `null` will now throw a `NullReferenceException` at `stream.CanSeek` instead of an `ArgumentNullException` with a clear parameter name. NRT only provides compile-time warnings and doesn’t protect against nulls at runtime (and callers outside nullable-aware code can still pass null). Consider restoring the guard (or using the PR’s `Unforgiving` helper) to keep the exception type/message consistent and more diagnosable.

This change drops the `OpenRA.` qualifier. Please ensure `Exts` is unambiguous in this file (no other `Exts` types in scope) and that the correct namespace is imported. If the intent is just to simplify the call, consider using an explicit `using OpenRA;`/`using OpenRA.Exts;` (or a `using static` if appropriate) to avoid accidental binding to a different `Exts` in the future. Also, since this is security-related token generation, double-check that `Random` here is the intended RNG (non-cryptographic) and not being confused with `System.Random` vs a project-specific RNG after namespace changes.

This assumes the field name `f` always exists on the defaults type. If it doesn't (e.g., YAML contains an unknown/renamed field, or reflection visibility/binding flags differ), `GetFieldUnforgiving(f)` will throw and abort loading instead of falling back to a default. Consider keeping the previous tolerant behavior (null-check and a safe fallback), or ensure `GetFieldUnforgiving` uses the same binding flags as the original `GetField` call and provides a clearer error that includes the section type and field name. At minimum, add context to the exception or handle missing fields gracefully so a single bad field doesn't crash settings load.

Good use of nullable annotations for the Try* pattern. However, consider whether the file-level `#nullable enable`/`#nullable disable` is the best scope here: enabling only around this interface can lead to inconsistent nullability expectations for `ISoundFormat` and any other types in this file. If the intent is to keep the rest of the file non-nullable-aware for now, this is fine, but please ensure all implementations of `ISoundLoader.TryParseSound` are updated/compiled with matching attributes (or also under `#nullable enable`) so the contract is consistently enforced and you don’t get mismatched nullability warnings across projects/files.

Changing `text` to nullable alters the API contract: callers can now pass null, but the method body must explicitly define what that means. Please ensure the implementation handles `null` deterministically (e.g., treat as empty string, write a 0-length prefix, or throw `ArgumentNullException`). If the intended behavior is “null not allowed”, keep `string text` and add a null-forgiving/guard at call sites instead; otherwise, add an explicit null-handling branch and document it to avoid silent behavior changes or NREs when encoding/byte conversion occurs.

Changing `Add` from `(string, object)` to `(string, string)` is a breaking API change and also changes behavior: callers previously could pass non-strings (e.g. ints, enums) and rely on `ToString()` plus escaping. If this is intentional, consider either (a) keeping an overload `Add(string name, object value)` that forwards to the string version (with a null check/annotation), or (b) using `Add(string name, object? value)` and explicitly handling `null` (e.g. throw `ArgumentNullException` or treat as empty) to preserve prior semantics while still being NRT-friendly. Also note that `Uri.EscapeDataString(value)` will throw if `value` is null, so the new signature implicitly enforces non-null; ensure all call sites comply.

Consider defensively copying the `members` array (e.g., `Members = (string[])members.Clone();`) and validating it (null/empty entries). As written, callers can mutate the passed-in array after construction, changing the attribute state unexpectedly. Also, if this is intended as a polyfill for older runtimes, ensure the API shape matches the BCL (including nullability annotations) to avoid subtle analyzer/runtime differences.

Switching from `Array.Empty<Type>()` to `Type.EmptyTypes` is fine, but note that `Type.EmptyTypes` is a `Type[]` singleton intended specifically for reflection APIs. If `RequiredTraits` is ever mutated (even accidentally) elsewhere, this would now mutate a shared global array. Consider keeping `Array.Empty<Type>()` (also a singleton but less semantically tied to reflection) or, better, ensure `RequiredTraits` is exposed/used immutably (e.g., `IReadOnlyList<Type>`/`IEnumerable<Type>` or a defensive copy) so neither singleton can be modified.

Changing `byPlayer` to nullable is a breaking contract change for all implementers/callers. Please double-check the call sites to ensure `null` is actually a valid/meaningful value here (e.g., "no player" / world-owned / spectator), and that implementations handle it without NREs. If `null` is only used as a sentinel, consider documenting it in XML docs (what does `null` mean?) or providing an overload/default behavior to avoid spreading null-handling across many traits.

This overload of ArgumentExOnNull is throwing an ArgumentException without a paramName, unlike the other reflection helpers in this file. That makes the exception less actionable and inconsistent with the rest of the API. Consider using the (paramName, message) overload here too (e.g. pass nameof(types) or nameof(name)/nameof(types) depending on intent), or add a dedicated helper for “not found” cases that throws a more appropriate exception type (e.g. MissingMethodException) while still including parameter context.

Be careful with this attribute: `MemberNotNull(nameof(currentBlock))` promises that `currentBlock` is non-null after `Rewind()` returns, but the method assigns `currentBlock = ((IEnumerable<VocBlock>)blocks).GetEnumerator();`. If `blocks` can ever be null (e.g., not initialized yet, or cleared on dispose), this will throw before returning and the attribute is misleading. Consider either (a) ensuring `blocks` is always non-null at this point (e.g., initialize to `Array.Empty<VocBlock>()` and/or add a guard/throw with a clear message), or (b) annotating/structuring so the non-null guarantee is actually enforced (e.g., make `blocks` non-nullable and set in constructor/Preload before any call to `Rewind`). Also note that `IEnumerator<T>` is a reference type; if `currentBlock` is declared nullable, you may be able to avoid the attribute by making it non-nullable and initializing it in the constructor or field initializer.

With `#nullable enable`, `out Func<Stream> result` is non-nullable but you assign `result = null;` at the start. The `[MaybeNullWhen(false)]` annotation helps callers, but it doesn't address the internal null assignment warning/contract mismatch. Consider changing the signature to `out Func<Stream>? result` (and keep/adjust the attribute if you want the stronger flow info), or avoid assigning null by using a local `Func<Stream>? tmp = null;` and only assigning `result = tmp!;` on the true path. Also ensure all `return false` paths leave `result` null and all `return true` paths assign a non-null delegate.

Enabling `#nullable` at file scope is fine for progressive migration, but please also add a matching `#nullable restore` (or `#nullable disable`) at the end of the file to avoid accidentally affecting any code appended later (including generated/partial additions) and to make the migration boundary explicit. If the intent is to keep the entire file nullable-aware permanently, consider adding a brief comment explaining that, or ensure the project-level nullable setting will eventually make this directive unnecessary.

Enabling `#nullable` at file scope is fine for progressive migration, but please ensure this file is fully annotated/clean under nullable analysis (e.g., no implicit null returns/fields, correct `string?`/reference annotations, and no new warnings). Also consider adding `#nullable disable` at the end of the file to avoid accidentally affecting any later code added outside the intended region (especially if this file is appended to in the future).

If this file interacts heavily with non-nullable-unaware code, consider using `#nullable enable annotations` (or `#nullable enable warnings`) to limit churn until callers are migrated, and add `#nullable restore` at the end if the project relies on file-level directives being scoped explicitly.

Enabling nullable for the whole file is fine, but please also add a matching `#nullable restore` (or `#nullable disable`) at the end of the file to avoid the directive unintentionally affecting any code that might be appended later (or in partial/merged contexts). Also ensure the rest of this file compiles cleanly under NRT (no new warnings) and that any public APIs in this file have correct nullability annotations, since `#nullable enable` changes the contract surface for consumers.

Enabling `#nullable` at file scope is fine for progressive migration, but please ensure the rest of this file is actually annotated/updated to satisfy nullable analysis (e.g., fields/properties initialized, APIs that can return null marked as nullable, and any null-forgiving operators justified). If this file is not yet fully migrated, consider scoping the directive more narrowly (around the specific type) or adding `#nullable disable` at the end to avoid unintentionally affecting any later code in the file (including future additions).

Adding an assembly alias changes how this reference can be consumed: any code that wants to use the alias must now use `extern alias OpenRAGame;` and qualify types (e.g., `OpenRAGame::OpenRA...`). If the intent is only to disambiguate a specific conflict, consider using a single non-`global` alias (or document why `global` is needed) to avoid surprising resolution/ambiguity changes across the project. Also ensure this is paired with the corresponding `extern alias` usage (and that no other references define the same alias), otherwise this line is effectively a no-op or can introduce hard-to-diagnose compile issues later.

Changing the "no codes" case from a silent `return` to throwing `InvalidDataException` is a behavioral change that may break callers that previously relied on this being a no-op (or on partially-initialized state). If this condition truly indicates corrupt/unsupported input, throwing is reasonable, but please confirm all call sites handle this (or that it should propagate) and consider adding a brief comment explaining why this is now exceptional. Also, if there is a more specific exception type used elsewhere in this decompressor (e.g., a custom format exception), it may be preferable for consistency.

Enabling nullable here is fine, but please also add a matching `#nullable disable` at the end of the file (or use `#nullable restore`) to avoid unintentionally changing the nullable context for any code that might be appended later or for partial-file patterns. Also ensure the file is actually annotated/clean under NRT (no new warnings) rather than just enabling it without follow-up changes.

Enabling `#nullable` at file scope is fine for progressive migration, but please ensure the rest of this file is actually annotated/adjusted to satisfy nullable analysis (e.g., any fields/parameters/returns that can be null are marked `?`, and any null-forgiving `!` is justified). If this file is intended to remain nullable-aware long-term, consider adding `#nullable disable` at the end (or scoping with `#nullable enable annotations` / `warnings`) to avoid accidentally affecting future additions or partial-class code in the same file.

Now that `currentVolume` is nullable, consider making the null-state unrepresentable instead of guarding at each call site. If a missing volume is an error, you could throw inside `SetVolume(...)` (or the constructor) and keep `currentVolume` non-nullable, which simplifies the rest of the class and avoids repeated checks. If missing volumes are expected/handled, then the current guards are fine, but please ensure *all* usages of `currentVolume` are protected (including any future additions) and consider documenting the invariant (e.g., `currentVolume` may be null when the requested volume is absent).

Now that `onProgress` is nullable, consider also validating the other parameters under `#nullable enable`. In particular, `filename` and `output` are still non-nullable but the method will throw later (or behave unexpectedly) if either is null. Either add explicit `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(filename/output)` (preferred for clear contract) or annotate them as nullable if null is a supported input. Also ensure all uses of `onProgress` are null-conditional (`onProgress?.Invoke(...)`) to avoid NREs.

Enabling nullable at file scope is fine for incremental migration, but please also add a matching `#nullable restore` (or `#nullable disable`) at the end of the file to avoid unintentionally affecting any code that might be appended later (e.g., via partials, generated code, or future edits). If the intent is to keep this file permanently nullable-aware, consider documenting that in a brief comment near the directive.

Good use of [MaybeNullWhen(false)] to express the out contract under NRT. Two follow-ups to consider: (1) since this method returns a bool and the out value is only meaningful on success, you could alternatively annotate as `[NotNullWhen(true)] out Func<Stream>? result` (or keep current) to make the success-path non-null explicit to callers; (2) with `#nullable enable`, `result = null;` requires `Func<Stream>?` unless you rely on the attribute + suppression—so ensure the signature/initialization aligns to avoid nullable warnings (e.g., declare `out Func<Stream>? result` and keep the attribute, or use `result = default!;` if you want to keep it non-nullable).

This field is initialized to a non-null literal ("Game"), so marking it as nullable weakens the contract and will force downstream null-handling unnecessarily. Prefer `string ModTabTitle = "Game";` (non-null) unless there is a real scenario where this can be absent. If the intent is to allow mods to omit the tab title, consider using `string? ModTabTitle` but then avoid providing a non-null default (or document that null means "use default" and set the default elsewhere).

Disabling nullable immediately after the interface limits the benefit of NRT in this file and can hide nullability issues in the adjacent types (e.g., ExternalConditionInfo). If the intent is progressive migration, consider keeping `#nullable enable` for the whole file (or at least through the related trait types) and only disabling around specific legacy blocks that are too noisy. Also consider adding a brief comment explaining why nullability is disabled here to avoid future confusion.

Disabling nullable immediately after the interface limits the benefit of the migration and can create an inconsistent nullability context for the rest of the file (especially if types below interact with this interface). Consider keeping `#nullable enable` for the whole file (or at least through the related trait/info types) and only using `#nullable disable` around specific legacy blocks that are too noisy to fix yet. If the intent is to scope nullability to just this interface, add a brief comment explaining why, and ensure any members that should be nullable are explicitly annotated while the context is enabled.

With `#nullable enable` in effect, changing `resourceType` to `string?` is fine, but the out parameter annotation is likely incomplete: `out string terrainType` will still be treated as non-null on success, yet the signature doesn’t communicate what happens when the method returns `false`. Consider annotating the out parameter with `[MaybeNullWhen(false)]` (as you did) *and* making the out type `string?` if implementations can legitimately set it to null on failure. If the contract guarantees a non-null value even on failure (e.g., empty string), then keep `string` but ensure all implementations uphold that. Also consider applying the same nullability/attributes consistently to `TryGetResourceIndex` (e.g., `[NotNullWhen(true)]` on `resourceType` if you require it non-null for success), so callers get accurate flow analysis.

Converting `VersionCheckProtocol` to string here changes the serialized value from whatever implicit conversion existed (likely numeric) to the enum name (e.g. "V1"). If the web service expects a numeric protocol value, this will break compatibility. Consider explicitly controlling the wire format: either keep numeric via `((int)VersionCheckProtocol).ToString(CultureInfo.InvariantCulture)` or, if the server expects the name, use `VersionCheckProtocol.ToString("G")` and document/ensure the server contract matches. Also consider invariant culture for any numeric formatting to avoid locale-dependent output.

This hunk removes the nested `ShowPlayerActionDropDownOption` type entirely. Please double-check that it is truly unused (no references in other partials/files, reflection/serialization, or XAML/widget binding patterns) and that any call sites were updated accordingly. If it was only used as a simple DTO for dropdown options, consider replacing it with a clearer alternative (e.g., `record`/`readonly struct` or `KeyValuePair`/tuple) and ensure nullability expectations are preserved (e.g., `Click`/`Title` should be non-null). Also ensure the removal doesn’t break binary compatibility if this type was part of a public API surface (even if nested).

Converting `ProtocolVersion` to string here is likely correct if `HttpQueryBuilder` now expects `IEnumerable<KeyValuePair<string,string>>`, but please ensure the string format is stable and culture-invariant. If `ProtocolVersion` is numeric, prefer `ToString(CultureInfo.InvariantCulture)` (or an explicit format) to avoid locale-specific output. Also consider whether `HttpQueryBuilder` could accept non-string values and handle conversion centrally to avoid scattered `ToString()` calls.

Enabling nullable for this file is fine, but please also add a matching `#nullable restore` (or `#nullable disable`) at the end of the file to avoid unintentionally affecting any code that might be appended later (e.g., via partial classes or future edits). Also consider placing the directive at the very top of the file (after any header comments, before `using` statements) to make the nullable context unambiguous for the entire compilation unit.

Disabling nullable immediately after enabling it for a single interface makes the file’s nullability context harder to reason about and can hide warnings in the rest of the test. Consider either (a) leaving `#nullable enable` in effect for the whole file and fixing/annotating the few members that need it (e.g., `Create` returning `null` should be `object?`), or (b) scoping the disable more narrowly with `#nullable disable warnings` / `#nullable restore` around the specific legacy code that can’t be updated yet. This keeps the migration incremental without losing coverage from NRT analysis.

Enabling nullable here is fine, but consider scoping it more narrowly to avoid unintentionally changing nullability analysis for the entire test file (and any future additions). If only specific types/methods in this file are being migrated, prefer `#nullable enable annotations` (or `#nullable enable` followed by `#nullable restore`/`#nullable disable` at the end of the migrated region) to keep the migration incremental and reduce churn in tests.

This now unconditionally queues a DockActivity whenever ammo is empty, but it doesn’t verify that a valid rearm dock exists/reachable (previous code explicitly searched for a rearmTarget and bailed if none). If DockActivity can fail to find a dock, consider adding a pre-check (e.g., via DockManager query) and returning true/aborting gracefully to avoid the minelayer getting stuck repeatedly trying to dock. Also, passing `null` as the third argument is unclear—if this is the dock target or a filter, prefer an explicit overload or named argument to document intent and avoid future NREs/behavior changes.

This count currently includes dead actors, unlike other helpers in this file (e.g. CountActorByCommonName uses `!a.IsDead`). That can skew AI decisions (e.g. thinking it has available dockables when they’re destroyed). Consider adding `!a.IsDead` (and possibly `a.IsInWorld`) to match the semantics of the other counting utilities. Also, `GetActorsWithTrait<T>` already filters by trait `T`, so if the intent is to count dockables by dock type, you may want to constrain `T` (or drop `T` and just query `IDockable`) to avoid surprising results when `T` is unrelated.

This replaces `ReturnToBase` with `DockActivity`, but the old behavior likely included more than just docking (e.g., selecting an appropriate base/airfield, handling cases where no dock exists, and potentially different logic for aircraft vs. ground dockables). Please verify that `DockActivity(rearmable.DockManager, rearmable)` preserves the previous semantics: (1) it can resolve a valid dock for aircraft rearm (not just any dock type), (2) it fails gracefully when no dock is available (no infinite retry / stuck state), and (3) it still respects `AbortOnResupply`/`returnToBase` flow (i.e., doesn’t immediately re-enter attack if docking can’t start). If `DockActivity` requires a target dock to be preselected, consider passing/deriving the intended dock type/target here or adding a guard before queueing.

Remove this direct Console.WriteLine debug logging (or gate it behind the engine logging facilities). Writing to stdout in a hot activity constructor will spam output, hurt performance, and is inconsistent with OpenRA's logging approach. Prefer using the project's logging API (e.g., Log.Write/Debug) with an appropriate channel and/or compile-time conditional (DEBUG) if this is temporary instrumentation.

This hard-codes the “actor below” detection to `RepairableInfo`, which is likely the root cause of the reported bug (ground units can’t rearm unless they also have `Repairable:`). Docking/resupply should not depend on the presence of `RepairableInfo` on the host; it should instead check for the relevant dock capability (e.g., `Dock` trait / `DockInfo` with the appropriate `DockType` such as Rearm/Repair/Refuel) or query `DockManager`/`Dock` for the current host actor. Suggest replacing this with a check for the dock/resupply provider trait(s) (or `IDockHost`/`Dock`), and avoid coupling rearm logic to repairability. Also consider filtering by `a != Dock.Self` if the dock itself occupies the cell, and ensure this works for non-repair docks (rearm pads, helipads, etc.).

This changes the “unblock if we’re sitting on the refinery entrance” check from the actual delivery/entrance cell (previously `lastproc.Location + DeliveryOffset`) to the dock actor’s origin cell (`lastproc.Location`). That can break the intended behavior for docks whose usable entrance is offset (common for refineries), causing harvesters to not move away when blocking the entrance, or to move away when merely standing on the building’s origin. Consider restoring the offset-aware logic by using the dock’s configured docking/delivery cell (e.g., via `Dock` trait offset or an interface on `DockManager` that returns the effective dock/entrance cell), rather than comparing to `lastproc.Location`.

This changes the callback argument from the harvester actor (`self`) to a `DockManager` trait instance. That’s a significant semantic/API shift: (1) `TraitOrDefault<DockManager>()` can return null, so `MovementCancelled` implementations must be null-safe or this will introduce NREs; (2) if `MovementCancelled` was designed to receive the actor, passing a trait breaks expectations and may silently skip important context (owner, position, etc.). Consider either keeping `self` as the argument, or updating the interface/implementations to accept both (e.g., `MovementCancelled(Actor self, DockManager dockManager)`), or at least guard against null and document the new contract.

This assumes `wda.DockLoopSequence` is always valid when `wda` is present. If `DockLoopSequence` can be null/empty (or missing in some YAMLs), `PlayCustomAnimationRepeating` may throw or leave the actor stuck in `DockingState.Loop` with no animation. Consider guarding for an empty loop sequence (e.g., only start repeating if non-empty, otherwise just play `DockSequence` and then proceed), or validate in `WithDockingAnimationInfo` so the contract is explicit.

This uses `Trait<WithVoxelUnloadBody>()`, which will throw if the dockable actor doesn't have the trait. If `VoxelDockSequence` can ever be selected/constructed for actors without `WithVoxelUnloadBody` (e.g., via YAML misconfiguration or future reuse), this will crash at runtime. Consider using `TraitOrDefault<WithVoxelUnloadBody>()` with a clear error/guard, or enforce the requirement earlier (e.g., in the factory/selection logic) and add an explicit validation message so failures are actionable.

This now hard-depends on the unit having a `Repairable` trait to find a dock. That likely reintroduces/doesn't fix the reported issue where ground units that are *rearmable* but not *repairable* can't get a docking cursor/order. If the intent is to unify docking, consider querying a more general dockable interface/trait (e.g., `IDockable`/`DockableBase`) or checking for other dock-capable traits (e.g., `Rearmable`) instead of only `Repairable`, so rearm-only units can still resolve a dock.

This queues a DockActivity even when either DockManager or Rearmable is missing (both are fetched via TraitOrDefault). If DockActivity assumes non-null dependencies, this can cause null-reference errors or silently do nothing. Consider validating prerequisites here (e.g., throw a script-friendly exception / log a clear error) or make DockActivity robust to nulls. Also, changing the script API from `Actor destination` to `Dock destination` is a breaking change for existing scripts; if possible, keep an overload that accepts `Actor` and resolves its `Dock` trait (or accept a more general type) to preserve compatibility.

This changes the activity construction from passing the Actor to passing the Harvester trait instance. Please ensure this is safe in scripting contexts: `Trait<Harvester>()` will throw if the actor lacks the trait (or if the trait is disabled/removed by upgrades), which would make `FindResources()` crash scripts instead of failing gracefully. Consider using `TraitOrDefault<Harvester>()` (or equivalent) with a null-check and a clear script error, or keep passing `Self` if the activity can resolve the trait internally. Also double-check that `FindAndDeliverResources` now consistently expects a `Harvester` (not an `Actor`) across all call sites to avoid API drift.

This condition changes the old "not currently on a resupplier" check (`GetActorBelow() == null`) to `dockManager?.LinkedDock == null`, but `dockManager` is optional (TraitOrDefault) and may be null for actors that still rely on legacy behavior or missing DockManager wiring. In that case, this will always be true and will enqueue `DockActivity(dockManager, rearmable)` with a null dockManager, which is likely to NRE or cause incorrect idle behavior. Consider guarding the enqueue (e.g., `dockManager != null && dockManager.LinkedDock == null`) and/or falling back to the previous terrain/altitude-based check when DockManager is absent. Also remove the `// error` comment or replace it with a proper TODO explaining the intended behavior.

This change drops the previous exemption for `ReturnToBase` (and `Resupply`) and only protects `DockSequence`. If `DockActivity` can now be the main activity (per PR description) or if there are other docking-related activities besides `DockSequence`, then Stop orders from non-Mobile/Aircraft traits may incorrectly cancel docking/resupply/return-to-base flows. Consider broadening the check to cover the full new docking pipeline (e.g., `DockActivity` and/or a common interface/base type for docking activities) or otherwise ensure `ReturnToBase` is no longer used anywhere. Also, the comment above still references Resupply/ReturnToBase and should be updated to match the new architecture to avoid confusion.

Using `activity.GetType() == typeof(DockSequence)` is brittle and will miss subclasses/proxies of `DockSequence` (and any future derived docking activities). Prefer `activity is DockSequence` (or, better, a shared interface/marker like `IDockingActivity`/`IDockSequence` if available) to make the bot logic resilient to refactors and mod-specific activity types. Also consider whether rearming can now occur via `DockActivity` (not just `DockSequence`) after the refactor; if so, this check may be incomplete.

This changes the building count from summing over `RearmActors` (explicit actor names) to counting `Building` traits by `rearmableInfo.DockType`. Please double-check that `AIUtils.CountActorsWithTrait<Building>(...)` actually accepts a dock type (e.g., `BitSet<DockType>`/`DockType`) and filters to *rearm-capable* docks, not just any building that happens to share a dock type. If `DockType` is a capability category rather than a concrete actor name list, this may overcount/undercount in mods where multiple structures share the same dock type but differ in rearm support/capacity. Consider either (a) counting actors with the `Dock` trait filtered by `DockType` (or a dedicated `RearmsUnits`/`ProvidesDock` trait), or (b) preserving the explicit actor list when available to avoid behavior regressions.

This allocates multiple times per call (Where iterator + ToList) and then iterates again via Do, which can be hot if RefreshOccupancy is called frequently (e.g., every CanDock/Reserve/Occupancy query). Consider rewriting as a single pass without LINQ/allocations, e.g. iterate reservedDockables backwards and Unreserve dead entries. Also be careful that Unreserve mutates reservedDockables; the current ToList snapshot avoids modification-during-enumeration, but a manual loop must account for that.

Potential functional regression related to the reported issue: this method only considers `Dock` traits on the target actor. If a structure exposes rearm/repair via other traits (or if the cursor logic previously checked `Rearmable` independently of `Repairable`), then units without `Repairable` may still fail to get a docking cursor unless the structure has a `Dock` trait configured for the relevant `DockType`. Please double-check that all rearm/repair providers now always include a `Dock` trait and that `DockingPossible` for rearm does not implicitly depend on `Repairable` being present on the unit. Consider adding a unit test / rules test case for the minelayer reproduction (rearm without `Repairable`) to ensure the cursor appears and the order can be issued.

This trait no longer implements IIssueOrder/IOrderVoice and the Info fields that defined docking/enter targeting (DockActors, cursors, voice, RequiresForceMove) were removed. As a result, the actor will no longer expose the "Enter" order/cursor or play the configured voice, and ResolveOrder no longer validates "Enter" targets. If this behavior is intended to be handled by the new DockManager/Dock traits, please ensure TransformsIntoAircraft is registered as a dockable/enter-capable unit there; otherwise this is a functional regression (players won't be able to issue Enter/Move orders via this trait, and invalid Enter orders may slip through). Consider either reintroducing the order targeters/voice here or adding an explicit integration point with the new docking system.

This changes the stop-cancel guard from `(Resupply || ReturnToBase)` to only `DockSequence`. In the refactor, the old comment says resupply could be the main activity or a child of ReturnToBase; if the new flow uses `DockActivity` as the main activity and `DockSequence` as a child (per PR description), then guarding only `DockSequence` may still allow Stop to cancel an in-progress docking/resupply when `CurrentActivity` is `DockActivity` (or other docking-related wrapper), reintroducing the original issue (rearm/repair interrupted / cursor logic depending on activity state). Consider expanding the check to include the new top-level docking activity type(s) (e.g., `DockActivity` and/or a common interface/base like `DockActivityBase`/`IDockActivity` if available), or update the comment and ensure the new activity graph guarantees `CurrentActivity` is always `DockSequence` during docking. Also, the comment above still references Resupply/ReturnToBase and should be updated to match the new architecture.

This now forwards `dockable.Self` instead of the `self` parameter used previously. Please double-check that `DockManager.Self` is always the same actor instance that the transport expects (i.e., the harvester being carried) and is non-null at this point. If `DockManager` can represent/mediate docking for something other than the requesting actor, this could cause transports to be requested for the wrong actor. Consider adding a guard/assert (or using the original `self` if available in this callback) to prevent subtle misrouting bugs.

The new `INotifyDockable.Docked(DockManager dockable, Dock dock)` signature introduces parameters that are currently unused in this implementation. Consider either using them (e.g., to only uncloak for specific `dock.Type`/dock owner, or to distinguish docking vs. rearming/repairing) or explicitly discarding them (`_`) to avoid warnings and make intent clear. Also, please ensure the `Docked/Undocked` logic still matches the new docking lifecycle: with the addition of `DockTick`, some traits may now need to set/clear `isDocking` based on tick/start/end events rather than only on Docked/Undocked, otherwise `isDocking` could get stuck if docking is interrupted/cancelled.

The `allowedToForceEnter || CanDock()` check is redundant/inconsistent with the preceding `DockingPossible(...)` call. `DockingPossible(target.MyDockType)` already includes `CanDock()`, so this line effectively reduces to `return allowedToForceEnter` when `CanDock()` is false, but it also re-evaluates `CanDock()` (which may be non-trivial or stateful) and can diverge if `CanDock()` changes between calls. Consider restructuring to avoid double evaluation and make the intent explicit, e.g. compute `var canDock = CanDock();` once and use `if (!DockType().Overlaps(...) || !target.CanDock(...)) return false; return canDock || allowedToForceEnter;` (or incorporate the force-enter logic into `DockingPossible` so the semantics are centralized).

Potential bug: `ToArray().Select(...)` is redundant and may also capture stale modifiers if traits are added/removed later. More importantly, `GetResourceValueModifier()` looks like it could be dynamic (e.g., depends on conditions/upgrades); caching the returned `IEnumerable<int>` at Created-time may cause incorrect resource values after modifiers change. Consider either (a) caching the modifier traits (`IResourceValueModifier[]`) and querying them each time in `AcceptResources`, or (b) materializing the values into an `int[]` and documenting that modifiers are static for the actor lifetime. Also, you can simplify to `self.TraitsImplementing<IResourceValueModifier>().Select(m => m.GetResourceValueModifier()).ToArray()` if you do want a snapshot.

This changes the production gate from the old global reservation system (`Reservable.IsReserved(self)`) to checking only the local `Dock` occupancy. That’s a behavioral change: buildings without a `Dock` trait will now always pass this check (`?? true`), and buildings with multiple docks/exit points may still be “occupied” even if another exit is free. Also, the method name `IsUncoccupied()` looks like a typo (should likely be `IsUnoccupied()`), which can hurt readability and suggests a potential API mismatch. Consider (1) keeping reservation semantics (or an equivalent) for non-dock production blocking, (2) checking availability per selected exit/dock rather than a single building-wide flag, and (3) fixing/renaming the method to the correct spelling for clarity/consistency.

Potential NRE / incorrect behavior if no matching AmmoPools are found. `RearmableAmmoPools` is only initialized in `Created`, and `CanDock()` can be queried before that (or if the actor has no AmmoPool traits matching `Info.AmmoPools`). In that case `RearmableAmmoPools` may be null, and even if it’s an empty array, `All(p => p.HasFullAmmo)` returns true, making `CanDock()` return false (unit can never dock/rearm). Consider initializing `RearmableAmmoPools` to `Array.Empty<AmmoPool>()` and/or guarding: `return RearmableAmmoPools != null && RearmableAmmoPools.Any(p => !p.HasFullAmmo);` (or treat missing pools as non-dockable with a clearer failure mode/log).

`DockTick` is implemented as a no-op. If this trait doesn't need per-tick notifications, consider providing a default/optional implementation in `INotifyDock` (or an adapter/base class) to avoid forcing empty methods across implementers. If `DockTick` is required for correctness (e.g., to handle long docking sequences or state changes), add a brief comment explaining why it's intentionally empty here, or consider using it to keep `docked`/overlay state in sync with the passed `dockable`/`dock` context.

After moving this trait from the Cnc mod into Mods.Common, double-check that the namespace change is matched by the rest of the codebase (YAML trait references, any `using OpenRA.Mods.Cnc.Traits.Render;` in C# code, and any reflection-based lookups). If any mod rules still reference the old fully-qualified type name, they will fail to load at runtime. Consider adding/confirming a migration step (e.g., updating rules, or providing a temporary type forwarder/alias) if backward compatibility is required.

This trait previously listened to harvester-specific events (MovingToRefinery) but now implements the generic docking callback. Please double-check that the interface change is complete for this class: (1) the class declaration should implement INotifyDockableAction (and no longer INotifyHarvesterAction) unless it still needs Harvested/MovingToResources; (2) the new signature uses DockManager/Dock instead of Actor refineryActor, so if the animation should only trigger for refinery docking, you may need to filter by dock.Type (or other dock metadata) to avoid unintended behavior for repair/rearm docks; (3) consider whether the callback should take Actor self for consistency/clarity—if the interface no longer provides it, ensure you can still access the owning actor where needed.

MovementCancelled now receives a DockManager instead of Actor self. If this trait ever needs to stop/reset harvest animations on cancel, ensure it can still identify the owning actor/renderer state from the provided DockManager (or that the interface provides another way). Otherwise, cancellation may no longer correctly reset visuals compared to the previous harvester-specific callback.

This refactor changes the notification interface from `INotifyHarvesterAction` to `INotifyDockableAction`, but the method signature now takes `DockManager dockable` instead of the actor (`Actor self`). For a render trait like `WithHarvestOverlay`, losing the `Actor` context can make it harder to validate the event source (e.g., ensure it matches the owning actor) and to access world/position/state without reaching back through `dockable`. Consider passing `Actor self` (or both `Actor self` and `DockManager`) to keep the callback consistent with other notify patterns and avoid tight coupling to `DockManager` from rendering code. Also, if this trait still implements `INotifyHarvesterAction.Harvested/MovingToResources`, ensure it now explicitly implements `INotifyDockableAction` too; otherwise these methods may never be invoked after the interface change.

This method signature changed from `BeforeResupply(Actor dockable, ResupplyType types)` to `Docked(DockManager dockable, Dock dock)`, but the body still relies on `types.HasFlag(ResupplyType.Repair)` (see line 76). As written, `types` is no longer in scope, so this won’t compile and/or indicates the refactor missed mapping resupply types into the new `INotifyDock` API. Consider either (a) extending `INotifyDock.Docked`/`DockTick` to include the resupply types (or a context object that exposes them), or (b) deriving the “repairing” state from `dock`/`dockable` (e.g., current dock operation) and updating the overlay logic accordingly. Also ensure the overlay only triggers for repair docking, not rearm/refuel-only docking, to avoid reintroducing the original issue where rearm depends on repairability.

Exposing `Docked` as a public mutable field makes this trait easy to misuse and hard to reason about (any other trait can flip it at any time, and there’s no notification/validation). Consider making this `private` with a controlled setter (e.g., `SetDocked(bool)`), or implement a condition/notification-based approach (e.g., reacting to docking state/condition changes) so the render state is driven by the docking system rather than ad-hoc mutation. This will also help when the TODO is addressed (granting a condition on docking) and reduce coupling to the refactor’s internal state.

Potential functional regression: `TakeCash(cost, true)` appears to be a *query/simulate* call (depending on API semantics) rather than actually deducting funds. If `true` means “check only”, then repairs will never charge the player even though the logic assumes payment happened. Please confirm the boolean parameter meaning and use the correct call/flag to actually spend cash (or explicitly call a separate `SpendCash`/`TakeCash(cost, false)` after the check). Also consider what should happen when `cost == 0` (currently it will always succeed and proceed).

Switching to `default` is fine stylistically, but please double-check this doesn’t change YAML/serialization behavior for `BitSet<DamageType>` (e.g., whether an omitted field vs an explicitly empty set are treated differently). If any code relies on `RepairDamageTypes` being `default(BitSet<DamageType>)` specifically (or on it being non-null/initialized in a particular way), add/adjust a regression test or ensure the `BitSet` default state is equivalent.

This interface change is a breaking API change (signature + semantics) and it also introduces a potential naming confusion: the parameter is named `dockable` but typed as `DockManager`, which reads like the *manager* rather than the dockable actor/trait. Consider renaming the parameter to `dockManager` (or changing the type to `IDockable`/`Actor` if the intent is to notify about the docked unit) to avoid ambiguity and reduce coupling to the manager implementation. Also, `DockTick` is now part of the notification contract; ensure all existing implementers are updated and that `DockTick` is only called when the dock is still valid (to avoid null/invalid dock references during undock/destruction).

This changes the hotkey from cycling only actual harvesters to cycling *all* dockable units (repairable/rearmable/aircraft/etc.) after the docking refactor. That’s a behavioral change that likely breaks the intent/UX of a “Cycle Harvesters” hotkey and may cause unexpected selections (e.g., minelayers, aircraft, units that dock to repair/rearm). Consider keeping this scoped to harvesters (e.g., query `Harvester`/`IHarvester`/a dedicated `DockType.Harvest` filter) or rename the hotkey/logic to reflect the broader selection. If the goal is still harvesters, filter `IDockable` by dock type/capability rather than the interface alone.

This change drops the `Owner == player` filter, so the harvester count will include *all* harvesters in the world, not just the specified player's. If `IsAliveAndInWorld` already implies alive/in-world/trait-enabled, consider combining it with the owner check (e.g., `a.Actor.Owner == player && a.Trait.IsAliveAndInWorld`) or ensure `IsAliveAndInWorld` is player-scoped (it doesn't appear to be). Otherwise observer economy stats will be incorrect.

Switching from `ActorsHavingTrait<Refinery>()` to `ActorsHavingTrait<Dock>()` broadens the search to *any* dock-capable structure, which may unintentionally select non-refinery docks (e.g., repair/rearm pads) that also happen to have `FreeActorWithDeliveryInfo`. If the intent is specifically “find a refinery-like dock that can spawn a free harvester with delivery”, consider additionally filtering by dock type (e.g., `DockInfo.Type` contains the harvester/resource dock type) or by a more specific trait/info that represents a refinery/resource dropoff. Otherwise this could change gameplay by spawning harvesters at the wrong structure when multiple docks exist.

Adding `DockManager:` here is likely required after the docking refactor, but this change also removed the `RepairActors` mapping from `Repairable`. If `DockManager` now owns the structure/actor compatibility logic, please ensure the equivalent of `RepairActors: fix` is expressed somewhere (e.g., via `Dock` traits on the repair structure and a matching `DockType` on the unit). Otherwise units may become “repairable” in name only, or lose the ability to find/validate a repair dock (and this may also relate to the reported rearm-vs-repair coupling). Consider explicitly setting `DockType` for this `Repairable` block (as done below for `repairHeli`) or documenting why the default is correct.

Typo in key name: `Ofset` should be `Offset`. As written, the dock offset will likely be ignored and the helipad docking position may default to (0,0) or another fallback, causing incorrect docking/resupply behavior (and potentially breaking cursor/validity checks).

Adding a hardcoded `TickRate` here changes harvester behavior (and potentially balance/perf) but the issue/PR context is about docking/rearming logic. If this is required by the new docking refactor, consider documenting why (e.g., comment in rules or PR notes) and ensure the default tick rate is consistent across dockables/harvesters so this doesn’t become an arbitrary per-unit override. Also verify the unit for `TickRate` (ticks vs frames) and that 15 matches intended cadence; otherwise this may introduce subtle desync/balance changes.

Adding `DockManager:` here is good for the refactor, but note that `Repairable:` now has no `RepairActors` configured (the old `RepairActors: repair_pad` line was removed). If `DockManager` doesn’t implicitly provide/derive the dock types/actors for repair/rearm, this will break repair/rearm discovery and can reproduce the reported issue (rearm cursor missing unless also repairable). Please confirm the new docking config path for D2K defaults: either reintroduce the equivalent mapping (e.g., via `Dock`/`RepairsUnits`/dock type bitset config) or remove `Repairable:` from defaults if it’s no longer meaningful without explicit dock definitions.

The new `Dock` definition for `repair_pad` sets `Type: repair` and an `Offset`, but does not specify a `Facing` (unlike the refinery change above). If `Dock` defaults differ by type or are not stable across mods, this can cause inconsistent docking orientation/entry behavior (and potentially cursor/validity issues) after the refactor. Consider explicitly setting `Facing` here (or documenting/ensuring a safe default in the trait) to avoid regressions and make the YAML self-contained.

Adding `TickRate: 20` changes the harvester docking/search cadence, but it’s unclear why this value is needed here and whether it matches the engine’s expected units (ticks vs frames) and defaults. Please add a brief comment in the rules (or ensure the trait’s docs/defaults cover it) explaining the intent (e.g., performance vs responsiveness) and verify this doesn’t unintentionally slow down/alter harvester behavior compared to other mods/units. Also consider whether this should be set on the relevant trait explicitly (and consistently across similar actors) rather than only on d2k harvester.

This sets `TakeOffOnResupply: false` for MIG/YAK but not for the helicopter variants below. Is this intentional behavior difference, or should it be consistently defined for all aircraft using `DockManager`? If the default is `true`, helis may unexpectedly take off during resupply after this refactor. Consider either adding the explicit setting for helis too, or documenting/ensuring the default matches previous behavior for each aircraft class.

Adding `DockManager` to the default vehicle template changes behavior for all inheriting ground units. Given the reported issue (rearm cursor missing unless `Repairable` exists), please confirm `DockManager` is required for *all* vehicles or only for dock-capable ones. If `DockManager` is only needed when a unit has at least one dockable trait (Repairable/Rearmable/Harvester/etc.), consider moving it into those specific templates/traits to avoid unintended side effects (e.g., new orders/cursors, extra trait overhead, or altered docking eligibility) on units that should never dock.

This replaces `RepairActors`/`CloseEnough` with `DockType: repairShip`, but it’s not clear from this hunk how the mapping from docks (e.g., SPEN/SYRD) to `repairShip` is defined. Please ensure the corresponding dock structures declare a matching `Dock` trait `Type: repairShip` (and that any previous per-actor restrictions are preserved). Otherwise ships may become unable to find valid repair docks, or may start accepting unintended docks if `repairShip` is too broadly assigned.

Removing `WaitDistanceFromResupplyBase` may change aircraft resupply/landing behavior (queueing, spacing, or circling distance) and could reintroduce congestion around pads/docks. If this is now handled by `DockManager`/`DockSequence`, consider keeping an equivalent configurable knob (even if renamed) or documenting the new default behavior, since this value was previously tuned per-mod for pathing/traffic control.

`Ofset` looks like a typo and likely won’t be parsed by the `Dock` trait (expected `Offset`). If this key is ignored, the dock point will fall back to defaults and can break/shift repair docking behavior. Please rename to `Offset: 1, 1` (and consider adding schema/validation coverage to catch unknown YAML keys).

Removing `RearmActors` from `Rearmable` changes (or relies on changed) docking eligibility rules. Please double-check that the new docking refactor still restricts these aircraft to helipads (gahpad/nahpad) and doesn’t accidentally allow rearming at any dock that supports rearm, or conversely prevent rearming if no default is inferred. If the intent is to move this restriction to the pad’s `Dock` trait (or a global DockType mapping), consider adding a brief comment in rules or ensuring the pad definitions explicitly declare the supported dock types so this remains data-driven and mod-safe.

This `Repairable:` block no longer specifies any docking/repair target (previously `RepairActors: gadept`). If `DockManager` now drives docking, `Repairable` still needs to declare how/where it can dock (e.g., `DockType: repair` or an equivalent mapping) or you risk breaking repair/rearm cursor availability and reproducing the reported issue (units unable to rearm unless also repairable). Please confirm the new schema: either re-add the appropriate dock selector here, or ensure `DockManager`/`Dock` traits on structures provide a default that makes this unit repairable at the intended structure(s).

The Dock `Type` list here mixes `repairAir` and `rearm`. If the intent is that the helipad can rearm *ground* units too (per the issue: rearm should not require `Repairable:`), please confirm that `rearm` is sufficient for all intended unit classes and that no additional dock type (e.g. a distinct `rearmGround`/`rearmAny`) is required by the new docking logic. Otherwise, ground units may still fail to show the rearm cursor if they only match a different dock type than `rearm` or if `repairAir` unintentionally restricts eligibility. Consider adding a brief comment or aligning the dock types with the expected consumers to avoid regressions.

Please verify that the Dock `Type` list here includes all dock capabilities that NAHPAD is expected to provide. In TS, helipads typically support aircraft repair + rearm, but the reported issue is that *ground* units can’t rearm unless they’re also `Repairable:`. If any ground unit is intended to rearm at this structure, you may need an explicit dock type for that (e.g., a dedicated ground rearm dock type) and ensure the corresponding dockable trait advertises that type. Also double-check naming consistency (`repairAir` vs any existing `repair`/`repair-air` conventions) because a mismatch will silently prevent the cursor/validity checks from triggering.

Adding `Repairable` here looks like a workaround for the reported issue (rearm cursor gated by `Repairable:`), but it’s unclear whether this actor is intended to be repairable at all. If the new docking refactor still requires `Repairable` for rearming/resupplying, that coupling should be removed in code (or replaced with a dedicated dockable/resupply trait), rather than forcing unrelated actors to become repairable. If this actor truly must be repairable only when `factundeploy` is active, consider also verifying that the dock types/targets are correctly defined (e.g., via `Dock`/`DockManager` config) so rearm/repair eligibility is driven by dock type instead of trait presence.

This adds `DiscardExcessResources: true` to the Harvester trait, but in this hunk it also coincides with removing `DeliveryBuildings: proc` (and `DeliverVoice`). If the new docking refactor no longer uses `DeliveryBuildings`, please ensure the replacement configuration exists elsewhere (e.g., via `Dockable`/`DockManager`/`Dock` traits on the refinery) so harvesters still know where to deliver. Otherwise, this risks harvesters discarding resources instead of delivering when no valid dock is found. Consider documenting why discarding is needed here, and verify behavior with a regression test/map: harvester fills, returns to refinery, unloads, and does not silently drop cargo when a proc is available.

Adding `PROV_CIPHER_FLAG_PAD_IV` changes the generic IV validation/handling semantics for this cipher. Please double-check that this is the intended behavior for *all* ChaCha20-Poly1305 uses: (1) confirm the generic code now enforces the correct constraint (e.g., ivlen <= 12, and not allowing >12), (2) ensure padding is well-defined (left vs right zero-padding) and matches existing provider expectations/interop, and (3) consider whether this should be gated to only allow shorter IVs (as per the issue) rather than broadly enabling padding behavior that might mask caller mistakes. If there are other AEAD ciphers using `CUSTOM_IV`, verify there’s no unintended interaction when both flags are set.

The new ivlen validation introduces `pad_iv`-dependent rules, but the semantics are a bit unclear and could be easy to misuse. In particular, when `pad_iv` is set you now allow any `ivlen <= ctx->ivlen`, which is fine for algorithms that define left/zero padding, but it would be safer to (a) explicitly document that `pad_iv` means “accept shorter IVs and zero-pad to ctx->ivlen”, and (b) consider rejecting `ivlen == 0` unless the cipher explicitly allows it. Also, since `sizeof(ctx->iv)` is a hard cap, it may be worth asserting/ensuring `ctx->ivlen <= sizeof(ctx->iv)` at init time so the `pad_iv` branch can’t accept values that later lead to inconsistent state. Finally, please double-check that all call sites that set `PROV_CIPHER_FLAG_PAD_IV` also implement the expected padding behavior (e.g., `memset` + copy) so this check doesn’t silently accept shorter IVs without actually padding them.

Adding a new internal flag is fine, but the name/semantics are a bit ambiguous: “PAD_IV” could be read as “always pad IVs” rather than “allow shorter-than-default IV and zero-pad”. Consider a more explicit name (e.g., PROV_CIPHER_FLAG_ALLOW_SHORT_IV / _IVLEN_LE_DEFAULT) or ensure the places that check this flag clearly document the exact rule (ivlen <= default, left/right zero padding, and whether longer IVs are still rejected). Also ensure this bit doesn’t collide with any existing/anticipated flags and that all users initialize/clear it deterministically (e.g., in ctx init) to avoid accidental behavior changes across ciphers.

This new bitfield duplicates the information in the new PROV_CIPHER_FLAG_PAD_IV macro; please confirm there is a single source of truth (either flags or bitfields) and that they are kept in sync. If both are required, add a brief comment explaining the relationship and where pad_iv is set from the flags. Also, “default size” is unclear here—does it mean the algorithm’s fixed IV length (e.g., 12 for ChaCha20-Poly1305) or the ctx’s current ivlen? Clarifying this in the comment (and ensuring consistent behavior across init/reinit) will help avoid future regressions.

The comment says “LSB zero padding is used”, but the IV shown is 8 bytes and the AAD/tag/ciphertext match the prior 12-byte-IV test. It would be clearer/less ambiguous to explicitly state the padding direction and resulting 12-byte nonce (e.g., “right/LSB padding with 4 zero bytes to form 010203040506070800000000” or “left/MSB padding …” depending on the implementation). Also consider referencing the relevant spec/behavior (RFC 8439 nonce handling) or the provider’s documented convention, since “LSB” can be interpreted differently (byte order vs integer).

This change removes the NULL guard and will now dereference `cipher` unconditionally, reintroducing a potential SIGSEGV for callers that pass NULL (previous behavior returned 0). Unless the API contract guarantees `cipher` is never NULL, please keep the ternary check (or add an assertion + document the precondition).
