Moving `glslify-bundle`/`glslify-deps` from `devDependencies` into `dependencies` changes what gets installed for consumers of `@parcel/transformer-glsl` (and affects default install size). If these are required at runtime by the published transformer (i.e., used in `lib/`), this is correct—but please double-check they are actually runtime deps and not only used for build/tests. If they are runtime deps, consider pinning/aligning versions more tightly (or documenting why caret ranges are safe) to avoid unexpected behavior changes across installs.

Switching from `options.packageManager.require(...)` to a static import means `glslify-deps` must now be a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformer-glsl` (and will be resolved relative to this package, not the user project). Please double-check that `glslify-deps` is listed in this transformer's `dependencies` (not just `peerDependencies`) and that this change is intended with the new "resolve relative to user" behavior—otherwise this could reintroduce workspace/Yarn PnP resolution issues or break auto-install expectations for optional plugins.

This change removes the separate `devDependencies` section and folds `graphql`/`graphql-import-macro` into `dependencies`. That means these packages will now be installed for consumers at runtime, increasing install size and potentially conflicting with the PR goal of reducing default installs. If the transformer needs these at runtime (it likely does), consider whether they should instead be `peerDependencies` (and auto-installed into the user project) to avoid forcing them into Parcel's own dependency tree, especially in Yarn 2/workspaces where resolution is stricter. At minimum, double-check that moving these out of `devDependencies` is intentional and aligns with the new plugin resolution/auto-install strategy.

Switching from `options.packageManager.require` to a static import means `graphql` will now be resolved relative to this transformer package (and must be present in its declared deps). This may reintroduce the same workspace/Yarn PnP resolution issue described in the bug report (loading deps relative to a runtime/plugin rather than the user project) unless `graphql` is a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformer-graphql` and not expected to be auto-installed. Please verify `graphql` is listed in this package’s `dependencies` (or `peerDependencies` + ensured install) and that we don’t rely on `shouldAutoInstall` behavior here anymore. If `graphql` is intended to be user-provided, keep `packageManager.require` or resolve from the project root instead.

This change moves `sharp` from `devDependencies` into `dependencies` by removing the `devDependencies` block. That will make `sharp` a required runtime install for everyone who installs this transformer, which can significantly increase install size and may break environments where `sharp` is optional or fails to build (native deps). If the intent is to keep `sharp` optional and only used when the image transformer is actually invoked, consider keeping it as an optional dependency (e.g. `optionalDependencies`) and handling missing `sharp` gracefully in code, or ensure this package is only installed when needed. Also double-check that removing `devDependencies` doesn’t drop other dev-only tooling that might exist in the full file.

Importing `sharp` at module scope makes it a hard dependency that will be loaded whenever this transformer is required, even if no resize/format/quality transform is requested. This is a behavior change from the previous lazy `options.packageManager.require('sharp', ...)` which only loaded sharp when needed and allowed installs without sharp present. Given the PR goal (reduce default install size / auto-install behavior) and the Yarn 2 workspace resolution issues described, consider keeping `sharp` as an optional/lazy dependency (e.g., dynamic `import('sharp')` inside the `if (width || height || quality || format)` block) and provide a clear error message when it isn't installed. Also ensure `sharp` is declared appropriately (dependency vs optionalDependency/peerDependency) in this package so package managers don't treat the require as undeclared/ambiguous.

Removing the `peerDependencies` entry for `@parcel/core` may break consumers that install `@parcel/transformer-js` outside of the monorepo (or in Yarn 2/PNP workspaces), because the transformer still relies on Parcel core APIs at runtime. Without declaring it as a peer (or dependency), package managers can treat `require('@parcel/core')` as undeclared/ambiguous and fail resolution. If the intent is to reduce install size, consider keeping `@parcel/core` as a peerDependency (and optionally add it to `peerDependenciesMeta` as optional only if truly safe), or ensure the transformer no longer imports/depends on `@parcel/core` directly.

Moving `less` from `devDependencies` to `dependencies` makes sense for a published transformer (it needs `less` at runtime). However, please double-check whether `less` should instead be a `peerDependency` to avoid forcing a specific Less version into user projects and to prevent duplicate installs when users already depend on `less` (similar to the @babel/core/workspace resolution issue described). If Parcel expects to auto-install missing plugins/peers, consider aligning this with the new dependency/peerDependency strategy and ensure the default config’s `parcelDependencies` (if applicable) is updated accordingly.

Importing `less` directly here changes resolution semantics vs the previous `options.packageManager.require('less', asset.filePath, ...)`, which intentionally resolved relative to the user's project and could auto-install. With this change, `less` will resolve relative to `@parcel/transformer-less`'s own `node_modules`, and will fail if `less` is not a direct dependency of this package (or if the goal is to use the project's `less` version). Please confirm `less` is declared appropriately (likely as a dependency, not peer) and that we don't need project-relative resolution for compatibility. If project-relative resolution is still desired, consider keeping `packageManager.require` (or an equivalent resolver) rather than a static import.

Moving `@mdx-js/mdx` from `devDependencies` into `dependencies` makes sense for runtime usage, but this is also a major version bump (1.5.3 -> 1.6.22). Please confirm the transformer code is compatible with MDX 1.6.x (API/AST changes, remark/rehype pipeline differences) and that this won’t introduce unexpected behavior changes for users. If compatibility isn’t guaranteed, consider pinning to the previous minor range or documenting the required MDX version in `peerDependencies`/changelog.

Switching to a static import means `@mdx-js/mdx` must now be a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformer-mdx` (and will be resolved relative to this package). Please ensure the package.json for this transformer declares `@mdx-js/mdx` appropriately (likely `dependencies`, not `peerDependencies`), otherwise this will reintroduce the kind of workspace/Yarn PnP resolution failures described in the issue. If the intent is to keep MDX optional/auto-installed, this change also removes the previous `shouldAutoInstall` behavior, so consider whether that’s acceptable or if you need to keep dynamic resolution for optional installs.

`postcss` is now listed in both `devDependencies` and `peerDependencies`. That’s often intentional for local development/tests, but it can be confusing and may affect how consumers interpret required deps. Consider moving `postcss` to `dependencies` if the transformer must always have it available at runtime, or keep it only as a `peerDependency` (and ensure the default config/auto-install flow installs it) while leaving it in `devDependencies` only if you explicitly want it for this package’s own tests/build. Also verify the repo’s policy for peer deps includes adding the corresponding entry to `parcelDependencies` (if required by the auto-install mechanism described in the PR).

Switching from `options.packageManager.require('postcss', asset.filePath, ...)` to a static import changes resolution semantics: `postcss` will now be resolved relative to `@parcel/transformer-postcss` rather than the user project. That can break Yarn 2 PnP/workspaces and also prevents using a project-pinned PostCSS version (and bypasses `shouldAutoInstall`/range enforcement). If the intent is to bundle PostCSS with the transformer, ensure `postcss` is declared as a direct dependency of this package (not just a peer) and consider keeping the dynamic require for cases where the project needs to control the PostCSS version. At minimum, add/verify dependency declarations and tests for PnP/workspaces.

This change moves `@parcel/plugin` into `dependencies` (and removes the `devDependencies` section). That’s likely correct for a published transformer, but please double-check that `@parcel/plugin` is indeed required at runtime by this package (i.e., it’s imported from the built `lib/` output). If it’s only used for build/test tooling, it should remain in `devDependencies`. Also ensure the resulting JSON is valid and consistent with other transformers (trailing comma + removed `devDependencies` block can be easy to get wrong in manual edits).

Switching to a static `import pug from 'pug'` changes resolution semantics vs `options.packageManager.require(...)`: it will now always resolve `pug` relative to this transformer package, not the user project. That’s likely intended for the “reduce default install size” work, but please ensure `pug` is declared as a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformer-pug` (not just in `parcelDependencies`/auto-install), otherwise Yarn PnP/workspaces can fail with “tried to access ... but it isn't declared in its dependencies”. If the goal is to allow auto-installing `pug` into the user project on demand, this static import will prevent that and you may need to keep `packageManager.require` (or add a conditional fallback) instead.

Using `require.resolve('react-refresh/babel')` will resolve relative to this transformer package (or the runtime that loads it), not necessarily the user's project. This can reintroduce the workspace/Yarn PnP issue described (module not found / undeclared dependency) if `react-refresh` isn't a direct dependency of this package or isn't resolvable from here. Consider resolving from the project root / asset file (e.g. via `options.packageManager.resolve('react-refresh/babel', options.projectRoot)` or similar) or ensure `react-refresh` is declared as a dependency/peerDependency of this transformer so the resolution is unambiguous under PnP.

Removing `@babel/parser` from `dependencies` is risky: this transformer (or its transitive deps) may still require it at runtime, and in Yarn 2/PNP/workspaces that can fail with MODULE_NOT_FOUND if it’s no longer declared. If the goal is to rely on the user’s Babel toolchain, consider moving `@babel/parser` to `peerDependencies` (and ensure it’s auto-installed via `parcelDependencies` if applicable), or verify that `@parcel/babel-ast-utils`/`@babel/template` no longer pull it in implicitly. Please double-check the runtime require graph for this package under PnP to avoid regressions like the reported @babel/core resolution issue.

This change removes the `peerDependencies` entry for `@parcel/core`. If this package is loaded as a Parcel plugin/transformer, keeping `@parcel/core` as a peer dep helps package managers (especially Yarn PnP) enforce correct host compatibility and avoid ambiguous/unsound requires. Unless you’re certain this package never imports `@parcel/core` (directly or indirectly) and is only used internally with guaranteed resolution, consider retaining `@parcel/core` as a peerDependency (or at least document why it’s safe to drop).

Moving `sass` from `devDependencies` to `dependencies` changes the install behavior significantly: it will now always be installed with `@parcel/transformer-sass`, which may conflict with the PR goal of reducing default install size and also removes the ability for consumers to control the Sass implementation/version via their own dependency graph. If the intent is that projects provide `sass`, consider making it a `peerDependency` (optionally with a fallback `dependency` if you want a bundled default), and ensure `@parcel/core` remains a peer if it’s still required at runtime. Also, this is a semver jump from `^1.22.9` to `^1.32.4`; please confirm compatibility and whether this should be pinned to a narrower range.

Importing `sass` statically here changes resolution semantics vs `options.packageManager.require('sass', asset.filePath, {shouldAutoInstall})`. This will now resolve `sass` relative to the transformer package (and require it to be installed as a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformer-sass`), and it also removes the auto-install behavior for projects that rely on it. Given the reported Yarn 2/workspaces resolution issues, please confirm this is intentional and that `sass` is declared appropriately (likely as a dependency, not a peer) in this package. If the goal is to resolve `sass` from the user project (and/or support auto-install), consider keeping `options.packageManager.require` or using a resolution strategy that explicitly targets the project root rather than the runtime/transformer location.

Moving `stylus` from `devDependencies` to `dependencies` changes the published install surface (it will now be installed for consumers). That’s likely intended for runtime resolution, but please double-check this transformer actually requires `stylus` at runtime (not only in tests/build). If it’s only needed when the transformer is used, consider whether it should be a `peerDependency` (so the user controls the version) or keep it as a dependency but ensure the transformer’s code resolves it relative to the project to avoid Yarn 2 PnP/workspace resolution issues like the @babel/core regression described in the issue.

Switching from `options.packageManager.require('stylus', asset.filePath, ...)` to a static `import stylus from 'stylus'` changes resolution semantics: it will now always load the transformer’s own `stylus` dependency rather than resolving relative to the user project. That may be intended for the “reduce default install size” work, but it can also break cases where the user relies on a different Stylus version (or Yarn PnP/workspaces hoisting behavior) and expects Parcel to use the project-local stylus. If the goal is to avoid ambiguous/unsound requires under Yarn 2, consider keeping project-relative resolution but anchoring it to the project root (or using Parcel’s plugin resolution rules) rather than the runtime/transformer location, or document/enforce the supported stylus version via peerDependency and validate it. Also, this file now has both `import {typeof default as Stylus} from 'stylus';` and `import stylus from 'stylus';`—it’s worth consolidating to a single import to avoid duplication/confusion.

Adding `sugarss` as a direct dependency makes sense if the transformer `require`s it at runtime (and avoids Yarn 2 PnP/workspace resolution issues). Please double-check that the transformer actually imports `sugarss` directly; if it’s only used via `postcss` plugin resolution, consider whether it should be a `peerDependency` instead. Also ensure the version range aligns with the supported PostCSS 8 ecosystem and matches any versions referenced in Parcel’s default config/parcelDependencies to avoid auto-install/version skew.

Switching from `options.packageManager.require('sugarss', asset.filePath, ...)` to a static `import sugarss from 'sugarss'` changes resolution semantics: it will now always resolve `sugarss` relative to this transformer package, not the user project. That may be fine for reducing dynamic requires, but only if `sugarss` is declared as a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformer-sugarss` (not just a peer/optional dep) and is guaranteed to be present in the install. Otherwise this can reintroduce workspace/Yarn PnP resolution failures (similar to the @babel/core issue) or prevent auto-install behavior when `shouldAutoInstall` is enabled. Please verify `packages/transformers/sugarss/package.json` includes `sugarss` in `dependencies` (or keep the packageManager.require approach if it must be resolved from the project).

This change moves `@parcel/plugin` into `dependencies` (by removing the separate `devDependencies` block). That’s likely correct for a published transformer, but please double-check that this package is actually published/consumed at runtime and that `@parcel/plugin` is required at runtime (not just for build/test). If it’s only needed for development/building the package, it should remain in `devDependencies`; otherwise keeping it in `dependencies` avoids runtime resolution issues (especially with Yarn PnP/workspaces). Also ensure the resulting JSON is valid and that there isn’t still a dangling `devDependencies` key elsewhere in the file after this edit.

Switching from `options.packageManager.require` to a static import changes resolution semantics: `@iarna/toml` will now be resolved relative to this transformer package, not the user project. That’s likely intended for non-peer deps, but please ensure `@iarna/toml` is declared in this package’s `dependencies` (not just `parcelDependencies`/peer) so Yarn PnP/workspaces don’t fail with an undeclared/ambiguous require. If the goal is still to allow user-provided versions, keep the dynamic require or make it a peerDependency and document the expectation.

Having `typescript` in both `devDependencies` and `peerDependencies` is potentially redundant and can be confusing for consumers (and some tooling). If the intent is that Parcel should use the project's TypeScript, keep it as a `peerDependency` (and optionally add it to `peerDependenciesMeta` as optional if it’s not always required). If you need it for this package’s own tests/build, keep it in `devDependencies` but consider narrowing the peer range (or documenting why both are needed) to avoid version skew between the package’s dev TS and the user’s TS.

Importing `typescript` directly here changes resolution semantics vs `options.packageManager.require('typescript', asset.filePath, ...)`. In workspace/Yarn PnP setups, this will resolve relative to the transformer package (and may fail or pick a different TS version than the project expects). If the intent is to reduce installs, consider keeping project-relative resolution (or using Parcel's plugin resolution rules) and/or ensure `typescript` is declared appropriately (likely as a peerDependency) so the user's project provides it. Otherwise this may reintroduce the same class of "resolve relative to runtime" issues described in the bug report, but for TypeScript.

This change replaces the existing peerDependency on `@parcel/core` with `typescript`, which is likely incorrect for a Parcel transformer package. Transformers typically need `@parcel/core` (or at least `@parcel/plugin`) as a peer dependency to ensure they run against the host Parcel version, while `typescript` is already listed in `parcelDependencies` above. If the intent is to move `typescript` to peerDependencies, it should be added without removing `@parcel/core` (or confirm the correct Parcel peer dep for this package). As-is, this may break plugin loading/version compatibility and also duplicates the `typescript` requirement already declared in `parcelDependencies`.

Importing `typescript` directly here changes resolution semantics vs `options.packageManager.require('typescript', asset.filePath, ...)`. In Yarn 2/PNP/workspaces, a direct import will resolve relative to this package (the transformer) and will fail unless `typescript` is declared as a dependency/peerDependency of `@parcel/transformer-typescript-types` and is available at runtime. If the intent is to use the project's TypeScript (and optionally auto-install it), this should keep using `options.packageManager.require` (or an equivalent helper) rather than a static import. If the intent is to bundle/ship TypeScript with the transformer, ensure `typescript` is listed in this package's `dependencies` (not just dev) and consider the impact on install size and version compatibility.

This removes the previous MODULE_NOT_FOUND handling + auto-install retry for missing template preprocessors. With this change, `preprocessor.render` will now fail hard when a required engine (e.g. `pug`, `sass`, etc.) isn’t installed, even when `options.shouldAutoInstall` is true. Given the PR goal (auto-installing plugins/peers on demand), this likely regresses behavior for Vue template/style preprocessors. Consider restoring a safer version of the retry logic (ideally not parsing error messages, but at least preserving the auto-install path), or ensure these preprocessors are declared/auto-installed elsewhere so `render` can succeed in workspace/Yarn PnP setups.

Having `typescript` in both `devDependencies` and `peerDependencies` is often redundant and can be problematic in workspace/plug'n'play setups (it may lead to multiple versions being installed/used, and it’s unclear which one consumers should rely on). If `ts-utils` only needs TypeScript for building/tests, keep it in `devDependencies` only. If it needs the host project's TypeScript at runtime (or must share the same TS instance as the consumer), keep it as a `peerDependency` (and optionally add it to `devDependencies` only for local development, but ensure tooling resolves the peer). Consider clarifying intent and aligning with the repo’s policy for peer deps (e.g., add `peerDependenciesMeta` optional if appropriate).

Importing `typescript` statically here changes the resolution behavior from “resolve relative to the user project (and optionally auto-install)” to “resolve relative to this package’s own dependencies”. That can reintroduce the same workspace/Yarn PnP issue described in the bug report (module not found / undeclared dependency) unless `packages/utils/ts-utils` declares `typescript` as a dependency/peerDependency and the intended behavior is to always use Parcel’s bundled TS. If the goal is to use the project’s TypeScript (so versions match the user’s config), consider keeping `options.packageManager.require('typescript', config.searchPath, ...)` or using a conditional fallback: try project resolution first, then fall back to the bundled import. Also ensure the package.json for this package is updated accordingly if you keep the static import.

Having `eslint` in both `devDependencies` and `peerDependencies` can be confusing and may lead to duplicate installs or resolution ambiguity (especially with Yarn PnP/workspaces). If the intent is that consumers must provide their own ESLint, keep it only in `peerDependencies` (and optionally add it to `devDependencies` only for local development/tests, but then consider aligning versions and adding a `peerDependenciesMeta` entry if it should be optional). Also consider whether the peer range should be broader (e.g. include ESLint 7/8) to avoid forcing older ESLint versions in user projects.

Switching to a static `import eslint from 'eslint'` changes resolution semantics: it will now resolve relative to `@parcel/validator-eslint` rather than the user's project. This can reintroduce the same workspace/Yarn PnP ambiguity the issue describes (plugin trying to access a dependency not declared/available at its own location) and also prevents using the project's installed ESLint version. Consider either (a) keeping `options.packageManager.require('eslint', asset.filePath, ...)` so ESLint resolves from the project root, or (b) declaring `eslint` as a proper dependency/peerDependency of this package and documenting that Parcel will use the bundled version. Also note that `eslint` is CommonJS; `import eslint from 'eslint'` may require interop handling depending on the build setup (often safer: `import * as eslint from 'eslint'` or `const eslint = require('eslint')`).

Having `typescript` in both `devDependencies` and `peerDependencies` is potentially redundant and can be confusing for consumers. If the intent is that Parcel should use the project's TypeScript (to avoid bundling it and to satisfy Yarn PnP/workspaces), then `peerDependencies` is correct, but consider moving the local build/test requirement to `devDependencies` only in the monorepo root (or keep it here but add `peerDependenciesMeta` with `optional: true` if this validator can run without TS in some cases). Also ensure the package has a corresponding `peerDependencies` entry in whatever mechanism Parcel uses for auto-install (e.g. `parcelDependencies`) so the peer is actually installed when needed; otherwise this change may still fail under Yarn 2 PnP.

Importing `typescript` directly makes this plugin always depend on a hoisted/accessible `typescript` at runtime. That’s a behavior change from the previous `options.packageManager.require('typescript', asset.filePath, {shouldAutoInstall})`, which resolved relative to the project and could auto-install. In Yarn 2/PNP/workspaces this can break if `typescript` isn’t a direct dependency/peerDependency of this package or isn’t available from this package’s location. Consider either (a) keeping `options.packageManager.require` (or equivalent) so resolution happens relative to the user project, or (b) ensuring `typescript` is declared appropriately (likely as a peerDependency) and adding a clear error message when missing. Otherwise this may reintroduce the same class of “resolve relative to runtime” issues described in the bug report.

This lockfile change pins `@mdx-js/mdx@1.6.22` to depend on `@babel/core` **exactly** `7.12.9` (not a range). Given the reported workspace/Yarn 2 issue about Parcel runtimes trying to resolve `@babel/core` relative to their own package location, introducing another hard-pinned Babel core version can increase the chance of multiple `@babel/core` instances and resolution ambiguity under PnP/workspaces. Can we avoid pulling in a separate pinned Babel core here (e.g., by ensuring MDX is not part of the default install, or by aligning/hoisting to the same `@babel/core` version/range used by Parcel’s JS pipeline)? At minimum, please verify this change doesn’t reintroduce the PnP error by running the provided Yarn Berry workspace repro and a PnP install with MDX enabled.

Mutating `request.input` here is a bit risky because `input` is also spread into the request above, and `request.input` may be treated as immutable elsewhere (or reused across runs). Consider creating a new input object (e.g. `request = createAssetRequest({ ...input, invalidations, optionsRef: ... })`) rather than assigning `request.input.invalidations` after the fact. Also, the comment says these invalidations are used to compute the cache key, but the PR description indicates they *can't* be part of the cache key and are instead used to validate cached entries after reading—worth aligning the comment/behavior to avoid confusion.

This removes the `File` type import, which is fine only if there are no remaining Flow type references to `File` in this module after deleting `getIncludedFiles()`. Please double-check there are no other `File` annotations (including in comments or exported types) that would now become unresolved, and ensure callers have been migrated to the new included-files representation (e.g., file paths/hashes) so this API removal doesn’t break downstream code.

Removing `getIncludedFiles(): Array<File>` is a breaking API change for `CommittedAsset`. If this is intentional as part of the new included-file hashing/invalidation work, consider either (a) providing a replacement accessor (e.g. `getIncludedFilePaths()` / `getIncludedFiles()` returning the new shape) or (b) ensuring all internal/external call sites are updated in the same PR. Also, if `includedFiles` is still stored on `this.value`, leaving it without an accessor may encourage direct access; consider keeping an accessor that returns a stable, documented structure.

Clearing invalidations here changes semantics vs the previous behavior (it used to happen when a request node was invalidated). This is probably correct for “replace invalidations on re-run”, but please double-check that `clearInvalidations` removes *all* invalidation edge types (update/create/delete) and not just `invalidated_by_update`. Otherwise, stale create/delete invalidations could accumulate and cause unexpected rebuilds. Also consider whether this should happen only after a successful request run (so a failed run doesn’t wipe the last-known invalidation set), or ensure the request runner re-adds the previous invalidations on failure.

This writes the cache entry under `resultCacheKey` (which includes invalidations discovered during the transform), but the read earlier in this method uses `initialAssetCacheKey` (computed from `this.request.invalidations || []`). If those two invalidation sets differ (common when invalidations are only known after transforming), you may end up never hitting the cache you just wrote, causing repeated re-transforms and cache growth. Consider either (a) writing under the same key you read (`initialAssetCacheKey`) and validating invalidations after reading (as described in the PR), or (b) ensuring the subsequent reads use the same `resultCacheKey`/invalidationHash source so read/write keys are consistent.

This changes the API from `addIncludedFile(file: File)` to `addIncludedFile(filePath: FilePath)`, but `UncommittedAsset.fromResult` no longer consumes `result.includedFiles` (the loop was removed below). If any transformers still return `includedFiles` in their result, those invalidations will now be silently dropped, which could reintroduce the “leaf dependency changes not applied” bug for those transformers. Consider either (a) keeping backward compatibility by still reading `result.includedFiles` and calling `addIncludedFile(file.filePath)` here, or (b) ensuring all transformers were migrated to call `asset.addIncludedFile(...)` directly and removing/typing out `includedFiles` from `TransformerResult` so this can’t regress silently.

Using `options.env[invalidation.key] || ''` will treat valid falsy values (e.g. `0`, `false`) as empty and can cause missed invalidations or incorrect hash stability. Prefer nullish coalescing to only default when the env var is actually unset: `(options.env[invalidation.key] ?? '')`. Also consider normalizing non-string values via `String(...)` to avoid implicit coercion differences.

This is a breaking public API change: `addIncludedFile` now takes a `FilePath` instead of a `File`. Please ensure all call sites (including external transformer plugins) are updated accordingly, and consider whether a compatibility shim/overload is needed (e.g. accept both `{filePath, hash}`-style objects and strings) to avoid silently breaking existing plugins. Also update Flow types/docs/changelog to reflect the new signature.

This now relies entirely on `invalidations` returned from `runTransform` rather than iterating `asset.includedFiles` / `asset.invalidateOnEnvChange`. Please ensure `invalidations` is always present (e.g. default to `[]` when older workers/transformers don’t return it) to avoid a runtime crash (`for..of` on `undefined`) and to preserve invalidation behavior. Also consider whether invalidations should be de-duplicated here (same file/env across multiple assets) to avoid registering the same invalidation many times.

Making `invalidations` optional on `AssetRequestInput` is a good incremental change, but it also makes it easy for callers to forget to pass invalidations and silently lose cache invalidation behavior (which is the core of this PR). Consider making this non-optional with a default empty array at construction sites, or at least ensure all request creation paths populate it (even as `[]`) so downstream logic can rely on it without extra null/undefined checks.

These tests previously constructed assets with `includedFiles: new Map()`, but that field is now removed from the test fixtures. Please ensure the underlying `Asset` type/defaults still initialize `includedFiles` (or its replacement) consistently, otherwise these tests may pass while masking a missing default in production code. If `includedFiles` was renamed (e.g. to a different structure that includes hashes), consider updating the test assets to include the new field explicitly where relevant, or add a focused test asserting the default value/shape so regressions in cache invalidation don’t slip through.

This test now asserts against `getInvalidations()` rather than `getIncludedFiles()`, which changes the semantics being tested (deduping included files vs. tracking invalidations). If the intent is to verify that repeated `addIncludedFile` calls dedupe to a single entry, consider either (a) keeping a dedicated assertion for included files (if that API still exists) or (b) explicitly asserting the invalidation list length/type AND that it dedupes (e.g. `assert.equal(asset.getInvalidations().length, 1)`) to make the behavior under test clear. Also ensure the expected object shape matches the new invalidation schema (e.g. if invalidations include additional fields like `hash`/`invalidateOnFileChange`, the test may become brittle or incomplete).

These assertions for `includedFiles` were removed. Given this PR is about invalidating cache when included files change, dropping the test coverage here may hide regressions. If the underlying API/shape changed (e.g., included files now tracked via file hashes or a different field), please update the expectation to the new representation rather than removing it entirely, and add/keep a test that verifies a change in an included file (e.g. `config.css`) actually invalidates and updates the output.

This test assertion removed `includedFiles` entirely. Given the PR is about invalidating caches when included files change, it would be good to keep coverage here by updating the expectation to the new representation (e.g., if `includedFiles` moved/renamed, now stores hashes, or is tracked via a different node type). Otherwise we risk losing a regression test for Pug include dependency tracking/invalidation.

These assertions for `includedFiles` were removed, but this PR is specifically about invalidating caches when included files change. Dropping the checks risks losing coverage for the regression (e.g. Sass @import leaf changes not triggering correct rebuild). Consider updating the expected `includedFiles` shape to the new representation (e.g. if it now stores hashes on `File` nodes rather than a per-bundle map) rather than removing the assertion entirely, or add a new assertion that verifies included-file tracking/invalidation via the new mechanism.

This change removes the `includedFiles` assertions entirely. Given the PR is about invalidating caches when included files change, dropping these expectations may reduce coverage and could let regressions slip (especially around tracking TS/TSX dependency files like lib.d.ts and referenced modules). If the included-files representation changed (e.g., now stored as hashes on File nodes rather than a plain list), consider updating the test to assert the new behavior/shape instead of removing it—e.g., verify that included file nodes/hashes are recorded, or add a test that modifying an included file triggers a rebuild and updates output.

This change removes `includedFiles` from the `assertBundles` helper entirely. Given the PR is about invalidating caches when included files change, dropping this assertion reduces test coverage for the exact behavior being fixed (e.g. SCSS imports / included file tracking). Consider keeping `includedFiles` support here (or adding a new dedicated assertion helper) so tests can verify that assets report correct `getIncludedFiles()` and that changes to those files trigger invalidation/rebuilds as expected.

This changes the public API from `addIncludedFile(file: File)` to `addIncludedFile(filePath: FilePath)`. Please ensure this is consistent with how included files are tracked elsewhere in the PR (e.g., if `File` nodes now store hashes, the hashing must be computed from the path at commit time). Also verify all transformer implementations and type definitions are updated accordingly—any existing callers passing `{filePath, hash}`-like objects will now silently break at runtime (Flow will catch in-repo, but external plugins may not). If external plugin compatibility is a concern, consider keeping an overload/alias or documenting the breaking change in the changelog.

This changes the `addIncludedFile` call signature from an object to a string. Please double-check that `asset.addIncludedFile` in the current Parcel core API expects a plain `filePath` (string) here, and that other transformers/plugins have been updated consistently. If the API still supports/needs options (e.g. `{filePath, ...}`), this will silently drop metadata and could break invalidation behavior. Consider adding/keeping the object form if backward compatibility is required, or update all call sites + types to avoid mixed usage.

This changes the `addIncludedFile` call signature from an object to a string. Please double-check that `asset.addIncludedFile` in the current Parcel core actually accepts a plain `filePath` string in all supported versions/paths (and that Flow/TS types are updated accordingly). If other transformers/plugins still pass `{filePath}` objects, this could introduce an inconsistent API usage or runtime breakage. If the new API is intended, consider updating any remaining call sites and adding a small regression test to ensure included-file invalidation works end-to-end (since this PR is about cache invalidation on included file changes).

This changes the call signature from `addIncludedFile({filePath})` to `addIncludedFile(filePath)`. Please double-check that `asset.addIncludedFile` in the current Parcel core API actually accepts a string here (and that Flow/TS types for `Asset` are updated accordingly). If both forms are supported, consider keeping the object form for backward compatibility with older core versions, or add a small compatibility shim to avoid runtime errors when this transformer is used with a mismatched Parcel core version.

This changes the call signature from `addIncludedFile({filePath})` to `addIncludedFile(filePath)`. Please double-check the `Asset#addIncludedFile` API across the codebase: other transformers may still be passing an object, and if the method still expects `{filePath}` this will silently break invalidation tracking for included files. If the API was intentionally changed to accept a string, consider updating all call sites consistently and adding a small regression test ensuring included file changes invalidate the transform cache (relevant to the SCSS/import invalidation bug).

This changes the call signature from `addIncludedFile({filePath})` to `addIncludedFile(filePath)`. Please double-check that `asset.addIncludedFile` now consistently accepts a string across the codebase (and that Flow/TS types, if any, were updated). If other transformers still pass an object, this will create inconsistent usage and potential runtime errors depending on the implementation. Also consider whether `render.dependencies` can include non-file entries (e.g. virtual/URL) and whether `addIncludedFile` expects absolute paths—if so, normalize/resolve here to ensure cache invalidation works reliably.

This changes the `addIncludedFile` call from passing an object to passing a string. Please double-check the `Asset#addIncludedFile` API across the Parcel version range you support (and Flow/TS typings, if any). If the method still expects `{filePath, ...}` in some contexts, this will silently break invalidation tracking for Sass imports. If the API was intentionally updated to accept a string, consider adding a brief comment or updating nearby code/tests to ensure included file hashing/invalidation works for `result.stats.includedFiles` (which may include non-file entries like `stdin` or virtual paths depending on the Sass implementation).

`addIncludedFile` is being called with a string now. Please double-check the API contract across Parcel core/other transformers: some call sites historically used `{filePath}` objects. If the method still accepts both, consider keeping the object form for backward compatibility, or update all call sites consistently. Also, if `addIncludedFile` now supports options (e.g. `invalidateOnStartup` / `hash` tracking), passing only the path may skip those defaults—worth confirming this still records the included file in a way that participates in the new cache invalidation logic for nested Stylus deps.

This conditional bypasses the normal `streamTextContent(params)` path for XFA, but it ignores `params` entirely and changes the return behavior (no streaming, potentially different normalization/disableCombineTextItems options). If this is intentional, consider (1) documenting that `getTextContent` params are not supported for XFA, or (2) plumbing relevant params into `XfaText.textContent(...)` to keep API parity. Also, relying on the private `_transport._htmlForXfa` flag is brittle; if possible, prefer a public/explicit capability (e.g. `this._transport.isXfa`/`this._pdfInfo.isXfa`) or at least guard for `getXfa` availability and add error handling so failures reject consistently with the non-XFA path.

Pushing raw Text nodes into `textDivs` makes the name a bit misleading (they’re not divs) and may confuse downstream consumers (e.g., code expecting `HTMLElement`s). Consider renaming to something like `textNodes` (or `textContentItems`) and documenting the expected type. Also, ensure this collection is only used for searchable/highlightable text: here all `#text` nodes are included unconditionally, which may capture whitespace/formatting-only nodes and affect search offsets; you may want to filter (e.g., skip empty/whitespace-only nodes) or apply the same `XfaText.shouldBuildText(...)` gating used below for element values.

Typo in the doc comment: "compaitble" → "compatible". Also consider clarifying the wording to match existing pdf.js terminology (e.g., "regular PDF's TextContent"), since this is public-facing documentation for the helper.

This waits for `#viewFind` to be visible, but you just clicked that same element. If the intent is to wait for the findbar UI to open, this should likely wait for the findbar container (e.g. `#findbar` / `#findbarInputContainer` depending on the viewer) or for `#findInput` to become visible/enabled. As written, the wait is probably a no-op and could make the test flaky if the findbar opening is async and `#findInput` isn't ready yet when `page.type` runs.

The condition change from `!PDFJSDev.test("PRODUCTION")` to `PDFJSDev.test("!PRODUCTION || TESTING")` is a semantic shift that may unintentionally enable XFA in additional builds (e.g., any non-PRODUCTION build, plus explicitly TESTING). Please double-check the intended build-flag logic and whether `PDFJSDev.test` supports boolean expressions with `!`/`||` in this context. If the goal is “enable in non-production or testing”, consider adding a short comment explaining why, and/or keep the previous simpler predicate if equivalent. Also ensure this stays consistent with the later override that forces `enableXfa` to `false` under the `if (...) { ... }` block below, to avoid confusing/contradictory defaults across build targets.

Passing `textHighlighterFactory: this` unconditionally may be problematic when `isPureXfa` is true and `findController` is expected to be null/unused (or when text layers are disabled). Consider aligning this with the `textLayerFactory` gating (e.g., only provide a highlighter factory when search/highlighting is supported for the current document type/mode), or ensure downstream code safely ignores/doesn't instantiate a highlighter when there is no text layer / no findController.

The new `highlighter` parameter is added after `eventBus`, but the signature change can easily break call sites if they still pass the old argument list. Please ensure all invocations of `createTextLayerBuilder` are updated accordingly (and consider giving `highlighter` a default value, e.g. `highlighter = null`, to keep backward compatibility and avoid `undefined` handling in `TextLayerBuilder`).

`createTextHighlighter` always creates a `TextHighlighter` even when `this.isInPresentationMode` disables the `findController`. If `TextHighlighter` assumes a non-null `findController`, this could lead to no-op work or errors. Consider short-circuiting here (return `null`) when `this.isInPresentationMode` or when `this.findController` is absent, and make downstream code handle a null highlighter.

Calling `getTextContent()` here can be expensive and may duplicate work already done for the text layer / find controller. Also, for XFA the mapping between `result.textDivs` and `text.items` may not be 1:1 (ordering/whitespace/hidden nodes), which could lead to incorrect highlighting. Consider (1) using `getTextContent({ disableNormalization: true })` if normalization breaks mapping, (2) reusing cached text content if available, and/or (3) adding a guard to ensure `textDivs.length` matches the derived `items` length (or otherwise handle mismatches) before calling `setTextMapping`.

Potentially unsafe assumption about `textDivs[n]` always being an Element. In `_renderMatches` you may wrap a text node (nodeType === 3) into a newly created `<span>` and replace `textDivs[divIdx]` with that span, but other indices can still be text nodes (or other non-Element nodes) depending on the XFA mapping. Here you unconditionally do `div.className = ""`, which will throw for Text nodes. Consider normalizing `textDivs` up-front (ensure all entries are Elements), or guard here (e.g. `if (div.nodeType === 1) div.className = "";`), and/or use `div.parentElement` when `nodeType === 3`. Also, if `textDivs[n]` can be null/undefined due to mapping mismatches, this will crash; a defensive check with a clear error would help.

Potential ordering issue: `setTextMapping(this.textDivs, this.textContentItemsStr)` is called right after `this.textDivs = []`, but before `renderTextLayer` populates `textDivs`/`textContentItemsStr`. If `TextHighlighter` expects the arrays to be fully populated (or uses their current length to build internal mappings), it may end up with an empty/incorrect mapping. Consider moving `setTextMapping` to after rendering completes (e.g. in `_finishRendering()` or right before `highlighter.enable()`), or ensure `TextHighlighter` can handle being given mutable arrays that will be filled later.

Using `position: initial` is a bit risky/unclear for compatibility and intent: `initial` resets to the CSS initial value (`static`), which may not be what you want if the element inherits/depends on other positioning rules. If the goal is simply to cancel a `position: absolute/relative` applied elsewhere, consider using `position: static` explicitly (clearer and more widely understood), or document why `initial` is required here.

The JSDoc says the first render returns an object with a `textDivs` property, but this method now returns whatever `XfaLayer.update/render` returns. Please ensure `XfaLayer.render()` actually returns that object (and `update()` returns `void`/a consistent shape), or adjust the JSDoc to match reality. Also consider using a more specific type than `Object` (e.g. `{ textDivs: Array<HTMLElement> }`) to make the contract clear for `TextHighlighter`.

Returning `Promise.resolve()` inside a `.then` is redundant and slightly obscures intent; `return;` already results in a resolved promise for the chain. If the goal is to satisfy the updated return type, consider returning `undefined` explicitly or documenting that cancellation resolves to `void` (and keep `return;`).

Re-emitting `'ready'` with a cached `launchInfoEventPayload` can be fragile: if the original `ready` event never fired (or fired before the listener was attached / with `undefined` payload), this will emit `ready` with `undefined`, which may break apps that expect a valid `launchInfo` object. Consider guarding this (e.g., only emit if payload is defined, or fall back to emitting without args), and ensure the listener is attached early enough for all Electron versions. Also, double-check that re-emitting `ready` after `require(appPath)` won’t cause double-ready side effects for apps that already observed the original `ready` (e.g., if `__playwright_run` is invoked after the app is already ready).

This log line runs at module evaluation time, which is good for capturing the pre-ready state, but it can be flaky if Electron has already transitioned to ready before this script is required (e.g., depending on how/when the entrypoint is loaded). Consider also logging `process.uptime()`/`Date.now()` or similar to help diagnose ordering, and ensure the test harness loads this file as the very first app script so `app.isReady()` here is meaningful.

This reads the log immediately after launch, but the PR is about ensuring the Electron `ready` event is consistently emitted after the app is loaded. As written, this assertion can be flaky if `__playwrightLog` is populated asynchronously and the evaluate happens before `whenReady` resolves. Consider explicitly waiting for readiness (e.g., `await electronApp.evaluate(({ app }) => app.whenReady())` or polling until `__playwrightLog` contains the expected terminal entry like `isReady == true`) before reading/asserting the full array, to make the test deterministic across slower CI machines.

There’s a likely typo/bug here: `true!` is a non-null assertion on a boolean literal and is unnecessary (and looks accidental). It should just be `true` like the other call sites. Also, for consistency with the other CSS attribute candidates in this block, consider using `quoteAttributeValue(...)` instead of `escapeForAttributeSelector(..., true)` unless there’s a specific reason to keep the latter for test id attributes.

This uses `page.frameLocator('[title="hello world"]')` to perform the action, but the assertion expects codegen to emit `page.frameLocator('iframe[title="hello world"]')`. That mismatch is fine if the recorder normalizes to an `iframe[...]` selector, but it also makes the test less direct for the reported bug (avoiding `internal:` selectors). Consider asserting explicitly that the generated selector does **not** contain `internal:` (e.g. `expect(...).not.toContain('internal:')`) in addition to (or instead of) relying on the exact `iframe[...]` prefix, which may change with selector generation heuristics.

Minor docs clarity: consider explicitly stating that the percentage is relative to the machine's logical CPU cores and that the computed value is rounded (e.g., floored) to an integer worker count. Also, the example currently uses a trailing period after the code span (`'50%'.`); you may want to drop the period or move it outside the code formatting for consistency with other docs.

This truthy check will skip valid values like `0` (and potentially `''` if types loosen), meaning a project cannot explicitly set `workers: 0` (or other falsy values) to override the global setting. Prefer an explicit undefined/null check, e.g. `projectConfig.workers !== undefined ? resolveWorkers(projectConfig.workers) : undefined;` (and consider whether `null` should be treated as “unset”).

This change moves `workers` validation from the global config to per-project, but it also removes validation for `config.workers` entirely. If global `workers` is still supported (as a default for projects), we should keep the original `validateConfig` check as well, otherwise invalid values in `config.workers` will no longer be caught early. Consider validating both `config.workers` and `project.workers` (and ensuring the precedence/merge logic elsewhere is consistent).

This check will skip valid values like `0` (which might be used to effectively disable running a project) and also treats any falsy value the same as "unset". Consider using an explicit check (`project.workers !== undefined`) and validating the value (e.g. non-negative integer). Also, if `workers` can be `0`, the later scheduling logic currently treats `0` as "no limit" because it checks `if (projectIdWorkerLimit)`; that should likely be `if (projectIdWorkerLimit !== undefined)` to make `0` meaningful.

Consider making this a getter (e.g. `get projectId(): string`) and explicitly typing the return value for clarity/consistency with other accessors. Also, if `projectId` can ever be undefined in `_params`, it may be worth asserting or documenting that invariant here to avoid propagating an optional value into scheduling logic.

Type should likely mirror `testConfig.workers` exactly (including any existing alias/type like `Workers` or `number | string` with a specific percentage-string format). If the config supports only integer counts and percentage strings, consider tightening this to `number | `${number}%`` (or reusing the existing exported type) to prevent arbitrary strings. Also consider documenting/validating edge cases (e.g. `0`, negative, non-integer numbers, `'0%'`) and whether `workers` is allowed in `--debug`/`--ui` modes where workers may be forced to 1.

This assertion relies on timing (setTimeout with 1–3s) to infer scheduling/order, which can be flaky on slow/loaded CI machines. Consider making the test deterministic by using an explicit barrier/handshake (e.g., a shared file/IPC via fs, or a promise controlled by the test runner) to ensure both "begin" logs are emitted before allowing either test to complete, and avoid depending on relative sleep durations for ordering.

This error message uses the full `selector` string when building `locator`, but the violation is detected while iterating `chunk`s. If the composite locator contains multiple branches (e.g. `internal:or`) it may be clearer to report the specific offending sub-selector (e.g. `stringifySelector(chunk)` or the nested part) rather than the whole original selector. Also consider guarding `asLocator(...)` in case it throws for some internal selector forms—otherwise we may mask the intended `InvalidSelectorError` with a different exception. A safe fallback could be to include `stringifySelector(selector)` when locator generation fails.

Switching from `allEngineNames(result)` to `visitAllSelectorParts` changes the validation granularity from “unique engine names” to “every selector part”. That’s fine, but please ensure `visitAllSelectorParts` actually traverses *all* nested parts (e.g. `internal:or`, `internal:and`, `has`, `has-not`, `nth`, etc.) so we don’t miss unknown engines inside nested bodies. Also consider whether this will now throw multiple times for the same unknown engine (once per occurrence); if so, it may be worth deduping for a cleaner error and to avoid extra work on large selectors.

This import change looks inconsistent with the rest of the file: `allEngineNames` was replaced by `visitAllSelectorParts`, but `allEngineNames` is no longer imported. Please double-check that `allEngineNames` is not used elsewhere in this file (or in adjacent hunks) and remove it if unused, or keep it if still referenced. Also, ensure `visitAllSelectorParts` is exported from `selectorParser` in all build targets (isomorphic) and that the import ordering/style matches the project conventions (there’s a formatting oddity in the diff context with `:import`).

Switching from iterating `allEngineNames(parsed)` to `visitAllSelectorParts(parsed, ...)` changes semantics: you now validate/mark `needsMainWorld` per selector part rather than per unique engine name. That’s likely fine, but please confirm `visitAllSelectorParts` traverses *all* nested parts (including `internal:or`, `internal:and`, `has`, etc.) so we don’t miss engines inside composite selectors. Also consider whether repeated parts could cause unnecessary work; if performance matters, you might want to dedupe engine names while visiting.

This logic removes the common frame prefix from the nested selector, but it no longer throws when frames are present inside nested selectors with a *different* frame chain. That means `internal:or`/`and`/`has` could now silently accept mismatched frame locators (or fail later in a less clear way). Consider keeping an explicit validation: if `lastFrameIndex !== -1` and the prefix is NOT equal, throw `InvalidSelectorError` with a readable message that includes `part.name` and the nested locator (e.g. `stringifySelector(nested.body.parsed)`), matching the PR description (“improve the error message and include the locator”).

The expected error string looks like it’s missing a closing quote after the locator text (it ends with `...or(frameLocator('#iframe').locator('span'))` but doesn’t include the terminating `"`). If the implementation includes the full quoted locator (as the message suggests: `while querying "..."`), this assertion may be brittle/off-by-one. Consider either including the closing quote in the expected substring or asserting on smaller, stable parts (e.g. the prefix + the locator without relying on exact quoting) to avoid test flakiness if formatting changes slightly.

A couple of concerns with this rewrite:

1) `process.env.GITHUB_ACTION` looks like a typo; GitHub Actions sets `GITHUB_ACTIONS` (plural) as the standard boolean env var. As written, this will almost always be falsy and the message won’t include the intended hint.

2) This branch overwrites `error.logs` entirely, which may hide the original Firefox startup output that could be useful for debugging. Consider appending/prepending the boxed message while preserving the original logs (or only rewriting when you’re confident it’s the ownership mismatch case).

3) The workaround text is very specific (`HOME=/root`). In some setups the correct fix is to ensure `$HOME` is owned by the current user or to set `HOME` to a writable/owned directory. You may want to phrase it more generally (e.g., “set HOME to a directory owned by the current user (often /root in the official container)”) to avoid misleading users outside the official image.

Also, the match string is very exact; if Firefox changes punctuation/wording, this won’t trigger. If possible, match a more stable substring or use a regex.

Widening the parameter type from `HTMLElement` to `Element` makes sense if you need to append non-HTML elements (e.g., SVG/defs/clipPath) for the recorder icons. However, please consider whether `Element` is too broad for this API: `appendChild` will accept `DocumentFragment`, `Text`, etc., but your signature now excludes those while still allowing `SVGElement` (good). If the intent is specifically “any DOM element”, `Element` is fine; otherwise consider `Node` to match the DOM API and avoid future friction. Also check for any call sites that relied on `HTMLElement`-specific properties (shouldn’t compile-break here, but it may hide incorrect assumptions).

Moving the shared `clipPaths` SVG defs to `this.highlight` makes sense for iframes, but please ensure this is idempotent across `install()`/`recreate()` cycles. `install()` can be called multiple times (e.g. navigation / reinjection), and this line will append a new SVG each time, potentially creating duplicate `<defs>`/`id` collisions and unnecessary DOM growth. Consider guarding (e.g. query for an existing marker element/id) or replacing the existing defs instead of always appending.

This assertion is brittle across browsers/engines: `getComputedStyle(...).clipPath` can serialize differently (e.g. `url(#icon-check)` vs `url("#icon-check")`, or even `none` depending on SVG/CSS implementation). Consider loosening the check to something like `toContain('icon-check')` or using a regex, and/or asserting the actual `<clipPath>` reference via DOM attributes (e.g. `clip-path` style/attribute) rather than exact computed string. Also, `evaluate` will throw if the locator resolves to 0 elements; if that's intended, fine, but otherwise you may want an explicit `toBeVisible()`/`toHaveCount(1)` before evaluating to improve failure diagnostics.

`boundingBox()` can return `null` (e.g. element not visible/attached), which would make `box.x` throw. Since this helper is used for “trusted” moves in tests, consider asserting `box` is non-null with a clear error (or waiting for visibility) before using it, e.g. `expect(box, '...').not.toBeNull()` or `await locator.waitFor({ state: 'visible' })` prior to reading the box.

The docs say the step timeout defaults to `0` (no timeout). Please double-check this matches the actual runtime behavior and how it interacts with the test timeout and the new `separateStepTimeouts` option described in the issue/PR. If step timeouts are independent only when `separateStepTimeouts: true`, consider documenting that here (or linking to the relevant option) to avoid implying that a step can always exceed the test timeout. Also consider clarifying whether the timeout includes nested steps/fixtures and what error is thrown on timeout.

Using `setTimeout` + `Promise.race` will reject the step, but it does not cancel/interrupt `body()`; the step callback may keep running in the background and potentially mutate state after the step is already marked failed. Is that acceptable for Playwright semantics? If not, consider wiring this into the existing test timeout/cancellation mechanism (e.g., via an AbortSignal/timeout manager) so the step can cooperatively abort, or at least document that step timeouts are non-canceling.

Also, `setTimeout` returns a `NodeJS.Timeout` in Node and a `number` in browsers; since this is TS in a Node context it’s probably fine, but `timer` is `undefined | ReturnType<typeof setTimeout>` to avoid type issues. Finally, the error message could include the step title for easier debugging and should probably use a consistent error type/message format with other timeouts in the runner.

Adding `timeout?: number` to the `test.step` options is fine, but the type should also document the semantics: is this timeout independent from the test timeout or does it consume the test timeout budget? The issue/PR mentions `separateStepTimeouts`, but that option is not reflected here—if it exists elsewhere, consider linking/mentioning it in the JSDoc for `step` and/or reusing a shared `StepOptions` type to avoid drift across overloads/implementations. Also clarify units (ms) and behavior for `0`/negative values (disable vs immediate timeout).

Please remove this debug logging from the test. Printing `result.output` will add noise to the test suite output and can make failures harder to triage. The subsequent assertion already validates the relevant message; if you need extra diagnostics, consider including `result.output` in the assertion failure message instead of unconditional `console.log`.

Adding `timeout?: number` to `test.step` options makes sense, but the feature request also mentions `separateStepTimeouts` (step timeouts independent from the test timeout). This type change alone doesn’t expose that capability, and it’s unclear how `timeout` interacts with the overall test timeout (e.g., does it cap the step within remaining test time, or can it extend total runtime?). Consider documenting/typing the intended semantics (possibly via a dedicated `StepOptions` type with JSDoc) and ensuring the public API also includes any required test-level toggle (e.g., `test.setTimeout(..., { separateStepTimeouts: true })`) if that’s part of the design. Also confirm whether `timeout` should accept `0`/`undefined` to disable, and whether it should be `number | undefined` only or allow `null` (usually not).

Wrapping is good for consistent debug logging, but note that `failure()` is often called in error/cleanup paths. Ensure `_wrapApiCall` does not itself throw or mask the original failure state if logging/telemetry fails, and that it preserves the original stack/exception semantics. If `_wrapApiCall` can add overhead, consider whether this method should be excluded or use a lightweight wrapper.

Inside the `_wrapApiCall` callback, `return this.waitForEvent('window')` will likely produce its own nested API log entry (since `waitForEvent` is typically wrapped too). That can lead to duplicate/noisy logs for a single `firstWindow()` call. Consider using an internal/non-logged wait helper here (if available) or passing a flag to suppress nested logging, so `firstWindow` produces a single coherent log entry.

Wrapping is good for consistent debug logging, but consider whether the API name should match the public surface. Since this is `FileChooser.setFiles`, the log label might be better as `fileChooser.setFiles` (as you have) only if other FileChooser methods use the same prefix/casing. Please double-check consistency across the codebase (e.g., `page.*`, `elementHandle.*`) so logs are searchable and uniform. Also, ensure `_page` is always available on `FileChooser` instances; if there are any code paths where `_page` could be undefined (e.g., constructed with a detached handle), this would throw before calling `setInputFiles`.

`waitForTimeout` previously returned a `Promise<void>` implicitly; wrapping it is fine, but consider explicitly returning/awaiting the inner promise to keep intent clear and ensure the wrapper correctly propagates completion/errors. E.g. `return this._wrapApiCall(..., () => new Promise(...))` or `return this._wrapApiCall(..., async () => { await ...; return; })`. Also double-check that `_wrapApiCall` is appropriate for a pure timer (no channel call) and that it won't add unnecessary overhead/log noise for a frequently-used utility.

Wrapping `evaluate` is good for consistent API logging, but consider whether the log name should match the public API surface (`JSHandle.evaluate`) rather than a lowercased internal prefix (`jsHandle.evaluate`). If other wrappers use a specific naming convention, align with it to keep logs searchable/consistent. Also be mindful that `String(pageFunction)` can be very large; if `_wrapApiCall` logs arguments by default, ensure it does not inadvertently dump the full function source into debug logs (or that it truncates/sanitizes).

`Route.abort` is declared as `async abort(...)` (i.e. returns `Promise<void>`), but now returns the result of `_wrapApiCall(...)`. This is fine only if `_wrapApiCall` is typed to return `Promise<void>` for this callback; otherwise it can widen the return type (e.g. `Promise<any>`) and weaken type safety. Consider adding an explicit return type (`: Promise<void>`) and/or ensuring `_wrapApiCall` is generically typed to preserve the callback's return type.

Consider including some logging even when `urlOrPredicate` is a `RegExp` or function predicate. As written, `trimUrl` only handles strings, so `logLine` becomes `undefined` for regex/predicate cases—exactly where debug output is often most useful. Suggest enhancing `trimUrl` to stringify `RegExp` (e.g. `param.toString()`) and provide a generic label for function predicates (e.g. `'<predicate>'` or `param.name || '<anonymous predicate>'`), possibly with truncation similar to the string case.

This will emit every Waiter log to the global `api` debug channel, which may be noisy and potentially duplicate existing `wrapApiCall` logging. Consider whether this should be gated (e.g., only when `debugLogger.isEnabled('api')`/similar) or use a more specific channel (e.g., `waiter`) to avoid flooding `api` logs. Also ensure `Waiter.log()` messages don’t include sensitive data (headers/tokens) since this now routes to debug output.

Wrapping `evaluate` in `_wrapApiCall` is good for consistent debug logging, but consider whether the log message should include any useful context (e.g., whether `pageFunction` is a function vs. string, or a truncated expression) to aid debugging. Also double-check that `_wrapApiCall` preserves the original stack/exception semantics for `evaluate` (errors thrown from `parseResult` should still be attributed to `worker.evaluate` and not lose relevant frames). If `_wrapApiCall` supports passing metadata, it may be worth adding it here.

This assertion is indirectly testing the logging/formatting via the TimeoutError message, but the test name says "should log the url". Consider either (a) renaming the test to reflect what it actually verifies (e.g. "should include the url in the waitForRequest log/message"), or (b) asserting against the actual debug log output (if the harness supports capturing Playwright debug logs). Also, this uses a hard-coded truncation character ("…") and a very long literal; that can be brittle across platforms/formatting changes. Prefer constructing the long URL programmatically and asserting on a more stable substring (e.g. the prefix before truncation) or using a regex that tolerates truncation differences.

This assertion checks the TimeoutError message, not the debug log output. If the feature request is about adding debug logs, consider capturing Playwright debug logs (e.g., via a logger hook/trace/DEBUG=pw:api) and asserting on the emitted log line instead of (or in addition to) the error message. Otherwise this test will pass even if no debug logging is produced, and it also makes the error message format part of the public contract (potentially brittle across versions/locales).

Consider clarifying behavior for remotely connected browsers and for already-deleted/missing files. The PR description mentions `video.path()` throws when remotely connected; does `video.delete()` also work remotely (it says it does) and what happens if the file is already deleted? Documenting whether it is idempotent (no-op) vs throws would help users rely on it safely.

`mkdirIfNeeded(path)` looks like it might be receiving the full file path, not the directory. If `mkdirIfNeeded` expects a directory, this could accidentally create a directory named like the target file (or fail on some platforms). Consider calling it with `path.dirname(path)` (and ensure `mkdirIfNeeded` semantics are clear), e.g. `await mkdirIfNeeded(dirname(path));`.

This registers a `'video'` channel event but the handler is a no-op. That can be confusing (and may hide missing functionality) since it suggests the client is expected to react to video artifacts. If the intent is to keep the subscription for future API surface, consider either (a) removing the listener until it’s needed, (b) wiring it to an internal collection/emit an event (e.g. `BrowserContext.Video` / `artifact` tracking) so it’s observable, or (c) at least add a TODO with a concrete follow-up issue reference. Also ensure the server side actually emits `'video'` for all relevant cases; otherwise this listener is dead code.

Since this change removes the explicit `Download`/`VideoImpl` imports and channel-owner mappings in favor of `Artifact`, please double-check that all former `Download`/`Video` channel types are now actually emitted as `Artifact` by the server side (or otherwise mapped) for every transport (local + remote). If the server still sends `type: 'Download'` or `type: 'Video'` for some versions/paths, the client will now throw on unknown type. Consider keeping backward-compatible cases (e.g. handle 'Download'/'Video' by instantiating `Artifact` or aliasing) if mixed client/server versions are supported.

This changes `download.path()` semantics: it now delegates to `artifact.pathAfterFinished()` and no longer throws for remote connections. Given the PR description explicitly says `video.path()` should throw when remotely connected, consider whether `download.path()` should keep the previous remote-guard (throw with guidance to use `saveAs`) for consistency and to avoid returning a misleading/unsupported local path in remote mode. If `Artifact.pathAfterFinished()` already handles remote by throwing, please ensure the error message matches the previous one (or is at least equally actionable) and add a test to cover remote `download.path()` behavior.

This sets a private/internal flag on the Artifact instance from outside the Artifact class. That’s brittle (relies on internal fields) and easy to miss for other Artifact creation sites. Consider moving the “remote-ness” propagation into `Artifact.from(...)` (or a dedicated initializer like `artifactObject._setRemote(isRemote)`), so all artifacts consistently know whether they are remote without Page needing to poke private state. Also, the remote check `!!this._browserContext._browser && this._browserContext._browser._isRemote` is a bit awkward—if `_browser` can be undefined, prefer optional chaining for clarity (`!!this._browserContext._browser?._isRemote`).

The error message now says `Use saveAs()` but the public API is `video.saveAs(...)`. Consider making the message explicit (`Use video.saveAs()`) to avoid ambiguity, and keep it consistent with the previous wording and docs. Also, if this is meant to be a breaking change in messaging, ensure other places (e.g. docs/tests) are updated accordingly.

`stream()` now uses `localPathAfterFinished()`, which will return `null`/`undefined` until the artifact is finalized. For downloads this is probably fine, but for videos (the main motivation of this PR) callers may expect to be able to stream while the page is still open. Consider either (a) documenting that `stream()` is only available after finish, (b) renaming to `streamAfterFinished()` for consistency with `pathAfterFinished()`, or (c) using a different API that supports streaming the in-progress artifact if that's intended. Otherwise this will silently return `{}` and be hard to debug.

Iterating over `context._browser._idToVideo.values()` reaches into a private/internal map and couples the dispatcher to Browser internals. This is brittle (e.g. if the storage changes or becomes per-context) and may also include videos from other contexts, requiring the extra filter. Consider exposing a context-level API/event to enumerate existing videos (e.g. `context._videos()` or `context._browser.videosForContext(context)`) so this logic stays encapsulated and doesn’t depend on `_idToVideo` implementation details.

Using `existingDispatcher` here assumes the `ArtifactDispatcher` has already been created for this `artifact` by the time the `Page.Events.Video` event fires. If that assumption can be violated (e.g. event emitted before dispatcher creation, or in some edge cases like remote connections / early video start), this will throw and break event delivery. Consider either (a) creating the dispatcher here via `ArtifactDispatcher.from(scope, artifact)` (or equivalent factory) to make the event robust, or (b) add an assertion/comment explaining the lifecycle guarantee and where the dispatcher is created. Also, ensure the dispatcher scope used matches the intended lifetime (video artifacts may need to outlive the page/context for saveAs/delete).

Adding a new `BrowserContext` event is fine, but please ensure this event is also wired up consistently across the protocol: (1) the dispatcher actually emits `video` on the context (not only on `Page`), (2) the client-side `BrowserContext` API exposes/handles this event (or it’s intentionally internal-only), and (3) the event name doesn’t conflict with existing semantics (there is already `PageVideoEvent`). If this is meant to be public, consider documenting it and adding it to the generated types/tests; if it’s internal, consider prefixing/keeping it out of the public surface to avoid accidental API commitment.

Renaming `path` to `pathAfterFinished` is a breaking protocol change. Please ensure all generated client/server bindings and any internal callers are updated accordingly, and consider whether we need a compatibility alias (keeping `path` as deprecated) to avoid breaking older drivers/clients. Also, the name suggests it may return null until completion—could we add/confirm documentation in the protocol comment that it only resolves/returns a value after the artifact is finished, and clarify behavior for remote connections (e.g., should it throw vs return null)?

This renames the protocol validators from Download* to Artifact* (and removes VideoSaveAs* earlier). Please double-check that the corresponding protocol/channel definitions were updated everywhere (client/server dispatchers and any generated protocol JSON) so we don't end up with mismatched method names at runtime. In particular, ensure any callers that previously used `DownloadPath`/`DownloadSaveAs` now call `ArtifactPathAfterFinished`/`ArtifactSaveAs`, and that backward compatibility is either intentionally broken (with a major bump) or handled via aliases.

Swallowing errors from `saveCallback` here (`.catch(e => {})`) can hide failures to persist the artifact (e.g. copy/write errors) and make debugging very hard. Consider either (a) returning/awaiting the promise so callers can observe failures, or (b) at least logging/propagating via a dedicated error handler. Also note that in `reportFinished()` you *do* await callbacks, so behavior differs depending on whether the artifact is already finished when `saveAs` is called.

Artifact likely needs the owning context (or a rootSdkObject) for proper lifecycle/telemetry and to support remote connections. Creating it as `new Artifact(path)` looks suspicious given the PR goal (“Works in remotely connected browser as well”) and the fact that downloads/videos were migrated to a common Artifact object. Consider constructing Artifact with the context (or passing a dispatcher/connection) so it can implement `saveAs/delete/path()` semantics correctly, and so events/cleanup are attributed to the right context.

This now waits on `artifact.finishedPromise()` for videos belonging to this context, which is good, but note that `_browser._idToVideo` is a global map and may retain entries for other contexts. Consider ensuring entries are removed when a video artifact is finished/closed to avoid unbounded growth and to prevent `close()` from iterating over stale artifacts. Also, if `finishedPromise()` can reject (e.g. video encoding failure), `Promise.all(promises)` will reject and potentially fail context close; if that's not desired, consider swallowing/handling errors here similarly to how downloads are handled elsewhere.

Using `gotFirstFrame.then(...)` without awaiting/returning it can lead to an unhandled rejection if `pageOrError()` rejects (e.g. page closes/tears down before first frame arrives). Consider attaching a `.catch(() => {})` (or routing to existing error handling/logging) to avoid noisy unhandled promise rejections. Also, if `_videoStarted` is expected to run before `_startScreencast` is considered complete, you may want to `await gotFirstFrame` (or `await gotFirstFrame.then(...)`) instead of fire-and-forget.

This error message is now computed from `acceptDownloads` and passed into `Artifact`, but the previous implementation also gated other operations on `_acceptDownloads` and returned a failure string from `failure()` when downloads were not accepted. Please double-check that `Artifact` preserves the same semantics (throw vs return message) for all public download APIs, and that the message is appropriate in all cases (e.g. remote connections vs local). Also consider whether this should be derived from a single source of truth (e.g. context option) inside `Artifact` to avoid duplicating policy logic across callers.

Calling `_markAsError(new Error('Page has been closed'))` only when `!this._initializedPage` is a bit surprising: a close after initialization can still leave pending init/async operations (including artifact/video wiring) that might need to be failed to avoid hangs.
