Consider whether `_markAsError` should be invoked unconditionally on `didClose()` (or at least also when there are pending promises), and ensure the error message is consistent with other browsers' delegates so consumers get uniform behavior.

This method was changed from `async` to sync. Please double-check all call sites: if any were doing `await page.reportAsNew()` or relying on it returning a Promise (e.g. for sequencing initialization), this becomes a silent behavior change. If the method is intentionally synchronous now, consider either (a) keeping it `async` for API stability (even if it doesn't `await`), or (b) updating call sites/tests accordingly and adding a brief comment explaining why it is safe to be sync.

Catching the error via `await ...catch(e => e)` makes the test less strict (it will also pass if `path()` unexpectedly resolves to a value, since `error.message` would then be `undefined` and the failure message is less clear). Prefer asserting the rejection explicitly, e.g. `await expect(page.video().path()).rejects.toThrow(/Path is not available.*Use saveAs\(\)/);` (or `rejects.toMatchObject({ message: expect.stringContaining(...) })`) to ensure `path()` actually rejects and to avoid relying on `.message` existing.

This assertion now expects a more generic message ('Use saveAs()...'), but it may be too ambiguous without the object name. Consider keeping the API name in the guidance (e.g. 'Use download.saveAs()') to make the error actionable and consistent with other Playwright errors. If the intent is to share the same message across Artifact-backed types, ensure the message still clearly indicates which method to call on which object.

Using a fixed 1s timeout makes the test slower and potentially flaky across environments. If the goal is to ensure at least one frame is recorded before closing the context, consider waiting on a deterministic signal (e.g., `page.waitForLoadState('domcontentloaded')`/`'networkidle'`, or a small `requestAnimationFrame`/`setTimeout(0)` in `page.evaluate`) or reduce the delay to the minimum needed. If the delay is required due to encoder startup, add a brief comment explaining why 1000ms is necessary.

The docstring says this is safe to call while the page is still open and will wait for the video to finish. Consider clarifying the contract here: does `delete()` wait for the artifact to be finalized and then delete, or does it cancel recording and delete the partial file? Also, since `video.path()` throws when connected remotely, it would be helpful to document whether `delete()` is supported in remote mode and what it deletes (remote-side artifact vs local file).

Since this is an intro doc snippet, consider adding a brief note (or linking) that `toHaveScreenshot` is auto-retrying and where the baseline image is stored/updated (e.g. `--update-snapshots`). Otherwise readers may not understand why this is preferred over `page.screenshot().toMatchSnapshot()` or how to regenerate the golden on first run/changes.

The indentation/formatting of this line looks inconsistent with the surrounding markdown (it lost the leading spaces before the colon). This may break the docs generator’s expected formatting for property descriptions. Consider keeping the original indentation (e.g. `  :The base directory...`) while updating the text to include `toHaveScreenshot`.

This doc line uses `expect(page).toHaveScreenshot(...)` without `await`, but `toHaveScreenshot` is an async web-first assertion (as described in the issue/PR) and should be awaited. Consider updating the example to `await expect(page).toHaveScreenshot(snapshotName)` to avoid misleading users and to match the recommended usage pattern.

This list of matchers now includes `toHaveScreenshot` but drops `toMatchSnapshot`, which is still supported and documented below. Consider keeping both in the introductory list (e.g. `toMatchSnapshot`, `toHaveScreenshot`) to avoid implying `toMatchSnapshot` was removed/deprecated.

The description for `omitBackground` looks incorrect/copy-pasted (it describes matching snapshots rather than omitting the default white background). Suggest updating to something like: "Hides default white background and allows capturing screenshots with transparency." (or whatever Playwright's actual behavior is) and keep the snapshot-matching description for the matcher itself, not this option.

This new locator example is useful, but it may be worth clarifying in the docs what the default screenshot behavior is for locators (e.g. whether it auto-waits for the element to be visible/stable, whether it scrolls into view, and whether it captures only the element bounding box). Without that, users may assume it behaves exactly like `locator.screenshot()` or `page.screenshot()` and get surprised by differences.

Adding `toHaveScreenshot` to the re-export list is fine, but please ensure the import list stays consistently formatted/sorted with the rest of the matchers (this block appears to be kept in numeric/commented order). Also verify that `./matchers/matchers` actually exports `toHaveScreenshot`; otherwise this will compile but fail at build time with a missing export error.

Registering `toHaveScreenshot` in `customMatchers` makes it available on `expect`, but consider whether it should be grouped near other screenshot/snapshot-related matchers (e.g. `toMatchSnapshot`) for readability/maintenance. Also double-check that any typings for `Expect`/matcher augmentation are updated elsewhere; adding it here alone may not expose correct TS intellisense/signatures.

The signature forces `nameOrOptions` to be present and typed as an intersection of snapshot-name/options and `PageScreenshotOptions`, but the implementation still checks `if (!nameOrOptions)` and throws. Consider making this parameter optional in the type (and/or providing overloads) to match runtime behavior and intended API (`toHaveScreenshot(name[, options])` and possibly `toHaveScreenshot(options)` if supported). Also, intersecting `Parameters<typeof toMatchSnapshot>[1]` with `PageScreenshotOptions` can produce awkward/overly-permissive types (e.g. allowing snapshot options where screenshot options are expected and vice versa). Prefer explicit overloads like `(pageOrLocator, name: string | string[], options?: PageScreenshotOptions & SnapshotOptions)` and `(pageOrLocator, options: PageScreenshotOptions & SnapshotOptions)` if you want to support name-less calls.

Using `Omit<ToMatchSnapshotOptions, 'name'>` for `optOptions` makes the call signature stricter, but it also changes behavior vs the previous `{ threshold?: number } = {}`: callers can no longer pass any other legacy/extra fields without a type error. If this matcher historically accepted additional options (even if ignored), consider keeping `optOptions` as `{ threshold?: number }` (or `Partial<ToMatchSnapshotOptions>` with runtime filtering) to avoid a breaking TS change. Also, since `optOptions` is spread into `options`, it might be worth explicitly picking `threshold` to prevent accidental future fields from being merged into `options` unintentionally.

Minor doc/style: the line break after “or” reads a bit awkwardly in generated docs. Consider keeping the phrase on one line (e.g. “created with `toMatchSnapshot` or `toHaveScreenshot`.”) or reflowing the sentence so it doesn’t split at “or”. Also ensure the new assertion name is consistently referenced elsewhere in the typings/docs (e.g. any other snapshot-related config comments) to avoid partial updates.

Consider importing `PageScreenshotOptions` as a type-only import to avoid generating a runtime import in the emitted JS (and to prevent potential module resolution issues for consumers). In a `.d.ts` this is usually fine, but using `import type { PageScreenshotOptions } from 'playwright-core';` is clearer and safer, especially if this file is ever used as a source for declaration emit.

Adding a new binary asset is fine, but please ensure it’s actually required for the new `toHaveScreenshot` tests and that its size is justified. Consider using a smaller/compressed image (or a PNG if lossless patterns are important) to keep repo bloat down. Also verify the file is referenced by tests in this PR and that any platform-dependent encoding/metadata won’t cause flaky comparisons (e.g., strip EXIF/metadata if present).

Good to forbid this cast, but consider improving the error message to include both source and target dtypes (and possibly the series name) for easier debugging, e.g. `cannot cast from Date to Time` / `cannot cast Series '<name>' from Date to Time`. Also, since this is a behavior change, ensure there are tests covering both `Date -> Time` and `Time -> Date` raising, and that the error type (`ComputeError`) is consistent with other invalid-cast paths in Polars (some use `InvalidOperation`/`SchemaMismatch`).

The error message suggests `dt.combine`, but that API may not be discoverable/available in all contexts (Series vs Expr) and might not be the exact name users expect. Consider either (a) using a more general hint (e.g. "combine with a date using `dt.combine`/`pl.datetime`"), (b) ensuring the referenced method name matches the public API for both eager/lazy, or (c) omitting the suggestion to avoid pointing users to a potentially non-existent/incorrect method. Also consider whether this should be a `InvalidOperation`/`SchemaMismatch`-style error (if available) rather than `ComputeError`, since this is a type-cast validation failure.

This hunk removes the explicit error for casting `Time -> Datetime` (the `polars_bail!(ComputeError: "cannot cast `Time` to `Datetime`; consider using 'dt.combine'")` arm). Given the PR goal is to forbid `Date <-> Time`, this change looks unrelated and may reintroduce a previously intentional guardrail. If `Time -> Datetime` should still be forbidden, please keep the bail arm (or add a test ensuring it errors). If the intent is to allow it now, it needs a clear rationale and tests, because the conversion semantics are ambiguous (what date/timezone is assumed?) and could silently produce surprising results.

This test was asserting the old (buggy) behavior that `Date -> Time` casts always yield `00:00:00`. Since the PR changes this to raise, we should replace this removed test with a new one that asserts an exception is raised (and ideally checks the error type/message). Otherwise we lose coverage for the regression and the intended breaking change. Consider adding something like `with pytest.raises(pl.exceptions.ComputeError, match="Date.*Time"):` around `pl.Series([...]).cast(pl.Time)` and similarly for `Time -> Date` if not already covered elsewhere.

Minor style/readability: `time(20, 00)` uses a redundant leading-zero literal for minutes. Consider `time(20, 0)` (or `time(20, 0, 0)`) to avoid any linting/formatting complaints and keep the test consistent with `time(0, 0)` above.

Changing the `apply_many_private` flag from `true` to `false` likely fixes the auto-explode behavior by ensuring the rolling_* expression is treated as list-producing in `group_by().agg(...)` rather than scalar-producing. However, this flag change can have broader semantics (e.g., whether the function is considered elementwise/returns scalar per group) and may affect other rolling-by expressions outside group-by. Please add/ensure a regression test that asserts `group_by().agg(pl.col(..).rolling_mean(.., by=..))` returns `list[...]` (no implicit explode), and also cover multi-row groups to confirm the list length matches the group size and ordering is stable. Additionally, consider verifying that non-grouped rolling-by behavior (and other rolling_* variants like sum/min/max/quantile) is unchanged by this flag flip.

This test only covers the trivial case where each group has a single row, so it would pass even if `rolling_mean` were incorrectly treated as a scalar agg (since the list would have length 1 either way). To actually guard against the auto-explode/scalar-agg regression, consider adding at least one group with multiple rows (and a `by` column spanning >1 value) and assert that the result is a list column with multiple elements per group (and correct rolling values). Also consider asserting the dtype is `List(Float64)` to ensure it doesn't silently explode.

Potential correctness issue: `conversion_function` is computed using the *current* `allow_object` value, but the LUT key is only `type_object_ptr`. This means the first time a type is seen will permanently cache either `get_object` (when `allow_object=true`) or an erroring path (when `allow_object=false`) for all future conversions of that type, regardless of the caller’s `allow_object` setting. That can lead to surprising behavior (e.g., a type first encountered in a permissive context later being accepted even when `allow_object=false`, or vice versa). Consider including `allow_object` (and possibly `strict` if it affects selection) in the cache key, or avoid caching the fallback/object decision and only cache deterministic conversions; alternatively, store both variants or compute per-call when not in LUT.

This change hard-codes the new third argument to `true`, which (based on the PR description) likely corresponds to `allow_object`. In dataframe construction from dicts, enabling `allow_object` unconditionally can silently coerce unsupported/unknown Python values into `Object` instead of erroring, which may weaken `strict` semantics and hide user mistakes. Consider threading an explicit `allow_object`/`strict` policy here (e.g., `allow_object = !strict` or a separate parameter), or at least document why dict->rows should always allow object literals. Also ensure all other call sites of `py_object_to_any_value` were updated consistently and that tests cover the intended behavior (temporal subclasses) without broadening acceptance of arbitrary objects in strict mode.

This now always calls `py_object_to_any_value(value, true, allow_object)` with the second arg hard-coded to `true`. If that parameter controls “allow temporal subclasses” (or similar), consider threading a named variable/flag (or using a more descriptive constant) so we don’t accidentally broaden conversions for all literals (e.g., accepting more subclassed types than intended). Also, since the error message below suggests `allow_object` is the only knob, ensure `py_object_to_any_value` doesn’t silently coerce unexpected types when `allow_object=false`—otherwise we may mask type errors that previously surfaced.

This change inlines the iteration/conversion logic instead of reusing `convert_to_avs`, and it also hard-codes `allow_object=true` for `new_from_any_values`. Two concerns: (1) duplication/consistency—future changes to conversion behavior now need to be made in two places; consider calling `convert_to_avs(values, strict, true)` here instead. (2) behavior change—previously `new_from_any_values` effectively used the default `py_object_to_any_value(..., strict)` behavior; now it always allows object literals, which may mask type issues and change error messages/typing for mixed inputs. If this is intended, it would be good to add a short comment explaining why `allow_object` must be `true` here (and/or add a targeted test to lock in the new behavior).

This test name/intent says "_w_allow_object", but the assertion uses `pl.lit(MyClass(*input))` without `allow_object=True`. If the goal is to verify that temporal subclasses are now handled without falling back to object (as per the issue/PR), consider renaming the test to reflect that (e.g. `test_lit_temporal_subclass`) and/or add an explicit `allow_object=True` variant to ensure both code paths behave as expected. Also, for `time`, `dt_class(*input)` with `(1,)` relies on positional args mapping to `hour`; it may be clearer/less brittle to pass explicit kwargs (e.g. `time(1, 0, 0)`) or use a tuple matching the full signature.

This change fixes the previous bug where `insert()` was used to both test membership and mutate `names_left/names_right`, which could incorrectly mark a projection as already pushed and/or prevent it from being pushed to the correct side. However, note that `is_in_left/is_in_right` are computed before any insertion in this loop iteration; if a projection can legitimately be pushed to *both* sides (e.g., expression references columns from both inputs or ambiguous leaf nodes), the current logic will still allow both pushes because `is_in_*` is false initially, but `already_projected` will remain false until the next iteration. If `already_projected` is intended to reflect "this projection was already pushed earlier in this same iteration" you may want to update it after the conditional insertions (or compute it from `!was_not_in_*` style but without side effects) to avoid subtle state inconsistencies. At minimum, consider adding a comment clarifying that `already_projected` only tracks prior iterations, not pushes performed later in the same iteration.

This test is meant to guard against projection pushdown dropping columns from the *left* side after a join, but `right` is reduced to only `playerID`, so it doesn't exercise the original failure mode where the RHS had additional columns that were filtered/dropped and the optimizer incorrectly removed/kept names (e.g., `lgID`). Consider making `right` closer to the reported repro: keep `right` as a filtered frame and explicitly drop/select columns (e.g., select all except `yearID`/`lgID`) so the optimizer has to track column state across join + projection. That would better ensure we don't regress on missing-column errors like `ColumnNotFoundError: lgID`.

This re-declares `addr` inside the `if` block (`:=`), shadowing the outer `addr` variable. While it works for setting `labels[model.AddressLabel]`, it’s easy to misread and can lead to bugs if later code expects the outer `addr` to be updated. Prefer assignment (`addr = net.JoinHostPort(...)`) or use a different name (e.g., `host := addr; addr = net.JoinHostPort(host, ...)`) to avoid shadowing.

These leading line-number prefixes (e.g., `1:`) look like they were accidentally included in the file content. They will break the Markdown formatting/rendering of the changelog. Please remove the numeric prefixes and align the new `## 2.18.0 / 2020-05-05` section with the existing style (no indentation, blank line after the header).

The new VERSION line appears to have its fields shifted (leading spaces before the major version) compared to the previous format. If tooling parses this file expecting the `major:semver` layout (e.g., `1           :2.17.2`), this change may break parsing or produce an incorrect major version. Please keep the same column/formatting and only bump the semver (e.g., `1           :2.18.0`), unless there is an intentional format change and all consumers have been updated accordingly.

This comment is a bit confusing/contradictory: it says “get is internal” but the method is still exported via the `LocalStorage` field (and used throughout this file). Consider either (a) renaming `get()` to `Get()` and keeping it as the public accessor, or (b) keeping `get()` unexported but updating the comment to explain why `Get()` was removed and what the intended public surface is (e.g., `readyStorage` now implements the needed interfaces directly and callers should not access the underlying `*tsdb.DB`). As-is, it’s unclear what “front implementation layer” means and may mislead future maintainers.

Changing the field type from the interface `storage.Storage` to the concrete `*teststorage.TestStorage` tightens coupling and reduces flexibility (e.g., callers/tests that want to inject a different `storage.Storage` implementation). If the goal is only to expose the underlying TSDB, consider keeping `storage storage.Storage` and adding an optional interface/unwrap helper (e.g., `type tsdbProvider interface { TSDB() *tsdb.DB }` or a type assertion to `*teststorage.TestStorage`) rather than hard-wiring the concrete type. Also ensure all constructors/initializers of `Test` are updated accordingly, since this is a breaking change for any code that sets `t.storage` directly.

There’s an extra blank line inside the import block. Go fmt will typically collapse/normalize import spacing, and CI may fail if files aren’t gofmt’d. If this was intended to separate stdlib/third-party/local imports, use proper grouped imports (blank line between groups) rather than an empty line within the same group; otherwise remove it.

This new `Stats` type and `Head.Stats` method are very generic names and may collide/confuse with other TSDB stats types (e.g., block/DB stats) and with existing `Head` methods like `NumSeries/MinTime/MaxTime`. Consider renaming to something more specific like `HeadStats` and `Head.Stats()` returning `*HeadStats`, or placing it in a more appropriate file/package if it’s intended for broader consumption. Also, since the comment says this is expensive, it may be worth documenting *why* (e.g., postings cardinality scan) and/or guarding the expensive part behind `statsByLabelName != ""` to avoid doing work when not requested.

Consider making Close a pointer receiver: `func (s *TestStorage) Close() error`. With the current value receiver, the struct (including the embedded `*tsdb.DB` pointer) is copied on call; while it will still work, a pointer receiver better communicates ownership/mutability and avoids accidental copies if fields are added later. Also, if `New` returns `*TestStorage`, using a pointer receiver is the conventional match.

The wrapper now normalizes `tsdb.ErrNotReady` to `errorUnavailable`, but it relies on `errors.Cause(r.err.err) == tsdb.ErrNotReady`. This is brittle if the TSDB layer wraps errors differently (e.g., multiple wrapping layers, sentinel vs. typed errors). Consider switching to `errors.Is(..., tsdb.ErrNotReady)` (from stdlib) or `pkg/errors` equivalent if available, so the check remains correct regardless of wrapping. Also ensure `r.err.err` is never nil when `r.err != nil` (otherwise this can panic); a defensive nil check would make this safer.

Using an empty subtest name makes failures hard to attribute and can also lead to confusing output (all subtests share the same name). Previously this used the loop index; consider restoring a unique, descriptive name (e.g., fmt.Sprintf("%d/%s", idx, tc.method) or include endpoint/admin flags) to keep test output actionable.

NewAdmin now accepts `dbDir` and the `Admin` struct has a `dbDir` field, but the constructor only initializes `db`. This will leave `s.dbDir` empty and cause snapshots to be created under a relative `snapshots` directory (or otherwise incorrect path). Please set `dbDir: dbDir` in the returned struct, and consider validating it (non-empty) or deriving it from the TSDB implementation to avoid config drift.

Using `errors.Cause(err)` introduces a dependency on `github.com/pkg/errors` and only works if the error is wrapped by that package. In Prometheus codebase, errors are often wrapped with Go 1.13 `%w`/`errors.Is`, so this check may fail and return 500 instead of 503. Consider switching to the stdlib `errors.Is(err, tsdb.ErrNotReady)` (and import `errors` from stdlib), or ensure `tsdb.ErrNotReady` is consistently wrapped in a way compatible with `Cause`. Also consider whether other readiness-related errors should map to 503 for consistency across endpoints.

Using `errors.Wrap(tsdb.ErrNotReady, "wrap")` is fine for exercising `errors.Is`-style unwrapping, but this test will only be robust if the production code checks readiness via `errors.Is(err, tsdb.ErrNotReady)` (or `errors.Cause` for pkg/errors). If the handler does direct equality (`err == tsdb.ErrNotReady`), this wrapped error will not match and the test may fail for the wrong reason. Consider either (a) adding a short comment here that the handler must use `errors.Is`/unwrapping semantics, or (b) returning `tsdb.ErrNotReady` directly if the intent is only to simulate the not-ready condition rather than validate unwrapping behavior.

This changes `MinTime` to come from `s.MinTime`, which is good, but note the previous code had a bug (`MinTime = db.Head().MaxTime()`). Please ensure `TSDBAdminStats.MinTime` is defined with the same semantics as the old `Head().MinTime()` (e.g., returns the minimum timestamp across all series, and what it returns when the head is empty). If `MinTime` can be uninitialized/zero when empty, consider guarding or normalizing it here to avoid confusing UI output.

This adapter dereferences `a.Head()` without checking for nil. In these tests `db` is expected to be non-nil, but making the adapter defensive would prevent panics if future tests pass a nil DB (similar to other tests in this file that previously used `TSDB: func() *tsdb.DB { return nil }`). Consider:
- returning an error when `a == nil || a.DB == nil` (or when `a.Head() == nil`), or
- documenting via comment that `dbAdapter` requires a non-nil DB.
Also, if the production interface expects `Stats` to potentially return an error, it may be worth propagating errors from the underlying call if available (currently always returns nil error).

This pre-filtering avoids deserializing args for non-matching jobs, but it duplicates (and potentially diverges from) the existing job-matching logic later in the method. Consider extracting a small helper/predicate (e.g., `job_matches?(expected, actual)`) and reusing it in both places, or restructuring so the `jobs.find` block first checks `job`/`queue`/`at` without deserialization, then only deserializes when those pass. Also, `present?` pulls in ActiveSupport; if this helper is intended to be usable without it, prefer `if expected_args[:job]`/`!nil?` (though Rails likely already depends on AS here).

Test name reads opposite of what it’s exercising. The scenario is that an *earlier* job has **non-deserializable** args (ExplodingSerializer#deserialize raises), and `assert_enqueued_with` should still succeed by matching the later HelloJob without blowing up. Consider renaming to something like `test_assert_enqueued_with_ignores_earlier_undecodable_args` / `...with_earlier_undeserializable_args` to make intent clear and avoid confusion for future maintainers.

This restores `column_types` population for all columns, which fixes the reported regression, but it also reintroduces the original concern from #39097: for some OIDs the pg gem may already have typecasted values via decoders, and `get_oid_type` here may not match the actual Ruby objects in `result.values`. That mismatch can lead to double-casting or incorrect casting downstream if any code relies on `column_types` to cast `rows`.

Consider either (a) keeping `column_types` complete but marking/handling “already decoded” columns explicitly (e.g., store the type but ensure later casting is a no-op when values are already of the target Ruby type), or (b) gating this behavior behind a config/flag so apps depending on complete `column_types` can opt in without changing casting semantics for everyone. At minimum, please add/adjust tests to cover both: `exec_query("SELECT 1 AS test")` returning a non-empty `column_types`, and a query where pg decoders are active to ensure no double-cast/regression.

This assertion is a bit brittle: `result.column_types["test"]` can be `nil` if the regression reappears, which would raise on `.class` and produce a less-informative failure. Consider asserting presence first (e.g., `assert result.column_types.key?("test")`) and/or using `assert_instance_of ActiveModel::Type::Integer, result.column_types.fetch("test")` so failures clearly indicate the missing key vs wrong type. Also, if the adapter returns a subtype (e.g., a decorated integer type), `assert_kind_of` may be more appropriate than strict class equality.

This adapter-specific branching makes the test pass, but it also bakes in a Postgres behavior that may not be stable across versions/config (and it obscures what the test is actually asserting). Since the underlying issue is that Postgres returns duplicate column names for these expressions, consider asserting on the *casting behavior* explicitly (e.g., that without aliases Postgres returns strings / no type casting occurs) and/or add an assertion about `result.column_types` being incomplete when names collide. Alternatively, prefer using explicit aliases in the query for all adapters and keep this test focused on pluck semantics rather than adapter quirks.

Changelog entry formatting looks off: the leading indentation and the `1:*`/`2:` style line prefixes suggest this was copied from a numbered/annotated diff rather than written as Markdown. In Rails changelogs, entries are typically plain `*` bullets under a version header, without extra leading spaces or line-number-like prefixes. Please rewrite this entry to match the existing style (no prefixed numbers, consistent indentation), and ensure it’s placed under the correct release section/header.

This fallback to `superclass.logger` changes the inheritance semantics for subclasses, but it only checks `self != LogSubscriber`. Consider tightening the condition to avoid surprising behavior for non-direct descendants or anonymous subclasses, e.g. `if self < LogSubscriber && superclass.respond_to?(:logger)` (or `if superclass <= LogSubscriber`). Also, if a subclass explicitly sets `@logger = nil` to disable logging, this implementation will still fall back to the superclass; if `nil` is a valid explicit value, you may want to distinguish “unset” from “set to nil” (e.g., `instance_variable_defined?(:@logger)` instead of `@logger`).

This test mutates global class state (`MyLogSubscriber.logger`) and then manually resets it to `nil`. If the test fails before reaching this line, the logger will remain set and can leak into subsequent tests, causing order-dependent failures. Consider wrapping the mutation in an `ensure` block (or using a helper like `with_logger` if available) so the original value is always restored, e.g. `old = MyLogSubscriber.logger; MyLogSubscriber.logger = logger; ... ensure MyLogSubscriber.logger = old end`.

Changelog entry formatting looks off: the leading line numbers (`1:*`, `2:` etc.) appear to have been accidentally included in the file content. Please remove those prefixes and follow the existing bullet/indent style (e.g., `* Fix ...` with a blank line and an indented author line). Also consider adding a period at the end of the bullet for consistency with nearby entries.

Using `options[:precision] ||= nil` is a no-op for the common case where `:precision` is absent (it stays absent), so it likely won’t restore the Rails 6.1 behavior you’re targeting. If the goal is to *explicitly* clear the default precision (6) when changing to `:datetime` under 6.1 compatibility, consider setting the key unconditionally when it’s not provided, e.g. `options[:precision] = nil unless options.key?(:precision)` (or `options = options.merge(precision: nil) unless options.key?(:precision)`). Also consider whether this should apply to `:timestamp` as well (depending on adapter mapping), and add a regression test to ensure the schema/column precision is actually nil after `change_column`.

This test hard-codes the Migrator constructor signature (`new(:up, migrations, schema_migration, internal_metadata)`), which has changed across Rails versions and is called out in the PR description as problematic for backports. To make the test more robust (and easier to backport), consider using the higher-level API used elsewhere in this file/test suite (e.g., `ActiveRecord::Migrator.migrate(...)` / `MigrationContext` / `connection.migration_context`) or at least gate the call based on the available signature. That reduces brittleness and avoids failures when the migrator API changes again.

This helper currently returns the same generic message as before, so it doesn’t address the Postgres-specific confusion described in the issue. Consider making this method overridable and updating the Postgres adapter to override it with an explanation (e.g., that Rails reserves characters for the `_pkey` suffix / internal objects), or include that rationale conditionally when the adapter reduces `table_name_length` below the database’s max identifier length. Also consider adding a trailing period for consistency with the updated index error message.

This message is much clearer, but it’s very long and includes implementation details that may not always be accurate (e.g., it says PostgreSQL adds `_pkey` to table names when creating default indexes; it’s actually the default primary key constraint/index name derived from the table name). Consider tightening the wording and/or splitting into two sentences, e.g. mention the PG identifier limit and that Rails reserves 5 chars for the default primary key name suffix (`_pkey`). Also consider formatting `_pkey` consistently (backticks are more Markdown-ish; Rails error strings often use plain quotes) and keeping the message stable for tests/localization by avoiding overly verbose prose.

This assertion hard-codes a very long, highly specific error message, which makes the test brittle (minor wording/punctuation changes will break it) and may be awkward if the message is ever wrapped/translated. Consider asserting key substrings instead (e.g., includes "PostgreSQL allows a maximum length of #{max_identifier_length}", includes "`_pkey`", includes "effective limit is #{name_limit}") or using a regex. Also, the message says "default indexes" but `_pkey` is the default primary key constraint/index; aligning terminology with PostgreSQL (primary key) would reduce confusion and keep the test aligned with the intended behavior.

This assertion hard-codes a very long, punctuation-sensitive error message, which is likely to be brittle across minor wording changes (and may also be awkward if the adapter message is ever wrapped/normalized). Consider asserting on key substrings instead (e.g., includes "PostgreSQL allows a maximum length of #{max_identifier_length}", includes "`_pkey`", includes "effective limit is #{name_limit}") or using a regex. That would still validate the improved clarity without making the test fail on trivial copy edits.

Relying on `current.effectTag` here is a bit fragile because effect tags are primarily for commit-side side effects, and they can be cleared/overwritten across renders. If this flag is meant to influence render-phase bailout behavior, consider whether it should live in a render-phase field (e.g. `current.flags` in newer codepaths, or a dedicated boolean on the fiber) and ensure it’s always cleared after it has served its purpose. At minimum, please confirm (and ideally add an assertion/comment) that `ForceUpdateForLegacySuspense` is guaranteed to be set on the *current* fiber at this point and will be reset so it doesn’t force updates indefinitely.

This adds a bailout guard based on `current.effectTag`, but `effectTag` is typically mutated during the render/commit cycle and can be cleared/overwritten. Relying on it for memo bailout correctness is a bit fragile. Can we ensure `ForceUpdateForLegacySuspense` is guaranteed to be present on `current` at the time `updateSimpleMemoComponent` runs (i.e., it survives the commit and is not lost when cloning to WIP)? If not, consider storing this signal in a more stable place (e.g., a dedicated flag on the fiber or update queue) or add an invariant/comment explaining why `effectTag` is safe here and when it gets cleared.

Setting `didReceiveUpdate = true` based on `current.effectTag` makes sense for preventing bailout after legacy suspense, but please double-check that this flag is only ever set in legacy mode and cannot leak into concurrent mode paths. Also, since this is a subtle behavior change, it would help to clarify in the comment *where* `ForceUpdateForLegacySuspense` is set/cleared and why checking `current` (not `workInProgress`) is the correct source of truth here.

Setting `ForceUpdateForLegacySuspense` on `sourceFiber` makes sense for preventing a bailout after a legacy-mode suspend, but please double-check that `sourceFiber` is always the correct target here (vs `workInProgress` or the nearest boundary) for the subsequent render path that performs the bailout check. In particular, if `sourceFiber` can be a current/alternate fiber depending on where the throw originated, we should ensure the flag is applied to the fiber instance that will be consulted during reconciliation. If the bailout logic reads from `workInProgress.effectTag`, consider mirroring the flag there or documenting why `sourceFiber` is guaranteed to be the one checked. Also consider guarding against redundant flagging (harmless but can complicate debugging) or adding a brief comment explaining why this is legacy-only and why it must be on the source fiber.

Setting `ForceUpdateForLegacySuspense` on `sourceFiber` is a subtle cross-fiber side effect. Please double-check (and ideally document inline) why the flag must live on the *source* rather than `workInProgress` (or both), and that it will be reliably cleared after the retry/commit so it doesn’t force unnecessary updates on subsequent renders. Also consider guarding this assignment to only run once per suspended render (e.g., if the flag is already set) to avoid repeatedly OR-ing and potentially masking other effectTag semantics in legacy mode.

Please double-check that `LifecycleEffectMask` is still the correct union after expanding/reordering the bitfield. The comment says `Passive & Update & Callback & Ref & Snapshot`, but the mask literal changed; it’s easy to accidentally omit/include a flag when shifting widths. Consider defining this mask as a bitwise OR of the named constants (e.g. `Passive | Update | Callback | Ref | Snapshot`) to make it self-documenting and resilient to future bit additions/reordering.

This looks like a typo fix (removing the stray `:`) and also changes the binding from `let` to `const`. If the test never reassigns `MemoizedChild`, `const` is preferable. However, please double-check the rest of the test case to ensure `MemoizedChild` isn't reassigned later (e.g., to swap implementations between assertions). If it is reassigned, keep `let` and just remove the `:`. Also, the name `MemoizedChild` is a bit misleading since `forwardRef` alone doesn't memoize; consider renaming to `ForwardRefChild` (or similar) to avoid confusion in the test intent.

Avoid committing this test with `only: true`. It will cause the test runner to execute just this case and skip the rest of the suite, which can mask regressions. If you need to focus locally, use `.only` during development but remove it before merging, or replace with a more targeted test name/comment without `only`.

Expanding `isHook` to accept `JSXIdentifier` makes sense for JSX attribute contexts, but please double-check that this doesn’t accidentally treat non-hook JSX identifiers as hooks in places where `isHook` is called on arbitrary nodes. In JSX, many identifiers (e.g. `<Foo />`, `<Bar.Baz />`) are `JSXIdentifier`/`JSXMemberExpression`; if upstream callers can pass those here, we may start flagging components/props that happen to start with `use` (e.g. `<UseSomething />`) as hooks. Consider either (a) narrowing this to only the specific JSX AST locations where a hook callee is expected (e.g. `JSXExpressionContainer` -> `CallExpression.callee`), or (b) adding a guard that the `JSXIdentifier` is being used as a callee identifier (not a tag/attribute name) before applying `isHookName`.

Returning `node.parent.parent.name` (a `JSXIdentifier`) makes the callback look like a component/hook based solely on the JSX attribute name (e.g. `<Child Component={() => { useState() }} />`). This is a behavior change that may introduce false positives/negatives: many props are capitalized for non-component reasons, and conversely lowercase props can still receive component-like callbacks. Consider tightening the heuristic (e.g., only treat specific prop names like `Component`/`render`/`children`? or require the function to be used as a JSX element elsewhere) or at least add/adjust tests to cover: (1) capitalized non-component props, (2) lowercase props that pass components, and (3) member namespaced attributes (if applicable). Also, ensure `isComponentName`/`isHook` callers can handle `JSXIdentifier` consistently across the rule.

This change makes insertion-effect cleanups run even when `offscreenSubtreeWasHidden` is true, while still skipping layout-effect cleanups in that case. That matches the PR intent, but please double-check the invariants around hidden/offscreen trees: if a subtree is merely hidden (not actually deleted), calling insertion-effect destroy here could cause premature cleanup and break re-show semantics. If the intent is specifically “deleting previously hidden subtrees”, consider guarding on an explicit deletion/unmount condition (or add a clarifying comment) to ensure we only run insertion cleanups when the fiber is truly being deleted, not just hidden.

This assertion expects `Text:One destroy insertion` to fire when the previously hidden subtree is deleted after the suspended content resolves. That matches the bug fix intent, but it’s also the most timing-sensitive part of the test: insertion-effect cleanup ordering can be affected by StrictMode/dev-only double-invocation or by future changes in effect flushing semantics. To make this test more robust, consider (a) explicitly ensuring the test runs in non-StrictMode (if applicable in this suite), and/or (b) asserting relative ordering only where necessary (e.g., that `Text:One destroy insertion` occurs before `AsyncText:Two create insertion`, without over-specifying the full log sequence). Otherwise this may become a brittle snapshot-style log test.
There’s trailing whitespace on this blank line, which will fail some linters/formatters and adds noise to diffs. Please remove the extra spaces (keep it as an empty line or delete it).

Switching to dictAddOrFind() avoids the crash when the same forgotten node is blacklisted twice, but note that this changes semantics: if the entry already exists, the newly allocated `id` key is not used and must be freed to avoid a leak. With dictAddRaw() the dict would take ownership of `id` on success; with AddOrFind(), ownership is ambiguous when the key already exists. Please confirm dictAddOrFind() frees the passed key on "found" (it typically doesn't), and if not, add logic to free `id` when `de` refers to an existing entry (e.g., compare `dictGetKey(de)` to `id` or use an API that returns whether it inserted). Also consider whether you want to always refresh TTL on duplicates (current code does), and add a brief comment explaining the intended behavior.

Ignoring the static library is fine, but this path is very specific. Please confirm the build always produces `deps/hdr_histogram/libhdrhistogram.a` (name and directory) across supported platforms/toolchains. If the dependency can also generate other artifacts (e.g., `.o`, `.d`, `.so`/`.dylib`, or a different output dir), consider ignoring the whole `deps/hdr_histogram/` build output (or a pattern like `deps/hdr_histogram/*.a`) to avoid accidentally committing generated files on some environments.

Hard-coding the archiver as `ar` reduces portability and ignores toolchain overrides (e.g., cross-compiling typically sets `AR`/`ARFLAGS` from the environment or via the parent build system). Consider using conditional assignment so external builds can override: `AR ?= ar` and `ARFLAGS ?= rcs` (or inherit from `$(ARFLAGS)` if already provided). This matches common Makefile conventions and avoids surprising behavior in downstream packaging.

This blindly dereferences `override` and copies function pointers without validation. If a caller passes NULL (or leaves some members NULL), this will crash later when the histogram code calls through the allocator hooks. Consider either (a) returning `orig` without changes when `override == NULL`, and/or (b) validating each function pointer and rejecting/keeping defaults for NULL entries. Also note this updates a global (`hdrAllocFns`) without any synchronization; if Redis can call this concurrently with histogram allocations, it’s a data race—document that it must be called at init time only or add locking.

Potential divide-by-zero/UB: if `size == 0`, `SIZE_MAX / size` is undefined. Even though `calloc(nmemb, 0)` is valid (implementation-defined return, but must be freeable), this check will crash. Please guard `size == 0` (and optionally `nmemb == 0`) before the division, e.g. `if (size != 0 && nmemb > SIZE_MAX / size) return NULL;`.

Switching from libc calloc/free to hdr_calloc/hdr_free is fine, but please ensure allocator pairing is consistent across the whole hdr_histogram integration. In particular: (1) verify hdr_calloc/hdr_free are always available/initialized in this build (and that including hdr_alloc.h is sufficient on all supported platforms), and (2) confirm no other code path frees `h->counts` or `h` with plain `free()` (or allocates them with `calloc/malloc`) to avoid mismatched allocators. It may be worth adding a brief comment here explaining why Redis needs the custom allocator (e.g., to hook into zmalloc / tracking).

Including <stddef.h> for size_t is reasonable, but since this is a vendored dependency header, please confirm this change is required for the specific toolchains Redis supports (and ideally upstreamed to HdrHistogram_c) to avoid carrying a local patch. Also consider whether <stdlib.h> or <sys/types.h> is already included elsewhere in this header/library and could be relied on; if not, this is fine, but add a brief note in the PR/commit message that this fixes a build error due to missing size_t.

Switching from linking the raw object (hdr_histogram.o) to a static archive (libhdrhistogram.a) is fine, but please ensure the deps build actually produces this archive on all supported platforms/configs. If the deps Makefile still only builds hdr_histogram.o, this will break builds. Also consider link-order implications: if libhdrhistogram.a depends on other libs/objects, it may need to appear after the objects that reference it (or be wrapped in --start-group/--end-group for some linkers).

Same concern as redis-server: verify ../deps/hdr_histogram/libhdrhistogram.a is built/installed by the deps build and available for redis-benchmark too, otherwise this change will cause link failures. If the intent is to reduce duplication, consider defining a variable (e.g., HDRHIST_LIB=../deps/hdr_histogram/libhdrhistogram.a) to avoid repeating the path in multiple targets.

Calling `hdrSetAllocators(&hdrallocfn)` from `initServerConfig()` makes allocator selection a side-effect of config initialization, and it may be invoked more than once in some code paths (e.g., tests, re-init flows). If HdrHistogram expects allocators to be set exactly once and/or before any histogram is created, consider moving this to a one-time global init (e.g., early in `main()`/`initServer()`), guarding it with a static `once` flag, and/or asserting it’s not called after any histogram allocations. Also verify thread-safety/reentrancy of `hdrSetAllocators` and document why Redis allocators are required here (and that they match the expected malloc/free semantics).

Including `hdr_alloc.h` from the HdrHistogram C library in the global `server.h` header may unnecessarily increase compile dependencies and can break builds if the vendored/packaged HdrHistogram version doesn’t ship this header (some distributions expose only `hdr_histogram.h`). If you only need the custom allocator APIs in a specific .c file, prefer including `hdr_alloc.h` there instead of in `server.h`. If it must be global, please ensure the build system vendors/installs this header consistently and consider guarding the include (or providing a compatibility shim) to avoid portability issues.

Potential integer overflow in `num*size` before passing to allocator/oom handler. If `num` and `size` are large, the multiplication can wrap and allocate a smaller buffer than intended, leading to OOB writes in callers expecting `num*size` bytes. Consider using an overflow-checked multiply (e.g., `if (size && num > SIZE_MAX/size) zmalloc_oom_handler(SIZE_MAX);` or a dedicated `zmalloc_size_mul_overflow()` helper) and reuse the computed `total` for both allocation and OOM handler to avoid recomputing.

Please ensure this new API is safe and consistent with existing allocation helpers. In particular: (1) `zcalloc_num(num, size)` should perform an overflow-checked `num * size` (like `calloc` does) and return NULL / panic consistently with `zcalloc` vs `ztrycalloc` semantics; (2) consider whether this should also have a `ztrycalloc_num` variant for symmetry; (3) add a brief comment in the header clarifying behavior on overflow and OOM, since callers may assume `calloc`-style overflow protection.

This tip name likely doesn't match the documented/implemented command tip vocabulary. In the issue/PR description, `blocking` is a boolean command tip (lowercase), while `BLOCKING` is a command flag / ACL category. If `command_tips` are meant to be consumed by clients/proxies, consider using the canonical tip string (e.g., `"blocking"`) and keep `"BLOCKING"` only in `command_flags`. Also verify whether BLMOVE should use the more specific `block_keyword` tip (e.g., `block_keyword:...`) if it only blocks under certain argument patterns; otherwise `blocking` is fine.

The tip name here should match the documented command tips schema. In the issue/PR description, tips are described as lowercase (e.g., `blocking`) and `BLOCKING` is also already represented as a command flag/ACL category. If the intent is to expose a boolean tip, consider using the canonical tip string (likely `blocking`) and reserve `BLOCKING` for the command flag. Also, for BL*MPOP specifically, it may be more useful to include the `block_keyword` key-value tip (e.g., `block_keyword:BLOCK`) since the command only blocks when `BLOCK <timeout>` is provided; otherwise clients/proxies may treat it as always-blocking.

The tip name here should match the documented command tip identifiers. In the issue/PR description, tips are described as lowercase (e.g., `blocking`) while this uses `"BLOCKING"` (same as the command flag). If the `COMMAND DOCS`/introspection output expects canonical lowercase tip strings, this will either be inconsistent or break consumers. The tip value here is uppercase ("BLOCKING"), while the issue/PR description and docs sketch define tips as lowercase (e.g., "blocking"). Please align the casing with the documented tip names and whatever the COMMAND DOCS output expects, otherwise clients/proxies may not recognize it or you may end up with two parallel conventions (flags vs tips). If the canonical representation is intended to be uppercase in JSON, consider adding/confirming a normalization step in the generator/output and documenting it explicitly.

The tip name here should match the documented command tips schema. Using `"BLOCKING"` (uppercase) risks breaking clients/proxies that parse tips case-sensitively and also duplicates information already present in `command_flags`. The tip name here should match the documented command tips schema. In the issue/PR description the tip is `blocking` (lowercase) and tips are generally separate from command flags. Using `"BLOCKING"` risks either being rejected by parsers expecting lowercase tip identifiers or duplicating semantics already conveyed by the `BLOCKING` flag. Consider changing this to the canonical tip string (e.g., `"blocking"`) and ensure the generator/COMMAND DOCS output treats tips case-sensitively and doesn’t double-report blocking via both flag and tip unless that’s explicitly intended.

The tip name here should match the documented command tip vocabulary. In the issue/PR description, `blocking` is a boolean command tip (lowercase), while `BLOCKING` is a command flag / ACL category. If `command_tips` is meant to expose tips to clients, consider using the canonical lowercase tip string (e.g., `"blocking"`) and keep `BLOCKING` only in `command_flags`/categories. The new `command_tips` entry duplicates the existing `BLOCKING` information already present in `acl_categories`. The new tip name here uses `RANDOM_OUTPUT`, but the issue/PR description and docs sketch refer to `random-output` / `random-order-output` (and elsewhere you mention `nondeterministic_output`). Please ensure the tip identifier matches the canonical naming used across the codebase and redis-doc (including case and separator), otherwise clients/proxies parsing `COMMAND DOCS` may not recognize it. Please double-check the tip name/casing here matches the final spec and what COMMAND DOCS actually emits. The issue/PR description uses `random-output` / `nondeterministic_output` (and `random-order-output` / `nondeterministic_output_order`), while this JSON uses `RANDOM_OUTPUT` (uppercase, underscore). Is `RANDOM_ORDER_OUTPUT` correct for `SUNION`? The union result is not guaranteed to be in a stable order (set iteration order), but it’s also not necessarily *random* in the sense of being intentionally randomized; it may be deterministic for a given internal state yet change across runs/rehashes. Please confirm the intended semantics of this tip and whether `SUNION` should be tagged here (and consistently with related commands like `SINTER`/`SDIFF`). If the tip is meant specifically for commands whose output order is unspecified/non-deterministic, consider whether the naming/usage matches that definition in the docs.

The tip name here doesn't match the naming described in the issue/PR text (which uses lowercase-hyphenated tips like `random-output` / `random-order-output`). Please double-check the tip name here: the issue/PR description and docs use `reply_policy` (and examples show `REPLY_POLICY`), but elsewhere in the sketch it’s referred to as `response_policy`. Ensure the implementation and documentation consistently use the same key (and casing) across all commands, otherwise clients/proxies may ignore this tip. Also, confirm that `AGG_SUM` is the intended aggregation for TOUCH in a sharded setup (i.e., sum of per-shard touched keys) and that it matches how the proxy is expected to merge replies.

TTL is deterministic for a given key at a given moment, but its output changes over time; it is not "random" in the sense used by the new tips (e.g., RANDOMKEY/SPOP). Marking it as RANDOM_OUTPUT may mislead proxies/clients into treating it as nondeterministic and potentially changing routing/aggregation behavior. If the intent is to signal time-variance, consider a different tip (if defined) or omit the tip entirely. Also double-check consistency with PTTL/EXPIRETIME/PEXPIRETIME, which were mentioned as previously mis-flagged as random.

UNLINK is a multi-key delete that returns the number of keys removed. In a sharded/proxy context, the request policy FEW_SHARDS makes sense, but the reply aggregation should be explicitly validated: AGG_SUM is only correct if each shard returns an integer count of deleted keys for its subset and the proxy guarantees no key is sent to more than one shard. If the proxy can duplicate keys across shard sub-requests (e.g., due to hash-tag parsing bugs or cross-slot handling), summing would overcount. Consider adding/confirming a note in the tip docs or ensuring the proxy split logic enforces uniqueness per key, and double-check whether the tip name should be RESPONSE_POLICY vs REPLY_POLICY to match the final spec/docs used elsewhere in this PR.

WAIT is a replication/connection command that should be handled by the node the client is connected to; it doesn't make sense to forward it to all shards in a clustered/proxy setup because each shard has independent replication state and the client expects semantics relative to the connected node. Consider whether the correct REQUEST_POLICY is the default (single shard / connected node) rather than ALL_SHARDS, and document/justify if ALL_SHARDS is intentional. If the goal is to support a proxy aggregating across shards, ensure this matches the redis-doc command-tips spec for WAIT and that proxies can actually implement it without changing WAIT semantics.

Removing the `RANDOM` command flag from XACK makes sense (XACK’s reply is deterministic), but please double-check that any previous consumers of `COMMAND` output that relied on this flag won’t regress. If the intent is to represent non-determinism via command tips now, confirm XACK does not need a replacement tip (e.g., `nondeterministic_output`/`nondeterministic_output_order`) and consider adding/ensuring a test or documentation note that XACK is deterministic to prevent the flag from being reintroduced accidentally.

XADD is deterministic given the same arguments; it shouldn't be tagged as RANDOM_OUTPUT. The PR description mentions moving the old RANDOM flag to a nondeterministic output tip, but for XADD the old RANDOM flag looks incorrect (unlike commands such as RANDOMKEY/SPOP). Please verify why XADD was previously marked RANDOM and either remove this tip or replace it with the correct one (e.g., if the intent was about ID generation when using "*", consider a more specific tip/keyword rather than RANDOM_OUTPUT). Also ensure the tip naming matches the documented final list (e.g., "random-output" vs "RANDOM_OUTPUT") to avoid schema/consumer mismatches.

Please double-check that XAUTOCLAIM’s reply is actually nondeterministic before tagging it as RANDOM_OUTPUT. XAUTOCLAIM returns entries based on the PEL and the provided start/id and count; while it can vary with concurrent stream activity, that’s different from inherently random output (like RANDOMKEY/SPOP). If the intent is to signal “may vary due to concurrent writes,” consider whether a different tip is needed/available; otherwise this may mislead proxies/clients into treating it as random and changing routing/aggregation behavior unnecessarily.

Is XCLAIM actually nondeterministic? Unlike XREADGROUP (with BLOCK) or RANDOMKEY/SPOP, XCLAIM’s reply should be deterministic given the stream state and arguments. The old CMD_RANDOM flag here may have been accidental (similar to the PR note about XACK). If there isn’t a concrete reason (e.g., reply order/content can vary across runs for the same inputs), consider removing this tip or switching to a more accurate one (e.g., blocking-related tip if applicable). Also ensure the tip name matches the documented naming convention (issue sketch uses `random-output` / `random-order-output`; here it’s `RANDOM_OUTPUT`).

Are we sure `XINFO CONSUMERS` has nondeterministic output? Unlike `RANDOMKEY`/`SPOP`, this command’s reply should be deterministic for a given stream state (ordering aside). If the intent is to mark that the *order* of consumers may vary, consider using the `RANDOM_ORDER_OUTPUT` tip instead (per the new tip taxonomy), or drop the tip entirely if ordering is stable/defined. Also double-check the tip naming matches the final documented names (`random-output` / `random-order-output` vs `RANDOM_OUTPUT`), since a mismatch will break clients parsing tips.

Please double-check that XPENDING's reply is actually nondeterministic. XPENDING returns pending entries for a given stream/group and is typically deterministic for a fixed dataset/args (aside from natural changes due to concurrent writes/acks). If the intent is to mark commands whose output order can vary, consider whether this should be `RANDOM_ORDER_OUTPUT` instead, or remove the tip entirely if there is no inherent nondeterminism in the command semantics. Also, ensure the tip name matches the documented final list (e.g., `random-output` / `random-order-output`) and the rest of the codebase’s expected casing/format for tips.

Good direction to avoid OOM from logging large byte[]. A couple of things to double-check here: (1) `params` is an `Object[]`, so `LogHelper.toString(params)` should handle nested arrays (e.g., `byte[]`, `ByteBuf`, `Object[]` containing `byte[]`) without delegating to SLF4J's deep array formatting; ensure it truncates/prints length/hash rather than content. (2) Preserve previous behavior for non-array elements (nulls, primitives wrappers) so `toString()` remains useful for debugging. (3) Consider whether `LogHelper.toString` should be null-safe and avoid allocating large intermediate Strings (e.g., by limiting total output size). Adding/adjusting unit tests around `CommandData.toString()` with large `byte[]` params would help prevent regressions.

Good change to avoid Arrays.toString() potentially expanding large byte[] params. Please ensure LogHelper.toString(Object) (or overload) handles nested arrays/collections and specifically byte[]/ByteBuf safely (e.g., log length + hash, not contents), and is null-safe. Also consider adding a small unit test covering byte[] and Object[] containing byte[] to prevent regressions, since this message is used in exception construction and may be triggered under memory pressure.

Potential off-by-one / confusing logic: `length` is actually the last index (`Array.getLength(array) - 1`), not the length. This makes the code harder to read and can lead to mistakes when maintaining (e.g., initial capacity calculation and loop conditions). Consider using `int len = Array.getLength(array); if (len == 0) return "[]"; int last = len - 1;` and use `last` for comparisons. Also, `new StringBuilder(length * 3)` under-allocates for large element strings and can overflow for very large arrays; a small constant (e.g., 64) or `Math.min(len, MAX_COLLECTION_LOG_SIZE) * ...` would be safer.

This assertion hard-codes the truncation length (100) and the exact suffix ("...") in the test, which makes it brittle if LogHelper’s limits/format are adjusted (e.g., different max length, different ellipsis, or inclusion of length metadata). Consider referencing the constant(s) from LogHelper (e.g., MAX_STRING_LENGTH / ELLIPSIS) or asserting more behaviorally (startsWith first N chars and endsWith "...") so the test validates intent (truncation) without duplicating implementation details.

Filtering deleted groups makes sense, but doing `getSubscriptionGroupTable().containsKey(group)` directly on the internal map couples this code to the manager’s internal data structure and may bypass any future logic (e.g., disabled groups, transient states). Prefer a dedicated API on `SubscriptionGroupManager` (e.g., `findSubscriptionGroupConfig(group) != null` / `containsSubscriptionGroup(group)`) if available, or add one. Also consider using `groups.stream().filter(...).collect(toSet())` for readability, and if response ordering matters to clients, use `LinkedHashSet` to preserve the original iteration order from `groups`.

This change alters the expected ACL config path from `conf/acl/plain_acl.yml` to `conf/plain_acl.yml`. If the runtime still uses `conf/acl/plain_acl.yml`, this test will become inconsistent and may pass/fail depending on environment setup. Consider centralizing the path construction in a shared constant/helper (or referencing the same constant used by the main code) to avoid hard-coded path drift across tests and implementation.

Avoid using Java `assert` in unit tests because assertions are disabled by default unless the JVM is started with `-ea`, which can make this test silently pass even when behavior is wrong. Use JUnit/AssertJ assertions instead (e.g., `Assert.assertFalse(flag1)` / `Assertions.assertThat(flag1).isFalse()`). Same applies to the later `assert flag2 == true` and `assert flag == true` in this file.

Adding `toString()` is useful for debugging/tests, but be careful about leaking sensitive ACL data. `plainAccessConfigs` may include access/secret keys or other credentials; logging this object could expose secrets. Consider redacting sensitive fields (e.g., mask secretKey/accessKey) or omitting `plainAccessConfigs` entirely from `toString()`, and/or ensure `PlainAccessConfig.toString()` is safe.

Be careful including `secretKey` in `toString()`. This object likely gets logged (especially in tests/debug), and printing credentials is a security risk. Consider masking (e.g., show only last 4 chars) or omitting `secretKey` entirely from `toString()`. If tests need visibility, assert fields directly rather than relying on `toString()` output.

This `unlock()` in the outer `finally` is unsafe: if `lockInterruptibly()` throws `InterruptedException`, the write lock was never acquired and this will throw `IllegalMonitorStateException`, potentially masking the interrupt and breaking callers. Keep the unlock in a `finally` that only runs after successful acquisition, or guard with a boolean `locked` flag (set true after lock acquisition) / `isHeldByCurrentThread()` (if using `ReentrantReadWriteLock.WriteLock`) before unlocking.

Switching from `messageStore.putMessage` to `escapeBridge.putMessageToSpecificQueue` changes the write path semantics (routing, queue selection, possible remote-escape behavior, and potentially different return codes). Please ensure this is intentional for *all* ChangeInvisibleTime checkpoint writes, not only slave-acting-master scenarios. In particular: (1) confirm `msgInner` has the required target topic/queue metadata for `putMessageToSpecificQueue` (otherwise it may default/route unexpectedly), (2) verify the returned `PutMessageResult` codes are compatible with the existing downstream handling/logging in this method (e.g., retry/exception paths), and (3) consider guarding this with a config/role check (only use escapeBridge when needed) to avoid behavior changes for normal master brokers. Adding/adjusting an integration test that asserts the checkpoint lands in the expected revive queue in both normal and slave-acting-master modes would help prevent regressions.

Setting the reviveQueueOffset to 0 for remote put looks risky: 0 can be a valid offset and may later be interpreted as “stored at offset 0” rather than “unknown/not applicable”, potentially breaking idempotency/dup-detection or any logic that relies on reviveQueueOffset being meaningful. Consider using a sentinel value (e.g., -1) or explicitly tracking “offset unknown” via a separate flag/field, and ensure downstream code paths that read reviveQueueOffset handle the remote-put case without assuming a real offset. Also, if remote put can still provide an offset via another field (e.g., remote result metadata), prefer populating it instead of discarding.

Initializing `getMessageTmpResult` to `null` is fine, but please verify all subsequent uses are null-safe. In this method, `getMessageTmpResult` is referenced later (e.g., for offset commit / nextBeginOffset) and if an exception occurs before assignment, it can lead to NPEs or incorrect behavior. Consider either (1) declaring it inside the `try` where it’s guaranteed to be assigned before use, or (2) guarding all later accesses with `if (getMessageTmpResult != null)` and ensuring the method returns a sensible `restNum`/status when it remains null.

Switching from `messageStore.putMessage` to `escapeBridge.putMessageToSpecificQueue` changes the write path semantics (queue selection, routing, and possibly HA/role behavior). Please confirm this is intentional for revive-retry: (1) does `msgInner` already have the correct target queueId set, and will `putMessageToSpecificQueue` honor it deterministically? (2) does this bypass any validations/metrics/hooks that `MessageStore#putMessage` would apply (e.g., topic/queue existence, transactional/trace hooks, stats)? (3) what happens if `escapeBridge` is null/disabled in some broker modes—should there be a fallback to `messageStore.putMessage` or a clearer failure? Adding a brief comment here (why revive must use escapeBridge in slave-acting-master) and/or a small guard would help prevent regressions in non-SAM deployments.

Typo in type name: `InnerSalveBrokerController` looks like it should be `InnerSlaveBrokerController`. If the project actually has the correct `InnerSlave...` class, this will either not compile or will propagate a misspelling into new code. Please verify the intended class name and fix the import/usage accordingly (and consider aligning naming across the codebase).

This early-return path sets SUCCESS unconditionally after calling cleanUnusedLmqTopic(topic). Please consider (1) checking/propagating the result of cleanUnusedLmqTopic (or catching exceptions) and returning an appropriate error code/remark on failure, and (2) ensuring any other topic-related metadata cleanup that should still happen for LMQ (e.g., offsets / queue mapping / topic config) is either unnecessary by design or handled inside cleanUnusedLmqTopic. Otherwise, LMQ deletions may report success while leaving stale metadata behind.

The limit check uses `>` which allows creating exactly `maxLmqConsumeQueueNum` LMQ consume queues and only rejects the next one. If the intent is to cap at the configured maximum, this should likely be `>=` (reject when current count already equals the max). Also consider whether `getLmqConsumeQueueNum()` is updated atomically with consume-queue creation; otherwise this check can be racy under concurrent puts and still exceed the limit.

Adding a new method to the public `MessageStore` interface is a breaking API change for any custom `MessageStore` implementations. Please ensure all in-repo implementations are updated in this PR, and consider whether this should be an optional capability (e.g., default method with no-op, or a separate sub-interface) to preserve compatibility. Also, the Javadoc warns about concurrent writes causing message loss; it would be safer to document/define expected thread-safety and return/throw behavior (e.g., boolean/exception) so callers can reliably handle failures.

This override is a pure pass-through to `next`. If `cleanUnusedLmqTopic` is newly added to the `MessageStore` interface, consider providing a default no-op implementation at the interface/base level (or in an adapter) to avoid forcing every plugin wrapper to add boilerplate overrides. Also, if `next` can ever be null during lifecycle/initialization, this will NPE—either assert non-null in constructor or guard here.

Potential double-counting: `lmqConsumeQueueNum` is incremented here when a new CQ is created, but it is also incremented in `putConsumeQueue()` when a new topic map is created. If `doFindOrCreateConsumeQueue()` ends up calling `putConsumeQueue()` for the same new LMQ CQ, the counter may be incremented twice for a single queue. Consider centralizing the increment in one place (preferably where you can atomically detect a *new* (topic,queueId) insertion, e.g., via `computeIfAbsent`/`putIfAbsent` on the inner map) and/or increment only when the specific queueId was absent. Also ensure decrements happen on CQ removal/cleanup to keep the count accurate over time.

This override is currently a no-op (it only delegates to `super`) and adds no tiered-store-specific behavior. Commenting out the assertion/behavior under test makes the unit test less meaningful and can hide a real bug/regression. If this line is unstable due to JDK's `ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent` throwing `IllegalStateException: Recursive update` for nested updates, consider rewriting the test to explicitly assert that behavior (e.g., `assertThrows(IllegalStateException.class, ...)`) or adjust the test to avoid recursive updates while still validating `ConcurrentHashMapUtils.computeIfAbsent` semantics. This sets Ruby to 2.7, but Prism parsing support in this PR is described as Ruby 3.3+ (and the workflow comment says “minimum Ruby version 2.7 required for Prism to run”, which is misleading in the context of using Prism as the parser engine). Running `PARSER_ENGINE=parser_prism bundle exec rake prism_spec` on Ruby 2.7 is likely to fail or not exercise the intended code paths. If you want to test “Prism gem can be installed” separately, that should be a different job from the parser-engine test.

This line looks like it accidentally includes a line number in the YAML key (`16:`) due to a bad reformatting of the todo file. The PR description says Prism is experimental and should not be a runtime dependency, but adding it unconditionally to the Gemfile will make it part of the development bundle for everyone (and may be interpreted as a required dependency in some workflows). Consider scoping this to a dedicated group (e.g., `group :prism do ... end`) and/or `require: false` to avoid auto-requiring, and ensure the minimum version aligns with the code (e.g., `prism/translation/parser33` availability) and the supported Ruby versions. If CI needs it, you can add it only for the prism spec task via a group or appraisal rather than always installing it.

Switching the default task from `ascii_spec` to `prism_spec` changes the default developer workflow and may break for contributors who don’t have the `prism` gem installed (the PR notes it’s not a runtime dependency). Consider keeping `ascii_spec` in the default task (or running both), or make `prism_spec` conditional (e.g., only if `ENV['PARSER_ENGINE'] == 'parser_prism'` or if `Gem::Specification.find_by_name('prism')` succeeds) with a clear message/fallback to `ascii_spec` to avoid surprising failures on `bundle exec rake`.

The comment refers to the default as `parser` and says "`parser` can be specified", but the actual config key/value uses `parser_whitequark` (and the PR description also uses `parser_whitequark`/`parser_prism`). Also consider clarifying that Prism support is via the `parser_prism` engine (and requires the `prism` gem) rather than implying it’s automatically enabled.
