Consider using a dedicated `LexicalErrorType`/parse error variant for invalid assignment targets instead of `OtherError(msg.to_string())`. Using a free-form string makes it harder for downstream consumers (and tests) to reliably match on the error kind, and it also allocates a new `String` each time. If `OtherError` is the intended bucket, at least consider using a static/borrowed message (if supported) or centralizing the message as a `const` to avoid repeated allocations and keep the wording consistent across the parser.

Adding a new internal module is fine, but ensure it’s actually referenced from `parser`/other modules; otherwise this will compile but be dead code (and may trigger `dead_code` warnings depending on crate lints). If `invalid` is intended to be part of the public API surface, consider whether it should be `pub(crate) mod invalid;` or re-export specific items instead of keeping it private. Also keep module ordering consistent with the rest of the file (e.g., group parser-related modules together) to avoid churn.

This validation runs on `targets` after `set_context(..., Store)` has already been applied. Good to validate assignment targets here, but note that `targets` have already been passed through `set_context(..., Store)` above. Also, please confirm this returns the intended `ParseError` variant (E999) with a useful span (ideally pointing at the invalid LHS token, e.g., the `5` in `5 = 3`), since `?` will propagate whatever `invalid` constructs.

This snapshot hard-codes `source: crates/ruff_python_parser/src/invalid.rs`, which is an absolute-ish repo path and can make snapshots brittle if the file is moved/renamed or if snapshot metadata changes. This snapshot asserts that assigning to an attribute of a string literal ("foo".y = 42) parses successfully (ctx: Store). Consider changing this to an `invalid` snapshot expecting an error (E999-equivalent) or, if Ruff intentionally diverges here, add a comment/justification and ensure downstream linting handles it explicitly.

This snapshot asserts that an IPython escape command containing `foo = 42` parses successfully. Consider adding/confirming a companion test that an escape command containing an otherwise-invalid assignment (e.g., `"5 = 3"`) still parses as `Ok(IpyEscapeCommand { value: ... })`, to prevent future regressions where the parser starts validating the embedded string as Python syntax.

This snapshot asserts that the RHS list expression has `ctx: Load`, which is correct for `[1, 2, 3]` in an assignment. Typo in the test description: "loos" should be "lose". Since these fixture files double as documentation for the intended formatting behavior, fixing the wording helps avoid confusion when debugging future regressions.

Making `has_parentheses` `pub(crate)` increases the surface area of this helper across the crate. If this is only needed by the new assignment formatting logic, consider keeping it private and exposing a narrower API (e.g., a method on `OwnParentheses`/a dedicated helper in the assignment module) to avoid accidental misuse and to keep invariants localized. This currently enables the style for *all* preview mode runs (`context.is_preview()`), which makes it impossible to gate the behavior behind the specific `prefer_splitting_right_hand_side_of_assignments` preview flag as described in the issue/PR. Consider checking the dedicated preview setting/flag (e.g., `context.options().preview().prefer_splitting_right_hand_side_of_assignments` or similar) instead of the global preview toggle, otherwise other preview styles will unintentionally pick up this behavior and users can’t opt in/out independently.

The condition for enabling the right-to-left layout is currently tied to `has_parentheses(annotation, ...)`. That seems like a leaky proxy for “annotation can/should split” and may miss cases where the annotation can break without being parenthesized (or where parentheses are present but splitting isn’t actually beneficial). It also risks diverging from the intended rule described in the PR (prefer breaking the *value* when the part before the operator can split). Consider using a more direct predicate (e.g., a `can_break`/`can_split` check for the annotation/type params, or reuse the same logic used in `stmt_assign` to decide when to use `RightToLeft`) rather than `has_parentheses`.

This early-return assumes that if the last target breaks, we should never parenthesize the value. That matches the stated style goal, but it also bypasses the `best_fitting!` variants that handle call-specific breaking and (more importantly) the “only parenthesize if it makes it fit” optimization for non-inlineable values. Please double-check that `will_break()` here is evaluated under the same grouping/indent context as the later layouts; otherwise it can be a false positive/negative and change formatting. If the intent is purely a perf shortcut, consider guarding it more narrowly (e.g., only when `before_operator` is an `Expression` target and not `TypeParams`, and/or only when there are no inline comments) or add a regression test where the target *can* break but doesn’t in the chosen layout, to ensure we don’t incorrectly skip parenthesizing the RHS.

The gating condition for switching to the RightToLeft layout seems too restrictive for augmented assignments. `has_target_own_parentheses(target, ...)` reads like a predicate about whether the target *already* has its own parentheses, but the intent here is to prefer splitting the RHS when the target can split (e.g., subscription/attribute chains) and would otherwise cause the target to break first. If this predicate is about “can/needs parentheses when broken” rather than “already parenthesized”, consider renaming/using a clearer capability check (e.g., `target_can_split` / `target_needs_parentheses_when_broken`) to avoid accidentally skipping the preview behavior for common `a[long] += value` cases. Also, if the goal is to avoid adding parentheses when the target already split, you may need to check whether the target *will* break in the chosen layout rather than whether it has/owns parentheses.

Switching `return` to `FormatStatementsLastExpression::left_to_right` may unintentionally change long-return formatting behavior beyond the assignment preview flag (e.g., prefer breaking the expression itself vs. earlier parts). Since the PR is about assignment RHS splitting, consider whether `return` should remain on the default constructor (or be gated behind the same preview flag) to avoid style drift/regressions. If this is intentional, please add/adjust tests for `return` with complex expressions (calls, subscripts, attributes, comments) to lock in the new behavior and ensure it matches Black’s preview/stable expectations.
Typo in the snapshot comment: "parantheses" -> "parentheses". Since this is a Black-compatibility snapshot, keeping comments accurate helps avoid churn and makes diffs easier to interpret.

This snapshot change makes the `Final`-annotated assignment collapse to a single line while the `Literal[...]` assignment is now parenthesized and split. This snapshot change reverts the preview behavior for multiple-target assignments back to `a = b = (rhs)` instead of the new right-to-left splitting style described in the PR/issue (which prefers breaking/parenthesizing the RHS before splitting targets). This snapshot now expects the RHS to be parenthesized even though `cccccccc.ccccccccccccc.cccccccc` likely fits on the same line. Consider renaming to `EndBestFitParenthesize` (and updating the corresponding `Tag` enum variant and all references) for consistency with `StartBestFitParenthesize`.

Typo in the variant name: `EndBesetFitParenthesize` likely should be `EndBestFitParenthesize`. As-is, it’s easy to miss in pattern matches and makes the API inconsistent with `StartBestFitParenthesize` and `TagKind::BestFitParenthesize`. Please rename the enum variant and update all matches/usages accordingly (including any printer logic that expects a start/end pair).

`EndBesetFitParenthesize` looks like a typo ("Beset" vs "BestFit"). This is easy to miss but can cause confusion and/or mismatches with other tag names/usages across the codebase (and makes grep/search harder). Consider renaming to `EndBestFitParenthesize` (and updating all references, including the `FitsMeasurer` match arm) to keep naming consistent with `StartBestFitParenthesize` and `TagKind::BestFitParenthesize`.

This regression fixture is hard to read and may not reliably exercise the “unstable call chain formatting” behavior because it’s crammed into a single physical line with a trailing inline comment mid-chain. Consider rewriting this input to more closely match the reported minimal reproducer (multi-line call chain with the comment after an argument list) and/or add an explicit `# output:` expectation showing the intended stable formatting (Format 1 vs Format 2). As-is, it’s unclear what exact layout is being asserted and the inline comment placement could make the test brittle across unrelated formatting changes.

This change makes *all* non-multiline, non-implicit-concatenated constants default to `OptionalParentheses::BestFit` (including `None`, `True/False`, and `...`), whereas previously those were explicitly `Never` and `BestFit` was gated by `should_use_best_fit`. That’s a significant behavioral shift and matches the PR note about new deviations (e.g., `= (False)`), but it may be undesirable for simple literals and could increase parenthesization noise. Consider keeping the previous exclusions (None/bool/ellipsis) or reintroducing a cheap predicate to avoid `BestFit` for trivially short constants / when there are no trailing comments, so we don’t wrap `False`/`None` just because best-fit is now cheap.

This change makes *all* single-line f-strings eligible for `OptionalParentheses::BestFit` by removing the `should_use_best_fit(self, context)` guard. That’s a behavior change beyond just “making BestFit cheaper”: it may introduce new parenthesization for f-strings in cases previously excluded by the heuristic (e.g., short/simple f-strings, or contexts where BestFit was intentionally avoided). If the new `best_fit_parenthesize` IR is meant to replace the heuristic, consider either (a) updating this to use the new IR/decision point explicitly, or (b) keeping an equivalent guard (or a narrower condition) to avoid regressions. At minimum, please add/adjust tests covering f-string assignments/call-chains to ensure stability and to document the intended new behavior.

This changes `ExprName` to *always* request `OptionalParentheses::BestFit`, whereas previously it was gated by `should_use_best_fit(self, context)`. That’s a behavior change that can introduce new parenthesization for simple names/constants (as noted in the PR description) and may increase formatting churn. If the new `best_fit_parenthesize` IR makes this cheap, it still might be worth keeping a small heuristic here (e.g., only BestFit when the name participates in a larger expression/call chain or when comments/trivia require it) to avoid unnecessary parentheses around short identifiers. At minimum, please add/adjust tests covering `ExprName` in assignments and call chains to ensure stability and that we don’t regress into over-parenthesizing trivial names.

This call uses a fully-qualified path (`ruff_formatter::prelude::best_fit_parenthesize`) while the rest of the module relies on imports. Consider importing `best_fit_parenthesize` (or `prelude::*`) and calling it directly for consistency/readability. Also, double-check that `best_fit_parenthesize` is intended to be part of the public prelude API; if it’s more internal/unstable, prefer importing from its defining module to avoid locking the formatter to prelude re-exports.

This change drops `FormatContext` and `FormatOptions` from the imports. Please double-check that they are no longer referenced anywhere in this module (including cfg/test-only code) and remove any now-unused `PyFormatContext`-related logic accordingly. If they’re still needed indirectly (e.g., via trait bounds or type aliases in this file), this will either fail to compile or force re-adding the imports; consider running `cargo check`/`cargo test` and ensuring `rustfmt`/clippy doesn’t reintroduce them.

This replacement makes the `quick_test` fixture a single `return` statement with a very long trailing comment, which no longer exercises the call-chain / indexing / best-fit-parenthesize behavior described in the issue/PR. As a result, this test is likely to miss regressions in the formatter logic you’re changing. Consider restoring (or adding alongside) a minimal reproduction that includes a chained call/indexing expression that conditionally parenthesizes to fit (e.g., `rgrid.rio.reproject_match(...).where(...)` or similar), and keep the long-comment case as a separate targeted test if needed.

This snapshot asserts a very specific comment-placement behavior (comment moved to the call-chain line after the closing paren). Given the PR notes this is a deliberate deviation from Black, it would be good to ensure the test also covers the stability aspect that motivated the issue: i.e., formatting the output again should be identical (no toggling between the two layouts). Consider adding an explicit idempotency/stability regression (or a paired input/output case) so we catch future changes that reintroduce the Format 1/Format 2 flip-flop for call chains with trailing comments.

This adds a `u''` literal to exercise W605, but the escape sequence here (`\ `) may not be the best minimal repro for the reported fix interaction (adding `r` to a `u`-prefixed string). Consider adding a companion case that actually triggers the fixer path (e.g., a `u"..."` that Ruff would attempt to convert to a raw string) or clarifying via comment why this specific escape is expected to be flagged at column 7. Also ensure the backslash-space is intentional and stable across Python versions/parsers, since it can be easy to misread and may not reflect the original `u`+`r` prefix conflict.

This line appears to be syntactically invalid due to nested double-quoted f-strings without escaping (the inner `f"\{1}"` closes the outer string early). If the intent is to test Ruff's handling of invalid escape sequences inside nested f-strings, consider rewriting using different quote styles (e.g., outer single quotes) or escaping the inner quotes so the fixture file itself parses. Otherwise, this will fail at parse time and may not exercise the intended W605 behavior.

This removes the W605_2.py test case from the pycodestyle test matrix. Given the PR is about avoiding invalid `ur` prefixes when applying fixes, dropping a regression test here could reduce coverage and potentially mask the original failure mode. Consider keeping the test and updating/renaming the fixture (or moving it to the rule/module that now owns the behavior) so it asserts the new, correct fix output rather than removing it entirely. If the fixture is obsolete, add a brief comment or ensure an equivalent test exists elsewhere to preserve coverage for the reported syntax-error scenario.

This replacement assumes the Unicode prefix is exactly one byte/char at `string_start_location..+1`. That’s true for `u`/`U`, but please ensure `string_start_location` actually points at the prefix (not at the opening quote) for all token forms, and consider handling other redundant prefixes that can’t be combined with `r` (e.g., `ur`/`ru` in Py2, or if the lexer ever represents `u"..."` differently). If `string_start_location` can ever be at the quote, this would incorrectly replace the quote with `r` and corrupt the string. A safer approach is to derive the exact prefix span from the token (or from the original source slice) and replace that span, rather than hard-coding `+ TextSize::from(1)`.

This snapshot now expects the fixer to rewrite a `u'...'` literal to `r'...'`. That matches the PR intent (avoid invalid `ur` prefix), but it also changes the literal prefix rather than preserving `u` (i.e., `u` is dropped). Please double-check that this is the intended behavior across all supported target versions: in Python 3, `u` is redundant, but in Python 2 `u` is meaningful and `r` alone would change the type. If Ruff still supports parsing/targeting Python 2 syntax in any mode, consider asserting the target version in the test or adding a separate case to ensure we only drop `u` when the target is Python 3+. Also consider adding coverage for `U'...'`/`u"..."` and for f-strings/bytes prefixes to ensure the prefix-replacement logic is consistent.

This suggested fix produces `rf"{rf"\\{1}"}"`, i.e., a nested raw f-string inside an outer raw f-string. That’s likely invalid syntax due to the unescaped inner double quotes (and even if it parses, it’s a very surprising transformation). Consider adjusting the fixer/snapshot expectation to avoid introducing nested string literals with the same quote style; e.g., prefer changing only the inner literal (or switching the inner quotes to single quotes) so the result remains syntactically valid and minimal.

Good fix for the empty-stream case (avoids EnumSet.copyOf throwing on an empty collection). One thing to double-check: this collector will throw a NullPointerException if any mapped DispatcherType is null (EnumSet doesn't allow nulls). If nulls are possible from configuration binding, consider filtering them out or failing with a clearer message. Also, since the target is an EnumSet, you could simplify/optimize by collecting directly with Collectors.toCollection(() -> EnumSet.noneOf(DispatcherType.class)) as done here, but ensure the stream contains only DispatcherType values (no duplicates issue; EnumSet handles it).

Good fix for the empty-stream case (avoids EnumSet.copyOf throwing on an empty collection). One thing to consider: this collector will throw a NullPointerException if any mapped DispatcherType is null (EnumSet doesn't allow nulls). That’s probably fine given the source is an enum, but if you want to be defensive you could add a filter(Objects::nonNull) before collecting. Also, since the target is an EnumSet, you can simplify the mapping lambda to `.map(type -> DispatcherType.valueOf(type.name()))` (no extra parentheses) for readability.

Using `withPropertyValues("spring.security.filter.dispatcher-types:")` relies on Spring Boot's property parsing treating an empty value as an empty collection. This can be brittle across binder changes and is a bit unclear to readers. Consider making the intent explicit by setting it to an empty list (e.g., `spring.security.filter.dispatcher-types=`) or adding a short comment explaining that the trailing colon is deliberate to bind an empty collection. Also consider adding an assertion that the context has no startup failure (e.g., `assertThat(context).hasNotFailed()`) to directly cover the regression (no exception thrown).

This test reaches into `FilterRegistrationBean` internals via reflection to read `dispatcherTypes`, which makes it brittle against framework changes (field rename/type change) and forces the `@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")` cast. If possible, prefer asserting via a public API (e.g., `registration.getDispatcherTypes()` if available in the Spring version used) or by verifying observable behavior (e.g., that the filter registration is created and no exception is thrown when the property is empty). If reflection is unavoidable, consider also asserting `dispatcherTypes` is non-null before casting to make failures clearer.

Switching to `upload_chunked_stream` addresses the 100MB limit, but note that Cloudinary’s chunked upload supports/encourages passing chunk-related options (e.g., `chunk_size`) and may have different defaults/behavior than `upload_stream`. Consider explicitly setting a reasonable `chunk_size` (and possibly making it configurable) to avoid suboptimal performance/memory usage for very large files. Also verify that the callback `image` shape and error handling remain identical for this method so downstream code doesn’t break (and add/adjust tests for >100MB uploads if possible).

Appending raw string params directly can reintroduce encoding/escaping issues and can also produce malformed URLs when `url` already contains a query string (double `?`) or when `params` includes a leading `?`. Consider normalizing/merging via `URL`/`URLSearchParams` (or at least handling existing `?` and stripping a leading `?` from `params`). Also, accepting arbitrary strings here bypasses any validation/encoding guarantees—if this is only meant for pre-serialized query strings, it may be safer to make that explicit (e.g., require no leading `?`, document it, or use a branded type).

Switching from the manually stringified/split `paramObject` to passing `params` directly makes sense to avoid double-encoding, but please confirm `useGetAllDocumentsQuery` (and its underlying fetch/baseQuery) accepts a nested params object and performs correct query serialization. Previously this code effectively flattened everything into string values (and would drop/overwrite duplicate keys). If `params` can contain arrays/nested objects (e.g. filters/sort/pagination), ensure the request builder uses the same serialization format the API expects (e.g. qs with `encodeValuesOnly`) and add/adjust a regression test for `_q` containing spaces and special chars (&, /, *). Otherwise this change could alter query shape or break multi-value params.

Passing a pre-stringified query into `config.params` is risky: most HTTP clients/interceptors (axios, fetch wrappers, RTK Query baseQuery, etc.) expect `params` to be an object and will either ignore a string, serialize it again, or produce an incorrect URL (e.g., `?params=...`). This change may also reintroduce double-encoding depending on how the underlying request layer handles `params`. If the goal is to control encoding for `_q`, consider using the client’s supported hook (e.g., `paramsSerializer` / `query` string in `url`) or ensure the request layer explicitly supports `params` as a string. Also, `encode: true` is the default for `qs.stringify`; if you keep this, it may be worth documenting why `qs` is needed here and adding a regression test for spaces/`&`/`%` to confirm no double-encoding occurs end-to-end.

With the `_q` encoding removed, ensure the request layer that actually sends these params (e.g., the injected content-manager endpoints / paramsSerializer) consistently handles encoding for `_q` and other free-text params. Otherwise, searches containing reserved characters (`&`, `+`, `%`, spaces) may regress depending on how the serializer builds the query string. Also consider adding/adjusting a regression test that asserts `_q="A & B"` is transmitted once-encoded (not double-encoded) and that spaces are not persisted as literal `%20` in the DB query.

The changeset frontmatter appears to include prefixed line numbers/indentation ("           1:---" etc.). Changeset files must start with plain `---` YAML frontmatter (no leading spaces or numbering), otherwise the changeset parser may not detect the release type and the entry will be ignored. Please rewrite this file to the standard format:

---
'svelte': patch
---

fix: expose 'svelte/internal' to prevent Vite erroring on startup

Exposing `./internal` is likely needed for ecosystem compatibility, but pointing exports at `./src/...` can be problematic for published packages if `src` isn’t shipped or if consumers expect built artifacts. Please confirm `./src/internal/index.js` is included in the published files and is valid ESM for consumers. Also consider adding a `types` entry (or explicitly documenting that this path is intentionally untyped) and/or conditional exports (`development`/`default`) consistent with the rest of the package to avoid bundler/resolution inconsistencies.

Throwing at module evaluation time will hard-crash any app that (directly or indirectly) imports `svelte/internal`, which may be too aggressive for the stated “best-effort” compatibility goal. Consider exporting a minimal stub surface (or at least a proxy that throws on property access) so tooling can resolve the specifier without immediately terminating, and so you can add deprecated shims later without changing the module shape. Also, if you keep the throw, please ensure this file is wired into `package.json` `exports` as `./internal` (and possibly `./internal/*` if needed) and add a small test that `import 'svelte/internal'` resolves to this module and produces the intended error message.

Wrapping `store.subscribe` in `untrack` addresses the reported `state_unsafe_mutation` by preventing reactive tracking during subscription setup, but it also changes semantics: any reactive reads inside a custom store's `subscribe` (or inside `run` if invoked synchronously during subscribe) will no longer be tracked. That’s likely intended for migration, but please consider (a) adding a brief comment explaining why `untrack` is required here (runes/store interop + start notifier side effects), and (b) ensuring `untrack` is available/appropriate in this module for all build targets (e.g., server/legacy) and that tests cover synchronous initial emissions and start-notifier behavior. Also, if `store.subscribe` can throw, confirm whether `untrack` preserves stack traces/behavior as expected.

This changes the `add_attribute` "boolean" flag from always `1` to `1` only for known boolean attributes. That seems necessary to ensure falsy-but-valid values like `0`/`""` still render for non-boolean attrs (e.g. `value`). However, please double-check that `name` here is the *actual attribute name* (not a property name or aliased binding name) and that `boolean_attributes` includes all SSR-relevant boolean attrs (including any SVG/ARIA edge cases). Also consider adding/confirming a regression test specifically for `bind:value={0}` on `<input type="range">` and `bind:value={""}` on `<input>` to ensure `@add_attribute` now emits `value="0"`/`value=""` rather than omitting the attribute.

This updates the expected SSR output to include `value=""` on a plain `<input>`. That matches the PR goal (don’t omit falsy values like `""`/`0`), but please double-check that this sample actually has `bind:value` (or otherwise sets `value`) in its source. If it’s just a bare `<input>` with no binding/attribute, SSR shouldn’t emit a `value` attribute at all, and this expectation would mask a regression by making all inputs render `value=""` by default.

Removing the `ssrHtml` expectation makes sense if the compiler no longer emits an invalid `value` attribute on `<select bind:value>`. However, this change also reduces SSR coverage for this sample. Consider replacing the removed `ssrHtml` block with an updated expectation that asserts the SSR output is just `<select>...</select>` (i.e., no `value` attribute) so the test continues to guard against regressions in SSR serialization for `<select>` bindings.

By removing the `ssrHtml` assertion entirely, this sample no longer verifies the SSR output for `<select bind:value>` (which is part of the PR’s stated behavior change: removing invalid `value` attribute on `<select>`). Consider keeping an `ssrHtml` block that asserts the correct SSR markup (e.g., `<select>` without a `value` attribute, and the appropriate `<option selected>` state), so the regression is covered explicitly rather than implicitly via client-side tests.

By removing the `ssrHtml` assertion entirely, this test no longer verifies the SSR output for `<select bind:value>` (which is the behavior being changed in this PR: removing invalid `value` attribute on `<select>`). Consider keeping an `ssrHtml` expectation that asserts the *absence* of `value` on `<select>` while still ensuring the correct `<option>` is selected (e.g., via `selected` attribute on the matching `<option>`). Otherwise, a regression could reintroduce `value` on `<select>` without failing tests.

Removing the `ssrHtml` expectation makes this test stop asserting SSR output for `<select bind:value>`, which is the behavior being changed in this PR. That’s fine if the intent is to no longer render a `value` attribute on `<select>` (invalid HTML), but we should replace this with an updated `ssrHtml` assertion that explicitly verifies the new correct SSR markup (i.e., no `value` attribute on `<select>`, and the selected option is represented via `selected` on the matching `<option>`). Otherwise we risk losing coverage and not catching regressions in SSR selection behavior.

Removing the SSR expectation for `value` on `<select>` makes sense because `value` is not a valid attribute for `<select>` in HTML (the selected option should be represented via `<option selected>`). However, this change also reduces coverage for SSR output in this sample. Consider replacing the removed `ssrHtml` block with an updated expectation that asserts the correct SSR markup (e.g., no `value` attribute on `<select>`, and the appropriate `<option selected>` once items are present / after hydration if applicable), so the test continues to guard against regressions in SSR behavior.

Removing the `ssrHtml` expectation makes sense if SSR should no longer emit an invalid `value` attribute on `<select>`. However, dropping the assertion entirely reduces coverage for SSR output. Consider replacing this with an updated `ssrHtml` that asserts the correct SSR markup (e.g., `<select multiple>` with the appropriate `<option selected>` states, if SSR is expected to mark selections), so the test still guards against regressions in SSR behavior.

Removing the `ssrHtml` assertion weakens coverage for SSR output of `<select bind:value>`. Since the PR changes SSR behavior (removing invalid `value` attribute on `<select>`), it would be better to keep an SSR assertion but update it to the new expected markup (i.e., no `value=...` on `<select>`, and selection represented via `selected` on the matching `<option>` if applicable). Otherwise, regressions in SSR select rendering could slip through unnoticed.

Adding `ssrHtml` makes sense for asserting SSR output, but this expectation hard-codes `value=""`. Please double-check that this sample is specifically about `bind:value` (or otherwise guarantees an empty string value) in SSR; otherwise the test may become brittle across changes in default value handling. If the intent is to cover the regression for falsy values, consider also adding/adjusting a case that asserts `value="0"` for numeric bindings, since that’s the reported bug, and keep this sample focused on what it’s actually exercising.

The `ssrHtml` string is indented differently than the existing `html` snapshot (extra leading spaces before the line numbers in the diff suggest the template literal may include unintended whitespace/newlines). Since these fixtures are usually compared as exact strings, please align indentation with the `html` block (tabs/newlines) to avoid brittle failures due to whitespace differences. If the harness normalizes whitespace, consider adding a brief comment or using the same formatting style for consistency.

The new SSR expectation hard-codes `value=""` for both inputs. This is fine for covering the regression (empty string should not be omitted), but it may be brittle if attribute ordering/serialization changes. Consider asserting more explicitly what matters (presence of the `value` attribute even when empty) or adding a separate case for `0` as well, since the issue is specifically about falsy values (0/"") being dropped in SSR.

The new `ssrHtml` block is a good addition to cover SSR output, but the indentation inside the template literal looks inconsistent with the existing `html` expectation (extra leading spaces before the line numbers in the diff). This SSR expectation hard-codes `value=""` for an `<input>`. That matches the PR goal (ensure `bind:value` renders even for falsy values), but please double-check that this sample is actually exercising `bind:value` on an `<input>` (and not just spreading props) and that the intended behavior is to *always* include the attribute for empty string. If the runtime/compiler only guarantees this for `bind:value` (not generic spread), the test name/fixture should reflect that to avoid locking in broader semantics. This updates the SSR expectation to always include a `value` attribute for a bound input even when the value is an empty string, which matches the PR intent. This expected output is missing a trailing newline (as indicated by the diff). Most repo files include a final newline, and some tooling/linting can flag this. Consider adding a newline at EOF to keep formatting consistent and avoid noisy diffs in the future.

Same trailing space before `>` as the previous line; recommend removing for consistency and to avoid snapshot/fixture mismatches. Also, the file is missing a final newline (noted by git), which can fail some linters/editors—please add a newline at EOF.

This logic will always filter out *all* params: you populate `seen` with every `param.name` first, then `filter` keeps only params whose name is *not* in `seen` — which will be none. If the goal is to dedupe while preserving the first occurrence, build `seen` during filtering (or use a Map) e.g. `const seen = new Set(); const uniqueParams = params.filter(p => (seen.has(p.name) ? false : (seen.add(p.name), true)));` Also consider whether `param.name` is always a string (patterns/destructuring may not have `.name`) and handle that case explicitly.

The new `char **error` out-parameter needs a clearly defined ownership/contract in the header: is `*error` set to NULL on success, is it always initialized, and who frees it (caller vs callee, and with `free()` vs `malloc`-family)? Without this, it’s easy to leak or double-free. Consider documenting this in a comment here (or using a consistent project pattern like returning an allocated string that the caller must `free()`, and guaranteeing `*error = NULL` on success). Also consider `const char **error` if you only ever return static strings, or a structured error type if more detail is needed later.

Minor style/readability: the continuation line indentation uses a tab and aligns oddly relative to the opening paren. Match the project’s preferred wrapping style (often aligning parameters under the first argument or the opening paren) to keep headers consistent.

This returns early on keymap compilation failure, but it only frees `error` in the failure path. If `sway_keyboard_compile_keymap()` can allocate/populate `error` even when compilation succeeds, this will leak on the success path. Consider freeing `error` unconditionally after the compile attempt (e.g., `free(error); error = NULL;`) once you no longer need it, or document/guarantee that `error` is only set on failure. Also, the message uses `"(null)"` when `error` is NULL; a more user-friendly fallback like `"unknown error"` may be preferable.

xkb_context_set_user_data(context, error) is called before verifying that `context` is non-NULL. If xkb_context_new() fails, this will dereference NULL and crash. Move both xkb_context_set_user_data() and xkb_context_set_log_fn() to after the `sway_assert(context, ...)` check (or guard them with `if (context)`). Also consider whether `error` can be NULL (as in current callers) and ensure the log handler tolerates that (it currently does via `if (data && !*data)`).

The example nests double quotes inside a single-quoted command string (`'swaymsg "output * dpms off"'`). This is easy to get wrong for users and can behave differently depending on the shell used to execute `exec` (and how swayidle spawns commands). Consider simplifying the quoting in the example (e.g., `timeout 600 'swaymsg output "*" dpms off'` or `timeout 600 "swaymsg 'output * dpms off'"`) and/or add a short note that the command is executed via `/bin/sh -c` so users know which quoting rules apply.

Adding `dpms_state` to `struct output_config` is fine, but please ensure it has a well-defined default value when an output config is created/merged (e.g., `DPMS_IGNORE`) so existing configs don’t accidentally force DPMS on/off due to uninitialized memory. Also consider naming/semantics: `DPMS_IGNORE` implies “don’t touch DPMS”, but the rest of the code should clearly distinguish between “unset” vs “explicitly on/off” when applying output configs (especially during reload).

Adding `wlr_idle` to the server struct is fine, but please ensure its lifecycle is clearly handled: it should be created during server initialization (after the display/event loop is set up) and destroyed/cleaned up on shutdown to avoid leaks. Also consider whether this should be optional/feature-gated (e.g., if built without the wlroots idle protocol support) and document the ownership semantics (server owns this pointer). If other code will access it, make sure it’s initialized before any input/seat code that may emit idle activity notifications.

Adding both libsystemd and libelogind as optional deps is fine, but this currently allows both to be found and linked into swayidle simultaneously. These libraries provide overlapping logind APIs and can conflict at link/runtime. Consider making them mutually exclusive (prefer systemd when both are present, or add a Meson option to select one), and ensure downstream code uses a single backend based on that selection.

This protocol is named "idle" but the interface is KDE/KWin-specific (org_kde_kwin_idle). Please confirm this is the intended stable API for sway to expose. If this is meant to be a sway/wlroots protocol, consider using a compositor-neutral namespace (e.g., ext-idle-notify / zwp_idle_inhibit / a sway-specific prefix) to avoid implying KDE ownership and to reduce compatibility/expectation issues. Also ensure the project has the right to vendor this XML under LGPL and that the build/install system treats it consistently with other protocol XMLs (naming, install path, and generated headers).

Adding `idle.xml` here assumes the file is available in the current working directory for this Meson subdir. If `idle.xml` is a new protocol shipped by this repo, consider referencing it via an explicit path (e.g. `meson.current_source_dir()` or a `protocols/` subdir) to avoid build failures when Meson is invoked from different directories or when the file is moved. Also ensure the protocol is installed/packaged consistently (and that any corresponding server header generation is wired up if needed).

Logging `dpms_state` with `%d` makes the output hard to interpret and potentially misleading if the enum values change. Consider logging a human-readable string (e.g., "on"/"off"/"unset") or using a helper to stringify the enum. Also ensure `output->dpms_state` is always initialized (including default/unspecified) so this log doesn't print an uninitialized value.

This added blank line appears to be a no-op formatting change. If it’s intentional for readability, consider ensuring it matches the project’s style (e.g., grouping related defaults) and avoid introducing whitespace-only diffs unless needed. If unintentional, please drop it to keep the diff minimal.

Using `src->dpms_state != 0` as the merge condition is brittle and likely wrong given the later use of `DPMS_IGNORE`. If `DPMS_IGNORE` is defined as 0 (common for “unset/ignore”), this condition prevents explicitly propagating an “ignore” value and also conflates “unset” with a valid enum value. Prefer comparing against the explicit sentinel (e.g. `!= DPMS_IGNORE`) or introduce a separate “is_set” flag / use an optional value so merge semantics are unambiguous. Also ensure `dst->dpms_state` is initialized to `DPMS_IGNORE` so the default behavior is consistent.

This assumes `cursor->seat->input->server->idle` is always initialized and non-NULL. If sway can be built/run without the idle interface (or if initialization fails), this will crash on any pointer motion. Consider guarding the call (e.g. `if (server->idle) ...`) or ensuring `server->idle` is always created before any input listeners are active. Also, this line is duplicated across many handlers in this file; it may be worth factoring into a small helper like `cursor_notify_activity(cursor)` to reduce repetition and keep the null-check consistent.

This unconditionally dereferences `keyboard->seat_device->sway_seat->input->server->idle`. If the idle interface isn't initialized (or is optional depending on build/runtime), this can crash on key events. Consider guarding with a NULL check (and possibly also `wlr_seat`) before calling `wlr_idle_notify_activity(...)`, or ensuring `server->idle` is always created during server init. Also verify the second argument matches the wlroots API version you target (some versions take a `wlr_seat*`, others may differ), since this PR mentions required wlroots changes.

Consider handling failure/NULL return from wlr_idle_create() and storing/initializing the field defensively. If wlr_idle_create can fail (OOM or backend constraints), server_init should either abort with a clear log message or gracefully disable idle/DPMS features. Also ensure server->idle is destroyed during server shutdown to avoid leaks, and that struct sway_server has this member initialized to NULL in all code paths.

Bug: the error message prints `optarg`, but this function parses `argv[1]` via `strtoul`. `optarg` is unrelated here (and may be NULL/leftover from getopt), so the log can be wrong or crash. Use `argv[1]` (or store the original string) in the message instead.

swayidle is a Wayland *client* daemon; linking against `wayland_server` is likely unnecessary and increases the dependency surface (and may fail on systems without server dev files). Unless `main.c` explicitly uses server-side APIs, consider dropping `wayland_server` here and keeping only `wayland_client` (and any other required client-side deps).

Manpage section headers should be consistently formatted. Here `#EVENTS` is missing a space after `#`, unlike `# NAME`, `# SYNOPSIS`, etc. This may render incorrectly depending on the scdoc parser. Consider changing to `# EVENTS` for consistency and correct formatting.

The example’s indentation uses a tab here while surrounding lines use spaces, which can render oddly in the generated manpage and is inconsistent with the rest of the block. Consider normalizing indentation (spaces only) for the whole example. Also, since this is a line continuation example, ensure the backslash alignment is consistent so copy/paste works cleanly.

Centering for opposing anchors is correct, but this uses integer division and can introduce a 1px bias when bounds/box sizes are odd. Consider using a helper (e.g. wlr_box_center) or explicitly document/handle rounding (e.g. compute with int64_t and decide whether to floor/ceil) to avoid subtle off-by-one placement differences across axes/outputs.

This branch now triggers whenever box.width == 0, regardless of anchors. That matches the earlier sizing logic, but please ensure margins can't make the computed width negative (e.g. left+right > bounds.width). If that can happen, clamp to 0 to avoid underflow/invalid geometry and potential downstream issues.

Skipping margins when both opposing anchors are set changes behavior from the previous code path (which applied margins when both anchors were set and size was 0). If the intent is "opposing anchors => centered" then this makes sense, but it would be good to add a short comment explaining why margins are intentionally ignored here (protocol expectation/compat with other compositors), and consider whether margins should still affect the centered position (e.g. center within the margin-adjusted bounds) rather than being fully ignored.
Same as horizontal: bounds.height - (top+bottom) can go negative if margins exceed available space. Consider clamping to 0 (and possibly adjusting y) to keep the box valid.

Adding this include is fine for the new touch event types, but please ensure it’s actually required in this header (i.e., the header needs the full struct definitions). If only pointers are used (e.g., `struct wlr_event_touch_motion *`), consider forward-declaring the event structs instead to avoid pulling in extra wlroots headers and increasing rebuild surface. Also double-check wlroots version compatibility: `wlr/types/wlr_touch.h` and these event structs must exist across the minimum supported wlroots version for this Sway branch.

These assignments were moved outside the `if (seat->touch_id == event->touch_id)` guard, which means *any* touch motion event (including other touch points/IDs) will overwrite `seat->touch_x/y`. That can break multi-touch tracking and also corrupt the coordinates used for the active grab/seatop logic. Consider updating `seat->touch_x/y` only when `event->touch_id` matches the tracked `seat->touch_id` (or store per-touch-id state if multi-touch is intended).

These new seatop touch dispatchers are fine, but please keep the ordering consistent with the rest of the seatop_* wrappers (typically down/up/motion or down/motion/up) and match the naming/placement used for pointer/tablet handlers. Also consider whether these should mirror the pointer wrappers by performing any common seat-level bookkeeping (e.g., updating focus/coordinates) before delegating, rather than being pure pass-throughs—otherwise different seatop implementations may duplicate logic or miss required state updates. If no shared bookkeeping is needed, a short comment explaining that these are intentionally thin wrappers would help future maintainers.

This uses `seat->touch_x`/`seat->touch_y` rather than the coordinates from the `wlr_event_touch_down`. That assumes those seat fields have already been updated for this event and are in the same coordinate space expected by `node_at_coords` (layout coords). If the seat fields are updated later (or are per-slot), this can resolve the wrong surface/sx/sy on touch-down, especially with multi-touch. Consider using the event’s coordinates directly (and converting to layout coords if needed), or explicitly update `seat->touch_x/y` from `event` before calling `node_at_coords`. Also ensure this is correct for multiple touch points (slot/id) since `seat->touch_x/y` looks like a single global position.

Using `sway_assert(found, ...)` here can crash sway if we ever receive a touch motion for an ID we didn't track (e.g., compositor state desync, canceled touch sequence, or a touch motion arriving after seatop transition). Since this is input-path code, it should fail gracefully: if not found, just ignore the event (or log at debug/warn) and return. Also consider handling `wlr_event_touch_cancel` to clear `point_events` so we don't leak/keep stale IDs and to avoid this assert firing.

This logic appears to re-introduce the old (incorrect per issue) behavior for `Preserve`: if all specifiers were removed as type-only/unused, it flips `is_side_effect_import` to `true` and keeps the now-empty import. But `tsc` with `importsNotUsedAsValues: "preserve"` still drops completely unused import identifiers; it does not keep an empty import unless it was originally a side-effect import (`import "x"`). Consider removing this branch entirely (or gating it only on `self.is_side_effect_import` being true from the original AST), and ensure `Preserve` only affects elision of type-only vs value-used specifiers, not turning a non-side-effect import into a side-effect import.

This test sets `import_not_used_as_values = Preserve` but the expected output still removes the unused named import (`{ a }`) and converts the type-only import into a side-effect import (`import './types';`). That matches the issue description (“never preserve completely unused import identifiers”), but it’s easy to misread as preserving unused bindings. Consider adding a short comment (or renaming the test) clarifying the intended semantics: `Preserve` keeps *value* imports that are used as values, but does not keep unused specifiers; type-only imports may be elided or rewritten to side-effect imports only when needed to preserve module side effects. Also, if the goal is to ensure side effects are preserved, it may be worth using a non-empty module (or a fixture) to make the intent explicit rather than relying on the transform’s generic behavior.

Changing the default from `Preserve` to `Remove` is a breaking behavioral change (as noted in the issue/PR). Please ensure this is reflected in user-facing docs/changelog and that any config parsing/mapping to `tsc`'s `importsNotUsedAsValues` semantics is consistent. Also consider adding/adjusting tests to lock in the intended behavior (e.g., completely unused import identifiers should be removed even under `preserve`, while value-used imports are retained).

Good fix to ensure we still traverse children when the `MemberExpr` object is an ident but not a tracked namespace import. However, this introduces a subtle behavior change vs the previous `e => e.visit_mut_children_with(self)` fallback: now we call `n.visit_mut_children_with(self)` from inside the `Expr::Member` arm, which will re-walk both `obj` and `prop`. That’s probably fine, but please double-check it won’t cause redundant work or unexpected recursion if `n` is replaced earlier in this arm in future refactors. Consider instead visiting `obj`/`prop` directly (or calling `n.visit_mut_children_with(self)` once in a single fallback path) to keep traversal structure consistent and avoid double-visiting risks.

This test hard-codes the injected value as a string containing JS source (`"{ 'var': 'value' }"`). That matches the current `constModules` API, but it’s brittle: formatting/printing changes (quotes, whitespace, trailing commas) can cause unrelated failures. Consider adding a second assertion that focuses on semantic behavior (e.g., that the import is eliminated and the member access is preserved) or using a more canonical object literal string (double quotes / no extra spaces) to reduce printer sensitivity. Also, if `constModules` supports JSON-like input, prefer a JSON string (`{"var":"value"}`) to avoid single-quote edge cases.

Bumping the crate version is fine, but please ensure this aligns with the workspace/root versioning and release process (e.g., if other crates depend on `swc_ecma_parser` with an exact version, they may need coordinated bumps). Also consider whether this should be handled by the repo’s release tooling/changelog automation rather than manually in a PR focused on a parser fix.

This change broadens when `?` is accepted after an object binding pattern by checking `ctx().in_declare` in addition to `syntax().dts()`. That seems aligned with the issue (ambient/declare contexts), but please confirm `in_declare` is only true for ambient declarations and not for non-ambient `declare` usages that still have implementation signatures (e.g., `declare function f(...) {}` in non-d.ts parsing, if allowed). If `in_declare` can be set in contexts where an implementation body exists, this may incorrectly allow optional binding patterns in real implementations. Consider tightening the condition to the exact ambient context used for signature-only parsing (e.g., a dedicated `is_ambient_context`/`in_ambient_context` flag) or add a regression test covering both: (1) `declare namespace`/`declare class` constructor signature (should allow), and (2) non-ambient implementation signature (should still error).

This change broadens when `?` is accepted after an array binding pattern from only `syntax().dts()` to also `ctx().in_declare`. That seems aligned with the issue (ambient `declare` contexts), but please double-check that `in_declare` is the right signal for *ambient* contexts and not also set for non-ambient `declare` usages where the TS rule should still apply. Also consider whether other ambient constructs (e.g. `declare global`, `declare module`, `declare namespace`) reliably set `in_declare` at this point in parsing. If not, you may need a more specific context flag (e.g. `in_ambient_context`) or ensure `in_declare` is propagated for all ambient declarations. Finally, ensure the same relaxation is applied consistently for object binding patterns and other binding-pattern parameter parsing paths, otherwise the fix may be incomplete/inconsistent.

This test case is meant to cover “optional binding pattern parameter in an ambient context”, but the parameter here is an *array binding pattern* (`[ t ]`) rather than the object destructuring pattern shown in the issue description. Consider adding (or replacing with) a `declare namespace` + `constructor({ ... }?: { ... })` fixture to match the reported regression more closely, and keep this array-pattern case as an additional coverage if desired. Also, the file is missing a trailing newline, which can cause noisy diffs and some tooling/lint failures—please add one.

This fixture is for issue #709 (optional binding pattern in ambient context), but the AST here shows an `ArrayPattern` parameter with `optional: true`. The issue description/example uses an *object* binding pattern (`{ bubbles, cancelable, composed }?: ...`) in a `declare namespace`/ambient context. Consider updating this test input/expected AST to match the reported repro (ObjectPattern + ambient `declare` context) so the test actually covers the intended regression and doesn't accidentally validate a different code path (array destructuring).

This line contains an unexpected trailing `w`, which will make the fixture invalid TypeScript and likely cause the test to fail for the wrong reason (lex/parse error) rather than exercising the optional binding pattern behavior. Remove the stray character so the input is syntactically correct. Also add a final newline to avoid `\ No newline at end of file` noise in diffs and keep fixtures consistent.

This fixture asserts that the destructuring parameter is marked optional (`optional: true`). Please double-check that this test is actually exercising the reported scenario (optional binding pattern in an *ambient* context). In the original issue the ambient-ness comes from `declare namespace` / `declare class`, but this JSON AST shows only a `declare: true` class and no surrounding `TsNamespaceDeclaration`. If the intent is to cover `declare namespace __event { ... }`, consider adding a separate fixture (or adjusting this one) that includes the namespace wrapper so the parser context matches the bug report. Otherwise the test may pass without guarding the ambient-context-specific behavior.

`is_simple_assign` currently treats only `Ident = ...` as "simple". That may be fine for the minifier use-case, but the name is a bit broad: it excludes other non-destructuring simple LHS forms like `obj.prop = ...` / `obj[expr] = ...` which are also `=` assignments. Consider either (a) renaming to something more specific (e.g. `is_simple_ident_assign`) to avoid future misuse, or (b) documenting explicitly that this is intentionally limited to identifier LHS. Also, if this is used for correctness checks, ensure it doesn't need to exclude `this`/`super`/TS-specific nodes (depending on what `as_ident()` returns).

This inserts a new var-usage entry keyed by `(id.0, *ctx)` but only if `self.data.vars.get(&id)` exists. Two concerns:
1) Key consistency: `get(&id)` suggests the map is keyed by `Id`, while `insert((id.0, *ctx), ...)` suggests a different key type (tuple). If `self.data.vars` is actually keyed by `(JsWord, SyntaxContext)`, consider using the same key type for the lookup (e.g. `self.data.vars.get(&(id.0.clone(), id.1))` or similar) to avoid silently skipping updates.
2) Overwrite risk: `insert` will replace any existing usage for the new context. If the new context key can already exist (e.g. multiple decls/pats or repeated passes), consider using `entry` and merging counts/flags instead of overwriting.
Also, cloning `usage` and then mutating is fine, but if `VarUsage` is large, consider cloning only when needed (or constructing a new usage from fields) to reduce overhead in hot minifier paths.

Setting `fn_scope` to `n.span.ctxt` assumes the function’s `SyntaxContext` is the correct identifier for “current function scope”. In SWC, `span.ctxt` is primarily a hygiene/marking context and may not uniquely represent a function boundary (e.g., nested blocks, transforms that change marks, or cases where the function body introduces a different scope context). If this field is used to detect “same var defined in outer scope”, consider deriving `fn_scope` from the actual scope tracking mechanism (e.g., the scope created/entered for the function in your scope analyzer) rather than the span’s context, or at least document why `span.ctxt` is a reliable proxy here. Also ensure `fn_scope` is updated for all function-like nodes (arrow, function decl/expr, class methods, constructors) consistently; otherwise this initialization may fix only some cases and regress others.

This scope check (`self.ctx.fn_scope != left_fn_scope`) is a bit suspicious: it only compares function-scope identity, but the reported bug is about a name being defined in an outer scope and then reordered across a call. Two different blocks within the same function will have the same `fn_scope`, so this may still allow unsafe reordering when the conflicting binding is in an outer *block* scope (or when `left_id` resolves to a different binding due to shadowing). Consider using a more precise scope/binding identity (e.g., comparing the actual resolved `Id`/binding mark for `left_id` vs the binding being referenced/assigned, or tracking lexical scope depth), or at least document why function-scope granularity is sufficient here. Also, `unwrap_or_default()` silently treats unknown vars as `false`/default; if `left_id` is missing from `data.vars`, we may incorrectly allow the optimization—might be safer to conservatively abort when the var metadata is unavailable.

This unconditionally overwrites `in_fn_scope` every time the var is encountered. If the same `Id` can be visited from multiple contexts (e.g., multiple passes, hoisted/merged info, or shadowing edge cases), this may record the *last* seen function scope rather than the defining/first scope, which could make the “outer scope var” detection flaky. Consider only setting it when it’s still the default/empty value, or asserting consistency (e.g., `debug_assert!(v.in_fn_scope == Default::default() || v.in_fn_scope == ctx.fn_scope)`) to avoid silently changing scope info. Also worth adding a short comment clarifying whether `in_fn_scope` is intended to represent declaration scope vs. last-use scope.

This change rewrites `setShape` from passing an expression (with side-effect assignments to `xmin/points`) plus an object literal, to passing a single object literal with an assignment in the `x` property initializer. While semantically similar, it changes evaluation order/shape of the AST and may mask the original regression rather than asserting it. For a minifier bugfix test, consider adding a smaller, purpose-built fixture that directly exercises the seq-inliner/outer-scope var case (like the reported `depth` example) instead of relying on large bench snapshots, and ensure the test asserts the *minified output* preserves statement order/side effects. If this file is intended as a golden output, please clarify whether it is input or expected output and keep transformations consistent across the suite.

This change alters evaluation order by moving the `axisChild = ...` assignment inside parentheses and then immediately reading `.length` from the call result. While it’s likely intended to preserve semantics, it also changes what `axisChild` refers to at the moment `.length` is accessed (array result vs. assigned var) and makes the expression harder to read/maintain. Since this is a golden/minified fixture, please ensure the new output is actually the expected minifier output for the regression (and not an accidental re-association). Consider adding/using a smaller targeted regression test for the seq-inliner bug rather than relying on large fixture diffs, and verify that `findAxisComponents` is only invoked once and `axisChild` remains the array used later in the ternary.

This rewrite changes `configDef` from being assigned an object and then having `.get` set, to assigning `.get` on the object literal in one expression. While semantically equivalent in most cases, it subtly changes evaluation/observable behavior if `configDef` is a getter/setter on an outer scope object, or if `configDef` is used by reference between the assignment and the `.get` write (e.g., via proxies/debug hooks). Since this is a golden minified fixture, please ensure this transformation is intentional and stable across minifier passes; otherwise it may cause fixture churn. If the goal is just to reduce bytes, consider keeping the original two-step assignment in fixtures unless the minifier is guaranteed to emit this form deterministically.

This change removes the redundant `code = (code = ...) + ...` self-assignment and directly passes the concatenated string. That’s good for readability, but note it also removes the side effect of assigning to the local `code` variable (which might be referenced later in the same scope in the original source). In this minified fixture it likely doesn’t matter, but please double-check that `code` isn’t used after this `if` in the surrounding function; otherwise the fixture would no longer reflect the original semantics. If `code` is intentionally unused, consider also dropping `code` from the `var` list to avoid dead locals in the fixture.

This refactor inlines `slotName` into the `_t(...)` call and stops assigning `slotName` entirely. That can be fine, but it also changes the order/shape of side effects: previously `slotName` was assigned before `res`, and `slotName` existed as a local binding (even if only for debugging). Since this is a benchmark fixture, consider whether you want to preserve the original variable assignment structure to reduce diffs across versions. If `slotName` is no longer used anywhere, also remove it from the `var` declaration (see line 3104) to keep the fixture consistent.

After inlining `slotName` at line 3094, this `var` list drops `slotName`, which is good. However, ensure there are no remaining references to `slotName` in this function (including in branches not shown here). If there are none, consider also checking whether other locals in this list (e.g., `children1`, `bind$$1`) are still needed post-transform; keeping dead locals in a minified fixture can mask regressions in the minifier’s dead-code elimination.

This fixture change introduces a new top-level binding (`obj`) in the minified output. Please double-check that the corresponding input fixture actually requires this extra alias (i.e., the minifier now preserves a reference to the pre-reassigned `ClassB` value) and that `obj` cannot collide with any existing binding in the same scope for this test. If the goal is to validate the seq-inliner abort behavior, consider adding/adjusting the input fixture (or an assertion comment) to make it clear why an additional alias is expected, otherwise this may look like an unrelated renaming/regression in the snapshot.

The fixture input appears to include prefixed line numbers/indentation (e.g., `           1:`) as literal source text. SWC fixture tests typically expect plain JS/TS code without these prefixes; otherwise the parser will treat `1:` as a label statement and the rest as code, which changes the AST and may invalidate the regression test. Please remove the numeric prefixes/leading spaces so the file contains only the intended program (`let depth = 0; ... blackbox(foo);`).

This expected output still introduces a sequence expression in the return (`return depth -= 1, k;`). While it preserves evaluation order (decrement happens before returning `k`), it changes the original control-flow shape and can be fragile if future transforms try to further inline/reorder sequences (the original bug was caused by such reordering). Consider keeping the statement form in the fixture output (i.e., `depth -= 1; return k;`) to more directly assert the intended behavior and reduce the chance of regressions from additional sequence optimizations.

This change reorders the `var` declarators (`a2` moved after `b1`). While `var` hoisting makes this semantically equivalent, this is a minifier output fixture: reordering can make the fixture unnecessarily brittle across minifier tweaks and may not be relevant to the regression being tested. Consider keeping the original declarator order unless the new order is required to validate the specific bugfix (seq inliner / outer-scope var collision). If it is required, add a short comment in the corresponding input/expected fixture or test description explaining why the order matters.

This fixture change introduces `nameSpace = argName = 'data'` and also reorders `str = templateString` before `argName` is used. Since this is a golden minified output, please double-check that the new ordering/extra alias (`nameSpace`) is intentional and stable across runs (i.e., not dependent on non-deterministic symbol generation). If the goal is to avoid outer-scope collisions, consider ensuring the minifier consistently renames the *parameter* used in `Function(argName, ...)` as well, otherwise `Function(argName, ...)` still depends on `argName` specifically and `nameSpace` is just an additional variable that could be optimized away or changed by future passes.

This fixture change is effectively a full-line replacement of a huge minified bundle, which makes the test very brittle and hard to review/maintain. Since the PR is about sequence inlining across outer-scope vars, consider adding (or switching to) a much smaller, purpose-built regression test that directly encodes the reported `depth`/`visit` ordering bug (and any outer-scope shadowing case) rather than updating a large Mapbox snapshot. If keeping this snapshot is necessary, it would help to also include a minimal dedicated test alongside it so future unrelated minifier changes don’t require rebaselining this massive output.

This change alters evaluation order by turning `interpolateFn = ... , interpolateFn && ...` into `(interpolateFn = ...) && ...`. While it’s likely equivalent here, it’s not always safe if the assignment expression can yield falsy values that still need to be stored/used later, or if downstream code relies on `interpolateFn` being assigned even when falsy. Since this is a golden output fixture, please ensure the corresponding input test and minifier transform are updated to guarantee this rewrite is semantically correct for all cases, and consider adding a targeted regression test for the original bug (outer-scope var + seq inliner) rather than relying on large project output diffs.

This change alters the short-circuit chain by turning the wrapperState assignment into a negated guard (`|| !(state = ...) || ...`). While it’s logically equivalent in many cases, it subtly changes the value that participates in the `||` chain (boolean vs the assigned object) and can affect behavior if `_wrapperState` can be falsy-but-not-null (e.g. `0`, `""`, `false`) or if downstream relies on the exact expression value (even though this is a statement context). Consider keeping the original pattern `|| (state = ... , !state || ...)` to preserve semantics more directly, or ensure via invariants/tests that `_wrapperState` is always an object/null/undefined so the negation is safe.

Adding `pub fn_scope: SyntaxContext` changes `Ctx`'s public API and likely requires updating all constructors/initializers (including any `Default` impls) to set a meaningful value. Please ensure this is initialized consistently (e.g., to `SyntaxContext::empty()` or the current function's scope context) and consider documenting what `fn_scope` represents (function-level scope vs. current lexical scope) to avoid misuse. If this is only needed internally for the minifier fix, consider reducing visibility (e.g., `pub(crate)`/`pub(super)`) to limit API surface.

Setting `fn_scope` to `n.span.ctxt` looks like it’s intended to track the current function’s hygiene context, but please double-check that `span.ctxt` is the right discriminator for “function scope” in all cases (e.g., nested functions, arrow fns, methods, and code produced by transforms). If `fn_scope` is later used to decide whether an identifier is shadowed by an outer binding, relying solely on `SyntaxContext` may be too coarse or too fine depending on how SWC assigns contexts. Consider adding a brief comment explaining why `n.span.ctxt` is correct here, and ensure there are tests covering nested scopes / shadowing across different syntax contexts so this doesn’t regress with other transforms.

This change is purely a formatting/idiom tweak, but the new one-liner is getting quite long and reduces readability in a hot/central utility method. Consider keeping the multi-line block (or wrapping with braces) to match surrounding style and make future diffs/blame easier. No functional change expected here, but please ensure rustfmt is run so line wrapping is consistent across the file.

Relying on dent->d_type == DT_DIR is not robust: many filesystems (and some sysfs configurations) can return DT_UNKNOWN, which would prevent recursion into subdirectories and reintroduce the original “missing ATTRS in subdirs” behavior. Consider handling DT_UNKNOWN by falling back to fstatat()/lstat() on the entry to determine if it’s a directory, or just attempt to opendir() and recurse on success. This would make attribute-walk complete across environments.

This pointer↔mode encoding relies on subtracting 1 from an integer representation of a pointer. Please ensure the macro is safe for the sentinel values you intend to support: if callers ever pass NULL to PTR_TO_MODE(), this underflows to (uintptr_t)-1 and then casts to mode_t, which may become MODE_INVALID (good) but is implicit/fragile. Consider either (a) explicitly documenting that NULL maps to MODE_INVALID and is the only special value, and/or (b) guarding in the macro (e.g., (p) ? ... : MODE_INVALID) to avoid relying on wraparound. Also, since mode_t may be narrower than uintptr_t, casting (uintptr_t)-1 to mode_t may not round-trip cleanly; if round-tripping is required, add a static assertion or constrain usage to sentinel-only conversions.

This hunk removes the public MODE_INVALID sentinel. Please ensure all users were migrated to an equivalent (and consistent) invalid-mode representation; otherwise this can silently change behavior in callers that relied on MODE_INVALID for initialization/comparison. If the intent is to stop exporting this macro from parse-util.h, consider either (a) moving it to a more appropriate header where it’s still available to remaining users, or (b) replacing it with a helper (e.g., mode_is_valid()/MODE_T_INVALID) to avoid relying on (mode_t)-1, which can be a valid value on some platforms/ABIs. Also verify no out-of-tree users depend on this header macro if it’s part of the installed API.

This uses a synchronous netlink dump (sd_netlink_call) which can block link setup if the kernel/netlink is slow or under load. Since this is called during link initialization, consider using the async dump API (sd_netlink_call_async / sd_netlink_add_match + dump) or at least a bounded timeout instead of `0` (infinite) to avoid potential hangs. If `0` is intentional, please add a short comment explaining why an unbounded wait is safe here.

Netlink address flags are typically a 32-bit field; storing them in `unsigned char` risks truncating flags and mis-detecting `IFA_F_TENTATIVE` (and other bits) on some kernels/architectures. Prefer `uint32_t flags` (or `unsigned flags` if the API expects that) to match the underlying type returned by `sd_rtnl_message_addr_get_flags()`.

The new setting name here (`SystemCallLog=`) doesn’t match the issue/PR description, which talks about `SystemCallErrorAction=` (and existing `SystemCallErrorNumber=`). Please double-check the final directive name(s) exposed to users and keep this list consistent with the actual unit file option implemented and documented elsewhere (e.g., `systemd.exec(5)`). If the intended interface is `SystemCallErrorAction=LOG`, then this line should likely reflect that instead of introducing a separate `SystemCallLog=` knob.

The wording here is confusing/incorrect: “all system calls executed by the unit processes for the listed ones will be logged” reads like *all* syscalls are logged, and “for the listed ones” is ambiguous. Consider rephrasing to something like “system calls executed by the unit processes that match the listed names will be logged” (and similarly for the inverted case). Also, since this is a new directive, it would be good to explicitly state what “logged” means (e.g., kernel/audit log via seccomp SCMP_ACT_LOG) and any kernel/libseccomp version requirements/limitations, to set expectations for users.

The syscall number is stored in the hashmap key with a +1 offset (PTR_TO_INT(id) - 1). This encoding is non-obvious and easy to break if the producer side changes. Please add a short comment explaining the +1 sentinel scheme (e.g., to avoid NULL/0 keys) and consider using a typed helper/macro (e.g., SECCOMP_SYSCALL_ID_TO_NR()/NR_TO_ID()) to centralize the conversion. Also, ensure PTR_TO_INT() is safe here on all supported architectures (pointer-sized storage); if syscall numbers can exceed int range, use PTR_TO_LONG()/PTR_TO_UINT64() consistently with how keys are inserted.

This loads a second seccomp filter after other sandboxing steps, but note that `apply_syscall_filter()` is called later and will also call `seccomp_load_syscall_filter_set_raw()`. If that helper creates and loads a *new* filter context each time (rather than adding rules to an existing ctx), the later `SystemCallFilter=` load may overwrite/replace the earlier `SystemCallLog=` program, effectively disabling logging. Please verify the helper’s semantics and, if needed, merge log+filter rules into a single seccomp context (or extend the helper to accept multiple rule sets/actions) so both directives compose predictably.

Storing per-syscall LOG rules in a generic `Hashmap *` is a bit underspecified and can become error-prone (what are the key/value types, ownership, and lifetime?). Consider using a more explicit type (e.g., `Set*` if it’s just membership, or a typed hashmap wrapper) and add a short comment documenting key/value semantics and who frees it. Also ensure this new field is covered in ExecContext init/copy/merge/free paths to avoid leaks or missing propagation when units are reloaded.

Adding a new unit setting is fine, but please double-check naming/semantics consistency with the feature request/PR description. The issue/PR talks about a generic "SystemCallErrorAction=" (KILL|LOG|errno), while this introduces a separate boolean-ish looking directive "SystemCallLog". If the intent is to support SCMP_ACT_LOG as an action, consider whether this should instead be integrated into the existing SystemCallErrorNumber/"action" mechanism (or renamed to something that clearly indicates it controls the seccomp default action). Also ensure the corresponding parser (config_parse_syscall_log) is wired into ExecContext, documented in man pages, and that the non-seccomp build path (warn_compat) matches the intended UX (e.g., warning message mentions SystemCallLog explicitly).

The flag combination includes `SECCOMP_PARSE_PERMISSIVE`, which typically makes unknown/unsupported syscall names non-fatal. For a logging-only feature, that may hide configuration mistakes (typos) and silently reduce coverage. Consider dropping `SECCOMP_PARSE_PERMISSIVE` here (or gating it behind an explicit compatibility mode) so invalid syscall names are reported as errors (or at least warnings with a clear indication that the entry was ignored). If permissive behavior is intentional, please add a comment explaining why it's needed for `SystemCallLog=` specifically.

Adding a new parser prototype here is fine, but please ensure the corresponding implementation is actually wired into the unit file parser tables (e.g., gperf/lookup in load-fragment.c) and that the naming matches the new directive semantics. In particular, if the PR introduces a more generic "SystemCallErrorAction=" (as described in the issue/PR), consider whether "config_parse_syscall_log" is too narrow/misleading and should instead be something like "config_parse_syscall_error_action" to avoid proliferating per-action directives and keep the API consistent with existing config_parse_syscall_errno.

Adding "SystemCallLog" here makes the parser treat it like RestrictAddressFamilies/SystemCallFilter (i.e., list parsing with allow/deny semantics). Please double-check that this matches the intended unit file syntax and the actual D-Bus property type for SystemCallLog. If SystemCallLog is meant to be a boolean or an action selector (LOG vs KILL/errno), it likely needs its own parsing branch rather than being handled as a syscall set. Also ensure the corresponding property is exposed consistently in unit load/DBus (e.g., in org.freedesktop.systemd1.* interface and unit file gperf tables), otherwise this will accept the directive in some paths but not others.

This branch compares `action` to `SCMP_ACT_LOG`, but the surrounding code uses `error` to decide the action (`if (error == SECCOMP_ERROR_NUMBER_KILL) ...`). This looks like a variable mix-up: either the condition should be `else if (error == SECCOMP_ERROR_NUMBER_LOG)` (or whatever constant represents the LOG error action in this codebase), or the earlier `if` should also be based on `action` for consistency. As written, LOG may never trigger (or may trigger incorrectly) depending on how `action`/`error` are populated. Please verify the intended control flow and use the correct variable/enum for selecting LOG.

Adding "SystemCallLog" here makes systemctl treat it like other strv-based list properties (e.g., SystemCallFilter) for pretty-printing. Please double-check that the actual D-Bus property name exported by PID1 is indeed "SystemCallLog" (and not e.g. "SystemCallLogFilter"/"SystemCallLog" vs a unit file directive name), otherwise systemctl will never hit this branch and output may be inconsistent. If the property is new, consider also updating any other places in systemctl that special-case syscall filter-related properties (e.g., expected_value comparisons, help/--property completion, tests) so the new property is handled consistently across output modes.

Marking these files as `binary` disables text-based diffs/whitespace checks, but it does not automatically enforce Git LFS. If these dmidecode dumps are expected to be large or numerous, consider using `filter=lfs diff=lfs merge=lfs -text` (or otherwise ensuring size limits are respected) to avoid bloating the repo. Also, if the intent is only to prevent EOL normalization, `-text` may be sufficient/preferable to `binary` depending on desired diff behavior.

This rule imports unconditionally for every device in the `dmi` subsystem, which may be broader than intended and can add overhead at boot/udev events. Consider narrowing the match to the specific DMI device(s) that actually represent the memory array/devices (e.g., matching on `KERNEL=="dmi/id"` or a specific `DEVPATH`/`ATTR{}`/`ENV{}` that identifies the SMBIOS memory-related node), and/or gating the import behind a presence check for the helper (e.g., `TEST=="/usr/lib/udev/dmi_memory_id"` depending on install path) to avoid log noise if the binary is missing. Also consider adding `ENV{SYSTEMD_WANTS}`/tagging only when properties are needed, rather than running the helper for all DMI events.
