Adding a new udev rules file here is fine, but please ensure the file is also installed/packaged consistently with the rest of the rules set (i.e., that `70-memory.rules` actually exists in `rules.d/` and is included in the same `files(''' ... ''')` block ordering expectations). Also consider whether `70-` is the right priority: if this rule depends on DMI enumeration or other properties being set first, it may need to run later (e.g., `80-`/`90-`) to avoid missing attributes or being overridden by subsequent rules.
Typo in the error message: "Faied" → "Failed". Also consider keeping error strings consistent with the other decode paths (smbios_decode/legacy_decode already use "Failed to verify checksum.").

Adding the new helper here is fine, but please ensure the build is properly gated to platforms where it can work (DMI is x86/UEFI/BIOS-centric and may not exist on many arches/containers). Consider wrapping this entry in a conditional (e.g., based on host_machine.cpu_family() or a feature check) and/or ensuring the source itself compiles cleanly with appropriate #ifdefs when DMI sysfs is absent. Also verify the corresponding udev rule installs/uses this helper and that it’s included in the right install set (and tests/CI won’t fail on non-x86 builders).

Adding a raw dmidecode binary dump to the repo can significantly bloat the tree and may be non-reproducible across firmware revisions. Please ensure this file is actually consumed by an automated test (and referenced from the test suite/meson) and consider whether a smaller/filtered representation (e.g., extracted type 16/17 tables only, or a compressed text dump) would be sufficient. Also double-check licensing/redistribution implications of shipping vendor firmware-derived DMI data in-tree.

This call clears the old syspath from the found-by sets, but it doesn’t actually ensure the corresponding Device unit is stopped/GC’d if it already exists. If the lingering units problem is that the old .device units remain loaded/active, consider explicitly looking up the unit by the generated name `e` (or by syspath) and triggering cleanup (e.g., mark not found + queue GC / stop) rather than only updating found flags. Also, `e` is currently computed but unused—either use it for the cleanup path or drop the `unit_name_from_path()` call to avoid dead code and extra work.

This special-cases only `kind == "bond"` when handling `-EEXIST`, but the comment says the collision is between bonding master and its slaves. Slaves typically have `kind == "bond"`? (often the master is kind "bond" and slaves are kind "ether"), so this condition may not reliably select the intended link to own the hwaddr entry. Consider basing the exception on whether the link is a bond master (e.g., `link->bond`/`link->master_ifindex`/netdev type) or on `link->iftype == ARPHRD_ETHER && link->master_ifindex > 0` etc., and/or ensure deterministic ownership (e.g., prefer master over slaves). Also, if multiple non-bond links share a hwaddr (MACVLAN, VRF, bridges, containers), this will still log+ignore and `link_get_by_hw_addr()` may return an arbitrary/incorrect link; please confirm that’s acceptable for the ACD/DAD fix or broaden the handling.

Adding a lookup by hardware address makes sense for filtering out self-originated ARP/ACD traffic across multiple interfaces, but please ensure the API contract is explicit: (1) clarify in the header comment/docs whether this matches on full L2 address bytes + length/type (not just first 6 bytes), (2) define behavior when multiple Links share the same MAC (common with veth/macvlan/containers) — should this return -ENOTUNIQ, the first match, or prefer an 'up' link? and (3) consider whether the lookup should be scoped to a network namespace/Manager instance (it is) and whether it needs to ignore the querying link itself (callers may need a variant that excludes a given ifindex). Without clear semantics, callers may incorrectly treat ambiguous matches as definitive and reintroduce false DAD conflicts.

If `m->links_by_hw_addr` stores `Link*` values (or other refcounted objects), freeing it with `hashmap_free()` may leak references because it does not run a value destructor. Consider using `hashmap_free_with_destructor(m->links_by_hw_addr, link_unref)` (or the appropriate destructor) to match `links_by_index`. Also ensure keys are freed appropriately (e.g., if keys are dynamically allocated) or that the hashmap is configured to not require key destruction.

Adding a new Manager-owned hashmap requires clear ownership/lifecycle handling. Please ensure this is initialized and freed alongside the other maps (e.g., in manager_new()/manager_free()), and kept in sync on link add/remove/rename events. Also consider key semantics: if this is keyed by raw hardware address, multiple links can share the same MAC (bond/bridge/veth/macvlan, WiFi randomization, containers), so a simple Hashmap may cause collisions/overwrites—an OrderedHashmap or Hashmap<HWAddr, Set/Array of Links> might be safer, or at least document/enforce uniqueness assumptions.

Adding the new IPv4 ACD implementation to the build is fine, but please ensure this is also reflected in any conditional build logic (e.g., feature options, platform-specific exclusions) and that the new files are included in the appropriate install/dist targets if the project expects that. Also double-check ordering/grouping: if there is a convention to keep related modules (dhcp4/acd/ipv4ll) together or sorted, keep it consistent to avoid merge conflicts and make future maintenance easier.

This readiness check is gated on `link->hw_addr` being a non-null Ethernet MAC, but it does not verify that the address being configured is actually associated with the same L2 identity used by IPv4ACD. In setups with multiple interfaces on the same L2 (the reported bug), ACD needs to ignore probes originating from the host itself on other interfaces. Please ensure `ipv4acd_address_is_ready_to_configure()` (or the underlying ACD callback logic) explicitly filters out ARP/ACD packets whose sender MAC equals *any* local interface MAC (or at least the MAC used by the ACD instance), otherwise this call site will still allow self-generated probes to be treated as conflicts. Also consider whether the `ETH_ALEN`/`ether_addr_is_null()` gating is sufficient for non-Ethernet links (e.g., Infiniband) and whether we should log/skip ACD more explicitly when the MAC is missing/invalid.

Adding `is_static` as a bitfield is fine, but please ensure it is explicitly initialized in all constructors/clone paths (e.g., `address_new()`, any `address_dup()`/`address_copy()`-like helpers, and when parsing .network/.netdev config). Otherwise this can become an uninitialized flag that changes ACD behavior nondeterministically. Also consider whether the name should reflect semantics more precisely (e.g., "configured" vs "learned") since "static" can be ambiguous with DHCPv4 static leases.

This drops a request by looking it up in request_queue, but ordered_set_get() may return NULL if the request is not queued (already processed/removed) or if the comparator/keying doesn't match the stack-allocated `req` exactly. Passing NULL into request_drop() is risky unless request_drop() explicitly tolerates it. Please either (a) store the return value and NULL-check before dropping, and/or (b) use a dedicated helper to cancel address requests for a given Address* to avoid relying on Request struct equality semantics. Also consider whether you should cancel only the DHCPv4 address request(s) associated with the lost lease, rather than any non-static IPv4ACD-probing address on the link, to avoid unintended cancellations when multiple dynamic addresses are in flight.

This unconditionally sends DHCP DECLINE on any ACD conflict. Given the reported bug (self-ARP seen on another interface in the same L2), please ensure the conflict is validated as coming from a different host before declining (e.g., ignore conflicts where the detected sender MAC equals this link's MAC, or where the conflict is otherwise attributable to our own probes). Otherwise, networkd may spuriously decline valid leases when multiple interfaces are on the same broadcast domain. If sd-ipv4acd already filters this, consider adding a comment here referencing that guarantee; if not, add the necessary guard or plumb conflict metadata (sender hwaddr/ifindex) into the callback to make the decision here.

This new API is exposed without any documentation of the return value semantics (e.g., 0/1 vs. negative errno) and what “ready to configure” means in terms of ACD state. Please add a short comment (kernel-doc style or a brief /* ... */) clarifying expected inputs (e.g., address must be IPv4, link must have ipv4acd context), and the exact meaning of the return value so callers don’t mis-handle transient states (probing/defending) as ready/not-ready.

This looks like a copy/paste mistake: you call `ipv4ll_update_mac(link)` twice, but the first log message says "IPv4 ACD client". The first call should likely be `ipv4acd_update_mac(link)` (or whatever the new ACD helper is named) to keep ACD state in sync after a MAC change. As-is, ACD won't be updated and IPv4LL gets updated twice.

Adding a dedicated `addresses_ipv4acd` set makes sense for tracking addresses under ACD/DAD, but please ensure its lifecycle is fully handled elsewhere: initialize it (likely alongside `addresses`/`addresses_foreign`), free it in `link_free()`, and keep it in sync on link teardown/reconfigure. Also consider whether the element type/keying matches how ACD identifies conflicts (e.g., by IPv4 address only vs (address, ifindex)), to avoid reintroducing the multi-interface same-L2 false-positive scenario described in the issue.

This early return means we never call dhcp_detect_mtu_reset_loop() when dhcp_use_mtu is disabled. That’s probably fine for the MTU-setting path, but it also means any existing dhcp4_mtu_reset_loop_detector event source won’t be disabled/unref’d here, and dhcp4_mtu_reset_loop_detected won’t be updated. If the detector can be armed from a previous configuration/lease and then dhcp_use_mtu gets turned off (or link is reconfigured), consider explicitly disabling/unref’ing the detector when dhcp_use_mtu is false (or ensure it’s always cleaned up elsewhere on reconfigure/link teardown) to avoid stale timers and confusing state.

Changing the return value from 0 to 1 alters the function’s success semantics and may break callers that treat 0 as success / <0 as error (common systemd convention). If the intent is to signal “MTU change request queued/in-progress” vs “no-op”, please introduce a named enum/constant (or document clearly in the header) and audit all call sites to ensure they handle 1 correctly (e.g., don’t treat it as failure, don’t skip follow-up logic, and don’t mis-handle in `if (r < 0)` vs `if (r != 0)` patterns). Also consider whether returning 0 with a separate state flag (already `link->setting_mtu = true`) would be less risky unless there is a strong need for a tri-state return.

Storing an sd_event_source* in Link implies you must manage its lifetime explicitly (unref on link free, and likely when restarting DHCP/MTU handling) to avoid leaks or stale callbacks firing after the link is gone. Please ensure the corresponding .c code consistently cancels/unrefs this event source on link teardown and on state transitions (e.g., carrier loss / DHCP restart), and consider naming/typing consistency with other Link timers/detectors (some use sd_event_source* but are wrapped in helper cleanup paths).

The bitfield flag name/comment is clear, but please verify it is always reset when appropriate (e.g., new DHCP lease, link down/up, admin MTU change) so a single transient detection doesn’t permanently disable DHCP-provided MTU for the lifetime of the Link. Also consider whether this should be grouped with other DHCPv4 state flags (or documented more precisely) to avoid future confusion about when it is set/cleared.

The error mapping here looks wrong for the success case: posix_fallocate() returns 0 on success, so this will return -0 (i.e. 0) only if r != EINTR, which is fine, but the comment and logic imply “returns positive errnos on error” and then “return -r”. However, if r is a positive errno, returning -r matches systemd style; if r is 0, returning 0 is also fine. The bigger issue is that the EINTR path never handles success on the first call (it only retries when r==EINTR), but also the non-EINTR error path returns -r even when r is a positive errno, while the comment says 'returns positive errnos on error'—please ensure callers expect negative errno values from this wrapper and that you don't accidentally return a positive value anywhere. Consider making the intent explicit with something like `if (r == 0) return 0; if (r != EINTR) return -r;` and add an assertion or doc comment in the header to avoid misuse.

This is a public header declaration; please ensure the signature matches the actual implementation and the expected libc API types. `posix_fallocate()` uses `off_t` for offset/len, so using `uint64_t` here may cause truncation or signedness issues on 32-bit or non-LFS builds. Consider using `off_t` (or `uint64_t` only if the implementation explicitly uses `posix_fallocate64`/LFS and validates ranges). Also, if this is intended to be part of the exported fs-util API, add a short comment describing EINTR retry semantics and include any required headers (e.g., `<stdint.h>`) if not already guaranteed by this header's includes.

Double-check the errno sign convention here. `posix_fallocate_loop()` (from fs-util) typically returns a *negative* errno-style value on failure, while `posix_fallocate()` returns a *positive* error number. If `posix_fallocate_loop()` already returns `-errno`, then passing `-r` to `log_error_errno()` is correct; but if it returns a positive errno (like `posix_fallocate()`), this will flip the sign and break `%m`/error reporting. Please confirm the return contract of `posix_fallocate_loop()` and adjust to either `log_error_errno(r, ...)` or `log_error_errno(-r, ...)` consistently with other call sites.

This changes the error-sign convention: previously `posix_fallocate()` returned an errno value and the function returned `-r` (negative errno). Now it returns `r` directly, which would be a *positive* errno unless `posix_fallocate_loop()` already negates it. Please ensure `posix_fallocate_loop()` returns a negative errno (systemd style) so callers of `journal_file_allocate()` continue to receive negative error codes. If `posix_fallocate_loop()` returns a positive errno, keep `return -r;` here (or rename/adjust the helper to make the contract explicit).

Switching the test to posix_fallocate_loop() makes sense to cover EINTR-retry behavior, but note that posix_fallocate() returns an error *number* (0 on success), not -1/errno. Please double-check that posix_fallocate_loop() preserves the same return-value contract (i.e., returns 0 on success and a positive errno value on failure) so that the ">= 0" assertion remains meaningful and doesn’t accidentally treat failures as success. If posix_fallocate_loop() is implemented to return negative errno-style values, this assertion should be updated accordingly (e.g., "== 0" or ">= 0" depending on the contract).

This macro expands to multiple comma-separated expressions and evaluates `(address).s_addr` four times, which can be problematic if the argument has side effects (e.g., a function call) and is also slightly inefficient. Consider rewriting as a helper inline function that formats into a buffer, or at least capture `be32toh((address).s_addr)` into a temporary (e.g., via a statement-expression or by requiring an lvalue) to ensure single evaluation. Also, please ensure callers always use it as `... , IPV4_ADDRESS_FMT_VAL(addr)` paired with `IPV4_ADDRESS_FMT_STR` to avoid format/argument mismatches.

This hunk removes the IPv4 formatting helper macros from the public address header. Please ensure all remaining users were updated to use a shared formatter (e.g., IN_ADDR_FMT/IN_ADDR_TO_STRING or similar) and that no other headers or C files still rely on IPV4_ADDRESS_FMT_STR/IPV4_ADDRESS_FMT_VAL. If these macros were used for logging/debug output, consider replacing them with the project-standard address formatting helpers to avoid duplicated logic and endianness pitfalls.

This cast relies on `struct in6_addr` being layout-compatible with `union in_addr_union` (or at least that the first bytes match), which is a strict-aliasing/UB risk and can break on some architectures/compilers. Prefer constructing a local `union in_addr_union uu = { .in6 = *u };` and passing `&uu`, or add a dedicated `in6_addr_to_string()` implementation that takes `struct in6_addr` without type-punning. If you keep the cast, please add a clear comment and a compile-time assertion about size/alignment to document the assumption.

This changes the behavior from failing fast to continuing after a lookup error, but it also changes the return value semantics: the function will now return 0 even if one or more lookups failed, potentially leaving m->prefix[i] unset/NULL and causing later code to misbehave in harder-to-debug ways. If the intent is to make missing paths non-fatal, consider explicitly setting m->prefix[i] to a safe default (e.g., NULL and ensure all consumers handle it) and/or track the first error and return it at the end (or return a specific error only for critical directory types). Also, the log message uses exec_directory_type_to_string(i) while the lookup uses p[i].type/p[i].suffix; please confirm that 'i' maps 1:1 to the path being looked up (otherwise log the actual p[i].type/suffix for accuracy).

The new wording is clearer, but the type description is a bit ambiguous: “boolean or a termination resistor value in ohm” could be read as allowing arbitrary strings. Consider explicitly documenting accepted forms (e.g., `yes|no|0|<uint16>`), and clarify whether values other than 120 are supported only when the kernel/driver exposes CAN termination (and what happens if unsupported). Also, “in ohm” is usually written “in ohms” in the manpages, and it may be worth stating whether whitespace/units like `120ohm` are accepted (likely not).

The debug message previously used yes_no() (implying a boolean), but termination is now a u16 ohm value. Consider clarifying the log output to avoid confusion and improve diagnosability, e.g. include units and/or special-case 0 as "disabled": `"Setting CAN termination to %u ohm"` or `"Setting CAN termination to %s (%u ohm)"` with 0 => disabled. Also ensure the corresponding .network option documentation/semantics match (boolean vs numeric).

Adding a new CONFIG_PARSER_PROTOTYPE here is fine, but please ensure the corresponding implementation is actually wired up end-to-end: (1) the function `config_parse_can_termination()` exists and is declared consistently (signature matches other config parsers), (2) the option is registered in the gperf table for .network parsing (e.g., `networkd-network-gperf.gperf` / `[CAN]` section key mapping), and (3) the parsed value is stored in the relevant `Network`/`Link` struct and applied in `can_set_netlink_message()` (or equivalent) so the setting has effect. Also consider whether this header is the right place for the prototype—if other CAN parser prototypes are grouped elsewhere, keep ordering/style consistent.

Switching from `config_parse_tristate` with `offsetof(Network, can_termination)` to `config_parse_can_termination` with both aux/offset set to 0 means the parser must now locate and write the target field internally (or apply the setting immediately). Please double-check that `config_parse_can_termination()` actually stores the parsed value into the `Network` object (and not just validates), otherwise `CAN.Termination=` will become a no-op. If it does store, consider passing the offset (or documenting why offset is intentionally unused) to keep the gperf table consistent and reduce the risk of future refactors breaking this silently.

Changing `can_termination` from `int` to `uint16_t` is a good tightening, but please ensure all parsing/serialization paths validate the range and handle negative/"unset" values correctly. Previously, `int` may have been used to represent an unset sentinel (e.g., -1); with an unsigned type you now rely on `can_termination_set` for that, so make sure all call sites are updated to check the boolean before using the value, and that config parsing rejects values > UINT16_MAX (and any negative input) rather than silently wrapping/truncating.

Adding an explicit `*_set` flag is helpful, but please confirm it is initialized to `false` in all constructors/initialization paths (e.g., network defaults, deserialization) and reset appropriately on reload. Also consider whether this should be a tri-state with "explicitly disabled" vs "not specified" depending on how termination is applied; if 0 is a meaningful termination value, the boolean is required to disambiguate, so ensure downstream logic uses it consistently.

Casting to uint8_t fixes the always-true comparison for signed/unsigned char, but the second clause still compares a (potentially negative) `char` to 127. For consistency and to avoid any future type/UB warnings, consider casting in the second comparison too (e.g. `return (uint8_t) p < ' ' || (uint8_t) p == 127;`) or change the parameter type to `uint8_t`/`unsigned char` if callers permit. Also, include/ensure availability of `uint8_t` (via `<stdint.h>` or existing project headers) in this header.

This hunk removes the hash/compare helpers and HASH_OPS definitions for `struct in_addr_prefix`. Please double-check that no remaining users (e.g. `Hashmap`/`Set` instances keyed by `in_addr_prefix`, or any `*_hash_ops_free` usage) still reference `in_addr_prefix_hash_ops`/`in_addr_prefix_hash_ops_free`, otherwise this will break the build. If the intent is to replace hashing with a different data structure for performance, consider either (a) moving these helpers to the new module that still needs them, or (b) keeping them here if they are still generally useful, to avoid future duplication.

This hunk removes the `in_addr_prefix` type and the `in_addr_prefix_hash_ops*` declarations. Please double-check that all remaining users were migrated to the new representation (likely `struct in_addr_data` or another prefix type) and that no external compilation units still include this header expecting these symbols. If any out-of-tree consumers exist, consider providing a compatibility typedef/alias or a clear replacement API in the header to avoid breaking builds.

This include swap changes the dependency from the generic address helpers to the prefix-specific helpers. Please double-check that this file no longer relies on any APIs that are only declared in in-addr-util.h (e.g., plain in_addr/in6_addr parsing/formatting helpers) and that all required declarations are available via in-addr-prefix-util.h (or are included elsewhere). If both headers are needed, consider including both explicitly to avoid relying on transitive includes, which can break with future header cleanups.

New header include looks fine, but please verify it’s actually required in this translation unit (i.e., symbols from in-addr-prefix-util.h are used after the parser rewrite). If it’s only needed indirectly via another header, prefer including it where the types/functions are used to avoid unnecessary rebuild dependencies and keep includes minimal. Also ensure the include order matches the project’s conventions (local headers grouped/sorted consistently).

The index/bit math here uses `sizeof(uint64_t)` (bytes) where it should use the number of bits in a `uint64_t`. As written, `(p->prefixlen - 1) / sizeof(uint64_t)` yields 0..15 for prefixlen 1..128, but `ipv6_prefixlen_bits` is sized as `128 / sizeof(uint64_t)` = 16, so the intended mapping is 128 bits across 2 uint64_t words (or 128/64=2), not 16. Similarly, the shift uses `% sizeof(uint64_t)` which limits shifts to 0..7, losing information. This will cause incorrect coverage detection/reduction for IPv6 prefixes and may lead to wrong firewall rules. Please change to `/ 64` and `% 64` (or use `CHAR_BIT * sizeof(uint64_t)`), and adjust the array size accordingly, e.g. `uint64_t ipv6_prefixlen_bits[128 / (sizeof(uint64_t) * CHAR_BIT)] = {};` and use `BITS_PER_LONG_LONG`-style helper if available.

The API takes a `const struct in_addr_prefix *prefix`, but the storage is a `Set` (which typically stores pointers). Please clarify/guarantee ownership semantics here: does `in_addr_prefix_add()` deep-copy `*prefix` into heap storage, or does it store the pointer as-is? If it stores the pointer, callers may pass stack/temporary objects and cause UAF. Consider documenting this explicitly in the header comment and/or changing the signature to take the struct by value (and allocate internally) or to take an owned pointer (e.g., `struct in_addr_prefix *`) to make lifetime expectations unambiguous.

Adding the new source/header here is fine, but please double-check that `in-addr-prefix-util.c` doesn’t introduce new external link dependencies (e.g., libm, libidn, etc.) that would require updating `shared_deps`/`link_with` elsewhere. Also ensure the new util is intended to be part of the generic `shared` library (used by multiple components); if it’s only needed by a specific binary/module, consider scoping it to that target to avoid increasing build surface and compile time for all users of `src/shared`.

This changes the temporary lookup key to point directly at the caller-provided `client_id` buffer instead of a duplicated copy. That’s fine only if `hashmap_remove()` (and the hash/compare functions for `DHCPClientId`) do not retain the pointer beyond the call and only read `length` bytes synchronously. If any hashing/comparison assumes NUL-termination or may access the buffer after this scope, this becomes a use-after-scope risk. Please double-check the `DHCPClientId` hash/compare implementation and consider either (a) keeping the `memdup()` for safety, or (b) adding a comment/assert that the buffer is valid for the duration of the call and comparisons are length-bounded.

There’s a grammar/wording issue here: “will used be” should be “will be used”. Also consider tightening the phrasing to avoid repetition (you mention DUIDType=custom twice) and clarify that the raw bytes are sent as-is (i.e., no DUID type prefix is added) and that this is DHCPv6-only. For example: “...then the value of DUIDRawData= is used verbatim as the DUID. DUIDRawData= is mandatory. This applies only to DHCPv6.”

Adding the new enum entry to the string table is good, but please ensure the user-facing string matches the expected config/API naming. Elsewhere the docs/PR use `DUIDType=custom`; if this table is used for parsing/serialization or logging, consider using a consistent token (e.g., "custom" or "DUID-CUSTOM") rather than capitalized "Custom" to avoid mismatches/confusion. Also verify `_DUID_TYPE_MAX` and the enum ordering were updated accordingly so this index is always in-bounds.

Adding a new enum value changes the on-the-wire DUID type if this is used directly in DHCP option encoding. A “custom/raw” DUID as described in the issue should likely *not* introduce a new DUID type code (5 is currently unassigned/unknown in RFC 8415), but instead reuse the existing DUID type field from the provided raw bytes (or explicitly allow “raw” to mean “user provides full DUID including the 2-byte type”). Otherwise packets will carry DUID type=5, which many servers may reject. Consider implementing this as a special internal mode that bypasses type generation rather than a new numeric DUID type, and ensure the config parser/serializer and docs clearly state whether DUIDRawData includes the type bytes.

For the custom DUID path, this copies `duid_len` bytes directly into `client->duid` without any visible bounds check against `sizeof(client->duid)`. If `duid_len` can exceed the storage size of `client->duid` (e.g., misconfiguration or malicious input), this becomes an overflow. Please add an explicit size validation (and return -EINVAL) before memcpy, and consider zeroing the remainder of `client->duid` to avoid leaking stale bytes if later code serializes the whole struct. Also ensure `duid_len` includes the 2-byte DUID type when using `DUID_TYPE_CUSTOM`, since this bypasses `htobe16()` and assumes the raw buffer is already in on-wire format.

Adding the new string mapping is fine, but please ensure the rest of the DUID parsing/serialization path actually supports DUID_TYPE_CUSTOM end-to-end. In particular: (1) `_DUID_TYPE_MAX` and the `DUIDType` enum must include `DUID_TYPE_CUSTOM` in a stable position; (2) the config parser should accept `DUIDType=custom` and validate `DUIDRawData` length/format appropriately; (3) the DHCPv6 client code must treat `custom` as "send raw bytes exactly" (no type/enterprise-id prefixing) and still enforce any protocol constraints you intend (e.g., minimum length, or allow 0-length?). Also consider documenting this new value in the man page and adding a test to prevent regressions.

This cast/destination looks wrong: `read_full_file_full()` expects a `char **` out-parameter, but `data` is declared as `void *` and is currently NULL. Casting `data` (not `&data`) to `char**` will pass a NULL pointer as the location to store the allocated buffer, likely causing a crash/UB. This should probably be `(char**) &data` (or change `data` to `_cleanup_(erase_and_freep) char *data = NULL;` and pass `&data`). Also consider whether `erase_and_freep` is appropriate for the encrypted blob (it might be fine), but the immediate bug is the missing address-of.

The inline guidance comment is helpful, but please avoid policy/usage guidance in a public header unless it’s enforced/accurate long-term. If the intent is to restrict `read_credential()` to service context, consider documenting this in the function’s doc comment (or in a dedicated header comment block) and/or encoding the distinction in naming (e.g., `read_credential_unencrypted()` vs `read_credential_with_decryption()`). Also ensure the new API is consistently declared/defined across all build configurations (including when encryption support is compiled out), otherwise consumers may hit missing symbol/ifdef issues.

The logic here only checks `CKF_EC_UNCOMPRESS` and otherwise assumes either `CKF_EC_COMPRESS` (convert) or “ignore”. But if neither flag is set, proceeding with the original (likely uncompressed) point may still fail on tokens that require compressed points or have stricter requirements. Consider making this behavior explicit: if `CKF_EC_UNCOMPRESS` is not set and `CKF_EC_COMPRESS` is not set, either (a) fail with a clear error, or (b) attempt both encodings (try uncompressed first, then compressed) based on what the token reports. Also, the current condition `if (!(flags & CKF_EC_UNCOMPRESS)) { if (flags & CKF_EC_COMPRESS) ... }` doesn’t handle the case where both flags are set (token accepts both) but you might still want to keep the original uncompressed data without conversion; an explicit preference order/comment would improve clarity.

This new API is declared unconditionally, but it depends on PKCS#11 types (CK_FUNCTION_LIST/CK_SESSION_HANDLE/CK_OBJECT_HANDLE/CK_OBJECT_CLASS). In this header those types are only available when HAVE_P11KIT is enabled; otherwise this will fail to compile. Consider guarding the declaration with `#if HAVE_P11KIT` (or providing a stub/typedefs) to keep non-P11Kit builds working, consistent with the rest of the pkcs11-util interface.

These calls to test_sd_journal_seek_realtime_usec() are passing e->ts.monotonic (and +/-1) as the timestamp argument, but sd_journal_seek_realtime_usec() expects a realtime (wallclock) timestamp. This makes the test incorrect and may hide/introduce failures unrelated to realtime seeking (especially around the intended "realtime goes backward" scenario). Please switch these to e->ts.realtime (and +/-1). If the intent was to test monotonic seeking, call test_sd_journal_seek_monotonic_usec() instead (once the FIXME is resolved).

This aliases `arg_tpm2_hash_pcr_values` to `arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values`, which can create ownership/lifetime issues (double-free, use-after-free, or leaks) depending on how these globals are cleaned up later. If both pointers are freed independently, this will double-free; if only one is freed, the other may leak. Prefer copying the array (or transferring ownership explicitly by setting `arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values = NULL` after assignment) and document the ownership semantics. Also consider whether this fallback should be gated on the user explicitly providing `--tpm2-public-key-pcrs` vs. auto defaults, to avoid silently changing binding behavior.

This error path is used to decide whether the firmware event log looks like a TPM2 log. Returning EBADMSG here may still propagate as a hard failure and trigger downstream waits (e.g., tpm2.target/dev-tpmrm0) on TPM 1.2 systems. Consider making the TPM2-vs-TPM1.2 distinction explicit: detect TPM2 presence (or TPM2 event log format) earlier and treat non-TPM2 logs as a non-fatal/unsupported case (e.g., return a dedicated error like -EOPNOTSUPP/-ENODEV and have callers skip TPM2-related units), rather than logging as an error. Also, the message is helpful, but if this is expected on TPM1.2 machines it might be better as debug/info to avoid alarming logs.

Minor grammar/clarity: “can contains” should be “can contain”. Also consider clarifying whether header names are case-insensitive (HTTP field-names are) and whether the allowed character set is intentionally more restrictive than RFC 7230 (which allows more than alnum/"_"/"-"). If the implementation enforces this restriction, it’s fine, but the wording should match the actual validation rules (e.g., whether digits are allowed, whether leading/trailing whitespace around name/value is trimmed, and whether empty value is permitted).

Consider trimming trailing whitespace from the header value too. Right now the value is only left-trimmed via skip_leading_chars(), so a config like "Header=Foo: bar   " will preserve the trailing spaces and send them on the wire, which is surprising and may break strict servers. Using strstrip(t) (or equivalent) after splitting would normalize both sides consistently. Also, if you intend to allow empty values ("Foo:"), ensure header_value_is_valid() accepts ""; otherwise this will reject such headers.

Consider making the API const-correct and explicit about ownership/lifetime. If `header_put()` duplicates `name`/`value` internally (likely), document that in a comment or rename to indicate copying; if it stores pointers, the signature should reflect required lifetime. Also, if this is intended to be used from multiple translation units, ensure the implementation is in a corresponding `.c` file and that the function name is sufficiently namespaced (e.g., `journal_header_put`) to avoid collisions in the wider systemd codebase.

Building the header line via `strjoin(name, ": ", joined)` should validate/sanitize `name` (and ideally `joined`) to prevent invalid or unsafe HTTP header injection (e.g., embedded `\r`/`\n`, leading/trailing whitespace, or illegal characters in the field-name). Since this is user-controlled config, consider rejecting names that don't match RFC 7230 token rules and values containing CR/LF, and/or use an existing helper (if available in systemd) to format/validate HTTP headers before passing to libcurl.

This test target compiles `journal-header-util.c` directly into the test. If `journal-header-util.c` is also part of `systemd_journal_upload_sources` (added above), consider linking the test against the corresponding library/object instead of re-compiling the same C file here to avoid duplicate compilation and potential divergence in compile flags/defines. Also ensure the test is only added when the required dependencies for the util (e.g., libcurl/remote enabled) are available, otherwise the build may fail in configurations where journal-remote is partially disabled.

This assertion assumes that re-inserting the same header name with the same value returns 0 (no-op). That’s a reasonable contract, but it’s not obvious from the test alone and could be brittle if `header_put()` later changes to always replace/normalize and return >0. Consider adding a short comment explaining the expected semantics (e.g., "returns 0 if unchanged") and/or explicitly verifying the stored value after each call (lookup + compare) so the test checks behavior rather than just return codes.

This test currently only verifies that upload+remote still work with `Header=` present, but it doesn't actually assert that the custom header was received/used by the server. That makes the test prone to false positives (e.g., header parsing ignored, header dropped, or sent on a different request) while the journal transfer still succeeds. Consider extending the test to validate the header explicitly, e.g. by enabling request logging in systemd-journal-remote (if available), capturing traffic with a local proxy, or adding a minimal HTTP endpoint in the test that checks for `TestHeader: TestValue` and fails otherwise. Also consider adding a second `Header=` line to ensure multiple headers are supported and ordering/duplication is handled as intended.

In the ST terminator path (ESC \\), this only treats the sequence as properly terminated when the next byte is '\\'. If the ESC is followed by anything else (e.g. split/garbled stream), we currently drop the OSC state and also drop that following byte (since it’s consumed here and not re-processed as normal text/state). Consider either (a) only leaving OSC state when you actually see '\\' and otherwise keep accumulating/handling, or (b) if the byte is not '\\', treat the prior ESC as literal/part of payload and re-process the current byte (e.g. by decrementing i / using a small pushback) so it isn’t lost. Also, please add a comment clarifying that non-'\\' after ESC is treated as abort and that the byte is intentionally discarded (if that’s the intended behavior).

The comment says the selection should be "1 row lower", but the assertion adjusts the expected Y by decrementing it. That reads as "1 row higher" in buffer coordinates. Please clarify the coordinate system here (viewport vs buffer) and align the comment with the actual expected behavior (or flip the adjustment if the intent is truly "lower"). This is especially important for future maintainers since this test is validating a subtle circular-buffer/viewport interaction.

This workaround is very subtle and easy to regress. Please add an explicit comment explaining *why* we manually bump the outer refcount here (C++/WinRT multi-level composition bug during construction) and what invariant it restores (outer must outlive inner / prevent premature destruction on natural exit). Also consider guarding this with a debug assert or a small helper to make the intent clearer (e.g. `FixupCompositionRefCounts()`), since calling `AddRef()` in a constructor is unusual and can mask lifetime issues if the surrounding composition model changes.

This append relies on pointer arithmetic across two different buffers: `args[0].data()` points into `sourceText`, but `sourceText.data() + sourceText.size()` is computed from the original view. This is only safe if `args[0]` is guaranteed to be a subview of `sourceText` (same underlying buffer) and `args[0].data() <= sourceText.data()+sourceText.size()`. That’s currently true given how `args[0]` is produced, but it’s subtle and easy to break with future refactors. Consider making the intent explicit by appending a `std::wstring_view` slice instead of raw pointers, e.g. `buffer.append(std::wstring_view{ args[0].data(), static_cast<size_t>(sourceText.data()+sourceText.size() - args[0].data()) });` or (clearer) compute `allParams = til::safe_slice_abs(sourceText, args[0].data()-sourceText.data(), sourceText.size())` and append that. Also, this currently preserves all original whitespace between args (including multiple spaces), which is likely desired for `$*`, but please add/confirm a unit test that `$*` keeps consecutive spaces exactly as typed.

This hunk removes a large set of private helper declarations (tokenization/macro replacement helpers). If these functions were moved/renamed (e.g., into an anonymous namespace in alias.cpp) that’s fine, but please ensure the header and implementation stay consistent: (1) no remaining references to these members from alias.cpp or unit tests, (2) no out-of-class definitions still using `Alias::s_*` (would become dead/ODR issues), and (3) if any of these helpers are still needed by tests, consider keeping declarations under `#ifdef UNIT_TESTING` or providing a test hook. Also double-check that removing these declarations doesn’t unintentionally change symbol visibility expectations for other translation units (even though they’re private, they can still be defined and referenced within the same TU).

This test data now includes multiple consecutive spaces in `originalString`, but the expected expansions below also preserve those spaces (e.g. in `$*`). That’s fine if the intended behavior is to keep the original whitespace verbatim, but it’s easy to accidentally make the test brittle/unclear. Consider adding an explicit comment here that `$*` should preserve the exact substring (including repeated spaces) and/or add a second case that asserts numbered args ($1..$n) are unaffected by extra whitespace (i.e. tokenization collapses separators for positional args). This will better document the contract and prevent future regressions in either direction.

Renaming `clamp_slice_abs` to `safe_slice_abs` is a breaking API change for any internal callers. Please ensure all call sites are updated in this PR (including any out-of-tree/partner code if applicable), and consider whether a deprecation/compat alias is warranted (e.g., keep `clamp_slice_abs` as an inline wrapper calling `safe_slice_abs`) to avoid churn. Also, the new name is a bit ambiguous: the function clamps indices rather than providing general "safety"; a name like `clamped_slice_abs`/`clamp_slice_abs` may be more descriptive unless there’s a broader naming convention change across `til`.

Switching from `til::clamp_slice_len` to `til::safe_slice_len` changes behavior: `clamp_slice_len` will return a shorter (clamped) slice when the requested range exceeds the buffer, while `safe_slice_len` typically returns an empty slice (or otherwise signals failure) when out-of-bounds. That may be desirable for safety, but it can also change rendering outcomes (e.g., previously partially-read soft font rows would render something; now they’ll be treated as missing and hit the `data.empty()` path). Please confirm this is intentional and that callers/tests cover the out-of-range case. If the goal is to preserve prior behavior while avoiding UB, consider keeping clamping semantics (or explicitly handling the partial-slice case) and add a comment explaining why `safe_slice_len` is correct here.

This change removes several numbered expectation lines (e.g., "1           :AAAAA", "9           :...", etc.). If this file is used as a golden/fixture for the spelling action, dropping entries can reduce coverage and may make the test less representative rather than reflecting an intentional behavior change. Please confirm these deletions are required by an upstream change in the spelling action output; otherwise consider keeping the removed cases or updating the generator so the expected file remains comprehensive and stable.

This changes the allowlisted spelling token from `DECAWM` to lowercase `decawm`. If the spelling action is case-sensitive (many wordlist-based checkers are), this may cause `DECAWM` occurrences in docs/comments to start failing spellcheck. Unless the codebase has been updated to consistently use lowercase, consider keeping both variants (`DECAWM` and `decawm`) in the expect list to avoid regressions.

This hunk removes the NOTICE entries for `kimwalisch/libpopcnt` (BSD-2) and `dynamic_bitset` (MIT). Please confirm these dependencies were fully removed from the repo/build (including transitive/vendor usage) before deleting their license attributions. If they’re still shipped/linked in any form, we should keep the corresponding NOTICE sections to remain compliant with the license terms.

This change removes the RendererVt project dependency from InteractivityWin32. Please double-check that no remaining code paths in InteractivityWin32 (or its transitive deps) still link against or include headers from the old vt renderer library. If any symbols were previously satisfied by RendererVt, this will surface as link errors only in certain configurations; consider verifying all solution configs (Debug/Release/AuditMode/Fuzzing across x86/x64/ARM64) still build cleanly after dropping this dependency.

Removing the VtEngine ETW provider from the WPR profile will drop render/translation-related traces for anyone using this perf preset. If the provider was removed because the component no longer exists, consider adding a replacement provider for the new synchronous VT translation path (or updating the profile name/comments) so perf investigations don’t silently lose key events. At minimum, it may be worth noting in the WPRP (or accompanying docs) that VtEngine events are no longer available and what provider(s) should be used instead.

Removing the VtEngine ETW provider from the WPR profile is consistent with the PR’s “Goodbye VtEngine” refactor, but please double-check that this provider isn’t still emitted/used in any remaining code paths or by existing performance/diagnostic workflows. If the provider GUID/name is still referenced anywhere (docs/scripts/CI perf traces), consider either updating those references or leaving a comment/changelog note so trace collection doesn’t silently lose expected events.

Adding a default constructor is fine, but please confirm the class invariants remain valid when default-constructed. If `OutputCellIterator` has members that must be initialized to a specific state (e.g., fill limit, current view, attribute/char mode), `= default` may leave them in an unusable/ambiguous state and could enable accidental use (e.g., default-construct then dereference/iterate). Consider explicitly initializing members to a safe sentinel state, or documenting/guarding that a default-constructed iterator is only meant for later assignment. Also ensure this doesn’t unintentionally change aggregate/constexpr/noexcept properties relied on elsewhere.

Adding a default constructor is fine, but please confirm this type is intended to be default-constructible in a valid/usable state. If `OutputCellView` contains reference-like members (e.g., `std::wstring_view`) or relies on invariants (non-empty view, specific `DbcsAttribute`/`TextAttribute`), a defaulted ctor may create an object that looks valid but is semantically unusable and could be accidentally used. Consider either (a) explicitly initializing members to safe defaults (empty view, `DbcsAttribute{}`/`TextAttribute{}`) and documenting the meaning, or (b) keeping it non-default-constructible and using `std::optional<OutputCellView>`/factory helpers where needed.

Removing the `_renderer->TriggerFlush(true)` call changes a subtle but important behavior: previously, advancing the circular buffer in the active buffer forced a renderer flush so the terminal/ConPTY would see output in sync with the buffer state (and potentially before/around scroll/resize-related reflow). Given the issue/PR context about ConPTY resize quirks and reflow overwriting, please confirm this flush is no longer required (or is now handled elsewhere) and add a short comment here explaining why it was safe to remove. Otherwise, this could reintroduce ordering/tearing issues where the buffer advances but pending output isn't emitted until later, making resize/scrollback behavior harder to reason about.

Setting `_flags` to `0` removes the previous default `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` behavior. Given the issue/PR context about documenting (and potentially working around) ConPTY resize reflow overwriting terminal content, this change likely alters user-visible behavior on resize. Please ensure this is intentional and either (a) gate it behind a setting/feature flag (e.g. default-on for older builds, default-off for newer), or (b) add/update documentation and release notes explaining the resize quirk and why the default changed. Also consider leaving a comment here clarifying why the quirk flag is no longer set by default, to prevent regressions/accidental reintroduction.

Removing the `ConptyRoundtripTests` forward declaration (and the corresponding `friend` below) will break compilation if that test class still exists and includes `Terminal.hpp` expecting this symbol, or if it still needs access to `Terminal` internals. Please ensure the test was deleted/renamed as part of this refactor, or update the test to avoid relying on `friend` access (e.g., via public test hooks) before dropping this declaration.

Dropping `friend class TerminalCoreUnitTests::ConptyRoundtripTests;` is fine only if the ConPTY roundtrip tests no longer exist or no longer require private/protected access to `Terminal`. If the tests are still present, this will cause access errors. Consider confirming the test removal/rename in the same PR, or replacing the friend dependency with a narrower public API for the specific behaviors under test.

Removing this note drops an important cross-test-suite contract: these ControlCore tests only validate viewport movement, while ConPTY roundtrip tests validate actual buffer clearing. Without the comment, future maintainers may incorrectly “fix” these tests to assert buffer contents (which will be flaky/incorrect given ConPTY ownership). Consider keeping a brief reference (or replacing it with a more general pointer to the ConPTY roundtrip coverage) so the intent and test boundaries remain clear.

This removal likely reflects an interface change (e.g., IRenderEngine no longer declares PrepareForTeardown). Please ensure the mock still fully implements the current interface across all build configurations: if PrepareForTeardown is still present behind a feature flag/ifdef, this will break compilation. Also consider whether any tests relied on PrepareForTeardown being callable (even as a no-op); if so, update the tests or provide an equivalent stub for the new teardown path.

Removing `ConptyRoundtripTests.cpp` (and the associated host/lib project references) drops the only end-to-end coverage that validated ConPTY output roundtripping into TerminalCore. Given the issue/PR context around ConPTY resize/reflow quirks, consider either (a) replacing these with new tests that exercise the resize behavior/quirk in a more isolated way, or (b) moving the roundtrip tests into a more appropriate test project that still has access to the required OpenConsole/host dependencies. Otherwise we risk losing regression detection for ConPTY-specific behaviors (including the resize overwrite/reflow behavior this issue is about).

This change drops `$(SolutionDir)\oss\dynamic_bitset` and `$(SolutionDir)\oss\libpopcnt` from the global `AdditionalIncludeDirectories`. If this is intentional cleanup, please confirm no projects still include headers from those folders (directly or transitively), otherwise this can cause build breaks that only show up in certain configurations/targets. If they’re still needed by any subproject, consider keeping them here or moving the include paths to the specific `.vcxproj`/`.props` that actually depends on them to avoid hidden coupling.

Swallowing `--vtmode` unconditionally is fine for backward compatibility with telnet.exe, but this change silently ignores any provided value (previously parsed via `s_GetArgumentValue`). If callers might still pass `--vtmode <something>`, consider consuming the value token as well (or explicitly rejecting/telemetry/logging) to avoid leaving a stray argument in `args` that could be misinterpreted later. At minimum, add a comment clarifying that the value (if present) is intentionally ignored and ensure `s_ConsumeArg` behavior matches that intent.

This hunk removes the `GetVtMode()` accessor and all related state/formatting/comparison usage. Please double-check that the underlying `vtMode` command-line argument (and any parsing/validation for it) has also been removed or is now intentionally ignored. If the argument is still accepted, consider keeping a deprecated field/accessor (or at least documenting the behavior) to avoid silently breaking existing callers/tests that may still pass `--vtmode` (or equivalent) and expect it to be reflected in diagnostics/logging.

This changes the ConPTY detection from `gci.IsInVtIoMode()` to `gci.GetVtIo(nullptr)`. Using `GetVtIo(nullptr)` as a boolean is a bit opaque and may have side effects (e.g., lazy initialization, locking, or returning non-null in scenarios other than ConPTY). Consider using a clearer predicate (e.g., `IsInVtIoMode()` or a dedicated `IsConpty()`/`HasVtIo()` accessor) or at least store the result in a well-named local (`const auto inConpty = gci.GetVtIo(nullptr) != nullptr;`) to preserve readability and ensure the intent matches the previous behavior. Also verify that passing `nullptr` is the intended/idiomatic way to query without creating/attaching VT IO.

This change drops the conditional `SuppressResizeRepaint()` call after a successful resize. That suppression was likely mitigating the ConPTY resize quirk where reflowed buffer output repaints/overwrites the connected terminal on grow. If the intent is to remove suppression as part of the refactor, please add an explicit comment here (or in `_api.ResizeWindow`) documenting the behavior/regression and why it’s acceptable. Otherwise, consider preserving the suppression (or moving it into `ResizeWindow`/VT IO layer) so ConPTY clients don’t get unexpected repaint on resize. Also note the old code only suppressed when `ResizeWindow` returned true; if suppression is still needed, ensure it’s only applied when a resize actually occurred.

Switching to `GetVtIoNoCheck()` bypasses whatever invariants/null checks `GetVtIo()` enforces. Please ensure `_Shutdown()` can’t run when VT IO is unavailable/tearing down, or guard the call (e.g., null check) to avoid potential AV during shutdown races. If this is required because `GetVtIo()` asserts during shutdown, add a brief comment explaining why the unchecked accessor is safe here.

Switching to `GetVtIoNoCheck()` avoids the null-check/assert behavior, but it also makes it easier to dereference a missing VT IO instance during shutdown/teardown. Can we either (a) document why VT IO is guaranteed to exist here (thread lifetime vs. ConsoleInformation lifetime), or (b) add a defensive null check before calling `CloseInput()` to prevent potential AVs in edge cases (e.g. early init failure, teardown ordering changes)?

This change replaces the previous setter-style API (`SetLookingForDSR`) with a getter (`IsLookingForDSR`), but this header no longer shows any member that actually stores the "looking for DSR" state (the old `_pfnSetLookingForDSR` callback was removed). Please ensure there is a corresponding boolean member (or other backing state) and that it is updated by the state machine when DSR is requested/received; otherwise `IsLookingForDSR()` risks always returning a default/incorrect value or relying on hidden global state. Also consider thread-safety/atomicity here since this is queried across the input thread boundary.

Defining `FileModeInformation` as a raw casted literal is brittle and obscures intent. If possible, prefer using the SDK-provided enum value (when available) or at least add a named constant with a comment pointing to the official `FILE_INFORMATION_CLASS` value and why it must be hardcoded here. Also consider guarding this with a compile-time check/`#ifndef` to avoid conflicts if future SDKs/WDK headers define `FileModeInformation` differently.

This helper always calls `_flush()` after formatting into `_back`, which defeats the purpose of the new corking mechanism and can cause excessive small writes (and potentially re-entrancy/ordering issues if `_flush()` can trigger other output). Consider making `WriteFormat` respect the cork state (e.g., only flush when not corked, or provide a `WriteFormatNoFlush`/`WriteFormat` + explicit `Flush` API). Also, since this is a header-defined function, ensure `fmt` and `<iterator>` includes are available transitively; otherwise compilation may depend on include order.

In the full-width path for WriteCharacter, the trailing cell is being marked as COMMON_LVB_LEADING_BYTE again. That looks like a bug: the second cell of a wide glyph should be flagged COMMON_LVB_TRAILING_BYTE (and typically have a placeholder/space char), otherwise downstream consumers (reflow, selection, rendering, ReadConsoleOutput semantics) may treat both cells as leading and corrupt width accounting. Also, the loop increments `i` twice without checking `i < len` after writing the lead; if `len` ends on the last column, this will write past the end of `infos`. Consider: (1) set `trail.Attributes` to include COMMON_LVB_TRAILING_BYTE (not LEADING), (2) guard against `i == len` before writing the trail (or reduce `len`/end when a wide char would straddle the boundary), and (3) ensure `result.lengthRead`/`cellsModified` reflect any truncation.

Mutating the caller-provided `std::wstring_view` backing store via `const_cast` is very risky/fragile: it assumes the view always points into a writable `_CONSOLE_API_MSG::_inputBuffer` and that no other code relies on the original contents (including potential reuse of the message buffer, logging, tracing, or concurrent readers). It also violates the const contract and can become UB if `text` ever comes from a literal/readonly mapping or a different call path. Consider avoiding in-place mutation by either (a) writing to VT in chunks while substituting control chars on the fly (no copy, just multiple `WriteUTF16` calls), or (b) allocating a temporary buffer only when control chars are present (e.g., detect with `find_if` first, then copy+replace). If you keep this approach, please add a hard assertion/guard that the buffer is writable and document the invariants at the API boundary, because this is a subtle footgun.

New API surface: please ensure this declaration is consistent with the rest of the stream write APIs. In particular, consider whether `WriteCharsVT` should also accept `til::CoordType* psScrollY` (or otherwise document why VT writes never need to report scroll) to avoid callers losing scroll accounting/telemetry that `WriteCharsLegacy` provides. Also verify the corresponding definition is added to the correct TU and that any required includes/forward declarations for `SCREEN_INFORMATION`/`til::CoordType` remain valid from this header.

This conditional mixes `&&` and `?:` without parentheses, which is easy to misread and can lead to subtle precedence bugs during future edits. Please add explicit parentheses (or split into named booleans) to make the intent unambiguous. Also, `auto& gci = ServiceLocator::LocateGlobals().getConsoleInformation();` inside `CONSOLE_INFORMATION::GetVtIo(...)` is surprising—`this` should already be the console information instance. If the goal is to ensure `context` matches the *global* active output buffer, consider documenting why `this` may differ from `gci`, or use `this->GetActiveOutputBuffer()` if appropriate. Finally, returning `nullptr` based on `context` is a behavior change from the old `GetVtIo()`; ensure all call sites handle null and consider marking the function `[[nodiscard]]` to prevent accidental ignoring of the nullability.

Potential out-of-bounds/overflow risk: `totalOffset` is computed from signed coordinate deltas (`offsetY/offsetX`) and then used as the start index for `buffer.subspan(totalOffset, ...)`. If `requestRectangle` is partially outside the buffer (negative left/top), `offsetX/offsetY` can be positive (fine), but if `bufferStride` is smaller than `writeRectangle.Width()` or if `bufferStride` doesn't match the caller's actual row pitch, `totalOffset + writeRectangle.Width()` can exceed `buffer.size()` and `subspan` will be UB in release. Consider validating upfront that `bufferStride >= requestRectangle.Width()` (or at least `>= writeRectangle.Width()`), and that the last row access fits: `totalOffset + (writeRectangle.Height()-1)*bufferStride + writeRectangle.Width() <= buffer.size()`. Also consider using `ptrdiff_t` for offset math and only converting to `size_t` after bounds checks to avoid negative-to-large wrap.

This new helper is declared in a public header; please ensure it’s either intended to be part of the header’s external surface area or move it to a more private header/translation unit to avoid leaking internal implementation details. Also consider whether `std::span` and `size_t` are guaranteed to be available via existing includes here—if not, add the appropriate includes (`<span>`, `<cstddef>`) to prevent fragile transitive-include dependencies. Finally, if this is only used within the host module, placing it in a `namespace` (or marking it `static`/`inline` if header-only) can help avoid ODR/linkage collisions.

Removing the vt renderer project reference is consistent with the PR goal, but please double-check that Host.EXE no longer has any direct/implicit link-time dependency on the vt project outputs (e.g., additional dependencies, include paths, or transitive references that previously came from vt.vcxproj). If any code still references vt symbols/headers, this will surface as build breaks in certain configurations (Debug/Release, x86/x64/ARM64). Consider also removing any now-stale references to the vt project GUID elsewhere in the solution (filters/solution dependencies) to avoid dangling project dependency metadata.

Removing the vt renderer project reference is consistent with the PR goal (dropping VtEngine), but please double-check that Host.FuzzWrapper doesn’t still include/link any headers/libs that were provided transitively by vt.vcxproj. If it does, this will become a build break only in certain configurations (e.g., fuzzing/CI). Consider verifying the fuzzer target’s additional dependencies/include paths and, if needed, replace this with the new VT I/O/translation library reference (or whatever supersedes vt.vcxproj) rather than relying on transitive linkage.

Unconditionally calling `WriteToScreen(context, context.GetViewport());` in ConPTY/VT I/O mode reintroduces the resize quirk described in the issue: on a resize to a larger viewport, ConPTY will emit the reflowed buffer and can overwrite existing terminal content. The removed guard (`IsResizeQuirkEnabled`) previously avoided invalidating/resending the entire viewport in that scenario. Please either restore a ConPTY-specific conditional (or a new equivalent check in the new VT writer path) and/or add an explicit comment/doc link here explaining the ConPTY behavior and why we intentionally avoid full redraws on resize. At minimum, ensure the behavior is covered by a test or a feature flag so clients can opt out of the destructive redraw.

This hunk removes the UNIT_TESTING-only helper `Globals::EnableConptyModeForTests(...)` (including the `resizeQuirk` toggle) from this translation unit. Please ensure the test surface area is preserved elsewhere: any unit tests that relied on forcing ConPTY mode and/or explicitly toggling the resize quirk will now either fail to compile/link or lose coverage. If the intent is to refactor away `VtEngine`, consider replacing this with a new test hook that injects the new VT writer/translator abstraction (or a mock) and still allows controlling the resize behavior, or update all tests to use the new mechanism. Also verify there isn't a remaining declaration in the header that would now cause an unresolved external symbol under UNIT_TESTING.

This hunk removes the UNIT_TESTING-only helper `EnableConptyModeForTests(...)` that allowed injecting a `VtEngine` and toggling the `resizeQuirk` behavior. Given the issue/PR context (documenting/handling the ConPTY resize reflow quirk and refactoring away VtEngine), please ensure tests still have an equivalent way to (a) enable ConPTY mode and (b) explicitly exercise the resize quirk path. If the quirk is still relevant, consider preserving a test hook (even if it no longer takes a `VtEngine`) or updating the unit tests to use the new writer/translator abstraction; otherwise, we risk losing coverage/regressing the documented behavior.

Removing the forward declaration is fine if `VtEngine` is no longer referenced from this header. Please double-check that no remaining declarations (e.g., member pointers/references, friend declarations, or method signatures) still mention `Microsoft::Console::Render::VtEngine` either directly or via macros/typedefs, and that any corresponding includes were also cleaned up in translation units that previously relied on this forward decl. Otherwise, this can surface as a compile error only in certain build configurations/unity builds.

This changes the cursor visibility decision from a simple mode check (`IsInVtIoMode()`) to calling `gci.GetVtIo(nullptr)`. Two concerns: (1) `GetVtIo` reads like an accessor that may have side effects (lazy init, locking, allocation) and could be more expensive than a boolean check in a hot path like buffer switching; and (2) passing `nullptr` is a bit opaque—if `GetVtIo` expects an out-param or uses the pointer for error reporting, this could be fragile. If the intent is just “cursor should be on when VT I/O is active/available”, consider adding/using a dedicated predicate (e.g. `HasVtIo()` / `IsVtIoActive()`) or at least document that `GetVtIo(nullptr)` is side-effect-free and safe with null. Also ensure the return type is unambiguous here (bool vs pointer) to avoid accidental truthiness changes.

This changes the mode check from `IsInVtIoMode()` to `GetVtIo(nullptr)` and relies on implicit pointer-to-bool conversion. That’s a bit opaque and also potentially changes semantics if `GetVtIo` can be non-null outside of “VT I/O mode” (or if it can be temporarily null while still in that mode). Consider using an explicit predicate (e.g. reintroduce/keep `IsInVtIoMode()` or add `HasVtIo()`), or at least make the intent explicit: `gci.GetVtIo(nullptr) != nullptr`. Also, if `GetVtIo` can fail/return null depending on parameters, passing `nullptr` here should be justified (comment) to avoid future regressions.

Same concern as above: `GetVtIo(nullptr)` used as a boolean is less readable and may not be equivalent to the previous `IsInVtIoMode()` check. Prefer an explicit comparison (`!= nullptr`) and/or a dedicated boolean API to represent “pty mode” so this function doesn’t accidentally start returning true in cases where a VT writer exists but the console isn’t actually operating as a PTY.

`_textBuffer->Reset()` is a much more destructive operation than the previous implementation (which effectively rotated the circular buffer to the cursor row and then erased to end). This may change observable behavior for clients that expect scrollback/circular-buffer semantics to be preserved (especially in ConPTY where resize/reflow quirks are being discussed). Please confirm that `Reset()` matches the intended contract of `ClearBuffer()` across modes (conhost vs ConPTY), and if not, consider keeping the prior logic or adding a mode-specific path. Also ensure `Reset()` preserves any state that should survive a clear (e.g., current attributes, line rendition, tab stops, etc.), or explicitly reinitialize those pieces here.

These resize helpers were moved from private to public. Please double-check that this is intentional API surface expansion for SCREEN_INFORMATION: it makes it easier for other components to bypass the existing resize entrypoints/invariants (e.g., window/buffer size coupling, VT emission, locking/notification ordering). If they’re only needed by a specific collaborator (e.g., ConPTY/renderer/adapter), consider keeping them private and exposing a narrower public method, or granting access via a friend/interface to avoid new call sites invoking reflow/traditional resize without the required surrounding state updates.

`GetVtIoNoCheck()` is a footgun: the name suggests bypassing invariants/locking/state checks that the old `GetVtIo()` likely enforced. Please document (in a comment and/or doxygen) exactly what checks are being skipped and what the caller must guarantee (e.g., console lock held, VT mode enabled, `context` matches active buffer, lifetime/threading constraints). Also consider making this harder to misuse (e.g., `private`/`protected` with specific friends, or returning a reference/optional-like wrapper) so new call sites don’t accidentally bypass required validation.

This changes the condition from a boolean `IsInVtIoMode()` to calling `GetVtIo(nullptr)` directly. Please ensure `GetVtIo` is safe to call here (no side effects, no implicit initialization, no locking) and that its return type is being used intentionally as a boolean (e.g., pointer/optional). If `GetVtIo` can return a non-null object even when not actually operating in PTY/VT-IO mode, this will change behavior. Consider keeping the semantic check (e.g., `IsInVtIoMode()`), or at least make the intent explicit: `if (ServiceLocator::...GetVtIo(nullptr) != nullptr)` and/or add a brief comment explaining why `GetVtIo` is the correct predicate now.

This change drops the `--resizeQuirk` flag, but the surrounding comment (GH#13211) still claims we must ensure the terminal obeys the resizing quirk. Either re-add the flag (if still required for correct behavior) or update/remove the comment and ensure the quirk is handled elsewhere now. As-is, this looks like a behavior change that could regress defterm/ConPTY resize behavior and makes the comment misleading.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` may leave global font state (or any resources it owns) initialized across tests, which can cause order-dependent failures or leaks. If this cleanup is no longer required due to the ConPTY/VtEngine refactor, consider either (a) deleting the underlying global font initialization entirely, or (b) adding a brief comment here explaining why font cleanup is intentionally omitted. Otherwise, keep the call to ensure test isolation.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` changes the teardown contract for this test fixture. If the font is still allocated/registered globally by `m_state` during these tests, this can cause cross-test contamination or leaks (especially when tests run in parallel or under different ordering). Please confirm that font cleanup is now handled by `CleanupGlobalScreenBuffer()`/`CleanupGlobalInputBuffer()` or by `m_state`'s destructor, and consider adding a brief comment or updating the helper API to make the new ownership/cleanup responsibility explicit.

This test case used to validate `--vtmode` parsing with tab-delimited args (and expected `vtMode == "xterm"`). After this change it no longer exercises `--vtmode` at all, which reduces coverage and may hide regressions in argument splitting/escaping. If `--vtmode` was intentionally removed from `ConsoleArguments`, consider adding a replacement assertion that the token is now treated as part of `clientCommandLine` (or add a dedicated test that verifies `--vtmode` is rejected/ignored with a clear expected behavior). Otherwise, keep a test that still includes `--vtmode` to ensure tab-delimited parsing remains correct for option+value pairs.

Removing `ConptyOutputTests.cpp`/`VtRendererTests.cpp` and the `vt.unittest` project reference will drop coverage for ConPTY output behavior (including resize/reflow quirks mentioned in the issue) and VT rendering/translation. If these tests were intentionally retired due to the VtEngine removal, consider either (a) moving/renaming them to the new VT writer/ConPTY pipeline test suite and adding the new files here, or (b) adding a replacement unit/integration test that asserts the current resize behavior (or documents it via a test name/expected failure) so the quirk stays tracked. Also ensure no other test projects still reference the removed `vt.unittest` outputs to avoid build/link regressions.

Removing this entry from the .filters file is fine for Solution Explorer organization, but please double-check that the corresponding .vcxproj no longer includes VtRendererTests.cpp (and that the file was deleted/moved). If the .vcxproj still references it, the build will still compile it and the filters file change will only hide it in the IDE, which can mask stale/accidentally-included tests during the refactor.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` may leave global font state allocated or mutated across tests, which can cause leaks or test-order dependencies. If the font cleanup is now handled elsewhere (e.g., in `CleanupGlobalScreenBuffer()` or `CleanupGlobalInputBuffer()`), consider adding a brief comment here or updating the test state cleanup contract to make that explicit. Otherwise, please ensure the font/global resources are still reset between test classes to keep the suite hermetic.

By removing the `inConpty` data property and the ConPTY-mode setup block, this test no longer exercises the VT/ConPTY code path (`gci.IsInVtIoMode()` true) that originally motivated the coverage. If the behavior under test differs between classic conhost and ConPTY (especially around resize/reflow quirks), consider either keeping a ConPTY variant (using the new VT IO plumbing instead of `Xterm256Engine`) or adding a dedicated test elsewhere that explicitly enables ConPTY mode and asserts the expected behavior. Otherwise we risk silently dropping regression coverage for the ConPTY-specific resize behavior described in the issue.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` may leave global font state allocated or dirty across tests, which can cause order-dependent failures or leaks (especially if other tests still rely on font initialization). If this cleanup is now handled by `CleanupGlobalRenderer()` or elsewhere after the refactor, consider adding a brief comment here (or an assertion in the cleanup routine) to document the new ownership and prevent future regressions. Otherwise, this call should likely remain to keep teardown symmetric with setup.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` changes test teardown behavior and may leave global font state allocated/dirty across tests, which can cause order-dependent failures or leaks. If font cleanup is no longer required due to the ConPTY/VtEngine refactor, consider either (a) deleting the underlying global font initialization entirely, (b) adding an explicit comment here explaining why font cleanup is unnecessary now, or (c) ensuring `CleanupGlobalScreenBuffer()` (or another teardown path) also releases any font-related globals so the test fixture remains self-contained.

Same concern as above: dropping `CleanupGlobalFont()` in this fixture can allow global font state to persist between tests. Please verify that no font globals are initialized in these tests anymore, or that they are cleaned up elsewhere. If cleanup is intentionally removed, add a brief rationale to prevent future reintroduction of hidden inter-test dependencies.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` may leave global font state initialized across tests, which can cause order-dependent failures or resource leaks (especially if other tests assume a clean font state). If this cleanup is no longer necessary due to refactoring, consider adding a brief comment explaining why, or ensure the font teardown is handled elsewhere (e.g., in `CleanupGlobalScreenBuffer()` or `m_state` destructor) to keep test isolation intact.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` may leave global font state allocated or mutated across tests, which can introduce order-dependent failures or leaks (especially if other test classes still rely on the global font being initialized/cleaned). If this cleanup is no longer necessary due to the refactor, consider adding a brief comment explaining why it’s safe to omit, or ensure the font cleanup is performed elsewhere (e.g., in `CleanupGlobalScreenBuffer()` or a shared test fixture teardown) to keep test isolation intact.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` from `TEST_CLASS_CLEANUP` risks leaking global font state across tests (or leaving a modified global font selected), which can introduce order-dependent failures and resource leaks. If the font cleanup is no longer required due to the ConPTY refactor, consider adding a brief comment explaining why it’s safe to omit, or ensure the cleanup is performed elsewhere (e.g., in `CleanupGlobalScreenBuffer()` or `m_state` destructor). Otherwise, please keep the call or replace it with the new equivalent cleanup API.

This is using `SetNamedPipeHandleState` on `rx`, but `rx` is created via `CreatePipe`, which returns an anonymous pipe handle (not a named pipe). `SetNamedPipeHandleState` will fail with `ERROR_INVALID_HANDLE`/`ERROR_INVALID_FUNCTION` on anonymous pipes. If the intent is non-blocking reads for the test, either (a) switch to the `createOverlappedPipe(...)` helper (named pipe + FILE_FLAG_OVERLAPPED) and use overlapped `ReadFile`, or (b) keep `CreatePipe` and remove this call, using `PeekNamedPipe`/blocking reads instead. As-is, this setup is inconsistent and likely makes the tests flaky or always failing depending on error handling.

Removing `ConptyOutputTests.cpp` (and `VtRendererTests.cpp`) drops coverage for ConPTY/VT output behavior. Given the issue/PR context around ConPTY resize/reflow quirks, please ensure equivalent tests were migrated/rewritten under the new synchronous VT translation path (or add new tests that explicitly cover ResizePseudoConsole/reflow behavior) so regressions don’t slip in silently.

This hunk removes the global includes for `libpopcnt` and `dynamic_bitset`. Please ensure no remaining translation units rely on these being transitively available via `LibraryIncludes.h` (common source of non-obvious build breaks). If they’re still used anywhere, prefer adding the includes locally in the specific .cpp/.h that needs them (or provide a dedicated wrapper header) rather than relying on this umbrella header. Also consider updating any build/nuget/vcpkg dependency declarations if these libraries are no longer required.

This header used to provide fallback definitions for CreatePseudoConsole flag bits (including the newly documented `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK`). Removing them can break consumers that include `conpty-static.h` without also including a Windows SDK/newer `winconpty.h` that defines these macros, leading to compile errors or inability to set flags. If the intent is to rely solely on SDK headers, consider adding an explicit include/requirement (and/or a version guard with a clear `#error`) or keep the `#ifndef` fallbacks (at least for `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK`) to preserve backward compatibility for older SDKs.

Switching from a raw pointer to a value member is a good simplification, but this line hard-codes a default font size (8x12) that can diverge from the `PrepareGlobalFont` default/parameter and any tests that expect an “uninitialized” font state. Consider either (a) default-constructing `m_pFontInfo` (if supported) and relying on `PrepareGlobalFont` to set it, or (b) centralizing the default font/size in a single constant/helper so the ctor and `PrepareGlobalFont` can’t drift.
