Also verify `FontInfo` is safely copy-assignable here (no owning raw pointers) since `PrepareGlobalFont` now assigns into an existing instance.

Adding `til/rect.h` here changes the public umbrella header’s transitive include set. Please ensure this is intentional (i.e., `til.h` consumers now require rect types) and that any code that previously relied on `til/bitmap.h` being transitively included is updated to include it directly. Also consider include ordering/grouping consistency (alphabetical or dependency-based) to keep `til.h` stable and predictable for downstream builds.

Replacing the include with `#pragma once` is fine, but this header now no longer includes anything it relies on. This file uses `PVOID`, `ULONG`, `uint32_t`, `std::enable_if_t`, `std::is_standard_layout_v`, and `FAIL_FAST_LAST_ERROR_IF`, so please ensure the required headers are included either here (preferred for self-contained headers) or guaranteed via a precompiled/umbrella header. Otherwise this change can introduce fragile build-order dependencies.

Good to make the call operator `const noexcept`, but consider keeping it `inline` (or defining it inside the class definition) if this header is included widely and you want to avoid any potential ODR/linkage surprises. Also, `noexcept` assumes `proc` cannot throw; if `proc` is a function pointer to a Win32 API this is fine, but it might be worth a brief comment/assertion that `proc` is always valid after construction (or guard against null in release builds too).

Marking this `noexcept` is reasonable, but note that `RtlGenRandomLoader`'s constructor uses `FAIL_FAST_LAST_ERROR_IF(!proc);` which will terminate the process rather than throw. If callers interpret `noexcept` as "cannot fail", consider documenting that this may fail-fast on systems where the proc cannot be loaded (or if that’s not acceptable, return an error/`bool` instead).

`noexcept` here inherits the same fail-fast behavior as `gen_random(void*, ...)`. Also consider value-initializing `T value{}` before filling it to avoid any potential uninitialized padding/UB concerns if `T` has padding bytes and later comparisons/hashing read them; alternatively constrain to `std::is_trivially_copyable_v<T>` and/or explicitly zero-initialize before calling `gen_random`.

This changes the call from `GetVtIo()` to `GetVtIo(nullptr)`. Please add a brief comment (or use a named constant) explaining why `nullptr` is the correct/required argument here and what behavior it selects (e.g., bypassing a specific output target, avoiding a renderer dependency, etc.). As-is, it’s non-obvious and easy to misuse later. Also consider whether this should be `GetVtIo()` overload/default parameter instead, to avoid sprinkling `nullptr` call sites.

This hunk removes the last-chance render flush (`TriggerTeardown`) that was intended to ensure pending VT output is painted before shutdown (MSFT:15506250). With the PR refactor away from the VtRenderer/VtEngine path, that may be correct—but please ensure the new synchronous VT writer path (or any remaining renderer) has an equivalent drain/flush on exit, otherwise the final chunk of output can be lost when the client dies or during fast teardown. If this is intentionally no longer needed, consider adding a brief comment here explaining why (e.g., output is now emitted synchronously / no renderer thread), and/or update/remove the old MSFT bug reference to avoid losing the rationale.

Removing `PrepareForTeardown` is fine if the interface/base class no longer requires it, but please double-check that this doesn't silently change teardown semantics for the onecore renderer path. If `IRenderEngine` (or whatever interface `BgfxEngine` implements) still declares `PrepareForTeardown`, this will either stop compiling or fall back to a default implementation that may differ (e.g., forcing a final paint or flushing). If the method was intentionally removed due to an interface refactor, consider also removing/adjusting the corresponding declaration in the header and any call sites, and ensure teardown still doesn't require a forced repaint for Bgfx.

Removing the `PrepareForTeardown` override changes the vtable surface of `BgfxEngine`. Please ensure the base interface no longer declares this method (or that it now has a default implementation) and that all call sites that previously relied on `PrepareForTeardown` semantics (e.g., forcing a final paint/flush before teardown) are updated accordingly. If the method still exists in the interface, this will either fail to compile or silently change teardown behavior if the base provides a no-op implementation.

Removing the vt.vcxproj reference is fine if the unit tests no longer directly/indirectly depend on the VT renderer library after the ConPTY refactor. However, please double-check that no test sources in this project still include/link against symbols that were previously satisfied by vt.lib (e.g., via transitive includes or static initializers). If any tests still rely on VT types/helpers, this will surface as unresolved externals only in certain configurations (x64/ARM64, Debug/Release). Consider verifying the project’s AdditionalDependencies/IncludeDirectories don’t still reference vt, and that CI covers all target platforms/configs for this project.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` changes the teardown contract for these tests. If `_state` no longer owns global font initialization, consider adding a brief comment here (or in the setup/fixture) explaining why font cleanup is no longer required, and verify that any corresponding `Init/SetupGlobalFont` call was also removed. Otherwise, this can leave global/static font state leaking across tests and cause order-dependent failures.

Good null-safety improvement, but consider whether silently skipping `CreatePseudoWindow()` is acceptable here. If `GetVtIo(nullptr)` returning null indicates an unexpected state, we should at least log/trace it (or `FAIL_FAST`/assert in debug) so we don’t lose the pseudo window and later ownership registration behavior without any diagnostics. Also, if `GetVtIo()` has side effects or is expected to be stable, it may be worth adding a brief comment explaining why it can be null in this thread/context.

Removing these include paths is fine only if no remaining code (or transitive includes) still references headers from `oss/dynamic_bitset` or `oss/libpopcnt`. Please double-check the build still succeeds across all configurations (including any optional/feature-gated code paths) and that any remaining references were migrated to other implementations. If these third-party deps are still used anywhere, consider keeping the include paths or removing the deps entirely to avoid future accidental include-order breakages.

This hunk removes `AtlasEngine::InvalidateFlush` entirely. Please ensure the corresponding declaration in the header/interface (and any vtable/ABI expectations) is also removed or updated, and that all call sites have been migrated to the new invalidation/flush mechanism. If `InvalidateFlush` was part of an `IRenderEngine`-style contract, deleting it can silently change behavior (e.g., callers expecting `*pForcePaint=false` to suppress paints) or break builds in other translation units. Consider leaving a stub implementation (possibly delegating to the new path) if binary compatibility or incremental refactors are a concern.

This hunk removes `AtlasEngine::PrepareForTeardown`, which likely was satisfying a virtual interface contract (e.g., an `IRenderEngine`/renderer base class hook). Please ensure the declaration was also removed/updated in the corresponding header and that the base interface no longer requires this override; otherwise you may get ODR/link errors or lose teardown behavior relied on by callers (e.g., forcing a final paint before swapchain/device teardown). If the method is intentionally obsolete, consider leaving a stub override (or documenting the new teardown path) to preserve interface stability and avoid regressions in shutdown/resize paths.

This removes the `PrepareForTeardown` override from `AtlasEngine`. Please ensure the base interface/class no longer declares this method (or that `AtlasEngine` still satisfies the interface via inheritance/default implementation). If the method still exists upstream, dropping `override` will either break compilation or silently change teardown behavior (e.g., missing a forced paint/flush on shutdown). Also verify any call sites that relied on `pForcePaint` being set by the atlas renderer are updated/removed accordingly.

This hunk also removes the `InvalidateFlush` override. If `InvalidateFlush(circled, pForcePaint)` is still part of the renderer interface, this will be a contract break. If it was intentionally removed as part of the refactor, please confirm that any semantics previously implemented here (notably the `pForcePaint` out-param used to trigger an immediate present) are preserved elsewhere; otherwise you may introduce subtle rendering regressions (missed flushes, stale frames) especially around resize/teardown paths.

This change removes the `dynamic_bitset.hpp` include from the atlas PCH. Please double-check that no remaining atlas renderer sources rely on `dynamic_bitset` types via transitive inclusion from the PCH. If any TU still uses it, compilation may start failing depending on include order; in that case, add a direct include in the specific .cpp/.h that needs it (preferred) or keep it in the PCH if it’s broadly used. Also consider whether the warning suppression for 4702 is still needed elsewhere; if other files include `dynamic_bitset.hpp` directly, they may now need the same pragma wrapper (or a more targeted fix).

This hunk removes the default `RenderEngineBase::InvalidateFlush` implementation. Please ensure the corresponding declaration in the header (and any interface/override expectations) is also removed or updated, and that all call sites have been migrated. If any code still calls `InvalidateFlush` through a `RenderEngineBase` pointer/reference, this will become a link error (or a pure-virtual requirement if moved elsewhere). Also consider whether the previous behavior (setting `*pForcePaint=false` and returning `S_FALSE`) is still required for compatibility with existing renderers; if so, the replacement path should preserve the out-param contract to avoid leaving it uninitialized.

With the removal of the final synchronous repaint (previously gated by VtEngine’s PrepareForTeardown/InvalidateFlush), teardown now only waits for the paint thread to stop. If any engine relied on that last on-caller-thread paint to flush pending output/selection invalidations before shutdown, this could regress “last frame” correctness (e.g., missing final VT flush or selection state). Please confirm no remaining engines require a final paint, or add an explicit comment/assert explaining why it’s safe to drop (e.g., VtEngine removed, all engines are now fully synchronous, or teardown guarantees no pending invalidations). Also consider whether any callers depended on TriggerFlush/TriggerTeardown to force a final frame before buffer reflow/resize quirks (ConPTY) and whether documentation/tests need updating accordingly.

This removes the `TriggerFlush(bool circling)` API from `Renderer`. Please double-check that all call sites were updated/removed and that any behavior previously gated by `circling` (e.g., forcing a present/flush when the render thread is "circling"/idle) is still covered by the remaining triggers. If the flush semantics are still needed for ConPTY/VT output correctness or tests, consider keeping a replacement entry point (or documenting why it’s no longer required) to avoid subtle regressions like missed final paints or delayed output delivery.

The UNIT_TESTING forward declaration block for `TerminalCoreUnitTests::ConptyRoundtripTests` was removed. Ensure no remaining test code includes this header expecting that symbol to be declared (or relies on it for friendship/access). If tests still need access, prefer moving the friend declarations/forward decls into a dedicated test-only header or using a narrower test seam (e.g., exposing specific state via accessors) to avoid brittle friend dependencies.

Removing the UNIT_TESTING `friend` declarations tightens encapsulation, but it may break existing ConPTY-related tests (`ConptyOutputTests`, `ConptyRoundtripTests`) that accessed private members. Confirm the tests were updated to use public APIs or a new test seam. If the intent is to retire those tests due to the ConPTY refactor, consider also removing/adjusting them explicitly so failures are clear and the coverage gap is intentional.

Removing the `vt` renderer directory from the build list is a broad change; please double-check that all remaining references to the VT renderer (project files, includes, tests, and any conditional compilation that expects `src/renderer/vt` to exist) have been cleaned up, and that this doesn’t break configurations that still rely on it (e.g., older build scripts or feature flags). If the directory is intentionally deprecated, consider leaving a comment or a stub entry/redirect in build docs to make the removal discoverable for downstream builders.

Removing the `PrepareForTeardown` override changes the vtable/contract with `IRenderer`. Please ensure either (a) `IRenderer` no longer declares `PrepareForTeardown`, or (b) `GdiEngine` still provides an override elsewhere (e.g., in the .cpp) and this header removal is intentional. If the method still exists in the interface, this will either fail to compile (missing override) or silently change teardown behavior (e.g., skipping a final forced paint), which could cause resource leaks or visual artifacts during shutdown/switching renderers.

This hunk removes `GdiEngine::PrepareForTeardown`, which previously explicitly set `*pForcePaint = false` and returned `S_FALSE`. Please ensure the corresponding declaration in the `GdiEngine` header/interface was also removed or updated, and that any call sites (likely via a common renderer/engine interface) won’t now fail to link or silently change behavior. If the interface still expects this hook, consider keeping a stub implementation (or providing a default implementation in the base class) to preserve ABI/behavior and avoid null/undefined `pForcePaint` handling at the caller.

This change removes `PrepareForTeardown` from the `IRenderEngine` interface. Please ensure all implementers and call sites were updated accordingly (including any teardown paths that previously relied on `pForcePaint` to force a final present/paint). If the intent is to eliminate the concept of a forced final paint, consider documenting the new teardown contract (e.g., whether `Present()`/`InvalidateAll()` is expected before destruction) to avoid regressions like missing final frame flushes or resource cleanup ordering issues.

Removing `InvalidateFlush` from the interface can be a behavioral change: callers may have depended on it to synchronously flush invalidations and determine whether a forced paint was required (via `pForcePaint`). Verify that the replacement mechanism preserves semantics for scenarios like resize/reflow, scroll, and title updates, and that any tests/telemetry that expected a flush are updated. If flushing is now implicit (e.g., via `Present()`), it may be worth adding a brief comment in the interface or updating documentation to clarify how/when invalidations are guaranteed to reach the output.

This hunk removes the `InvalidateFlush` override from `RenderEngineBase`. Please double-check that the corresponding virtual method was also removed (or made non-pure) in the base interface; otherwise derived render engines may no longer satisfy the interface or callers may lose a required invalidation/flush path. If the method still exists elsewhere, consider leaving a stub implementation here (or updating all call sites) to avoid breaking ABI/behavior for components that relied on `InvalidateFlush` to force a paint after certain state changes.

This hunk removes `UiaEngine::PrepareForTeardown`. Please double-check that the method was also removed from the corresponding header/interface (e.g., `IRenderEngine`/`UiaEngine` declaration) and that no call sites remain. If the interface still requires it, consider keeping a stub implementation (or providing a default implementation in the base interface) to avoid breaking ABI/virtual dispatch expectations and to keep COM-style contracts consistent.

This removal changes the class' override set. Please ensure `IRenderer` (or the relevant base interface) no longer declares `PrepareForTeardown`, or that `UiaRenderer` still satisfies the interface via inheritance/default implementation. If the base still requires it, this will either fail to compile or silently change teardown behavior (e.g., missing forced paint/flush before shutdown). Also verify any call sites that relied on `pForcePaint` being set are updated accordingly.

This hunk removes `WddmConEngine::PrepareForTeardown`. Please ensure the method is also removed from the corresponding header/interface (or that the base class provides a default implementation). Otherwise, any call sites expecting this override may now either fail to compile (pure virtual) or change behavior (base implementation might force a repaint or return a different HRESULT). If the intent is to rely on a default no-op, consider adding a brief comment in the base implementation or here explaining why WDDMCon no longer needs teardown preparation.

Removing the `PrepareForTeardown` override changes the vtable surface for `WddmConRenderer`. Please ensure the base interface no longer declares this method (or that it now has a default implementation) and that any teardown path that previously relied on `pForcePaint` is still handled (e.g., forcing a final paint/flush before destruction). If the method was removed from the interface, also verify all other renderer implementations were updated consistently to avoid ODR/vtable mismatches and build breaks in configurations that still compile WDDMCon.

Adding ProjectTelemetry here is fine, but this header can be relatively heavy and may introduce ordering/macro side effects. If it's only needed in a small portion of this TU, consider including it in the corresponding .cpp section (or a narrower header) to reduce compile impact. Also, please ensure it doesn't rely on precompiled header ordering (e.g., Windows headers) since this file previously only included project headers first; if telemetry requires specific include order, document it or adjust accordingly.

By dropping the `if (_api.ResizeWindow(...)) { ... SuppressResizeRepaint(); }` block, we no longer suppress the ConPTY resize repaint behavior. Given the linked issue/quirk (ResizePseudoConsole can cause ConPTY to emit a reflowed buffer and overwrite terminal content), this change likely reintroduces or worsens that behavior. If the suppression is still required for ConPTY correctness, consider keeping it (or moving it into the proposed dedicated `ResizeConpty` path) and only invoking it when the resize actually succeeds and when VT I/O is present. At minimum, please add a comment explaining why suppression is no longer needed, or ensure the new refactor provides an equivalent mitigation elsewhere.

This now unconditionally calls `textBuffer.TriggerScroll({ 0, -delta });` even in ConPTY mode. Previously this was explicitly skipped to avoid a double-scroll when the ED sequence is also propagated to the connected terminal (see the removed comment about passing through ED in pty mode). With the other changes in this hunk (many ConPTY-specific `return false` paths removed), it’s unclear whether ED is still forwarded elsewhere; if it is, this will reintroduce the “scrolled twice” behavior. Please either (a) restore the ConPTY guard here, or (b) add/update a comment and tests demonstrating that ED is no longer forwarded in pty mode and that this scroll is required/correct. Also consider whether this impacts the resize/reflow quirk being documented, since extra scrolling can overwrite visible content in the terminal.

These handler factory declarations were removed. Please double-check that (1) their definitions were also removed or made unreachable (to avoid dead code / unused symbols), and (2) any call sites were migrated to the new synchronous translation path. If passthrough/DRCS handling is still required for ConPTY/VT passthrough scenarios, consider leaving a brief comment or replacement API here to document where that functionality moved to, since this header previously advertised it.

This hunk appears to have accidentally swapped/removed the setter for `_lookingForDSR`. Previously `SetLookingForDSR(bool)` existed and assigned `_lookingForDSR = looking;`, but now line 110 defines `EncounteredWin32InputModeSequence()` again, leaving no way to mutate `_lookingForDSR` from this translation unit. If this is intentional, please ensure all call sites were updated and that `_lookingForDSR` is set elsewhere; otherwise, restore `SetLookingForDSR` (and keep `IsLookingForDSR()` as the getter) to avoid breaking DSR tracking logic.

Adding a getter is fine, but this change also removes `SetLookingForDSR(...)` from the public interface. Please double-check that no callers (including tests or other components) relied on toggling this state after construction. If the intent is to make the flag immutable post-construction, consider documenting that in the header (or making the underlying member `const`) to avoid confusion. If mutation is still required internally, ensure there is an alternative mechanism (private/protected setter) and that the state remains consistent with any parser transitions that depend on it.

This now unconditionally returns true and ignores both `string` and `flush`, effectively swallowing all passthrough VT sequences. That’s a behavior change from the previous “forward to attached TTY if present, otherwise eat” logic, and it also means callers can no longer detect that passthrough didn’t actually happen. If passthrough is intentionally removed as part of the ConPTY refactor, consider either (a) removing this method entirely and updating the state machine to not call it, or (b) returning `false` (or otherwise signaling) when passthrough is unsupported so higher layers/tests don’t assume the sequence was delivered. Also, if `flush` semantics are still relevant elsewhere, document why it’s safe to ignore here.

Marking this override as `noexcept` is a behavioral contract change: if the implementation (or anything it calls, e.g. dispatch/connection writes) can throw, the process will `std::terminate`. Please ensure the corresponding definition is also declared `noexcept` and that the body is exception-safe (either truly non-throwing or catches and converts failures to `false`). If the implementation can throw today, consider removing `noexcept` or adding a local try/catch to preserve prior behavior.

Removing these include paths is fine if nothing in the fuzzer (or its transitive includes) still depends on `dynamic_bitset`/`libpopcnt`, but this file is a build manifest and the failure mode will be a non-obvious include error later. Please double-check that no headers under `parser/ft_fuzzer` (or the parser code it pulls in) still include those OSS headers, and if they are still needed elsewhere, consider keeping them or adding a brief comment explaining why they can be dropped (e.g., dependency removed by the ConPTY refactor).

Removing `til/bitmap.h` from the unit test project may be fine if the header was deleted/renamed or is no longer intended to be part of the public TIL surface, but please ensure this change is consistent with the rest of the repo: (1) verify `inc/til/bitmap.h` is actually removed or unused; otherwise this can hide missing-include issues in tests, (2) if the file still exists and is part of the installed headers, consider keeping it listed here (or add a comment/commit note) so the project continues to track all public headers, and (3) confirm no unit tests rely on it indirectly (MSBuild filters can affect IDE visibility and some tooling).

This removes the filter entry for `til/bitmap.h`. If the header still exists and is referenced by the `til.unit.tests.vcxproj` (or expected to show up under the `inc` filter in Solution Explorer), it should remain in the `.vcxproj.filters` file. Please double-check that `bitmap.h` was also removed/renamed from the project (and not just from the filters), otherwise VS will show it as an unfiltered item or it may indicate an incomplete refactor.

This refactor changes the clamping semantics: the old code independently clamped each edge, which guarantees a viewport within `*this` even if `other` is disjoint (it collapses to the nearest boundary). Using intersection (`&`) will instead produce an empty/invalid rectangle when there is no overlap, and may also change behavior when `other` is partially outside (depending on how `FromExclusive` handles empty/negative extents). Please confirm all callers expect intersection semantics here; if they rely on the previous “snap to nearest edge” behavior, this could introduce subtle bugs. At minimum, consider adding/adjusting unit tests for disjoint and out-of-bounds cases, and/or explicitly handle the non-overlapping case (e.g., clamp-to-boundary fallback) if the old behavior is required.

This change removes the `--resizeQuirk` switch from the conhost command line, but the issue/PR context suggests we still need to *document* (or otherwise preserve) the ConPTY resize behavior. If the flag is being deprecated/removed, please ensure: (1) `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` is also removed/ignored consistently across the API surface (headers, validation, docs) to avoid a silent no-op flag; and (2) the resize overwrite/reflow behavior is documented somewhere user-facing (e.g., ConPTY docs/README) since callers can no longer opt into/out of it via this switch. Otherwise this becomes a breaking behavioral change for any client relying on the flag.

This change removes the local fallback definition for `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` and introduces `PSEUDOCONSOLE_WIN32_INPUT_MODE` with value `0x4`. Given the issue/PR context is specifically about documenting/handling the ConPTY resize quirk, dropping the `RESIZE_QUIRK` flag here may break consumers that rely on this header to compile against older SDKs (where the flag may be missing) and may also silently change behavior if code elsewhere still references `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK`. Consider keeping the `#ifndef PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` fallback (and documenting what it does) alongside the new flag, or ensure all references are updated and that the resize quirk is documented elsewhere. Also double-check the bit value: `0x4` assumes `0x2` is still reserved/used by `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK`; if that define is removed, it’s easy for future code to accidentally reuse `0x2` and create flag collisions.

This uses a hard-coded top boundary check (`result.y > 0`) instead of the buffer's origin. If the buffer origin can be non-zero (e.g. viewport/scrollback origin), this could incorrectly allow/deny wrapping at the top edge. Consider using `bufferSize.Top()` / `bufferSize.Origin().y` (or an equivalent helper) to ensure the boundary logic matches the rest of the selection code and avoids underflow when the origin isn't 0.

This test is mutating the buffer state to work around GH#780 ("Ugh"), which makes the test brittle and potentially masks real regressions in wrap handling. If possible, adjust `WriteLinesToBuffer` (or add a test helper) to correctly set `WasWrapForced` for non-wrapped rows, or explicitly construct the buffer rows with the desired wrap flags rather than patching them post-write. At minimum, consider replacing the comment with a clearer explanation of why row 2 is wrong and what invariant is being enforced here, so future changes don't accidentally rely on this manual override.

Adding project-specific tokens to the spelling allowlist is fine, but please double-check whether this is a typo rather than an intentional identifier. `swrapped` looks like it might be a truncated/misspelled form of `isWrapped`/`wrapped`/`wordIsWrapped` (especially given the nearby additions `wordi`, `wordiswrapped`, and `wrappe`). If these came from line-wrapped output or partial tokens, it would be better to fix the source text/identifier so we don't permanently allowlist accidental fragments.

Switching from `std::wstring_view` to owning `winrt::hstring` avoids lifetime issues (good), but please double-check the call sites: any code that previously inserted/queried with `std::wstring_view`/`std::wstring` should still work efficiently via the transparent hash/equal. If not, consider adding explicit overloads/helpers to avoid accidental allocations/conversions on hot paths. Also, ensure the set is only used for case-sensitive comparisons; if namespaces should be case-insensitive, the hash/equal need to reflect that.

Using a fixed 512-wchar stack buffer for a REG_MULTI_SZ policy value is risky: if the value exceeds the buffer, RegGetValueW will return ERROR_MORE_DATA and the policy will be silently ignored. Consider a two-call pattern (first call with nullptr to get required size, then allocate a std::vector<wchar_t> of that size) and handle ERROR_MORE_DATA explicitly. Also, the comment is a bit misleading here (512 wchar != 640K).

This relies on `std::wstring_view` being constructible from `hstr` via implicit conversion. That’s true for `winrt::hstring`, but the `auto` parameter makes this callable with other types (e.g. `std::wstring`, `wchar_t*`, etc.) where the conversion may be ambiguous or fail, and it also hides intent. Consider constraining the overload (e.g. `operator()(const winrt::hstring&)` plus overloads for `std::wstring_view`/`const wchar_t*`), or explicitly converting via `std::wstring_view{ hstr.c_str(), hstr.size() }` for `winrt::hstring` to avoid accidental instantiations and make the hashing semantics clear. Also ensure the required headers for `std::hash<std::wstring_view>` are included in this header or guaranteed by transitive includes.

The comment indicates this is a workaround for WinRT events “not working” in TerminalPage, but the code below is now taking a raw pointer from `terminalPage->GetTabRow()` and then dereferencing it into XAML APIs and event wiring. This is risky: (1) raw pointer lifetime is unclear (could dangle if the page recreates the TabRow), (2) it bypasses WinRT ref-counting, and (3) it makes the ownership boundary between App and Page fuzzy again. Prefer returning a WinRT projected type (e.g. `TerminalApp::TabRowControl` or `winrt::TerminalApp::TabRowControl`) by value/`com_ptr`/`weak_ref`, or expose a `UIElement`/`FrameworkElement` property on TerminalPage for the titlebar content. Also, if the underlying issue is event hookup, consider exposing explicit methods/events on TerminalPage (or using `winrt::event` with proper add/remove) rather than reaching into the visual tree from App.

Now that most UI/tab logic has moved out of App, keeping `_OnLoaded` here is a bit suspicious: it often ends up being the place where event hookups and UI initialization happen. If `_OnLoaded` still touches tab state, keybindings, clipboard, or terminal events, it should likely move to `TerminalPage` as well (or be reduced to settings-only initialization). Also consider whether this handler is still actually wired up in XAML; if not, remove it to avoid dead code and confusion about App's responsibilities.

Removing these App-level events is fine for moving tab/title responsibilities into TerminalPage, but make sure all consumers are updated accordingly. In particular, TitleChanged/LastTabClosed were part of the public WinRT surface of TerminalApp.App; deleting them is a breaking ABI/API change for any external component that might reference the App WinRT type. If this is intended, consider documenting it in the PR and ensuring the corresponding implementations/usages are removed (or moved to TerminalPage with an equivalent event) so window chrome/titlebar updates and last-tab-close shutdown behavior still work. Also verify any XAML bindings or AppHost hookups that previously subscribed to these events are migrated to the new source.

TraceLoggingRegister is called unconditionally here, but the provider is defined at file scope and may be registered multiple times if multiple TerminalPage instances are created (multi-window scenario) or if Create() is called more than once. That can lead to double-register/unregister mismatches and undefined behavior. Consider moving provider registration to a true process-wide owner (e.g., App) or guarding with a static once-flag/refcount so registration happens exactly once and unregistration happens when the last owner is destroyed.

Adding a non-trivial destructor on a XAML/WinRT type is a common source of lifetime issues if it’s used to tear down event handlers. If this destructor is intended to unhook events, make sure it’s `noexcept` and that you’re using `winrt::event_revoker`/RAII revokers (or explicit `event_token` removal) for all subscriptions to avoid callbacks after destruction. Also consider whether `~TerminalPage()` is actually needed—often `OnNavigatedFrom`/`Unloaded` is a safer place to detach UI event handlers in XAML pages.

Exposing a raw pointer to an internal UI control as a workaround for WinRT events is a leaky abstraction and risks lifetime/dangling-pointer bugs (especially if the page is unloaded/reloaded). Prefer exposing a WinRT event on `TerminalPage` (or a narrow interface) that `AppHost` can subscribe to, or return a reference/`com_ptr`/`IInspectable` with clear ownership semantics. If you must keep this, document the lifetime guarantees and consider returning `TerminalApp::TabRowControl&` (or `winrt::TerminalApp::TabRowControl`) instead of a raw pointer.

Using `std::shared_mutex` for dialog synchronization is risky in a XAML UI context: most UI operations must run on the UI thread, and locking can easily deadlock if any awaited continuation tries to reacquire the lock on the same thread. If the goal is to prevent multiple dialogs, consider an atomic/flag + dispatcher-queue serialization, or a `winrt::slim_mutex`/`std::mutex` with very tight scope and no `co_await` while holding the lock. Also ensure `_ShowDialog` never awaits while `_dialogLock` is held.

Using a relative import to "..\\App.idl" couples TerminalPage's public WinRT surface to App and can create fragile build dependencies/cycles as the boundary is being cleaned up. If the goal is just to reference LastTabClosedEventArgs, consider moving that type into a more neutral/shared IDL (e.g., TerminalAppTypes.idl) or defining it in TerminalPage.idl, and import that instead. Also verify the path separator/escaping is consistent with the rest of the project's IDL imports (some toolchains are picky about backslashes).

Relying on a comment + manual wiring here suggests an architectural leak: AppHost now assumes the root is a TerminalPage and reaches into it to hook events. This is brittle (root type can change, `as<>` can fail) and makes multi-window/page reuse harder. Consider exposing these events on the App (or a dedicated interface like `ITerminalHost`/`IRootPage`) and forwarding them internally, or have `GetRoot()` return a strongly-typed `TerminalPage` (or `IInspectable` + `try_as` with null-check) so failure is handled explicitly. At minimum, use `try_as<winrt::TerminalApp::TerminalPage>()` and guard against null to avoid a potential crash if the root isn't a TerminalPage.

Calling `OnSize` directly from `WM_DPICHANGED` may be incomplete/incorrect because `lParam` points to the *suggested window rect* (in screen coordinates) that the window should be moved/resized to via `SetWindowPos`. Using its width/height to drive layout without actually applying the suggested rect can desync the real window size from the layout, and it also ignores the new DPI value in `wParam` (which is typically needed to update scaling state). Consider: (1) applying the suggested rect with `SetWindowPos(hwnd, nullptr, left, top, width, height, SWP_NOZORDER|SWP_NOACTIVATE)` (or ensuring the base handler does), (2) updating the window/UI scaling using the new DPI from `wParam` (or calling the existing DPI-change handler) before recalculating layout, and (3) guarding against re-entrancy if `OnSize` triggers further sizing messages.

This change shifts `munges` to a new line number (1126). Please double-check that the expect file’s line numbers still match the spelling action’s reported output; these files are typically sensitive to exact line/column alignment. If the tool expects entries to be sorted or grouped in a specific way (e.g., by token then by location), ensure the reordering from the previous `1125        :munges` format to `1126:munges` is intentional and consistent with the rest of the file.

Potential lifetime/encoding issue: `wil::zwstring_view` is a non-owning view, and converting it to `winrt::hstring` assumes the view is valid and null-terminated for the duration of the conversion. Please confirm `parameter` is guaranteed to be a stable, null-terminated wide string when this callback fires. If it can be empty or non-null-terminated, consider explicitly handling `parameter.empty()` and/or constructing from a length-aware API (e.g., `winrt::hstring{ parameter.data(), static_cast<uint32_t>(parameter.size()) }`) to avoid reading past the buffer.

Changing the callback signature to include `parameter` will require updating every call site and any interface/registration that binds this method as a renderer callback. Please ensure the corresponding implementation and all invocations are updated consistently (including any std::bind/lambda adapters), otherwise this will break compilation or silently drop the extra context. Also consider whether `wil::zwstring_view` is safe here: it is non-owning, so the warning producer must guarantee the referenced string outlives the call; if the renderer constructs a temporary string, this could dangle. If lifetime is uncertain, prefer passing an owning `std::wstring`/`winrt::hstring` or document/enforce that the view points to stable storage.

Constructor takes `winrt::hstring parameter` by value and then moves it. That’s fine, but consider taking it as `const winrt::hstring&` (or `winrt::hstring&&`) to avoid an extra ref-count bump/copy at call sites that already have an lvalue. Also, if `parameter` is optional, consider defaulting it to `{}` to keep existing call sites simple and avoid forcing callers to pass an empty string.

Changing the type from `UInt64` to `HRESULT` is a breaking ABI/API change for this WinRT runtimeclass property. Please ensure all projections/consumers are updated accordingly (C++/WinRT, C#, etc.), and consider whether you need to preserve the old `UInt64` property for compatibility (e.g., add a new `HResult` property and keep `Result` as `UInt64`, or bump the interface/version if applicable). Also verify the IDL imports/metadata support `HRESULT` here and that the generated headers map it consistently (some WinRT surfaces prefer `Int32` for HRESULT values).

Adding `Parameter` is useful for richer warnings, but the name is quite generic. Consider a more specific name (e.g., `FontFamily`, `FontName`, or `SettingValue`) and document what it contains (single font name vs. the full comma-separated list, and whether it can be empty/null). Also ensure this is always populated for existing warning types so consumers don't have to special-case missing data.

The new string reads a bit ungrammatical/ambiguous: "install the missing" is missing a noun (fonts), and it’s unclear whether {0} is already formatted/quoted and how multiple fonts are separated. Consider rephrasing to something like: `Unable to find the following fonts: {0}. Please install the missing fonts or choose different fonts.` Also, if {0} can contain user-provided font family names, it may be worth quoting/escaping them consistently (e.g., `"{0}"`) to avoid confusing output when names contain commas/quotes.

This change adds a second placeholder and a format specifier (`{0:#8x}`), which is great for readability, but please double-check that the code path that formats `UnexpectedRendererError` now supplies *two* arguments (HRESULT/error code and message) and that the formatting engine used for `.resw` resources supports the `#8x` hex format. If it doesn’t, this may render literally or throw at runtime; a safer alternative is to pre-format the hex string in code and keep the resource as `{0}: {1}`.

FormatMessageW expects a DWORD message id; passing an HRESULT directly can fail for facility-coded errors (e.g. D2D/DWrite) and may return len==0, producing an empty `msg`. Consider using `HRESULT_CODE(hr)` when `HRESULT_FACILITY(hr) == FACILITY_WIN32`, and otherwise fall back to `wil::GetFailureLogString(hr)` / `winrt::hresult_error(hr).message()` (or at least handle `len==0` by providing a default string). Also, `len` includes the trailing CR/LF for many system messages—trimming would avoid odd formatting in the notice.

Potential null deref / unsupported interface: `font.query<IDWriteFont1>()` can return null if the system only provides an `IDWriteFont` (or if the query fails). Dereferencing it to call `IsMonospacedFont()` would crash. Consider using `if (const auto font1 = font.try_query<IDWriteFont1>()) { ... } else { /* fallback: assume monospace? or treat as proportional? */ }` and/or handle the failure explicitly. Also, `IsMonospacedFont` is only available on `IDWriteFont1`, so this should be guarded for compatibility.

This constructor now takes a `wil::com_ptr<IDWriteFontFamily>` by value, which will AddRef/Release on copy and may be more expensive than necessary when constructing many `Font` entries. Consider taking it as `const wil::com_ptr<IDWriteFontFamily>&` (or `wil::com_ptr<IDWriteFontFamily>&&` if you want to enforce move-only) to avoid an extra refcount churn. Also note this changes the member type from `winrt::com_ptr` to `wil::com_ptr`; please ensure the rest of the implementation consistently uses WIL (e.g., no remaining `.copy_from`/WinRT com_ptr APIs) and that required headers are included.

Adding warning message properties is fine, but please ensure these are actually surfaced as bindable properties in the corresponding ViewModel implementation (INotifyPropertyChanged) and that they update when the font family string changes (including comma-separated fallback edits). Otherwise the UI may not refresh the warning text when the user modifies the font list.

Binding the AutoSuggestBox Text as OneWay means user edits won’t propagate back to the view model, so typed font families (especially comma-separated fallbacks) may be lost unless you manually update Appearance.FontFace in TextChanged/LostFocus. Consider switching this to TwoWay (and possibly setting UpdateSourceTrigger=PropertyChanged if available) or ensure the event handlers always commit the final text (including when the user presses Enter or the control loses focus) to avoid settings not being saved.

This now relies on `DefaultAppearance().HasPowerlineCharacters()` instead of resolving the font via `FindFontWithLocalizedName`. That’s good for supporting comma-separated fallback lists, but please ensure `HasPowerlineCharacters()` is computed from the *effective* font fallback chain (or at least the first valid font) and not just the raw `FontFace` string. Otherwise a value like `"Consolas, MS Gothic"` could incorrectly return false if the method doesn’t parse/resolve the list. Also consider null/empty safety: if `DefaultAppearance()` can be unset, calling `.HasPowerlineCharacters()` should be guaranteed safe (or guarded) to avoid unexpected exceptions in the editor preview.

Same concern as above: `HasPowerlineCharacters()` should reflect the resolved font/fallback list and be safe when appearance/font is unset. If this is recomputed on every property change, also confirm it’s not doing expensive font enumeration synchronously on the UI thread (the old code did a lookup; the new method might do more work).

This removes the declaration of `_looksLikePowerlineFont()`. Please ensure the corresponding definition and all call sites were also removed/updated; otherwise this will either leave dead code in the .cpp or break compilation where it’s referenced. If the method is still needed for UI hints/warnings (e.g., powerline glyph guidance), consider keeping it or replacing it with a more general check that works with comma-separated font fallback lists.

This change repurposes the old `...ProportionalFontWarning.Title` resource into a new title string, but it also removes/renames the previous `...ProportionalFontWarning.Message` entry entirely. Please verify the UI code no longer expects a `.Message` resource for the proportional-font infobar; otherwise this will cause a missing resource lookup at runtime. If the infobar still has a message body, consider adding a new `Profile_FontFace_ProportionalFontWarning.Message` (or updating the code to use only the title + list) to avoid regressions.

`const auto t = target;` makes a copy of `target` and then the setter calls `t.name(value)` (and similarly `t.Clear##name()` below). If `target` is a reference/wrapper to the actual settings object, this will update only the copy and not the underlying settings, effectively breaking the setter/clear behavior. If the intent is to avoid repeated evaluation of `target`, prefer binding a reference instead (e.g. `auto&& t = target;` or `auto& t = target;`), or just call `target` directly. Also consider whether `target` can be a temporary; if so, `auto&&` is safer than `auto&`.

Consider using the WinRT API for checking emptiness: `value.size() == 0` (or `value.length() == 0`) instead of `value.empty()`. `winrt::hstring` doesn’t always expose `empty()` consistently across projections/headers, and using `size()/length()` is the more idiomatic and broadly compatible approach. Also, if this is only used to implement `StringNotEmptyToVisibility`, you could inline it there to avoid adding another converter method unless it’s reused elsewhere.

Consider naming consistency with the existing `StringsAreNotEqual`/`StringNotEmptyToVisibility` helpers. `StringNotEmpty` reads a bit ambiguous (is it checking for `nullptr`, empty, or whitespace-only?). If this is intended to be a general-purpose converter predicate, it may be worth clarifying the semantics (e.g., `StringIsNotEmpty` vs `StringHasValue`, and whether it trims whitespace) and ensuring it matches whatever `StringNotEmptyToVisibility` expects. Also, if this is only used internally by `StringNotEmptyToVisibility`, you could keep it private/`static` in the .cpp to avoid expanding the public surface unnecessarily.

Consider naming consistency and redundancy: there is already `StringNotEmptyToVisibility(String value)` right below, and `StringNotEmpty` may overlap with existing XAML binding capabilities (e.g., `x:Bind`/`String.IsNullOrEmpty` patterns). If this is intended as a general-purpose converter helper, please ensure the implementation treats whitespace-only strings appropriately (e.g., `"   "`), and document whether it checks `IsNullOrEmpty` vs `IsNullOrWhiteSpace`. Also verify this new IDL method is actually used; adding IDL surface area has ABI/API implications and should be justified by a concrete binding scenario.

Using `str.find(L"-")` treats any '-' anywhere in the string as a sign (e.g. `"12-34"` becomes negative) and also accepts multiple/embedded signs. Consider restricting sign handling to a leading sign only (e.g. `!str.empty() && str.front() == L'-'`) and then parsing `str.substr(1)`. Also consider handling a leading '+' consistently (either accept it or reject it explicitly) to match typical numeric parsing expectations.

This `try_query_to` result is ignored, which can silently leave `fontFallback1` null if the created fallback doesn't implement the expected interface (or if `fontFallback` is null). Consider checking the returned HRESULT (e.g. `THROW_IF_FAILED(...)` or at least logging/warning) and/or guarding with `if (fontMetrics->fontFallback)` to make the failure mode explicit. Otherwise later code that assumes `fontFallback1` is valid may crash or behave inconsistently.

This changes the error check semantics: `GetUserDefaultLocaleName` returns an `int` length (0 on failure), not an HRESULT. Using `FAILED(...)` was incorrect, but please ensure the new condition matches the API contract and that the fallback string is written safely. Consider also explicitly null-terminating after the `memcpy` (or use `wcscpy_s(localeName, L"en-US")`) to avoid relying on the copied bytes including the terminator and to make the intent clearer.

Storing `systemFontFallback` in `Resources` makes sense, but please ensure it’s initialized and refreshed when the DWrite factory/device context changes (e.g., on adapter recreation, DPI changes, or when `_recreateAdapter()`/font updates rebuild DWrite objects). `IDWriteFontFallback` can be tied to the factory it was created from; caching it across factory rebuilds may lead to subtle failures or stale fallback behavior. Consider documenting the lifetime/ownership expectations here (or resetting it alongside other DWrite resources) to avoid using a fallback created from an old factory.

This changes the callback invocation to pass an empty second argument. Please confirm that `{}` is unambiguously the intended type (e.g., `std::vector<std::wstring>{}` / `std::span<const std::wstring>{}` / `std::wstring_view{}`) and won’t bind to an unintended overload or initializer_list constructor. If the callback expects a specific container/view of invalid font names, consider using an explicit type here for clarity and to avoid future overload-resolution surprises (e.g., `std::vector<std::wstring>{}` or `std::span<const std::wstring>{}`). Also ensure all other call sites were updated consistently to the new signature.

This early-return avoids drawing when soft fonts aren’t enabled, but it also silently falls back to default shading for any U+EF2x glyph. Consider whether this should also trigger a warning (similar to other invalid/unsupported rendering paths) so users can diagnose why a glyph didn’t render as expected. Also, please ensure `p.s`, `p.s->font` are guaranteed non-null here (or add a defensive check) since this function now returns before touching other members and may be called in more situations after the signature change.

Changing `_drawSoftFontGlyph` to return `ShadingType` is a signature-breaking change; please ensure all call sites are updated to use/propagate the returned value (and that the previous behavior wasn’t relying on side effects only). Also consider marking it `[[nodiscard]]` if the return value is required for correct rendering, to prevent accidental ignoring. Finally, verify the header/implementation signatures match exactly (including `const`/`noexcept`) to avoid ODR/link errors.

Changing the warning callback signature to include a `wil::zwstring_view` is useful for reporting invalid font names, but please ensure the lifetime of the referenced string is guaranteed for the duration of the callback. `zwstring_view` is non-owning; if you pass a view into a temporary (e.g., a local `std::wstring` built for the warning), the callback may observe a dangling pointer. Consider documenting the lifetime contract here, or switching to an owning type (`std::wstring`) if the callback might be invoked asynchronously or stored for later use.

This is an ABI/API change to the renderer interface: all implementers/callers of SetWarningCallback must be updated accordingly. Please ensure the new parameter is consistently documented/used (what does the zwstring_view represent—invalid font name, warning text, etc.). Also consider lifetime/threading: wil::zwstring_view is non-owning, so the callback must not store it beyond the call; if warnings can be queued/async, prefer passing an owning std::wstring (or explicitly guarantee synchronous invocation). Finally, include/forward-declare requirements: this header now depends on wil::zwstring_view—verify the appropriate WIL header is included transitively or add an explicit include to avoid fragile builds.

Defining a `VerifyOutputTraits` specialization for `std::vector<std::wstring>` in this TU can be risky: it’s a specialization of a WEX template for a standard-library type, and if another test file adds the same specialization you’ll hit ODR/redefinition issues (or inconsistent formatting across TUs). Consider moving this into a shared test helper header (included once) or using a local helper `ToString`/custom comparer for this test instead of a global specialization. Also, ensure the specialization is in the correct namespace scope expected by WEX (some frameworks require the specialization to be in the primary template’s namespace, but not nested-qualified like this).

Restarting the system FontCache service is a high-impact operation (requires admin, can disrupt other apps) and this script is described as a “failed attempt” left for reference. Consider guarding this with an explicit confirmation prompt (or a -Force switch), and/or checking for elevation before calling Restart-Service to avoid confusing failures. Also, it may be safer to use `Restart-Service -Name FontCache -ErrorAction Stop` and handle errors explicitly so the script doesn’t silently proceed when the restart fails.

This lambda captures `this` strongly and is registered as an event handler on `term`. If `TerminalPage` can be destroyed before the `TermControl` (or if the handler isn’t explicitly revoked), this can lead to a use-after-free when `TitleChanged` fires. Consider using an event revoker / storing the token and unregistering in teardown, or capture a `weak_ref`/`weak_ptr` to `TerminalPage` (or otherwise ensure `term`’s lifetime is strictly bounded by the page). Also, `newTitle` is unused; either omit the parameter (if the delegate type allows) or mark it unused to avoid warnings.

The new comment block above this method still describes the old Enter-key handler and has a duplicated/awkward bullet ("Funnels focus..." and "Selects all text."). Please update it to match the new semantics (GotFocus handler that forwards focus to the TextBox and selects all text) and keep the style consistent with the surrounding "Method Description/Arguments/Return Value" format used elsewhere in this file.

Adding an `OnGotFocus` handler is a good direction for letting XAML manage focus, but please ensure this is actually wired up in the corresponding XAML/InitializeComponent (e.g., `GotFocus="OnGotFocus"`) and that it calls `args.Handled(true)` only if you truly want to stop focus bubbling. Also consider whether this should be `OnGettingFocus` instead (or in addition) if the intent is to redirect focus into the inner TextBox before focus is finalized, which can avoid focus flicker and improve keyboard navigation.

Removing these methods from the public WinRT surface is a breaking change for any external consumers of TerminalControl/SearchBoxControl (and will also require updating any generated headers/usages). If this control is intended to be used only internally, consider explicitly documenting/enforcing that (e.g., keep it non-public or ensure no external projections rely on it). Otherwise, consider keeping these APIs (possibly deprecated) or providing an alternative focus API/event pattern so downstream code can still programmatically focus the textbox / query focus state.

Setting `IsTabStop="True"` on the SearchBoxControl itself may reintroduce it into the tab order even if the intent of this PR is to reduce/curate tab stops and keep focus primarily on an internal focus-holder control. Since the actual interactive element is likely the TextBox inside this control, consider whether the tab stop should instead be on that inner TextBox (and keep the UserControl `IsTabStop="False"`), or explicitly document why the container must be tabbable. Also verify that this doesn’t cause double-tab behavior (tabbing to the container, then tabbing again into the TextBox) depending on `TabNavigation="Cycle"` and the inner control’s `IsTabStop` settings.

Focusing the SearchBox control itself may not reliably place the caret into the inner TextBox (and may not preserve the previous behavior of selecting all text). Previously this called SetFocusOnTextbox() on the SearchBoxControl implementation. If SearchBox is a composite control, consider either (a) keeping an explicit API on SearchBoxControl to focus/select the textbox (public method/event), or (b) ensure SearchBoxControl handles GotFocus by forwarding focus to its text field and selecting text. Otherwise keyboard input/IME and selection behavior may regress depending on template/focusability.

Adding `OnGotFocus` is fine, but please ensure it’s actually wired up (either via XAML `GotFocus="OnGotFocus"` or `GotFocus({ this, &TermControl::OnGotFocus })`) and that the signature matches the event you’re subscribing to. In WinUI/UWP, `GotFocus` is typically `RoutedEventHandler` (sender + args) or `FocusEventHandler` (sender + FocusRoutedEventArgs). If you intend to handle focus transitions (e.g., redirecting focus to the internal focus-holder control), consider using `FocusRoutedEventArgs` so you can inspect `OriginalSource`/reason and avoid focus loops. Also consider marking it `noexcept` if it cannot throw, consistent with other event handlers in this codebase.
There’s a trailing whitespace after the attribute value. Please remove it to avoid noisy diffs and potential style/lint failures (the rest of the file appears to avoid trailing spaces).

Renaming the include to `TfEditSession.h` is fine for spelling consistency, but it will break builds on case-sensitive filesystems (and potentially Windows) unless the actual header file was also renamed and all other includes updated. Please ensure the header on disk is `TfEditSession.h` (matching case), update any other references to `TfEditSes.h`, and consider adding a compatibility shim header (e.g., keep `TfEditSes.h` that includes `TfEditSession.h`) if external/older code might still include the old name.

After renaming the file/header, make sure the include path matches the actual header name on disk and that all other translation units were updated too. This is especially important for case-sensitive filesystems (e.g., Linux CI) where `TfEditSession.h` vs `TfEditses.h`/different casing would break builds. Also verify any include guards/`#pragma once` and project file references (CMake/vcxproj) were updated consistently with the rename.

The header comment line appears to have lost its original indentation/alignment (the leading spaces before `8:` were removed). If this file uses a fixed-format comment block (common in this repo), keep the spacing consistent to avoid noisy diffs and maintain readability. Consider changing this to match the surrounding lines, e.g. ` 8:    TfEditSession.h` (with the same leading spaces as lines 5–7/9–11).

This looks like a header rename/fix, but please verify the actual file name and include path match the repository (case-sensitive on some CI runners) and that all other translation units were updated accordingly. If the old header was intentionally named `TfEditses.h` (e.g., abbreviation for EditSession), changing only this include may break builds unless the file was also renamed/aliased. Consider adding a compatibility shim header (old name including the new one) or updating all references in the project to avoid Windows-only success and Linux/macOS CI failures.

This looks like a rename from `TfEditses.cpp` to `TfEditSession.cpp`. Please ensure the actual file was renamed in the repo (and not just the listing), and that any build/project files (e.g., vcxproj/cmake lists) and includes referencing the old filename are updated accordingly. Also double-check the surrounding formatting: the removed line had a different spacing pattern (`- 51          :`) while the new line aligns with the other entries; make sure this file’s expected column alignment/format is consistent so tooling that parses it doesn’t break.

Renaming the compile unit is fine, but please ensure the underlying file rename is reflected everywhere: the file must exist on disk with the exact casing, and any references in filters (.vcxproj.filters), includes, and build scripts should be updated too. Also confirm the old TfEditses.cpp is removed from the repo to avoid duplicate/unused sources or case-insensitive collisions on Windows vs. case-sensitive CI environments.

Same concern for the header rename: verify TfEditSession.h exists and that any #include "TfEditses.h" usages were updated. If this is purely a spelling fix, consider adding a temporary compatibility header (or ensuring no external consumers rely on the old name) to avoid breaking downstream builds.

This renames the file reference from `TfEditses.cpp` to `TfEditSession.cpp`. Also ensure any includes/usages were updated consistently (e.g., `TfEditses.h` -> `TfEditSession.h`) to avoid build breaks on case-sensitive filesystems/CI.

This entry appears to include leading whitespace and a numeric prefix ("1:") that may be specific to a generated/annotated wordlist format. Consider adding just `IBox` (and similarly `oaidl`/`ocidl`) without the index/colon, or confirm this file is intentionally in the `N:word` format and that alignment whitespace is ignored by the checker.

Adding a binary .xlsx to the repo can be problematic for reviewability, diffing, and long-term maintenance. Consider exporting the relevant calculations/notes to a text-based format (e.g., .md/.csv) or at least include a short README alongside it describing what the spreadsheet contains, how it relates to the code changes, and how to regenerate/update it. Also confirm the project’s contribution guidelines allow committing binary Office documents and that this won’t bloat the repo unnecessarily.

Tying the `_newBottomLine` fast-path to `coord.Y == _lastViewport.BottomInclusive()` makes sense to avoid skipping erases on non-bottom lines, but please double-check the invariants around `_lastViewport` here. If `_lastViewport` can be stale relative to `coord` (e.g., after a scroll/viewport update within the same paint pass), this condition could incorrectly fall back to the erase path (or miss the optimization) and reintroduce artifacts like the “first line not cleared” behavior. Consider adding a brief comment explaining why the bottom-inclusive check is required and/or asserting that `_lastViewport` corresponds to the current frame when `_newBottomLine` is true.

This change drops the `removeSpaces` guard and will now always emit `numSpaces` spaces when `numSpaces > 0`. That’s a behavior change: callers that set `removeSpaces=false` likely expected trailing spaces to be preserved (or at least not actively written). If the intent is to *always* write spaces here, please update the surrounding comment/variable naming to reflect that `removeSpaces` is no longer honored in this branch, or remove the now-dead parameter/flag usage to avoid confusion. Otherwise, consider keeping the guard and ensuring the bug fix is achieved by tightening the condition elsewhere.

Using `PROPVARIANT` with `VT_LPWSTR` and `const_cast<wchar_t*>(name.data())` is risky because `std::wstring_view::data()` is not guaranteed to be null-terminated, and the property store may treat it as a null-terminated string. This can lead to reading past the view bounds or garbage in the title. Consider using `InitPropVariantFromString` (or `wil::unique_prop_variant` / `wil::make_prop_variant`) with a real null-terminated `std::wstring` copy, or set `VT_LPWSTR` with an allocated copy (e.g., `SHStrDupW`) and clear it appropriately. Same concern applies to the icon path below.

Marking this as `noexcept` is only safe if the implementation cannot throw (including from any WinRT/C++/STL calls used inside). If `UpdateJumplist` uses winrt projections (e.g., `winrt::hstring`, `winrt::to_hstring`, `winrt::com_ptr`, etc.) or any code that may throw `winrt::hresult_error`/`std::bad_alloc`, `noexcept` will turn those into `std::terminate`. Consider either removing `noexcept`, or ensuring the .cpp wraps the body in a `try/catch (...)` and converts exceptions to an `HRESULT` (e.g., `winrt::to_hresult()`), so the contract remains "returns HRESULT" without risking termination.

Same concern as above: `noexcept` on an `HRESULT`-returning helper is fine only if the implementation is exception-free or explicitly catches and translates exceptions. Also, `[[nodiscard]]` is good, but make sure all call sites actually check/propagate the returned `HRESULT` (otherwise this will start producing warnings).

With `noexcept` here, any allocation/conversion while building `name/path/args` or COM/WinRT helper usage inside `_createShellLink` must not throw, or must be caught and converted to `HRESULT`. Additionally, consider documenting ownership semantics for `IShellLinkW** shLink` (e.g., "out param, caller releases") and ensure the function sets `*shLink = nullptr` on failure to avoid leaving callers with an uninitialized pointer.

This change removes a dangling, empty reference definition line. Good cleanup, but please ensure the markdown reference list remains valid and that there isn’t an intended link label missing here (e.g., a `[#NNNN]: ...` entry that got accidentally dropped). If this was just a stray line, consider also running/adding a markdown lint check to prevent reintroducing empty reference definitions.

Typo fix looks good. Since this comment is warning about test fragility due to resource string changes, consider also mentioning the specific resource keys involved (or pointing to the relevant .resw entries) to make future maintenance easier when the test breaks due to localization/resource updates.

Typo fix looks good. While you're here, consider tightening the wording to be more precise about what "_init_" refers to (e.g., "static initialization" or "module init") and why resources can't be loaded then. This comment is explaining a subtle lifetime/initialization-order constraint, so clarity helps future maintainers.

Good change making GenerateName() const, but please ensure the corresponding declaration in the header/IDL (e.g., ActionArgs.h / generated winrt interface) is also updated to `const` for all these methods. Otherwise you can end up with a signature mismatch (won't override/implement the intended method, or fails to compile depending on how it's declared). Also double-check that GenerateName() doesn't mutate any cached state; if it does, consider `mutable` for the cache or avoid caching to keep const-correctness meaningful.

Making GenerateName() const is a good signal that it shouldn't mutate state, but please ensure the corresponding definitions in ActionArgs.cpp (and any overrides/implementations across all *Args types) are also updated to `const`. Otherwise you'll get signature mismatches or accidental overloads. Also double-check that GenerateName() doesn't lazily cache/modify any members; if it does, consider `mutable` for the cache or refactor to keep this method logically const.

The ternary mixes a value-type `args.Equals(...)` call with a raw `==` comparison on the else branch, which can be fragile if `Args()` is a WinRT interface/nullable type (pointer identity vs value equality). Consider normalizing the comparison to a single semantic: e.g. explicitly handle the null case (`if (!args && !actionAndArgs.Args())`) and otherwise use `args.Equals(actionAndArgs.Args())`. Also, `actionAndArgs.Args()` is called multiple times; caching it in a local `const auto otherArgs = actionAndArgs.Args();` would avoid repeated property access and make the null/equality logic clearer.

Using `result->Name()` as the map key means command identity is tied to the *display name*. That can be unstable (localization changes, user renames, duplicate names across different actions) and can cause unintended overrides/removals when layering. Consider introducing a stable identifier (e.g., explicit `id` in JSON, or a derived key from action+args) and key the map on that, while keeping `Name` purely for UI. If name-based override is intentional, it would be good to document this behavior and potentially warn on duplicates to avoid silent clobbering.

This change removes the `IconPath` observable property from `Command`. If the command palette UI (or any other consumer) still expects to bind to an icon (e.g., showing an icon next to the command name), this will be a breaking change and may cause XAML binding failures at runtime. Please verify all bindings/usages of `IconPath` were removed or migrated (e.g., to a different icon representation like `Icon`/`IconSource`/`Symbol`), and consider keeping a deprecated alias for compatibility if other parts of the app/extensions still rely on it.

Removing `IconPath` from the IDL is a breaking change for the WinRT surface. Please ensure all projections/usages are updated (XAML bindings, serialization/deserialization, any JSON model mapping, and tests) and consider whether this should be a deprecation instead of outright removal. If icons are still needed for the command palette UI, an alternative property (e.g., `Icon` as a structured type) or keeping `IconPath` for compatibility may be preferable.

Good to replace the placeholder TODO with a real issue reference. Consider also adding a brief note about the intended behavior/criteria for “recently used” ordering (e.g., MRU list persisted per profile/session, decay, pinning) so the TODO remains actionable even if the issue context changes.

Setting `AutomationProperties.AcceleratorKey` to the displayed `KeyChordText` is a good start, but note that this property is intended to describe an actual keyboard shortcut that activates the element. In the command palette, the chord shown may be informational (or may not be a real accelerator for the ListViewItem itself), which can mislead screen readers. Consider using `AutomationProperties.HelpText`/`AutomationProperties.Description` (or include the chord in `AutomationProperties.Name`) unless the item is truly invokable via that accelerator. Also ensure `KeyChordText` is localized/normalized (e.g., "Ctrl+Shift+P") so AT reads it correctly.

Changing these direction strings from Title Case to lowercase will affect any UI that displays them (not just the command palette). If the goal is to match VS Code-style key labels, consider keeping the resource values in their canonical form (e.g., "Left") and applying casing/styling in the XAML template for the keycap (e.g., TextTransform/typography) or introduce separate resources specifically for keycap labels (e.g., DirectionLeftKeycap) to avoid unintended regressions elsewhere.

This helper is still hardcoding English modifier names ("Ctrl", "Shift", "Alt") and even calls out the localization gap in the comment. Since this is used for UI shortcut display, consider routing through a localized resource (e.g., string resources / XAML accelerator label conventions) or a central formatter shared with other shortcut UI to avoid duplicating non-localized strings. Also, if Win key is a possible modifier in Settings::KeyModifiers, it should either be handled here or explicitly documented as intentionally omitted.

Removing `CommandSerialization.cpp` from the project will exclude it from the build. Please confirm this file is truly unused/removed (or moved to another project) and that no symbols from it are still referenced. If it was replaced/renamed, consider updating the project to include the new file instead, and ensure any unit tests/CI configurations that depended on it are updated accordingly to avoid link-time or missing-feature regressions.

Removing the `ST` (String Terminator, 0x9C) constant may break or complicate parsing of 8-bit C1 string-terminated sequences (DCS/APC/PM/OSC) when the input stream uses single-byte C1 controls instead of `ESC \\`. Even if the current implementation only targets 7-bit forms, keeping this constant is low-cost and helps ensure correct handling/ignoring of sequences in environments that emit 0x9C. Consider retaining `ST` (and potentially other C1 equivalents) or adding a comment explaining that 8-bit C1 controls are intentionally unsupported/normalized elsewhere.

This `if` should likely be `else if`. As written, when `wch` is a C0 control or DEL, you call `_ActionIgnore()` but then still fall through and evaluate `_isNumericParamValue/_isParameterDelimiter` and the rest of the chain. That can cause unintended behavior (e.g., treating control chars as parameter input or transitioning state) and also breaks the intended mutually-exclusive structure used in the other state handlers. Consider changing to `else if` (and/or returning early) to ensure ignored characters don't get processed further.

This test no longer asserts the state transition after receiving ':' in DcsEntry (unlike the similar test above at line 612). Without a VERIFY here, the test could pass even if ':' is mishandled (e.g., treated as data or routed to the wrong DCS substate) as long as the subsequent ESC \\ eventually returns to Ground. Consider adding `VERIFY_ARE_EQUAL(mach._state, StateMachine::VTStates::DcsIgnore);` after processing ':' to explicitly validate the intended behavior for the 0x90 (DCS) introducer path.

This change hard-codes `allowBottomExclusive` to `true` and removes the parameter from `MoveToPreviousGlyph`, which alters the API/behavior for all callers. If some call sites previously relied on `allowBottomExclusive == false` to prevent landing on the EndExclusive (nonexistent) cell, this could introduce subtle boundary/selection bugs. Consider either (a) keeping the parameter and passing it through, (b) renaming the function to reflect the new always-allow-EndExclusive behavior, or (c) adding a comment explaining why previous-glyph movement must always allow EndExclusive (e.g., to support degenerate ranges at end-of-buffer for accessibility) and auditing/updating all call sites accordingly.

This change removes the `allowBottomExclusive` parameter from the public API. Please ensure all call sites are updated accordingly and that the intended behavior at the bottom-exclusive/end-exclusive boundary is still achievable (or explicitly no longer supported). If bottom-exclusive handling is still needed for some scenarios (e.g., UIA movement at EndExclusive), consider either keeping the parameter (possibly with a clearer name) or providing a separate helper/overload to avoid silently changing semantics for existing callers.

The comment says "_end will always be endInclusive", but the assertion here is on `utr->_start` and uses `secondToLastCharacterPos` when `!degenerate`. This is a bit confusing and also potentially brittle: `secondToLastCharacterPos` is computed as `RightInclusive() - 1`, which can underflow/produce an invalid coordinate if the buffer width is 1 (or if `RightInclusive()` equals `Left()`). Consider either (a) guarding the test with an assumption that the buffer width is >= 2, or (b) computing the expected start via a helper that clamps within the line. Also, update the comment to explicitly describe the expected `_start` behavior for degenerate vs non-degenerate ranges to match the assertion.

This change drops `allowBottomExclusive` when moving backward by character. That alters the boundary semantics for EndExclusive/degenerate ranges and may reintroduce the original crash/incorrect movement at the end of the buffer. If `MoveToPreviousGlyph` no longer needs the flag, consider removing `allowBottomExclusive` from this code path entirely (and any related state) to avoid confusion. Otherwise, pass the appropriate value (or add an overload/explicit comment) so backward movement from EndExclusive is handled consistently with the line/unit logic and the updated tests.

Hard-coding `true` here changes behavior vs. the previous `allowBottomExclusive` parameter and makes the call site ignore the caller's intent. If the goal is to always allow decrementing from an EndExclusive position when moving backward by line, please add a brief comment explaining why this must always be `true` (and consider renaming the parameter at the callee for clarity). Otherwise, this should likely remain `allowBottomExclusive` to keep endpoint movement semantics consistent across units and avoid subtle off-by-one/degenerate-range issues.

The return value from `SystemFunction036` (`RtlGenRandom`) is ignored here. Even though failure is rare, the API contract is that it returns `BOOLEAN` indicating success. Consider checking the result and failing fast (or throwing) on `FALSE` to avoid silently returning uninitialized/weak data. Also, `operator()` can be marked `noexcept` and should probably take `ULONG`/`uint32_t` consistently with `gen_random` (or add a `static_cast<ULONG>(length)` at the call site) to avoid implicit narrowing/ABI mismatches on unusual platforms.

Removing the `desktop6` namespace is fine only if no remaining elements reference it. Please double-check other hunks/files (including the non-Dev manifest) to ensure `desktop6:` elements were removed everywhere; otherwise the manifest will fail schema validation at build/package time.

This change removes `desktop6:RegistryWriteVirtualization` (disabled). Given the PR description mentions opting out of registry redirection to edit the default terminal setting, dropping this may re-enable registry virtualization in the packaged Dev build and break persistence/lookup behavior (writes going to the virtualized hive). If the intent is to keep unvirtualized registry access, consider keeping this element (and the required namespace) or document why it’s no longer needed (e.g., switching to a different capability/manifest element or using a full-trust process outside virtualization).

Removing `rescap:Capability Name="unvirtualizedResources"` may prevent access to unvirtualized registry/resources in packaged scenarios. Since the feature needs to read/write the system default terminal registration, please confirm the app still has the necessary capability in the correct manifest(s) (Dev/Release) and that the settings UI can actually persist changes when running packaged. If this capability is intentionally removed, add a note explaining what now provides the required access.

This removes the `desktop6` namespace, but the PR description says the manifest was changed to opt out of registry redirection so Terminal can edit the default-terminal registry setting. If other hunks still reference `desktop6:*` elements (or if we still need `RegistryWriteVirtualization`), dropping the namespace will break manifest validation/build. Please confirm the opt-out mechanism was intentionally reverted for the Preview package, or ensure the required namespace/element remains consistent with the chosen approach.

Removing `desktop6:RegistryWriteVirtualization` re-enables registry write virtualization for the packaged app. Given this feature needs to write to the real registry (per PR description), this may prevent persistence from working or cause writes to go to the virtualized hive. If the intent is to keep virtualization disabled, this line should likely stay (and the `desktop6` namespace too). If the intent is to rely solely on `unvirtualizedResources` or another capability, please document/verify that it actually covers the specific registry keys being written.
