Dropping `rescap:Capability Name="unvirtualizedResources"` may remove the ability to access unvirtualized system resources (including certain registry/file locations) from the packaged app. Since the PR explicitly mentions adding this capability to edit the setting, please double-check whether this capability is still required for the default-terminal write path and whether removing it will break the feature in Preview builds. If it’s no longer needed, consider adding a brief comment in the PR/commit message explaining why.

Removing the `desktop6` namespace is fine only if *all* `desktop6:`-prefixed elements are gone. Please double-check there are no remaining `desktop6:` usages elsewhere in this manifest (or included fragments), otherwise the manifest will fail schema validation at build/install time.

This change removes `desktop6:RegistryWriteVirtualization` (disabled). Given the PR description mentions opting out of registry redirection to edit the default terminal setting, dropping this may re-enable registry write virtualization and prevent writes from affecting the real HKLM/HKCU locations you expect. Can you confirm the settings code no longer requires unvirtualized registry writes, or that it now uses an API/path that isn't subject to MSIX registry virtualization? If it still needs real registry access, this should likely remain (or be replaced with the correct modern equivalent).

Removing `rescap:Capability Name="unvirtualizedResources"` may break scenarios where the app needs to access unvirtualized system resources/registry (often paired with disabling registry virtualization). If the default-terminal persistence still depends on writing to unvirtualized locations, this capability removal could cause the feature to silently fail in packaged builds. Please verify packaged Terminal can still persist the selection on Save Changes, and consider adding a comment/commit note explaining why this restricted capability is no longer needed.

This changes the call to `s_SetDefaultByPackage` by hardcoding the new boolean argument to `true`. Please add a brief comment (or use a named constant/enum) explaining what this flag means (e.g., opt-out of registry redirection / write to the real HKLM vs redirected view) and why Settings should always pass `true`. Also consider whether this should be conditional (e.g., based on elevation/capability availability) and whether failures should be handled more gracefully than throwing, since this is a UI-driven action and may fail due to permissions/policy.

Building a reg.exe command line via string formatting is fragile and potentially unsafe. `value` and the CLSID string are inserted without quoting/escaping, and `CreateProcessW` is invoked with `lpApplicationName=nullptr`, so Windows will parse `command.data()` and can mis-handle paths/spaces or allow argument injection if `value` ever contains unexpected characters. Consider calling `CreateProcessW` with `lpApplicationName=regExePath.c_str()` and a separately constructed, properly quoted argument string (or use `wil::QuoteCommandLineArg`/equivalent). Also, you should wait for reg.exe to exit and check its exit code; currently this returns S_OK even if reg.exe fails, which can make the UI think the setting was persisted when it wasn't.

These new parameters are declared as `const bool useRegExe` without a default, while `s_SetDefaultByPackage` provides a default. This creates an inconsistent API and may break existing call sites that previously passed only an IID. Consider either (a) adding a default value here as well (e.g. `= false`) to preserve source compatibility, or (b) updating all callers and documenting why these must be explicit. Also, `const bool` is unnecessary for a by-value parameter; prefer `bool useRegExe` for clarity.

`itow` is a CRT helper and is generally discouraged in favor of safer/standard alternatives (e.g. `std::to_wstring`, `wil::str_printf`, or `StringCchPrintfW`). Before adding it to the spelling allowlist, consider whether the new code can avoid introducing `itow` usage altogether; otherwise we risk normalizing a non-portable/deprecated API across the codebase.

This assumes any non-SingleWidth rendition should be treated as double-width (scale=1). If `LineRendition` can represent other states (e.g. double-height/top-bottom halves, or any future variants), this will silently mis-scale. Consider switching explicitly on the supported width-affecting renditions (e.g. `SingleWidth` vs `DoubleWidth`) and either `static_assert`/`FAIL_FAST`/default-to-0 with a comment for the rest, to avoid incorrect coordinate mapping as the enum evolves.

Calling ColorSelection unconditionally changes behavior from the previous guard that avoided doing anything when no result was selected. If `_coordSelStart == _coordSelEnd` is now a valid single-cell inclusive selection, that’s fine, but we still need a way to represent “no active result” to avoid coloring at (0,0) or a stale coordinate when search failed/cleared. Consider either (a) keeping a guard that checks an explicit “has result” flag, or (b) validating coordinates are within the buffer and/or that a match is active before calling into UIA. Also ensure `_uiaData.ColorSelection` treats start/end as inclusive; if it expects an exclusive end, this will be an off-by-one for single-character cases.

This comment appears to be incorrect/out-of-place for `GetTextRects`. It now says it returns rects, but the surrounding header still includes unrelated wording (the old “delimiter class” comment was also wrong). Please ensure the method header is accurate and consistent: update the `Return Value` section to describe `std::vector<til::inclusive_rect>` (and ideally mention inclusive coordinates and how block vs non-block selection affects the returned rects). Also check for alignment/formatting consistency with the rest of the file’s comment blocks.

Returning `const std::vector<...>` by value is redundant (the `const` doesn’t add immutability for the caller and can inhibit moves in some contexts). Prefer returning `std::vector<std::tuple<til::point, til::point>>` (or better, a named `struct`/`using TextSpan = std::pair<til::point, til::point>` for readability). Also consider whether `std::tuple` is necessary here—`std::pair` or a dedicated type would make call sites clearer (start/end semantics).

`SpanLength` takes `til::point` by value; consider taking `const til::point&` to avoid copies (even if small) and to match the style used elsewhere in this header. Also, clarify whether `coordEnd` is inclusive/exclusive (especially since other APIs use `inclusive_rect`), because off-by-one behavior will matter for selection coloring and search matches.

Same return-type concern as above: `const std::wstring` by value is unnecessary; prefer `std::wstring`. Additionally, `start`/`end` are passed by const-ref here but by value in `GetTextSpans`—it’d be good to standardize parameter passing and document whether `end` is inclusive/exclusive and whether coordinates are buffer vs viewport (since this overload doesn’t have a `bufferCoordinates` flag).

Consider handling the return value / failure path from `control.ColorSelection(...)`. Right now the lambda always reports success to `_ApplyToActiveControls` (it returns `void`), so `res` may be `true` even if the active control can't apply color selection (e.g. feature disabled, no selection, invalid match mode, etc.). If `ColorSelection` can fail, it should return a `bool`/`HRESULT` and the lambda should propagate that so `args.Handled(res)` accurately reflects whether anything was actually applied. Also, if `MatchMode()` is an int, it may be worth validating/clamping it here (or in `ColorSelection`) to avoid silently accepting unsupported modes.

Using `matchMode > 0` as the condition for a full redraw is a bit too loose/implicit. If `matchMode` is intended to be an enum-like value (0=current selection, 1=all matches, future modes), consider switching to a strongly-typed enum (or at least named constants) and explicitly handling the cases that actually require `TriggerRedrawAll()`. As written, any future nonzero mode will force a full redraw even if it could be scoped, and it also makes it harder to reason about correctness/perf. Also consider validating/guarding unexpected values (e.g. defaulting to 0) to avoid accidental expensive redraws from bad input.

The indexed-color detection/encoding here looks inconsistent with the comment and with the RGB path below. `if (_TextColor & 0xff000000)` treats any non-zero alpha byte as "indexed", but the comment says alpha==1 indicates indexed. Also, the RGB branch extracts red from `(_TextColor & 0xff000000) >> 24`, which is the alpha byte, not red (red would be `0x00ff0000 >> 16`). This likely swaps channels / misinterprets ARGB vs RGBA and will cause incorrect colors and false positives for indexed colors. Consider making the encoding explicit (e.g., reserve a dedicated flag bit or use a separate boolean), and fix the channel extraction to match the actual packed format used by the action/settings layer (document whether `_TextColor` is 0xAARRGGBB, 0xRRGGBBAA, etc.).

Exposing `TextColor` as a raw `UInt32` that is "literally" an internal `TextColor` type couples the WinRT surface to an implementation detail and makes the ABI hard to reason about/validate (and potentially brittle if `TextColor` changes). Consider defining a WinRT-friendly representation instead (e.g., a struct/enum with explicit fields like `isIndex`, `index`, `rgba`, or separate properties), or at least document the exact bit layout and versioning expectations in the IDL. Also consider whether this should be `UInt16`/`Int32`/`Windows.UI.Color` + an index flag, depending on how you intend to support indexed colors long-term.

This is a thin pass-through, but it’s missing the usual input validation/guarding you often see at the TermControl layer. Consider validating `matchMode` (e.g., reject/normalize unknown values) and/or early-out if the control/core isn’t in a state where selection operations are valid (e.g., no active selection). Also consider whether this should be `noexcept` (or explicitly allow exceptions) to match the WinRT surface expectations—if `_core.ColorSelection` can throw, it may be worth translating failures into a predictable HRESULT/behavior at this boundary.

Consider making the signature more self-documenting and type-safe. `uint32_t matchMode` is a magic-number parameter; if this is an enum-like concept (e.g. selection-only vs all-matches), prefer a strongly-typed enum (e.g. `Control::ColorSelectionMatchMode` or `enum class MatchMode : uint32_t`) to prevent invalid values and improve readability. Also, if `SelectionColor` is a WinRT type, passing by value may be fine, but if it’s a heavier struct/class, consider `const Control::SelectionColor&` to avoid copies. Finally, ensure this method is placed in the appropriate interface/region (public vs private) and that it matches any corresponding implementation/ABI expectations (e.g. noexcept if it should not throw).

IDL API surface: consider making `matchMode` a strongly-typed enum (e.g. `ColorSelectionMatchMode`) instead of a raw `UInt32` to improve readability, versioning, and projection quality across languages. Also, passing `SelectionColor` for both `fg` and `bg` implies callers must always provide both; if either is optional, consider overloading or allowing `IReference<SelectionColor>`/nullable semantics (or a sentinel) so projections can omit one without constructing a dummy value. Finally, since this is a new public WinRT method, ensure it’s added to the appropriate versioning/contract story and documented in the schema/docs so it’s discoverable and stable.

Creating `Search` with hard-coded `CaseInsensitive` and `{0,0}` start anchor may produce surprising behavior: it will recolor *all* case-insensitive matches in the entire buffer (depending on `Search` semantics), not just within the selected span(s), and it may also repeatedly recolor the same region if multiple spans are processed. Consider (1) honoring a sensitivity/mode consistent with conhost (or making it configurable), and (2) constraining the search to the selection range (or starting at `start` and stopping at `end`) to avoid recoloring outside the intended area and to improve performance on large buffers. Also, if `Search` can mutate the buffer, ensure it won’t invalidate subsequent search state or cause an infinite loop when the match text equals the selected text.

Consider making `matchMode` a strongly-typed enum (e.g. `enum class ColorSelectionMatchMode : uint32_t`) instead of a raw `uint32_t`. The PR description suggests only a couple of discrete modes (current selection vs all matches), and using an enum will improve readability, prevent invalid values, and make call sites self-documenting. If this is part of a public/ABI surface, also document the valid values and expected behavior for unknown values (clamp? ignore? fail?).

Returning `std::vector<std::tuple<til::point, til::point>>` makes the meaning of the two points unclear (start/end? inclusive/exclusive? normalized order?). Consider introducing a small named struct (e.g. `SelectionSpan{ til::point start; til::point end; }`) or a `using SelectionSpan = std::pair<til::point, til::point>` with clear naming, and document whether the span is inclusive and whether it is guaranteed to be ordered. This will reduce mistakes when iterating spans for coloring/search.

SpanLength/Write here assumes coordStart/coordEnd are valid, ordered, and within the active buffer. Since this is now wired up via UIA/action plumbing, it’s worth hardening: (1) normalize start/end if they can be reversed, (2) clamp to buffer bounds (or early-return) to avoid exceptions/UB, and (3) consider guarding against zero-length/invalid spans. Also, if SpanLength is inclusive, double-check it matches the comment on coordEnd being inclusive to avoid off-by-one coloring.

Using an empty string here as a sentinel is brittle: it’s easy for future code to treat this as a valid localized name and accidentally surface a blank entry in UI (command palette, settings UI, etc.). Consider making the intent explicit in the type/logic instead (e.g., omit the entry from the map entirely and ensure GenerateName is always used for ColorSelection; or use std::optional/hstring{} with a clear check, or a dedicated flag/branch in the name-resolution path). At minimum, add an assertion or a comment pointing to the exact code path that guarantees GenerateName is invoked so this can’t regress silently.

Including the generated `ColorSelectionArgs.g.cpp` here is fine, but please double-check that the corresponding IDL/runtimeclass is actually referenced by this TU and that the include order matches the project’s convention (typically alphabetical/grouped with other Args.g.cpp includes). If this file is only needed for `GenerateName()`, consider whether it should live alongside other args implementations to avoid pulling in extra generated code into a widely-included compilation unit (can impact build times).

Hashing a sentinel via `h.write(-1)` is potentially type-ambiguous/unstable depending on the `hasher::write` overload set (it may treat this as a signed int, which can differ in size/representation across platforms/overloads). Since this is meant to represent a null `SelectionColor`, consider hashing a fixed-width value (e.g. `h.write(uint32_t{0xFFFFFFFF})`) or a dedicated tag byte (e.g. `h.write(uint8_t{0})` for null / `h.write(uint8_t{1})` for present) before hashing `TextColor()`. That also makes the intent clearer and avoids accidental collisions with legitimate `TextColor()` values if the hasher normalizes types.

Consider making `MatchMode` a strongly-typed enum (e.g. `ColorSelectionMatchMode`) instead of a raw `UInt32`. This improves schema clarity, prevents invalid values, and makes projection into C++/C# safer. If you intentionally want extensibility, you can still use an enum with explicit values and an `Unknown`/`Default` member, or document the accepted values in the IDL comments. Also consider whether this should be a property with a default value (or nullable/optional) to preserve backward compatibility when deserializing older settings.

Removing the `assert(_parents.size() <= 1)` changes an implicit invariant: these methods were written assuming at most one parent ActionMap. If multiple parents are now supported (e.g., layering defaults + profile + feature-generated bindings), please ensure the merge semantics are well-defined and deterministic (ordering of `_parents` affects which bindings/commands win) and that cycles/duplication are impossible. If multiple parents are *not* intended, consider keeping a runtime check (or at least a comment) to catch accidental multi-parent construction in debug builds.

Same concern as above: `_GetCumulativeActions()` now aggregates from potentially multiple parents without any guard. If multi-parent is intentional, consider documenting/encoding precedence (e.g., first parent wins vs last parent wins) and ensure the accumulation logic handles duplicates consistently. If not intentional, keep an assert/FAIL_FAST to avoid subtle action duplication or unexpected overrides.

Keybinding inheritance across multiple parents can easily become ambiguous (two parents bind the same chord to different actions). With the assert removed, please confirm `_PopulateKeyBindingMapWithStandardCommands` resolves conflicts predictably and matches the intended layering model; otherwise, consider reintroducing a debug assertion or adding explicit conflict resolution (and possibly logging) when multiple parents provide the same chord.

Copying `_parents` without the previous single-parent invariant means `Copy()` will now preserve potentially multiple parents. Please ensure this doesn't create unexpectedly deep/branching graphs (or cycles) that could cause exponential work in later traversals. If cycles are possible, add protection (visited set) in parent-walking code paths.

`_GetActionByKeyChordInternal` now queries all parents. If multiple parents can exist, the returned command may depend on parent iteration order. Consider making the precedence explicit (e.g., iterate in reverse, or sort by layer priority) and add a comment/tests to lock in the intended behavior for conflicting bindings.

Similarly, `GetKeyBindingForAction` will now return the first match found among parents, which may be nondeterministic if `_parents` ordering isn't guaranteed. If multi-parent is required, please define and enforce ordering (or detect multiple matches and choose consistently) to avoid flaky behavior when two parents bind the same action/args differently.

Adding `ColorSelection` to `ALL_SHORTCUT_ACTIONS` means it will be treated as a no-args action in any code that iterates this list. But this PR description indicates `ColorSelection` has parameters (foreground/background, matchMode, etc.), and you also added it to `ALL_SHORTCUT_ACTIONS_WITH_ARGS` below. Having it in both lists is likely to cause duplicate enum/string mappings, duplicate command palette entries, or ambiguous parsing/serialization depending on how these macros are consumed. Consider removing it from `ALL_SHORTCUT_ACTIONS` and keeping it only in `ALL_SHORTCUT_ACTIONS_WITH_ARGS` (or splitting into two actions: a parameterless toggle/default and a parameterized action) to avoid double-registration and ensure args are always available.

Parsing and materializing a full `GlobalAppSettings` from a JSON blob during `FinalizeLayering()` can be relatively expensive and also makes failures here harder to diagnose (e.g. malformed JSON would likely throw/terminate during settings load). Consider (1) making `_parseJson`/`FromJson` error-tolerant here (catch exceptions / validate parse result) and logging a settings warning instead of failing the whole load, and/or (2) avoiding JSON round-tripping by constructing the default keybindings programmatically (or via a dedicated helper that returns an `IActionMap`/actions list) so this path is cheaper and less brittle. Also, if this is intended to be a one-time static JSON, ensure `EnableColorSelectionSettingsJson` is guaranteed valid at compile time (e.g. unit test or static validation) to prevent regressions.

Adding this as an INHERITABLE_SETTING on GlobalAppSettings implies it can be set globally and overridden per-profile. Please confirm that’s the intended UX (vs. a purely profile-level setting), and ensure the rest of the pipeline is updated accordingly: JSON serialization/deserialization + defaults, schema/docs, and any settings migration/versioning. Also consider whether this should be marked experimental (naming/placement) to avoid committing to a stable setting name prematurely.

Since this is a new top-level setting, please ensure it’s also wired through the rest of the settings pipeline (schema generation, defaults, serialization/deserialization, and any layering/merging behavior). Also consider whether the `experimental.` prefix is intended long-term: if this is meant to be user-facing and stable, it may need a non-experimental name (or a migration path/alias) to avoid breaking configs later. Finally, verify the JSON key naming matches the rest of the repo’s conventions for experimental settings and that docs/schema will be updated accordingly.

The PowerShell invocation uses a Unicode en-dash in `–ExecutionPolicy` (and is also missing quoting around the script path). MSBuild/Exec will pass this through literally, which can cause PowerShell to not recognize the parameter on many systems/encodings. Please change to a normal hyphen `-ExecutionPolicy` and quote the script path like the target above (e.g. `"$(OpenConsoleDir)\tools\GenerateHeaderForJson.ps1"`). Also consider matching the exact command structure of the previous target (including `"..."` around the script) for consistency and reliability.

Potentially unsafe/incorrect conversion from `std::wstring_view` to `std::string`: `std::string(&str.at(1))` assumes a null-terminated narrow string, but `str` is a wide string view and not guaranteed to be null-terminated. This can read past the view and also mis-handle non-ASCII. Prefer an explicit conversion of the substring, e.g. `const auto indexView = str.substr(1);` then convert via `til::u16u8(indexView)`/`winrt::to_string(indexView)` (or parse directly from the view without allocating). Also consider validating that the remaining chars are hex digits before calling `stoi` to avoid exceptions during settings load.

`std::stoi` will throw on invalid input (and can also accept partial parses like "0j" -> 0 as noted). Throwing during settings deserialization can be user-hostile. Consider using a non-throwing parse (`std::from_chars` on a narrow buffer) and require full consumption of the substring, plus range-checking (e.g. 0-255) before narrowing to `uint8_t`.

`CanConvert` is too permissive for the indexed form: it only checks length==3 and leading 'i', but doesn't ensure the next two chars are hex digits. This means values like "iZZ" will pass `CanConvert` and then throw in `FromJson`. Consider tightening validation here (and/or making `FromJson` non-throwing) so schema/type errors are reported cleanly.

`ToJson` appears to emit RGB without the leading '#', but `CanConvert`/`FromJson` expect "#NNN" or "#NNNNNN". This makes round-tripping inconsistent and may generate settings that won't parse back. Consider returning `fmt::format("#{:06x}", raw)` (and similarly ensure indexed form matches the documented/expected format).

This file doesn’t appear to be valid JSON as written: it starts with `1:{` (line-number prefixes) and includes `//` comments throughout. If this is intended to be consumed by the settings loader, it will fail strict JSON parsing. Please remove the line-number prefixes and either (a) convert comments to a supported format (e.g., JSONC if the parser supports it) or (b) move explanatory text to documentation. Also consider whether this should be a fragment merged into defaults rather than a standalone JSON file, depending on how it’s loaded.

IsDefaultScheme() compares against ProfileDefaults().DefaultAppearance().ColorSchemeName(), but this assumes _settings is always a live, up-to-date settings model. Since _settings is captured in the VM constructor, it may become stale after the user changes the default scheme (or other settings) unless the same settings instance is mutated and shared. Consider either (a) storing a weak reference/pointer back to the owning settings/viewmodel and querying the current default on demand, or (b) ensuring RefreshIsDefault is always called after any operation that can change the default and that _settings reflects that change. Also consider null/empty handling: if ColorSchemeName() can be empty/unset, this will never mark any scheme as default; you may want to treat empty as "use system/default" and handle accordingly.

Passing `const Model::CascadiaSettings& settings` into the ctor but storing it as a value member (`Model::CascadiaSettings _settings;`) can be expensive and may also capture a stale snapshot if the settings model is expected to change while the editor is open. Consider storing a reference/weak reference (or a shared/observable model type used elsewhere in the editor) instead of copying, or at least document that this VM intentionally snapshots settings at construction. Also ensure the ctor is marked `explicit` if implicit conversions are possible for these parameter types.

Exposing `IsDefaultScheme` as a read-only property is fine, but please ensure it’s actually kept in sync with changes to the base-layer default scheme (e.g., when the user clicks “set as default”). The PR description mentions a `RefreshIsDefault` API—make sure the implementation raises `PropertyChanged` for `IsDefaultScheme` (and any dependent UI state) so bindings update immediately. Also consider whether this should be computed (derived from current settings/default name) vs. stored, to avoid stale state after rename/delete operations.

This click handler delegates to the view model, but it doesn’t handle the case where no scheme is selected or the selected scheme can’t be set as default. Consider either guarding here (e.g., check a SelectedScheme/CanSetAsDefault property) or ensuring RequestSetSelectedSchemeAsDefault() is a no-op with appropriate notifications when selection is null/invalid. Also, if the UI needs to update the “default” tag immediately, confirm this call triggers the necessary PropertyChanged/RefreshIsDefault notifications for all affected scheme VMs (old default and new default).

New click handler is added to the page interface; please ensure it’s actually wired up in `ColorSchemes.xaml` (Button Click="SetAsDefault_Click") and that the implementation in `ColorSchemes.cpp` exists and updates the underlying settings model (e.g., profiles.default/base layer) rather than only the view model selection. Also consider whether this should be a view-model command instead of a code-behind handler for consistency with the rest of the page’s MVVM approach, and verify it triggers any necessary property change notifications (e.g., refreshing `IsDefault` tags) so the UI updates immediately.

Binding `Visibility` directly to `IsDefaultScheme` is risky unless that property is explicitly a `Windows.UI.Xaml.Visibility` (or `Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Visibility`) value. If `IsDefaultScheme` is a `bool` (as the name suggests), this will either fail to compile or behave unexpectedly without a `BooleanToVisibilityConverter`. Consider changing the binding to use a converter, or expose a `Visibility DefaultTagVisibility` property from the VM to keep the XAML simple and type-correct. Also consider whether you want `Collapsed` vs `Hidden` when not default.

Setting the default scheme by name should validate that the selected scheme is still present in GlobalSettings().ColorSchemes() (and that Name() is non-empty) before writing it into ProfileDefaults. Otherwise, a deleted/renamed scheme (or an empty name) could be persisted as the default and leave the base layer pointing at a non-existent scheme. Consider guarding with a lookup in the scheme map and/or falling back to a known-good default when not found.

Adding a new public request method is fine, but please ensure this is fully wired end-to-end: (1) the implementation updates the underlying settings model (profiles.default/base layer) rather than only the view model state, (2) it triggers the necessary property change notifications so the UI updates (e.g., default tag / button enabled state) and persists on Save, and (3) it handles edge cases like no selection / null scheme gracefully. Also consider whether this should return a bool (or fire a result event) to indicate success/failure, consistent with RequestRenameCurrentScheme().

Adding a new WinRT method here is a breaking surface-area change for any consumers of this component. Please ensure the corresponding implementation is added in the C++/WinRT class (and that it’s wired to actually update `profiles.defaults` / base layer and trigger any necessary property change notifications so the UI refreshes, e.g. `IsDefault` tags). Also consider whether this should be an `IAsyncAction` (or return a success `Boolean`) if it can fail (no selection, read-only scheme, settings write failure), so the caller can handle errors instead of silently doing nothing.
This file is missing a trailing newline ("No newline at end of file"). Please add a final newline after the closing </root> to match repo style and avoid noisy diffs/tooling issues.

This guards against the panic, but the condition/message are a bit misleading: an empty `workspaces` set could also come from other corruption/partial state, not strictly a 0.12 schema mismatch. Consider validating the expected shape more directly (e.g., ensure `wsConfig[0]` is a `map[string]interface{}` and contains the expected keys like `name`/`prefix`) and return a more actionable error (possibly mentioning that the cached backend config may have been written by a newer Terraform and suggesting re-running `terraform init -reconfigure` or upgrading). Also, if you keep this message, it might be better to include the backend name/context to aid debugging.

The error assertion here is overly complex and can mask failures (e.g., if tc.err is nil but err is non-nil, the condition won’t fail because `tc.err != nil` is false). Consider using a clearer pattern: if tc.err == nil { if err != nil { ... } } else { if err == nil || !strings.Contains(err.Error(), tc.err.Error()) { ... } }. Also, comparing `err != tc.err` is generally unreliable since errors are rarely the same instance; prefer substring match (as you already do) or, if possible, a typed/sentinel error with errors.Is.

Aliasing the import to `multierror` is fine, but please ensure this is actually needed (e.g., to avoid a local identifier collision) and that all references in this file use the alias consistently. If there is no conflict, keeping the default import name reduces noise in diffs and keeps style consistent with other files that import go-multierror.

This block now assumes `s.Backend` is non-empty because the case condition includes `!s.Backend.Empty()`, which is good. However, note that the later warning log previously printed `s.Backend.Hash` but now prints the potentially rehashed `hash`. That changes semantics: the log message says "saved" but may now show a derived value (rehash) rather than the persisted hash. Consider logging both (e.g., `savedHash := s.Backend.Hash` and `effectiveHash := ...`) or adjust the message to avoid confusion when debugging backend changes during `init`.

The warning message labels the first value as "saved", but `hash` may be `s.Backend.Rehash()` when `opts.Init` is true. That can mislead users/operators trying to understand whether the cached backend changed vs. the merged/normalized config changed. Suggest either logging `s.Backend.Hash` explicitly as the saved value, or updating the message to reflect that the first value is the effective/rehash value used for comparison during init.

This comment is helpful, but it’s a bit ambiguous about *when* and *how* this variable is set/used. Since the PR introduces a config `new_collations_enabled_on_first_bootstrap`, consider updating the comment to explicitly state that `mysql.tidb.new_collation_enabled` is a persisted bootstrap flag (set during first bootstrap based on config) and is used to determine cluster-wide collation behavior on subsequent restarts/upgrades. Also consider mentioning expected values (e.g., "0/1") if that’s the convention in `mysql.tidb`.

This section header uses a nonstandard glob syntax. EditorConfig typically expects patterns like "[*.result]" to match files in the current directory only; to reliably match "*.result" in all subdirectories, use "[**.result]" (or "[**/*.result]" depending on the EditorConfig implementation). Otherwise, some editors/CI formatters may still trim trailing whitespace in nested testdata result files.

This expected plan switches from HashAgg to StreamAgg for `GROUP BY -1` (a constant). Please double-check that this is stable across planner changes and execution modes: the key assertion for the bugfix is that the constant group-by is eliminated and not pushed down to TiKV, not necessarily the specific physical operator name/IDs. If StreamAgg vs HashAgg can vary, consider loosening the golden output (or adding a more targeted test elsewhere) to avoid flaky explaintest failures due to harmless plan shape changes.

This only runs `EXPLAIN` and doesn't assert the expected plan shape/output. To prevent regressions, add the expected `EXPLAIN` result lines (or use a stable format like `EXPLAIN FORMAT="brief"`/`"dot"`) so the test actually verifies that constant GROUP BY items are pruned/ignored and no coprocessor HashAgg group-by constant is pushed down.

This test is asserting that `GROUP BY` on a foldable constant is ignored, but `group by 1-0` is a bit ambiguous because `GROUP BY 1` is also valid positional syntax in MySQL/TiDB (refers to the 1st select item) and may be treated differently depending on parsing/resolution. To avoid conflating positional `GROUP BY` with constant-folding behavior, consider using a constant expression that cannot be interpreted as a position (e.g. `group by 1+0.1`, `group by 1=1`, or `group by cast(1 as signed)+0`) or add an explicit test for positional `GROUP BY 1` separately. This will make the regression test more robust and aligned with the issue description (non-position constants like -1, 'a', etc.).

This expected output change drops the `group by:1` and also changes the aggregate from `count(case(gt(a,10), b))` to `count(b)` while introducing a separate `Selection` node. Please double-check that this rewrite is actually intended for the rule being tested (i.e., the PR is about pruning constant GROUP BY items, not about rewriting `count(case when cond then b end)` into `Selection(cond) -> count(b)`). If the optimizer rule set now performs this additional transformation, consider adding/adjusting a dedicated test case to assert the GROUP BY constant pruning specifically, so this fixture update doesn’t mask unrelated behavior changes.

Good improvement: adding `Commentf(sql)` will surface the failing SQL in assertion output. One suggestion: consider including the case index as well (e.g., `Commentf("case %d: %s", i, sql)`) to make it easier to locate the failing entry when multiple cases share similar SQL or when `sql` is empty/whitespace.

Removing the fallback `GROUP BY 1` avoids pushing an un-resolved constant into coprocessor (fixing #15488), but please double-check semantic equivalence for cases where all group-by items are constants. In MySQL, `GROUP BY <constant expr>` still produces a single group (same as no GROUP BY) for non-empty input, but differs from no-GROUP-BY when the input is empty (no-GROUP-BY returns 1 row for aggregates like COUNT, while GROUP-BY returns 0 rows). If TiDB follows that behavior, pruning all constant group-by items to empty may change results on empty tables. Consider adding/adjusting tests to cover empty-table aggregates with `GROUP BY -1/'a'/(10-9)` and ensure the intended semantics, or keep a marker that preserves the "group-by-present" behavior without pushing an invalid expression to TiKV.

Adding a mutator for builtin function args increases the surface for unintended aliasing/mutation bugs because `[]Expression` is a reference to an underlying array. Consider either (1) documenting that callers must not reuse/mutate the passed slice after calling `setArgs`, or (2) defensively copying the slice (e.g. `b.args = append([]Expression(nil), args...)`) to avoid sharing. This is especially relevant if `setArgs` is used during cloning/rewriting where the original and cloned expressions should not accidentally share arg slices.

Using `Clone()` here preserves internal flags (e.g. `keepNull`) which fixes the reported issue, but the direct type assertion `.(*ScalarFunction)` can panic if `Clone()` ever returns a different concrete type (or nil) due to future refactors. Consider either (1) cloning via a `ScalarFunction`-specific method (if available), or (2) using a checked assertion with a fallback to `NewFunctionInternal`/error to avoid hard panics. Also, since you now return `FoldConstant(newExpr)` instead of a raw function, please ensure this behavior change is intended for all callers of `EvaluateExprWithNull` (it may fold more aggressively than before).

This new SQL string should end with a comma to keep the JSON array valid/consistent with surrounding entries, and it should also terminate with a semicolon like the other statements in this testdata file. Without the comma, the JSON will be invalid (or at least inconsistent with the formatting used here), and missing semicolon may break statement parsing in the test harness if it expects it.

This expected plan string looks suspicious/unstable: `istrue(cast(, double BINARY))` appears to be casting an empty expression (likely intended to be an empty string literal `''` or a constant like `0`). If this is meant to represent `(col OR "")`, the constant side should be explicit and valid in the serialized plan to avoid brittle golden tests and to ensure the plan actually matches a real expression tree. Please verify the underlying test SQL and the plan encoder output; consider updating the expected string to include the proper literal (e.g. `cast('', double BINARY)` or `0`) and/or adjust the plan string formatting to avoid emitting an empty argument.

Setting NotNullFlag here seems risky: even if the parser filters illegal parameters, SYSDATE() can still evaluate to NULL in some execution contexts (e.g., error/invalid time zone handling, or other runtime failures), and more importantly this PR is about removing unexpected NOT_NULL flags (notably for JSON). Please double-check MySQL/TiDB semantics for SYSDATE() and whether it is truly guaranteed non-NULL at runtime. If the intent is to mark it non-NULL only when args are valid, consider deriving nullability from function properties (or leaving it nullable) and add a unit test asserting the field flags for `select sysdate()` / `select sysdate(0)` match MySQL. Also, the comment claims parser filtering implies non-NULL, but that doesn’t cover runtime evaluation errors; consider rewording or removing if you keep the flag.

Setting `NotNullFlag` here based solely on `!c.Value.IsNull()` can be semantically wrong for constants whose declared/expected type is nullable (e.g. `CAST(NULL AS JSON)` / `CONVERT(NULL, JSON)` should remain nullable even if `RetType` is reused elsewhere). This also mutates `RetType` lazily, which can leak into other references if `RetType` is shared (you clone, but only when non-null). Consider deriving nullability from the expression/type inference rules rather than the current datum value, or ensure `RetType` is always cloned before any mutation (including the null case) to avoid shared-state surprises. Also, if the goal is to fix unexpected NOT_NULL on JSON, double-check that this logic doesn't reintroduce NOT_NULL for JSON constants/columns where it shouldn't apply.

Switching from Assert to Check will allow the test to continue after a mismatch, but it also risks hiding the first/most relevant failure and producing a cascade of follow-up mismatches (tp.Tp/Charset/Flag/Flen/Decimal are tightly coupled). If the intent is to improve diagnostics, consider using a single assertion on the whole RetType (or a struct/DeepEquals comparison) or keep Assert for the first mismatch and use Check for the rest. At minimum, add a short comment explaining why Check is preferred here to avoid weakening the test unintentionally.

This change only adjusts indentation/whitespace in a comment. If this is intentional, consider running gofmt (or your repo’s formatter) on the whole file to avoid mixed indentation in comment blocks. Also, the comment text has a likely typo: `t.b < t1.b=10` should probably be `t.b < t1.b+10` (as the next line suggests) to avoid confusing readers.

Removing the implicit `NotNullFlag` injection for binary-flagged types makes sense for JSON/BLOB/TEXT (they can be NULL), but please double-check whether any callers relied on this behavior for fixed-length `BINARY`/`VARBINARY` columns. In MySQL, `NOT_NULL` should reflect the column definition (or expression nullability), not the collation/binary attribute, so this change is likely correct—but it would be good to add/adjust tests to ensure `dumpFlag` now matches MySQL for: (1) JSON/BLOB/TEXT with/without NULL, (2) BINARY/VARBINARY with/without NULL, and (3) expression results like `CONVERT(NULL, JSON)` to prevent regressions.

This test only covers a non-NULL JSON expression (`convert('{}', JSON)`), but the reported bug also shows `convert(NULL, JSON)` incorrectly returning `NOT_NULL`. Consider adding an additional assertion for `select convert(NULL, JSON)` (and/or `select cast(NULL as JSON)`) to ensure the NOT_NULL flag is not set when the expression can be NULL. That would better prevent regressions for the original scenario described in the issue.

Setting `NotNullFlag` based solely on `value != nil` is likely incorrect for expressions where the result can still be NULL (e.g. `CONVERT(NULL, JSON)` / `CAST(NULL AS JSON)`), and it also makes the flag depend on the runtime value rather than the declared/derived type semantics. Since the switch already handles `case nil` by setting `TypeNull`, consider moving this logic into the non-nil cases (or explicitly clearing `NotNullFlag` in the `nil` case) and/or basing NOT_NULL on expression nullability instead of the current value. Otherwise this may reintroduce/keep the unexpected NOT_NULL flag behavior for JSON and other types.

Adding `mysql.NotNullFlag` for a plain string literal changes the semantics of `DefaultTypeForValue`: it now asserts that any non-nil Go value implies `NOT_NULL` in the resulting FieldType. Please double-check this matches MySQL/TiDB behavior for expression results (e.g. `SELECT 'abc'` vs `SELECT CAST(NULL AS CHAR)`), and consider adding/keeping explicit test coverage for nullable expressions (like `nil` and `types.NewDatum(nil)`/`CAST(NULL AS ...)`) to ensure `NOT_NULL` is only set when the expression is guaranteed non-NULL. Otherwise this test may overfit to the new implementation and mask regressions in nullability inference.

This test adds coverage for an empty TABLESAMPLE clause, which is good. However, `tablesample ()` is a syntax-level construct in many parsers; depending on TiDB's grammar, this might fail with a generic parse error instead of `ErrInvalidTableSample`. If the intention is to validate semantic rejection (preprocessor) rather than parsing, consider asserting the exact error message/code returned by the parser for this input, or add a companion case that is definitely syntactically valid but semantically invalid (e.g. `tablesample regions` without `()` if that parses, or `tablesample regions(1)` if it parses but should be rejected). Otherwise this test may be brittle across parser changes.

Returning 0 here disables *all* logical optimizations for any sampling query, which is a very broad hammer and may cause unexpected regressions (e.g., losing harmless rewrites unrelated to Selection/Limit/Sort). Consider narrowing the disabled rules/flags to only those that can change TABLESAMPLE semantics, or document explicitly why a full opt-out is required. Also, the comment mentions "operands"; "operators" might be clearer in optimizer terminology.

By removing `ast.SampleMethodTypeNone` from the allowed cases, any `TableSample` node that still carries `SampleMethodTypeNone` (e.g., parser default/placeholder when `TABLESAMPLE` is present but method parsing fails or is omitted) will now hit the default branch and return `ErrInvalidTableSample`. If `SampleMethodTypeNone` can still appear when no sampling is intended, this change could reject valid queries. Consider either (1) ensuring `v.TableSample` is only non-nil when a real TABLESAMPLE clause exists and method is set to `TiDBRegion`, or (2) keeping `SampleMethodTypeNone` allowed but treating it as "no-op" (or producing a clearer error like "TABLESAMPLE requires REGIONS()" if the clause is present but method is none). Also, the error message says "REGIONS sampling method" while the syntax in the PR uses `regions()`; consider aligning the wording to the exact supported syntax.

Comment is a bit misleading: this `default` branch is taken for any table that is neither common-handle nor `PKIsHandle`, and it sets `pkCols` to `ExtraHandleID` (i.e., the implicit row handle). That corresponds to `_tidb_rowid` only for non-clustered tables; for clustered index tables the first case applies. Consider rewording to something like `// fall back to implicit row handle (ExtraHandleID / _tidb_rowid for non-clustered tables)` to avoid implying this branch is exclusively about `_tidb_rowid`.

Locking around the read fixes the reported race, but consider using `defer c.mu.Unlock()` (or a small helper like `c.getMinCommitTS()` with internal locking) to make the critical section exception-safe and harder to accidentally extend incorrectly in future edits. Also double-check that *all* other reads/writes of `c.minCommitTS` are consistently protected by the same mutex (or use `atomic` if this is a hot path) to avoid leaving other race sites.

Behavior change: previously this branch only set `StmtCtx.MaybeOverOptimized4PlanCache = true` and still continued to `evaluateExprWithNull`, whereas now it returns the original `expr` early. That can change semantics beyond plan-cache marking (e.g., callers expecting NULL-propagation simplification may no longer get it). If the intent is to avoid constant-folding/rewriting for plan-cache safety, consider still setting the flag (so the statement is marked) and document why skipping evaluation is correct here. At minimum, add a comment explaining why returning `expr` is safe, and ensure tests cover cases where `evaluateExprWithNull` would have produced a different expression (especially for DNF/OR predicates and PointGet extraction).

This condition disables PointGet conversion whenever `StmtCtx.UseCache` is true, which seems to contradict the PR goal (“PointGet plans … can be cached”). The comment explains a correctness risk for range predicates that can collapse to PointGet only when parameters are equal, but applying this blanket ban here may unnecessarily prevent safe PointGet cases (e.g., `pk = ?`, `IN` with single value, constant equality) from being cached and may regress performance.

Consider tightening the guard to only the unsafe patterns (e.g., when the access condition is a range that can degenerate based on runtime params) rather than all PointGet conversions under plan cache. Alternatively, if the intent is to avoid PointGet *only when it is generated in physical stage* for cache, please ensure this is the right stage/flag and add a targeted check (or a separate “cacheable pointget” marker) so safe PointGet plans remain cacheable. Also, please double-check the semantics of `StmtCtx.UseCache` (is it “this statement uses plan cache” vs “this plan is cacheable”) to avoid inverted logic.

The test drops table `t` but then creates/uses `t1`/`t2`. This looks like a copy/paste mistake and can leave stale `t1`/`t2` from previous runs, making the test flaky (e.g., `create table t1` fails if it already exists). Consider dropping `t1` and `t2` explicitly (or use `create table if not exists` + `truncate`) to ensure isolation: `drop table if exists t1, t2` before creating them.

Removing the `MaybeOverOptimized4PlanCache` flag here changes plan-cache safety semantics for the heuristic-selected access path. If this heuristic can still depend on mutable constants (e.g., values from parameters, session vars, or runtime-constant folding), we may end up caching a plan that was previously marked as potentially over-optimized. Please confirm that the underlying bugfix guarantees this optimization is now plan-cache-safe, or add a more precise guard (e.g., only set/clear the flag when the selection is independent of mutable constants). Also consider adding/adjusting a regression test to ensure prepared plan cache hits for the reported case without introducing incorrect cache reuse in other queries.

This changes behavior from setting `StmtCtx.MaybeOverOptimized4PlanCache = true` to an early return that disables extraction entirely. That’s a much stronger action and may affect non-plan-cache paths too (e.g., range detaching/point-get optimization). Please ensure this early return is only taken when plan-cache is enabled (or when the caller explicitly wants to avoid these optimizations), otherwise it could regress normal planning. Also consider whether the previous flag is still needed for downstream logic/metrics; if other code relies on `MaybeOverOptimized4PlanCache` being set, this change could silently break it. At minimum, add a comment explaining why returning `(nil, conditions, nil, nil, false)` is the correct contract here and how callers interpret it.

This test mutates a global process-wide flag (new collation enabled). Even with the deferred reset, it can still race with other tests if they run in parallel, causing flaky behavior. Since this file is intended to be serial, consider using a dedicated helper that enforces non-parallel execution (or ensure the package test runner won’t run other tests concurrently), and/or guard the toggle with a mutex / use t.Cleanup for clarity. Also consider restoring the previous value instead of hardcoding `false` in case the default changes.

This hunk removes imports and deletes `TestInFunc` entirely. If the intent is only to migrate tests to testify / stabilize RNG, consider moving `TestInFunc` to the appropriate test file (e.g., builtin_string_test.go or a new file) rather than dropping coverage. `IN` has important type/coercion/collation/vectorized paths (including `collate.SetNewCollationEnabledForTest` and `vecEvalInt`) that should remain tested somewhere; otherwise regressions in collation-aware comparisons and vectorization may slip through.

This test mutates a global switch (`collate.SetNewCollationEnabledForTest`) which can race with other tests if they run in parallel. Since this file is intended to be "serial", please ensure the test runner actually enforces non-parallel execution for this package/file (e.g., via a serial test suite or build tag). Otherwise, consider guarding the global toggle with a package-level mutex or using `t.Cleanup` plus an explicit note/constraint that no other tests in the package call `t.Parallel()`.

This hunk removes `TestCIWeightString` entirely (and drops the related `collate`/`utf8` imports). Given the issue/PR context mentions moving `TestCIWeightString` to serial tests to fix flakiness, please ensure the test is reintroduced in the intended serial test file/suite rather than being deleted. Otherwise we lose coverage for `WEIGHT_STRING` behavior under new collations (`utf8mb4_general_ci`/`utf8mb4_unicode_ci`) and may miss regressions. If it was moved, consider adding a short comment or reference (e.g., new file name) to make the relocation discoverable.

This loop appears to intend comparing each appended row pair, but it always uses `chk.GetRow(0)` for both operands, so it repeatedly compares only the first row and never exercises rows 1..3. This reduces test coverage and could mask regressions (e.g., around trailing spaces / unicode cases). Consider using `chk.GetRow(i)` (or `chk.GetRow(i)` for both rows) and/or removing the loop if repetition was only meant to catch flakiness. Also, if the goal is to compare col1 vs col2 within the same row, passing the same row for both arguments is fine, but the row index should vary.

This hunk removes `TestCompareString` entirely (and its related imports). Given the issue is about unstable collation/weight-string behavior, dropping this test reduces coverage for `types.CompareString` and `CompareStringWithCollationInfo` (including the important trailing-space cases like `"a "` vs `"a  "`). If the intent is to migrate tests to testify or move them to a different file/suite (e.g., serial tests), please ensure the equivalent assertions are reintroduced elsewhere and that `collate.SetNewCollationEnabledForTest(true/false)` is still exercised. Otherwise, this change may mask regressions rather than stabilizing the test.

Adding `TiDBRCReadCheckTS` to the instance config is fine, but please ensure this is actually treated as *instance-only* and not still overridable via GLOBAL/SESSION sysvar elsewhere. In particular: (1) confirm the config loader/wiring reads this field and applies it to the runtime/session behavior at startup; (2) consider using `AtomicBool` (or at least documenting immutability) if this value can be read concurrently and/or reloaded dynamically; (3) verify naming consistency with other instance options (e.g., `TiDBEnableDDL` uses `AtomicBool` and `TiDB...` prefix) and add a short comment describing the behavior ("RC reads skip PD ts when enabled") to avoid ambiguity.

Removing `SessionVars.RcReadCheckTS` is consistent with moving `tidb_rc_read_check_ts` to INSTANCE scope, but please double-check that no code path still reads/writes this session field (e.g., in variable setters, session initialization/cloning, statement context, or tests). If any logic previously depended on per-session overrides, it should now read from the instance config (or a global/atomic instance-level variable) and the old session field should be fully removed/renamed to avoid silent behavior changes or nil/zero-default fallbacks.

This switches `tidb_rc_read_check_ts` to `ScopeInstance`, but the implementation now only updates a global atomic (`EnableRCReadCheckTS`) and removes the per-session field (`s.RcReadCheckTS`). Please double-check that all read paths that previously consulted `SessionVars.RcReadCheckTS` are updated to consult the instance-level flag, otherwise session behavior may silently diverge (e.g., session SET no longer works, but code may still read the old session var default). Also, for an INSTANCE-scoped sysvar, consider whether `SetGlobal`/`GetGlobal` are the right hooks (naming is a bit misleading) and ensure the variable is wired to config loading / instance sysvar persistence as intended (so it can be set via config file and/or `SET INSTANCE`).

This assertion reads the expected value from `EnableRCReadCheckTS.Load()`, which is a process-wide atomic and may be affected by other tests running in parallel (or by previous tests not restoring the value). To avoid test flakiness, consider explicitly setting/resetting `EnableRCReadCheckTS` within this test (e.g., save old value, set to a known value, `defer` restore), or ensure the test suite runs serially for this variable. Also, since the variable is now INSTANCE-scoped, it may be clearer to assert behavior via the instance-scope getter (if available) rather than `GetSessionOrGlobalSystemVar`, to avoid ambiguity about which scope is being exercised.

This introduces a new instance-level atomic flag, but it’s initialized to `false` unconditionally. If `tidb_rc_read_check_ts` is now configured via `[instance] tidb_rc_read_check_ts = true`, please ensure there is corresponding config loading/wiring that sets this atomic value during bootstrap (and clarify whether it’s intended to be immutable at runtime). Also consider adding a short comment explaining what this flag controls and why it’s instance-scoped, to avoid confusion with the existing sysvar plumbing.

Switching from `sessionVars.RcReadCheckTS` to the instance-level `variable.EnableRCReadCheckTS.Load()` makes sense for the feature change, but this also changes semantics for internal sessions: `sessionVars.ConnectionID > 0` will still exclude background/internal sessions even if the instance config enables RC read check TS. Please confirm this is intended; otherwise consider removing/relaxing the ConnectionID guard or adding a comment explaining why internal sessions must not set RCCheckTS. Also ensure `EnableRCReadCheckTS` is an `atomic.Bool` (or equivalent) and is initialized from config before any statements run, to avoid defaulting to false unexpectedly.

This sets the instance-level default at startup, but please ensure the config value is actually wired to the new INSTANCE-scoped sysvar semantics. In particular: (1) confirm `cfg.Instance.TiDBRCReadCheckTS` has a well-defined default (so instances that "do nothing" in config keep the intended behavior), and that it matches the previous default of `tidb_rc_read_check_ts`; (2) consider whether this should be applied earlier/later in bootstrap relative to sysvar initialization so it can’t be overwritten by persisted/global/session settings; and (3) if the variable is now INSTANCE-only, make sure any remaining code paths that read from session/global sysvars are updated to consult `variable.EnableRCReadCheckTS` (or equivalent) consistently, otherwise this store may have no effect or lead to mixed behavior.

This failpoint panics on `val.(bool)` without type checking, which can itself panic if the failpoint is enabled with a non-bool value (or default). Consider using a safe assertion (`b, ok := val.(bool); if ok && b { ... }`) or treating any non-nil value as enabled. Also, a hard `panic` in DDL code can be disruptive in tests; if the intent is to assert behavior, prefer `failpoint.Return(...)`/`errors.New(...)` or a test-only assertion path so it doesn't crash the whole process unexpectedly.

Using an empty partitionIDs slice to force the global-index key prefix is a bit implicit and relies on `insertJobIntoDeleteRangeTable` treating `len(partitionIDs)==0` as “non-partitioned / table-level prefix”. This is fragile and easy to break in future refactors. Consider making the intent explicit by passing a dedicated flag/enum (e.g. `isGlobalIndex` or `deleteRangeScope`) or by adding a helper that constructs the correct delete-range prefix for global indexes (t{tableID}_i{indexID}) without overloading the meaning of partitionIDs. At minimum, add a comment in/near `insertJobIntoDeleteRangeTable` documenting that empty partitionIDs is a required contract for global index drops, and ensure it won’t accidentally skip partition range cleanup for non-global indexes.

Changing the expected delete-range count from `max(len(physicalTableIDs), 1)` to `len(physicalTableIDs) + 1` is a semantic shift: it now always expects an extra delete-range even when `physicalTableIDs` is non-empty. Please confirm what the `+1` represents (e.g., global index key range, table meta/prefix range, or something else) and document it here, otherwise this looks like an off-by-one that could make the sanity check flaky. Also consider edge cases: if `physicalTableIDs` is nil/empty, this returns 1 (same as before), but if the job can include multiple global indexes or multiple rules, `+1` may still be insufficient/incorrect. If the intent is “partitions + global index”, it may be clearer to compute explicitly (e.g., `cnt := len(physicalTableIDs); if hasGlobalIndex { cnt++ } else if cnt == 0 { cnt = 1 }`).

`err` is not set in this new branch, so `b.err = err` will likely assign a stale value (or nil) and hide the real failure. If `TableByID` fails, set a concrete error (e.g. `b.err = ErrBuildExecutor` or return the error from `TableByID` if it provides one). Also consider including context like table ID in the error to aid debugging.

This assumes `w.idxLookup.partitionIDMap` is always initialized when `partitionTableMode`/global index is used. If it's nil, this will panic on map access. Consider guarding with `if w.idxLookup.partitionIDMap != nil { ... }` (or initializing it to an empty map during executor build) and clarifying the intended behavior when the map is empty (filter all handles vs. no filtering). Also, if `partitionIDMap` is meant to be read-only after build, add a comment or ensure no concurrent writes to avoid races.

This test only validates the `use index (idx)` hint path, but the reported bug is about the executor choosing a (global) unique index and returning wrong/empty results without explicit hints. To better cover the regression, consider adding an assertion for the unhinted query (and/or `where id = ...`) and verifying the plan uses the expected access path (e.g., `EXPLAIN` contains global index / PointGet) and returns correct rows. Also, since this test relies on dynamic pruning via failpoint, it may be worth asserting behavior both with and without the failpoint (or at least documenting why the hint is required here).

The last argument to `getPossibleAccessPaths` changed from `b.is.SchemaMetaVersion()` to `b.optFlag&flagPartitionProcessor > 0`. This looks like a semantic change (schema meta version vs. whether partition processor is enabled) and could unintentionally affect access path selection for non-partitioned tables or for partitioned tables when `flagPartitionProcessor` is toggled for reasons unrelated to schema version. Please double-check the callee signature/meaning and consider passing the original `b.is.SchemaMetaVersion()` (or both via a struct/options) if it is still required for correctness. At minimum, add a clarifying comment or rename the parameter at the call site to avoid confusion, and ensure tests cover cases where schema meta version impacts path selection (e.g., stale stats / schema change scenarios).

The comment/logic here is a bit too broad and may disable global index in cases where it is actually safe/desired. `hasFlagPartitionProcessor` sounds like a planner-internal flag that can be set for multiple reasons (not only partition pruning), so using it as a proxy for "partition pruning optimization exists" risks over-filtering global index paths and causing regressions. Consider tightening the condition to the exact scenario that is incompatible with global index (e.g., only when the plan will be `LogicalPartitionUnionAll/LogicalPartitionAll` due to explicit partition selection or pruning), or rename the parameter to reflect the real meaning. Also, the comment says "LogicalPartitionAll" but the PR description mentions "PartitionUnionAll"—please align terminology to avoid confusion.

This change hard-codes the last argument to `true`, replacing `ds.is.SchemaMetaVersion()`. If that parameter controls schema version / metadata consistency for access path resolution, forcing `true` may bypass correctness checks (e.g., stale schema, plan cache invalidation) or change behavior for non-partition/global-index cases. Please avoid magic booleans here: either keep using `ds.is.SchemaMetaVersion()` or pass a clearly named variable (e.g., `useLatestSchema` / `schemaMetaVersion`) derived from `ds.is` with a comment explaining why partition processing requires `true`. Also consider adding/adjusting tests to cover schema change / plan cache scenarios to ensure this doesn’t introduce regressions.

Setting `sessVars.ResourceGroupName` here mutates a session-scoped variable during optimization. If this value isn’t reset after the statement finishes, the hinted resource group could leak into subsequent statements on the same session (especially if later statements don’t specify a hint). Please confirm there is per-statement restoration/cleanup (e.g., save old value and defer restore, or store the hint in StmtCtx instead of SessVars). Also consider whether the hint should override only for this statement rather than permanently changing the session default.

This rewrites `ErrDataTooLong` into `ErrTruncated` with a hardcoded message. Two concerns: (1) MySQL’s warning for LOAD DATA in this case is typically `Data too long for column 'col' at row N` (error 1406), not `Data truncated...` (1265). Changing the error class/code may break compatibility and tests that assert warning codes. Consider generating `types.ErrDataTooLong` (or the corresponding MySQL error) with the column/row context instead of switching to `ErrTruncated`. (2) `colName` can be empty when `col == nil`, producing `column ''`; if that can happen, consider a safer fallback (e.g., "?" or omit the column clause) or ensure callers always pass a non-nil column for LOAD DATA paths.

This test hard-codes a full sql_mode string including deprecated/removed flags (e.g. NO_AUTO_CREATE_USER) and may be brittle across TiDB/MySQL compatibility changes. Potential correctness issue: `format` is a single `rowInDisk` instance reused in the producer loop, and you send it by value on the channel. `reader.err` is written from the worker goroutine and read from the caller goroutine via `Error()` without synchronization, which is a data race under the Go race detector. With the removal of the `elif resume_from_checkpoint is None: resume_from_checkpoint = args.resume_from_checkpoint` branch, `Trainer.train()` will no longer automatically honor `TrainingArguments.resume_from_checkpoint` unless the caller explicitly passes `resume_from_checkpoint=...`. That’s a behavior change that can silently break existing scripts/users relying on the argument being picked up by default. If the intent is to move this logic to the example scripts, consider either (a) keeping a backward-compatible fallback here (e.g., `if resume_from_checkpoint is None: resume_from_checkpoint = self.args.resume_from_checkpoint`) or (b) adding a clear deprecation path / release note and ensuring all official examples pass `resume_from_checkpoint=self.args.resume_from_checkpoint` when calling `train()`.

This docstring change says `resume_from_checkpoint` is "not directly used" by `Trainer`, but in the PR description you mention it "is used as a default for the argument of the same name in the `train` method". If `Trainer.train()` (or `Trainer.__init__`) actually reads `TrainingArguments.resume_from_checkpoint` implicitly anywhere, this wording is misleading. Consider clarifying the contract explicitly, e.g. "Trainer does not automatically resume from this field; example scripts should pass it to `trainer.train(resume_from_checkpoint=...)`" (or update the implementation/docs consistently if Trainer does use it as a default). Also, the new text removed the previous explanation about being a default for `Trainer.train`, so ensure the examples/docs elsewhere cover how users should resume now to avoid regressions/confusion.

This fixes the reported `None` case, but it also changes behavior for models where `self.model.encoder.main_input_name` exists yet differs from the actual keys provided by the dataset (or from `self.model.main_input_name`). Consider making this more defensive: (1) default `main_input_name` to `getattr(self.model, "main_input_name", "input_ids")` before the conditional, and (2) when iterating batches, if `isinstance(batch, dict)` and `main_input_name` is missing, fall back to common keys (e.g. `input_ids`) or to `self.model.main_input_name` to avoid a hard `KeyError`. Also, if `encoder.main_input_name` can be `None` for some TF encoder-decoder wrappers, guard against that (e.g. `if self.model.encoder.main_input_name is not None:`) so we don't reintroduce `batch[None]`.

This re-injects the value under the deprecated key (`use_auth_token`) after popping `token`, which will likely keep triggering deprecation warnings downstream (and defeats the purpose of adding `token` support). If the downstream APIs already accept `token`, prefer passing `kwargs["token"] = token` instead. If they *don’t* yet, consider a compatibility shim that sets `token` and only falls back to `use_auth_token` when needed (or suppresses the warning when the mapping is internal). Also, the comment suggests a follow-up PR, but this line is the core behavior change—worth aligning now to avoid propagating deprecated usage internally.

This re-injects the value under the deprecated key (`use_auth_token`) after accepting `token`, which will keep internal deprecation warnings alive in downstream calls and partially defeats the purpose of adding `token` support. If the underlying APIs already accept `token`, prefer passing `kwargs["token"] = token` (or forwarding both only when needed). If they don’t yet, consider centralizing the compatibility shim (e.g., a shared helper used across auto classes) and ensure we don’t emit duplicate warnings (this method warns, and deeper layers may warn again when seeing `use_auth_token`). At minimum, add a comment explaining why we must map to `use_auth_token` here and verify no double-warning occurs in typical `from_pretrained` flows.

This re-injects the value under the deprecated key (`use_auth_token`) after accepting `token`, which can re-trigger deprecation warnings downstream (e.g., in `cached_file`/hub utilities) and partially defeats the purpose of adding `token` support. Prefer passing `token` through to the underlying calls (and only mapping to `use_auth_token` at the lowest-level API boundary if absolutely necessary), or at least ensure the internal call sites won’t emit warnings when `use_auth_token` is set programmatically. If you must keep this mapping for now, consider adding a comment explaining where the warning is suppressed/handled, or use a dedicated internal kwarg to avoid user-facing deprecation warnings.

This re-injects the value under the deprecated key (`use_auth_token`) after popping both args, which will likely keep triggering deprecation warnings downstream (and defeats the purpose of adding `token` support). Prefer passing `token` through to the underlying `from_pretrained` calls (or updating downstream utilities to accept `token`) and only map to `use_auth_token` at the lowest-level boundary if absolutely necessary. If you must keep this mapping for backward compatibility, consider adding a comment explaining where the warning is emitted and/or suppressing duplicate warnings to avoid warning spam.

This re-injects the value under the deprecated key (`use_auth_token`) after popping both args. That’s fine as a temporary bridge, but it will keep triggering downstream deprecation warnings in code paths that warn on `use_auth_token`. If the goal of this PR is to make `token` work *and* avoid the warning, consider passing `token` through to the underlying `from_pretrained` (or updating the downstream utilities to accept `token`) rather than mapping back to `use_auth_token`. At minimum, add a short comment explaining why the mapping is required here and ensure tests cover that `token` does not emit a deprecation warning (if that’s expected).

Using a string literal here is a bit brittle and the comment is misleading: even if you don’t call `fit()`, `model.compile()` will still validate/instantiate the optimizer, and Keras’ accepted identifiers can change. Prefer passing an actual optimizer instance (e.g., `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.0)` or `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD()`) or, better, avoid calling `compile()` at all when `do_train` is false. If you keep a dummy optimizer, consider naming it `dummy_optimizer` and explain that it’s required because Keras no longer accepts `optimizer=None` during `compile()`.

Using a string identifier here works, but it’s a bit implicit and may be backend/version dependent (e.g., Keras 3 / tf-keras differences). Consider passing an actual optimizer instance (e.g., `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.0)` or `keras.optimizers.SGD(...)` depending on what the script imports) to make the intent explicit and avoid any registry/alias issues. Also, the comment “Just write anything” is misleading—better to explain that Keras requires a non-None optimizer even when not training, and that this is a dummy optimizer used only to satisfy `compile()`.

Using a string optimizer here avoids the Keras error when `optimizer=None`, but it’s a bit implicit/magic. Consider using an actual optimizer instance (e.g., `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.0)` or `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD()`) and add a short comment explaining this is only to satisfy `compile()` when we won’t call `fit()`. Also, if `do_train` is false, we could avoid calling `compile()` entirely unless it’s required for `evaluate()/predict()` in this script—skipping compile would be cleaner and avoids any optimizer-related side effects.

Using a string literal here is a bit brittle/implicit and the comment is misleading: the optimizer *will* be used if someone later calls `model.fit()` (or if code paths change), and passing a string relies on Keras' name lookup. Prefer constructing an actual optimizer instance (e.g., `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.0)` or `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD()`), or better: only call `model.compile(...)` when `training_args.do_train` is true, and skip compile entirely for eval/predict-only runs. If you keep a dummy optimizer, consider a clearer comment like "Keras no longer accepts optimizer=None; provide a placeholder for non-training runs" and import/use the optimizer class to avoid backend/name-resolution issues.

The added lines include what look like diff/line-number artifacts (`-1:`, `0:`, `1:`, `2:`) that should not be present in the README command snippet. This will confuse users and makes the command invalid if copy-pasted. Please remove the numeric prefixes and keep consistent indentation, e.g. `--test_file ... \\` then `--do_train \\`, `--do_eval \\`, `--do_predict`.

Using a string placeholder optimizer is a bit brittle across Keras/TF versions (and can still trigger parsing/validation differences). Since the intent is “compile but never fit”, consider passing an actual optimizer instance (e.g., `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.0)` or `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD()`) and update the comment to explain why. Alternatively, avoid calling `compile()` at all when `not training_args.do_train` (if only needed for `fit()`), which would remove the need for a dummy optimizer entirely.

Using a string identifier here fixes the Keras error, but it’s a bit implicit and may change behavior across TF/Keras versions (string resolution differs, and defaults like momentum/learning rate are opaque). Consider using an explicit optimizer instance (e.g., `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.0)` or a clearly named constant) or, better, only pass `optimizer` to `compile()` when `do_train` is true. If the goal is “compile for eval/predict only”, also consider compiling with `optimizer=None` by *omitting* the argument entirely (so Keras uses its default) rather than forcing SGD, and add a short comment explaining why compile is needed when not training.

Using a string literal here is a bit brittle and the comment is misleading: even if `do_train` is false, `compile()` will still validate/instantiate the optimizer, so we *are* using it at compile-time. Prefer passing an actual optimizer instance (e.g., `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.0)`), or avoid passing `optimizer` at all when not training (let Keras default apply) by conditionally calling `model.compile(metrics=...)` in the eval-only path. This also avoids potential issues if Keras changes accepted string identifiers.

Minor: `x` is only used to carry dtype/device into the RoPE modules, but creating a 1-element tensor is a bit opaque and the comment has a typo ("exlusively"). Consider using `torch.empty((), device=torch_device)` (scalar) or `torch.empty(1, device=torch_device)` and explicitly setting dtype if needed, and fix the comment spelling. This will make the intent clearer and avoid implying `x` is a real input batch.

This creates a 1-element tensor just to carry dtype/device, but it’s a bit odd/fragile: the RoPE forward typically expects an input with at least 2 dims (batch/seq) in some implementations, and using a scalar can accidentally pass today but break if the signature starts validating shapes. Consider using a minimal shaped tensor instead (e.g., `x = torch.zeros(1, 1, head_dim, device=torch_device)` or `torch.empty(1, 1, device=..., dtype=...)`) or pass `dtype`/`device` explicitly if the embedding supports it. Also fix the typo in the comment (“exlusively”).

This creates a 1-element tensor just to carry dtype/device, but it’s a bit odd and can be fragile: RoPE forward typically expects an input shaped like (batch, seq_len, head_dim) (or at least something broadcastable to the computed cos/sin shapes). Passing a scalar may work today due to implementation details, but it makes the test less representative and more likely to break on refactors. Consider constructing a minimal dummy hidden state with the expected rank, e.g. `x = torch.zeros((1, 1, head_dim), dtype=torch.float32, device=torch_device)` (or `(1, short_input_length, head_dim)`), so the test validates the intended API contract rather than relying on broadcasting quirks.

This tensor is created as a scalar and the comment says it's used only for dtype/device, but it’s a bit confusing and also doesn’t actually exercise any shape-dependent behavior in the RoPE forward. Consider making it explicit and representative (e.g., `x = torch.empty(1, 1, 1, dtype=..., device=...)` or `torch.zeros(1, dtype=..., device=...)`) and/or rename to something like `dummy` to clarify intent. Also fix the typo in the comment (“exlusively” -> “exclusively”).

This test uses `parameterized.expand`, but `parameterized` is an optional dependency and may not be installed in all CI environments for `transformers`. To avoid introducing a new external test dependency, consider rewriting this using `pytest.mark.parametrize` (pytest is already imported) or `unittest` subTests. That will keep the test suite self-contained and consistent with the rest of the repo.

This tensor is a scalar and the comment has a typo. More importantly, passing a 0-dim tensor into the RoPE module is a bit brittle if the implementation ever starts relying on `x` shape (some RoPE variants use `x.dtype`/`x.device` only, but others may infer batch/seq dims). Consider using a minimal shaped tensor like `torch.empty(1, 1, 1, device=torch_device, dtype=torch.float32)` (or `torch.zeros(1, 1, head_dim, ...)`) to better match expected inputs, and fix the comment spelling ("exclusively").

Setting `_no_split_modules = []` enables `device_map="auto"`, but it also allows Accelerate to split *any* submodule, potentially splitting inside a logical block (e.g., MBConv / InvertedResidual / EfficientNetBlock) which can increase cross-device communication and hurt performance or even trigger edge-case device mismatch bugs in some models. Consider listing the minimal block-level modules that should stay intact (e.g., the repeated stage/block class used in EfficientNet) rather than an empty list, and add a short comment explaining why the chosen modules are safe to split or not.
