If you intentionally want full split freedom, it may still be worth confirming the actual block class names and adding a regression test that exercises `device_map="auto"` with a multi-GPU map to ensure no unexpected splits occur.

This change drops support for non-str inputs and will raise if `image` is `bytes`/`bytearray` (since those don’t have `.encode()`), even though the previous code handled both. If `load_image` is intended to accept base64 as either `str` or raw `bytes`, keep the conditional (or explicitly normalize via `image_bytes = image.encode() if isinstance(image, str) else image`). Also consider using `base64.b64decode(..., validate=True)` after stripping whitespace/newlines, since `encodebytes()` inserts newlines and `decodebytes()` is permissive but the failure in the issue suggests upstream validation/regex may still reject whitespace—so normalization (e.g., `re.sub(r"\s+", "", image)`) may be needed before decoding.

The section underline length should match the title length in reStructuredText for consistent rendering. Here the underline is extremely long compared to `GPT2ForSequenceClassification`, which can look odd and may trigger doc style/lint issues. Consider shortening it to exactly the same number of `~` characters as the heading text (as done for the other sections in this file).

When adding a new public model class to the top-level namespace, please also ensure it’s included in the corresponding `_import_structure` entry (for lazy imports) and any `TYPE_CHECKING` blocks in this file. Otherwise `from transformers import GPT2ForSequenceClassification` may work in some contexts but fail under lazy import / optional dependency setups. Also consider whether this should be gated consistently with the other PyTorch-only GPT2 modeling imports.

Avoid using a bare `assert` for runtime validation here. Python can strip asserts with `-O`, which would silently remove this safety check and lead to hard-to-debug indexing errors for batch_size>1 when `pad_token_id` is None. Prefer raising a `ValueError` (or `AssertionError` explicitly) with the same message. Also consider allowing batch_size>1 without `pad_token_id` by using `attention_mask` (if provided) to compute `sequence_lengths` instead of hard-failing.

`logits` is annotated as `torch.FloatTensor` but defaults to `None`, which is inconsistent with the type hint and can confuse static type checkers (and readers). Consider making it `Optional[torch.FloatTensor] = None` (as done for other outputs) or remove the default and require it. Also, for consistency with other output dataclasses, you may want to use `Tuple[Tuple[torch.FloatTensor]]` for `past_key_values` (layer-wise tuples) rather than `List[torch.FloatTensor]`, since most models return nested tuples for cache.

Remove this debug `print`. Tests should be silent unless failing; this will add noise to CI logs and can break tooling that expects clean output. If you need to validate shapes/values, rely on assertions (you already assert `result.logits.shape`) or use the test framework's logging facilities guarded behind verbosity flags.

In the embedded schema example, `type` is shown as a function returning `AddressEntity`. Please ensure this matches the actual supported API for `EntitySchema` embeddeds (some versions accept `type: Address`/`type: AddressSchema` directly). If the implementation specifically requires a factory to avoid circular deps, consider adding a short note explaining why `() => AddressEntity` is needed and whether `type: AddressEntity` is also valid. Also, the example currently defines `AddressEntity` as `const` without `export`; if consumers typically define embeddeds in separate modules, exporting it in the example may be clearer.

The `type` signature here is overly broad/odd: `((type?: any) => Function | EntitySchema)` suggests a factory function returning either a `Function` or an `EntitySchema`, but it doesn't allow passing the class/schema directly (common in other schema options), and it also loses generic typing (`EntitySchema<any>`). Consider aligning with existing TypeORM patterns (e.g., `Function | string | EntitySchema<any> | (() => Function | EntitySchema<any>)`) and avoid `any` in the callback parameter unless it's actually used. This will improve DX and prevent TS compile issues when users provide `type: User` or `type: () => User`.

Import style is inconsistent with the rest of the file (spaces inside braces). Consider matching the existing convention (`import {EntitySchemaEmbeddedOptions} ...`) to keep formatting consistent and avoid lint/prettier churn.

`embeddedSchema.type` is passed through as-is, but in EntitySchema users often provide either a class constructor, an EntitySchema instance, or a string name. If `EmbeddedMetadataArgs.type` expects a function/constructor, this may break at runtime or lose metadata when `type` is an `EntitySchema`/string. Consider normalizing here (e.g., if `type` is an EntitySchema, use `type.options.target || type.options.name`; if it's a string, resolve to the registered target) and/or validating with a clear error message. Also, if `embeddedSchema.type` can be a function returning the type (lazy), ensure it’s supported consistently with relations.

This new import appears unused in this file (the interface only references `EntitySchema` as a type). Consider switching to a type-only import to avoid emitting a runtime `require`/import and to satisfy linting/tsc settings like `importsNotUsedAsValues`: `import type { EntitySchema } from "../entity-schema/EntitySchema";`.

Instantiating `EntitySchemaTransformer` inside the recursion will create a new transformer for every embedded schema (and for every nested embedded), which can be unnecessarily expensive and may lead to subtle inconsistencies if the transformer ever becomes stateful. Consider reusing a single instance (e.g., as a private field on `EntityMetadataBuilder` or passed into `createEmbeddedsRecursively`) and/or caching the transformed `MetadataArgsStorage` per schema name/target. Also, this branch assumes `embeddedMetadata.type` is an `EntitySchema` and that `options.name` is defined; it would be safer to guard with `instanceof EntitySchema` (or a structural check) and throw a clear error if an unsupported type is provided.

Returning `{}` when `type` is an `EntitySchema` avoids `new`-ing a non-function, but it also means embedded instances won’t have the embedded class prototype (methods/getters/default field initializers) and may break code that expects an instance of the embedded target. Consider resolving the actual embedded target from the schema (e.g., `this.type.options.target`) and instantiating it when it’s a function, or at least using `Object.create(target.prototype)` to preserve prototype. If no target exists, document/guard this behavior explicitly (and consider throwing a clearer error) so consumers understand embedded objects are plain objects in schema-only mode.
File is missing a trailing newline (note the `\ No newline at end of file`). This can cause lint/formatting failures and noisy diffs; please add a final newline.
Add a trailing newline at end of file to satisfy common linting/formatting rules and avoid noisy diffs across platforms/tools.

This test is intended to validate EntitySchema embeddeds across drivers, but the driver list is hard-coded and may not match the project’s standard test matrix for schema/embedded coverage. Consider using the common test-utils pattern (e.g., `enabledDrivers: [...]` consistent with other functional tests, or omitting `enabledDrivers` to run on all supported drivers in CI) to avoid missing regressions on other DBs. If the feature is known to be unsupported on some drivers, add a short comment explaining why they’re excluded.

Using a thunk for `type` is good to avoid circular deps, but here it returns `AddressSchema` (an `EntitySchema`) rather than the embedded class/type. Please double-check the expected contract for `embeddeds.*.type` in `EntitySchema` (some parts of TypeORM expect a class/constructor or a schema name, not an `EntitySchema` instance). If the new feature explicitly supports schema references, consider adding a small assertion test that metadata is built correctly (columns are prefixed/flattened as expected) to prevent regressions. Also, if `AddressSchema` is a value, you can simplify to `type: AddressSchema` unless lazy evaluation is required.

Bug: `rawFieldSpecs` is computed from the already-sliced `propertyPath`, so it will always be empty. You likely meant to slice from the original `fieldSpec` (or store the original string before truncating). E.g. `rawFieldSpecs = fieldSpec.slice(spIdx)` (and keep `propertyPath = fieldSpec.slice(0, spIdx)`). As written, the JSON operator/cast suffix after the space will be dropped, so the fix won’t work for `property ->'field'`.

The `ExtraSetting` type is used only as a TypeScript shape for a `jsonb` column, but it’s currently a plain class with required fields. Consider making it an `interface` or marking properties optional (e.g., `someStr?: string; someNb?: number;`) to better reflect that JSONB can be missing keys and to avoid strictPropertyInitialization/undefined issues in tests. If you keep it as a class, you may also want to export it as a type-only import where used to avoid runtime artifacts.

The inverse side function for `ManyToOne` should typically reference a relation property on `FormTmpl` (e.g., `formTmpl => formTmpl.forms`) rather than the primary key (`formTmpl => formTmpl.id`). Using the PK here is unusual and may prevent proper relation metadata/inverse-side behavior. If `FormTmpl` doesn’t define a `OneToMany` back-reference, consider omitting the inverse side argument entirely or adding the proper `OneToMany` on `FormTmpl` for correctness.

Setting `eager: true` on the relation can make the test less focused and may mask issues by always loading the relation regardless of join usage. Since the bug is specifically about JOIN + pagination + ORDER BY, it may be better to keep this non-eager and rely on the query builder join in the test to ensure the JOIN path is actually exercised.

The inverse side of the OneToMany relation should point to the property on `Form` that holds the `ManyToOne` back-reference to `FormTmpl`, not to `form.id`. Using `form.id` will prevent TypeORM from correctly wiring the relation metadata and can make joins in the test behave incorrectly. Consider changing to something like `(form) => form.formTmpl` (or whatever the property is named on `Form`).

This test relies on Postgres JSON operator ordering semantics, but `->` returns `jsonb` and ordering may be implementation-dependent (and can differ from numeric ordering). To make the intent unambiguous and avoid flaky behavior, consider ordering by a scalar via `->>` plus an explicit cast, e.g. `"form.extraSettings ->> 'someNb'"` with `::int` (or `::numeric`) after a space: `"form.extraSettings ->> 'someNb' ::int"`. That also better matches the issue description about supporting extra syntax after a space.

Switching from `Promise.all` to sequential connects changes behavior/performance for all callers (connections are no longer established in parallel). If the goal is to avoid dangling connections when one connect fails, consider keeping parallelism but using `Promise.allSettled` (or a try/catch around `Promise.all`) and explicitly closing any successfully-opened connections on failure. Also, the comment is very test-specific; it would be better to explain the general failure mode (e.g., "avoid leaving partially-open connections when one connect rejects") and/or link the issue/PR number for traceability.

Changing the root package version in package-lock.json from 0.3.15 to 0.3.14 looks unrelated to the junction-table subscriber fix and can cause confusion/inconsistency with package.json (and the actual published version). In TypeORM, the lockfile should generally reflect the version declared in package.json, and PRs typically shouldn’t bump/downgrade the project version unless that’s explicitly part of the change. Suggest reverting this lockfile version change (and any corresponding package.json change, if present) or clearly justifying it in the PR; otherwise CI/release tooling may treat this as an unintended version change.

This changes the return type from a readonly tuple (`as const`) to a mutable tuple (`[ObjectLiteral, SubjectChangeMap[]]`). If callers relied on the readonly/precise tuple typing (e.g., destructuring with exact types), this is a breaking type change. Consider keeping `as const` and updating the method signature to return a readonly tuple instead (e.g., `readonly [ObjectLiteral, SubjectChangeMap[]]`) or explicitly returning `as const` to preserve prior behavior while still providing an explicit return type.

The default `From<&ResolutionGraph>` now hard-codes `include_annotations = true` via `Self::new(resolution, false, true)`. Given the PR adds `--no-annotate/--no-header` for pip-compile compatibility, consider whether the *default* output should remain annotated or match pip-compile defaults (often unannotated unless requested). If other call sites rely on `From` for a “minimal” requirements.txt, this change could be a behavior regression; it may be safer to keep `From` producing the most compatible/minimal output and let the CLI opt into annotations explicitly.

Using `env::args()` to reconstruct the invocation can be lossy/incorrect (non-UTF8 args, quoting/escaping, and it may include wrapper args depending on how the binary is invoked). Since this is meant to be `pip-compile` compatible output, consider either (a) omitting the command line entirely when `include_header` is true but you can't reliably reproduce it, or (b) using a more robust argv capture (e.g., `std::env::args_os()` with proper shell escaping) so the header is accurate and doesn't break consumers that diff/parse the header.

These inverted booleans (`!args.no_annotate`, `!args.no_header`) are easy to misread and can lead to accidental argument-order bugs at the call site. Consider renaming the callee parameters to match the CLI flags (e.g., `no_annotate`, `no_header`) or using a struct / builder / named-arg pattern so the intent is explicit. At minimum, add a short comment or reorder/format the call to make it clear these are `include_*` toggles derived from `--no-*` flags.

The snapshot for `--no-annotate` still includes the autogenerated header and command line, but it omits `--exclude-newer [EXCLUDE_NEWER]` even though the test passes that flag. This makes the snapshot less faithful to the actual invocation and could hide regressions in header rendering. Consider updating the expected header command to include all args (including `--exclude-newer`) or, if the header intentionally only prints a subset, add an assertion/comment clarifying that behavior so future changes don't break this test unexpectedly.

Using `parent().unwrap_or(working_dir)` silently falls back to the CWD when `requirements_txt` has no parent (e.g., a bare filename like `dev.txt`). That reintroduces the old behavior in a subtle way and may diverge from pip, which effectively resolves relative includes against the directory containing the requirements file (which, for a bare filename, is `.`). Consider using `requirements_txt.as_ref().parent().unwrap_or_else(|| Path::new("."))` (or `Path::new("")`) and then joining, or otherwise ensuring the base is the requirements file’s directory even when no parent is present. Also consider normalizing/canonicalizing if you rely on `..` segments later.

This change alters the golden test output formatting (removes the leading space and shifts the line number column). If the intent of this PR is only to change path resolution for `-c`/`-r`, consider keeping the output formatting stable to avoid unrelated churn, unless the formatter/parser now intentionally normalizes whitespace. If the formatting change is intentional, it would be good to update/confirm other fixtures to ensure consistent alignment across test-data and avoid brittle diffs.

This test only exercises nested `-r` resolution. Given the PR summary also changes `-c` (relative to containing file) and explicitly keeps `-e` relative to CWD, consider extending coverage here (or adding sibling tests) to assert those behaviors too. In particular, add a case where the included file uses `-c constraints.txt` and verify it resolves relative to `subdir`, and a case where an editable path in an included file is still resolved relative to the process CWD (to prevent regressions in the opposite direction).

Adding `url` to the lockfile is expected if a crate now depends on it, but please ensure the corresponding `Cargo.toml` change is present in this PR (and that the dependency is actually used). If this is only needed for PEP 517 metadata extraction, consider whether `url` should be a direct dependency of the specific crate that parses/handles build backend URLs rather than being pulled into a broader crate, to avoid unnecessary dependency surface.

`simplify_extras` currently treats any marker expression involving `extra` as "true" if the referenced extra is present, regardless of the operator. That means `extra != "dev"` (or other operators like `in`/`not in`) would be incorrectly simplified/removed when `dev` is present. Consider checking `expression.operator` and only folding cases you can prove: e.g. `==` is true when present, `!=` is true when absent, and handle `in`/`not in` if supported. Otherwise, leave the expression unchanged to avoid unsound simplification. Also consider whether `extras.contains` should be case-normalized consistently with `ExtraName` parsing (it likely is, but worth ensuring).

Adding `url` is fine, but please ensure it’s actually required by this crate (and not only by a downstream module) to avoid unnecessary dependency bloat. If it’s only needed behind a feature or for a specific codepath (e.g., PEP 517 metadata extraction), consider gating it with a feature and documenting why it’s needed here. Also double-check that the workspace `url` dependency has the intended features enabled (e.g., `serde`/`idna`/`percent-encoding`) in the workspace definition, since this line inherits those settings implicitly.

Re-exporting the entire `source_tree` module from the crate root expands the public API surface. If this is only needed internally (or only a couple of types/functions are intended to be public), consider re-exporting specific items (e.g., `pub use crate::source_tree::SourceTree;`) or keeping the module private to avoid locking in an API you may want to change later. Also ensure `source_tree` has appropriate rustdoc/docs since it becomes part of the public interface.

Using a hard-coded concurrency limit of 50 here may be too aggressive for PEP 517 metadata builds (it can spawn many subprocesses, do I/O, and contend on caches/temp dirs). This could lead to resource exhaustion or flaky behavior on CI/low-core machines. Consider making the limit configurable (via context/config), or at least tie it to available parallelism (e.g., `std::thread::available_parallelism()`), and/or use a smaller default. Also consider whether `buffer_unordered` is acceptable if ordering doesn't matter, to avoid head-of-line blocking.

`is_empty` currently treats only `ExtrasSpecification::None` as empty. If `ExtrasSpecification::Some` can ever contain an empty set/list (e.g., parsed from `[]`), this will return `false` even though there are no extras. Consider either normalizing `Some(empty)` to `None` at construction/parsing time, or implement `is_empty` as `matches!(self, ExtrasSpecification::None) || matches!(self, ExtrasSpecification::Some(extras) if extras.is_empty())` to avoid subtle logic bugs in callers.

This canonicalizes the `pyproject.toml` path using `fs_err::canonicalize`, which can fail for valid-but-nonexistent paths (or in some sandboxed/virtual FS scenarios) and also resolves symlinks. Since you only need the containing directory to run PEP 517 hooks, consider using `path.parent()` on the original path (or `absolutize`/`normalize` without requiring existence) to avoid surprising failures and to preserve symlink semantics. If canonicalization is intentional, it’d be good to add context to the error (e.g., `with_context(|| format!("Failed to canonicalize `{}`", path.user_display()))`) so users understand why metadata extraction failed.

Gating extra validation on `source_trees.is_empty()` avoids false negatives when metadata is resolved via PEP 517, but it also means we may silently accept misspelled/unknown extras for source-tree inputs. Consider moving this validation to after `SourceTreeResolver` runs (once extras/metadata are known), or at least emitting a warning when skipping validation so users still get feedback. Also double-check that `source_trees` is the right proxy for “PEP 517 metadata needed” (vs other cases where static metadata fails but `source_trees` might still be empty).

Passing `&extras` into `SourceTreeResolver::new` is good, but ensure the resolver’s behavior is well-defined for `ExtrasSpecification::All` and for `None`/empty extras. In particular, if extras were provided and are invalid, this path currently won’t error (due to the earlier skip); it would be better if `SourceTreeResolver` surfaced an explicit error for unknown extras once it has metadata, or returned which extras were actually used so the caller can validate consistently.

Skipping the fast-path when `source_trees` is non-empty makes sense, but note that this changes behavior even if the source tree ultimately resolves to zero requirements (e.g., empty/optional deps). Consider whether you can cheaply detect “no-op” source trees (or at least document why any presence of `source_trees` must force a full resolve/install). Also ensure this condition stays in sync with other early-exit checks (e.g., if other sources are added later).

When extending `requirements` with results from `SourceTreeResolver`, consider deduplication/precedence rules if the same requirement appears both in the original `requirements` and in a source tree (or across multiple source trees). If duplicates are allowed, downstream resolution may do extra work or produce confusing diagnostics. If precedence matters (e.g., constraints/overrides), it may be worth normalizing/merging here or adding a comment/tests to clarify expected behavior.

Hard-coding `ExtrasSpecification::None` here may be incorrect/inconsistent with user intent. `pip_sync` already receives an `extras` field from `RequirementsSpecification::from_simple_sources(...)` (currently ignored as `extras: _`), but source-tree resolution can depend on extras (e.g., `.[dev]` or `--extra`). Consider threading the parsed extras through and passing the actual `ExtrasSpecification` into `SourceTreeResolver::new(...)` so editable/source-tree requirements include the right optional dependencies. At minimum, add a comment explaining why extras are intentionally ignored for sync.

This test is meant to validate behavior around `tool.poetry.dependencies`, but the fixture currently omits a `[project]` table (and likely `name`/`version`). If the new implementation uses PEP 517 metadata extraction, Poetry may require full project metadata to build/prepare metadata, and this test could become brittle across Poetry versions. Consider adding a minimal `[project]` (or `[tool.poetry]` name/version if that’s what the backend expects) to ensure the test is exercising dependency extraction rather than relying on permissive defaults. Also, switching from `numpy` to `anyio` changes the transitive set; if the intent is just “non-empty deps are respected”, a comment explaining why `anyio` was chosen (small, stable transitive closure) would help future maintainers.

This uses `self.python_requirement.target()` to validate `Requires-Python`. Please double-check that `target()` is the right notion here (vs. the full interpreter marker environment / selected Python version range). If `python_requirement` can represent a range (e.g., project requires >=3.8,<4), validating against a single `target` could incorrectly reject/accept. Consider clarifying via naming/comment, or using an API that checks intersection between `requires_python` and the resolver's allowed Python range, if available.

This test claims to cover a *direct URL* requirement, but the requirement string is missing the `file://` scheme. `example @ /path/to/dir` may be parsed as a local path (or even rejected) rather than a PEP 508 direct URL, which could make the test pass for the wrong reason and not actually exercise the direct-URL code path. Consider using an explicit file URL (e.g., `format!("example @ file://{}", editable_dir.path().display())` with proper URL escaping) or a VCS URL to ensure the resolver treats it as a direct URL requirement.

This test claims to cover a *direct URL* requirement, but the requirement string is `example @ {path}` without an explicit URL scheme. Depending on how the parser classifies bare paths, this may be treated as a local path requirement rather than a PEP 508 direct URL (e.g., `file://...`). To ensure the test actually exercises the direct-URL codepath (and matches the issue/PR intent), consider using an explicit `file://` URL (or `path().to_str()` -> `Url::from_directory_path`) and/or adding an assertion that the requirement is parsed as a direct URL. Otherwise the test could pass while not covering the intended behavior.

This test is annotated as a TODO and currently asserts a successful install, which makes it a “known failing behavior” test but without any enforcement mechanism (e.g., `#[ignore]`, `#[should_panic]`, or asserting the expected error output). As written, it will keep passing even after the bug is fixed unless someone remembers to update it, and it doesn’t protect the intended behavior. Consider either (a) changing the snapshot to assert failure (non-zero exit + error message about `Requires-Python` mismatch) once the feature is implemented, or (b) marking the test `#[ignore]` with a tracking issue link until the behavior is enforced, to avoid baking in the wrong expectation.

Cargo.lock changes are usually auto-generated; please ensure this reflects an intentional dependency graph update (e.g., adding `same-file`/`tokio` and removing the explicit `itertools 0.13.0` entry) rather than a manual edit. If this PR is meant to be reproducible across toolchains, consider regenerating the lockfile with the project’s pinned Rust/Cargo version and verifying CI uses the same, otherwise reviewers may see noisy diffs or inconsistent resolution.

Relying on `debug_assert!` means this precondition is skipped in release builds; if `base_dir` can ever be relative (e.g., from user input or a caller bug), `join` will silently produce an incorrect path and may lead to confusing behavior. Consider either (a) enforcing this at the type/constructor boundary (pass an absolute `PathBuf`/`AbsolutePath`-like wrapper), (b) converting to absolute here (e.g., `std::fs::canonicalize` or a deterministic absolutization without IO), or (c) returning a `VerbatimUrlError` when `base_dir` is not absolute. Also, since the parameter was renamed from `working_dir` to `base_dir`, ensure all call sites were updated consistently and that the semantics match (is this truly a base directory for resolution, not necessarily the process working dir?).

Adding `same-file` (and `tokio` below) increases the dependency surface of `uv-requirements`. Please double-check that these are actually used in this crate (or will be in the same PR) and not only in another crate; otherwise this should be removed to avoid unused deps and longer compile times. If it is needed, consider adding a short comment in the code where it’s used (e.g., for symlink/realpath comparisons) to justify the dependency choice.

Using `to_string_lossy()` to populate `with_given(...)` can silently mangle non-UTF8 paths (and may produce a different string than the user provided in TOML). Since `with_given` is used for display/round-tripping, consider preserving the original TOML string when available (e.g., pass the raw `String` into `path_source` alongside the resolved `Path`), or use a stricter conversion (`to_str().ok_or(...)`) to error on non-UTF8 paths rather than lossy conversion. This will make error messages and lock/metadata output more predictable across platforms.

Typo in variable name: `project_in_exiting_workspace` should be `project_in_existing_workspace`. This is user-facing only in code readability, but it’s easy to propagate and makes the intent harder to follow. Consider renaming for clarity (and to avoid repeating the typo elsewhere).

`user_display()` is a nice improvement for user-facing paths, but this is a `debug!` log and may be used for diagnostics. Consider whether you want a stable, unambiguous representation here (e.g., `project_path.display()` or both), since `user_display()` can be lossy/normalized and may hide details needed to debug path issues (especially across platforms). If the intent is to make this message user-facing, it may be better as `info!`/`trace!` with consistent formatting across the codebase.

Potential correctness issue: `seen` is checked/updated *before* normalizing the path, but the value inserted is the raw `glob` result. The same directory can be reached via different spellings (relative vs absolute, `..`, symlinks, case differences on Windows), so this may still read the same `pyproject.toml` multiple times. Consider normalizing (or even canonicalizing, if appropriate) first, then dedup on the normalized path. Also, if `absolutize_path` can fail for some entries, you may want to normalize before inserting into `seen` to avoid marking an un-normalizable path as processed.

Minor: the new message adds a colon before the path, which is fine, but consider making the error more specific/consistent with the variant name (e.g., "Failed to parse pyproject.toml: `{0}`" vs a generic "Failed to parse"). Also, if `{0}` is a full path, you may want to avoid backticks/formatting differences across errors and ensure other parse errors in the codebase follow the same pattern for log/search consistency.

Passing a bare `None` here makes the call site hard to understand and brittle if the callee’s parameter list changes (especially with the new workspace-related plumbing). Consider using a named variable (e.g., `let workspace = None;`) or, if this is a struct/enum option, a more explicit constructor/constant. If the function supports it, prefer a builder or a parameter struct to avoid positional `None` arguments and improve readability.

Passing a bare `None` here makes the call-site hard to understand and brittle if the callee’s parameter list changes. Consider using a named variable (e.g., `let workspace = None;`) or, if this is a new optional argument, prefer a builder/struct or a dedicated options type so the meaning is explicit. At minimum, add a short comment indicating what this `None` represents (e.g., workspace root / workspace config / project selection) to avoid future confusion.

Adding `workspace: Option<&Workspace>` is fine, but please ensure the lifetime/ownership model is consistent with the async call chain: `read_requirements` is `async` and forwards `workspace` into `RequirementsSpecification::from_sources`. If that function stores the reference beyond the call (e.g., inside a struct that lives across `.await` points), this can become a borrow-across-await issue or force unnecessary lifetime constraints. Consider passing an owned/cheaply-clonable handle (e.g., `Arc<Workspace>` or an ID) if the workspace needs to be retained, or keep it strictly as an input-only parameter that is not stored. Also, if `workspace` is required for correct behavior when `-r pyproject.toml` / `-e` points into a workspace, consider whether `None` should be treated as an error in those cases (or document the fallback behavior).

Passing a bare `None` here makes the call site hard to understand and brittle if the callee’s parameter order changes. Consider using a named variable (e.g., `let workspace = None;`) or, if possible, switching the callee to a builder/struct or adding a helper wrapper so the intent is explicit (especially since this PR adds workspace support and this likely corresponds to an optional workspace/discovery argument). Also double-check that `None` is the correct behavior for `pip sync` (should it attempt workspace discovery from the provided `-r`/`-e` inputs instead?).

Switching `ProjectWorkspace::discover` to async is fine, but this call now depends on Tokio/async runtime behavior and can introduce new I/O failure modes (e.g., cancellation) compared to the previous sync discovery. Please ensure all other call sites were updated consistently and that `discover` is truly async for a reason (e.g., filesystem walking) rather than just wrapping sync work. If it’s mostly synchronous, consider keeping a sync API (or providing both) to avoid forcing async at higher layers unnecessarily.

Passing `Option<&Workspace>` through `update_environment` is fine, but please ensure the call sites and downstream resolver/installer logic treat `None` vs `Some` consistently. In particular, if `workspace` is `Some`, confirm that workspace members are actually added as a source/index (and that editable/default behavior is applied) rather than being ignored silently. Also consider whether this should be `&ProjectWorkspace` (already imported) instead of `&Workspace` to avoid type confusion between “discovered workspace context” vs “workspace definition/model” types; the current imports include both and it’s easy to accidentally pass the wrong one.

`ProjectWorkspace::discover` is now async; consider avoiding `std::env::current_dir()?` directly in the call so errors can be contextualized (e.g., with `anyhow::Context`) and to make it easier to test/memoize the cwd. Also double-check that `discover` doesn't perform blocking filesystem work on the async runtime; if it does, it should use `tokio::fs`/`spawn_blocking` internally to avoid stalling the executor.

Now that `ProjectWorkspace::discover` is async, consider avoiding a potentially blocking `std::env::current_dir()` call inside an async command. While it's usually fast, it still performs filesystem/syscalls on the async executor thread. If this function is called frequently or in latency-sensitive paths, prefer capturing the cwd before entering async work, or use a spawn_blocking/async-friendly wrapper (if available in this crate) to keep blocking IO off the runtime. Also ensure the error context remains clear (e.g., wrap with `anyhow::Context` if used elsewhere) since `.await?` can make it harder to see whether failures come from cwd lookup vs discovery.

This adds a new `None` argument to `update_environment(...)`. Please consider using a named variable (e.g., `let workspace = None;`) or a small comment to clarify what this `Option` represents (workspace root/current project/lockfile context/etc.). As-is, the call site becomes harder to read and is easy to misuse when future callers need `Some(...)` for workspace support. If this is intended to disable workspace discovery for `uv tool run`, it would be good to make that explicit here.

Setting `current_dir` to `self.temp_dir` may make this helper behave unexpectedly when callers pass a relative `file` path (it will be resolved relative to the temp dir, not the test's source/fixture directory). Consider either (a) documenting that `file` must be absolute or relative to `temp_dir`, or (b) resolving `file` against `temp_dir` explicitly (e.g., `self.temp_dir.join(file.as_ref())`) to make the intent clear and avoid accidental misuse. Also, if the goal is to run within the venv context, you may want to ensure env vars like `VIRTUAL_ENV`/`PATH` are set consistently (depending on what `venv_to_interpreter` does).

This change makes the test assert on an exact error string that includes punctuation/formatting ("Failed to parse: `pyproject.toml`"). That’s brittle across future refactors (e.g., switching error types, changing Display impls, or tweaking wording). If possible, prefer asserting on a stable substring/pattern (e.g., contains "Failed to parse" and "pyproject.toml") or use a snapshot/structured error assertion helper so minor formatting changes don’t break the test unnecessarily.

This change updates the expected error string to `Failed to parse: `pyproject.toml``. Please double-check that the new punctuation/format is intentional and consistent across all parse-error sites (e.g., other config files or other commands) to avoid brittle snapshot-style assertions. If the goal is to standardize messaging, consider updating all related tests in one pass or centralizing the formatting so future tweaks don’t require widespread test edits.

This change updates the expected error string. Please double-check that the new wording ("Failed to parse: `...`") is stable across all code paths and platforms (e.g., different parsers/feature flags), since tests that assert exact stderr lines can become brittle. If the message is expected to evolve, consider matching on a less specific substring/pattern (e.g., assert it contains "Failed to parse" and the requirement) rather than the full formatted line.

This test name suggests it should install the `albatross` package from the root workspace, but `current_dir` is pointing at `.../packages/bird-feeder` (same as `test_albatross_root_workspace_bird_feeder`). As written, the snapshot also matches the bird-feeder install case, so this test is likely a copy/paste mistake and doesn’t cover the intended scenario. Consider changing `current_dir` to the albatross package directory (e.g., the workspace root or `packages/albatross` depending on fixture layout) and updating the expected snapshot accordingly, or rename/remove the test if it’s meant to duplicate the bird-feeder case.

Adding a full wheel binary to the repo can significantly bloat the repository and may be problematic for licensing/compliance and long-term maintenance. If this is only needed for tests/fixtures, consider replacing it with a smaller, purpose-built test wheel (minimal package), generating it during CI (e.g., via a build script), or storing it as a compressed fixture/artifact fetched at test time. If keeping it is intentional, please document why this specific upstream wheel is required and ensure the project’s license/NOTICE requirements are satisfied.

Adding `uv-git` to the lockfile dependencies is expected if the crate graph changed, but please ensure the corresponding `Cargo.toml` (workspace member/dependency) change is included in this PR and that `cargo update -p uv-git` (or a full `cargo build`) was run to keep `Cargo.lock` consistent. Also verify this new dependency is intentional for the URL parsing change (i.e., not pulling in git support transitively when it should remain optional/feature-gated).

Bumping the size ceiling this much (248 -> 336) weakens the intent of this test and may hide future regressions. Since this PR adds parsed URL fields, consider reducing the impact by boxing larger fields (e.g., `Url`/`PathBuf`/parsed structs) or storing them behind `Arc`/`Box` consistently (you already box `GitUrl`). At minimum, please update the comment with the new observed sizes per platform (Unix/Windows) and consider asserting tighter, platform-specific bounds (via `cfg(target_os)`), so we still catch accidental growth.

Using `unwrap()` in tests is often acceptable, but here it can make failures less diagnosable (and it bypasses the test’s `Result<()>` error propagation). Consider using `Url::parse(url)?` (and similarly for `VerbatimUrl::from_str(url)?`) so parse errors surface with context and the test remains consistent with the `?` style used elsewhere. Also, since `url` is already a `&str`, you could parse once and reuse if other fields need it later.

The error message and match arm don’t align with what’s being checked. `ParsedUrl::Archive` likely represents an archive URL (which could be `http`, `https`, `file`, etc.), but the message says “Only https is supported”. If the intent is to restrict to HTTPS, consider explicitly validating `archive.url.scheme()` (or equivalent) and bailing with a scheme-specific message. If the intent is to restrict to archive URLs (vs. VCS/path/etc.), update the message to reflect that (e.g., “Only archive URLs are supported”). Also consider including the actual parsed variant/scheme in the error to aid debugging.

Adding `uv-git` as a dependency is fine, but please ensure it’s actually used by `uv-installer` in this PR (or will be in the immediately following hunk). If it’s only needed behind a feature or for specific targets, consider scoping it with `optional = true` and/or a feature flag to avoid pulling git-related deps into all builds unnecessarily. Also run `cargo machete`/`cargo check` to confirm it’s not an unused dependency.

Constructing a new `GitUrl` here may reintroduce the reparsing/normalization work this PR is trying to avoid, and it also allocates (`Box`) on every match. If `RequirementSource::Git` already carries a parsed git URL (or enough structured data), consider storing/passing that through directly (e.g., `git: repository.clone()` if it’s already a `GitUrl`, or `git: GitUrl { .. }` without re-parsing) and avoid boxing unless the `GitSourceDist` type truly requires indirection. Also, if `GitUrl::new` can fail/validate, ensure this path can’t panic or silently change semantics vs the original parsed value.

Storing `git` as `Box::new(GitUrl::new(repository, reference))` is a good step toward preserving parsed URL parts, but please double-check ownership/clone costs here: if `repository`/`reference` are borrowed or cheaply clonable, consider whether `GitUrl::new` should take references (or whether this site should avoid allocating a `Box` if the `GitSourceDist` can store `GitUrl` inline). Also ensure `GitUrl::new` preserves the original/verbatim URL semantics expected elsewhere (e.g., normalization vs. round-tripping), since `url` is still stored separately and divergence between `url` and `git` could become a source of subtle bugs.

This statement is potentially misleading/too broad: “reuse any binary distributions that are already present in the local cache.” If `--no-binary` is meant to restrict *installation* to sdists, clarify whether cached wheels are only reused for metadata extraction (as described in the PR) vs actually installed. Otherwise readers may interpret this as uv installing cached wheels despite `--no-binary`, which would contradict the intent. Consider rewording to explicitly distinguish (a) metadata reads from cached/downloaded wheels and (b) installation behavior, and note any exceptions (e.g., editable/local paths) if applicable.

Passing `self.build_options` into this call changes the behavior of metadata fetching/building based on install-time build options (e.g., `--no-binary`). That matches the PR intent, but please double-check that this is the *right* options struct for this layer: if `build_options` includes install-only flags (like `--no-build-isolation`, `--config-settings`, etc.), it may unintentionally affect metadata-only operations or caching. Consider whether a narrower/explicit "metadata build options" (or a cloned/filtered view) should be passed here, and add a regression test covering `--no-binary` with a custom index where range requests are unsupported (the reported 405 path) to ensure we only download wheels for metadata and still enforce `--no-binary` at install time.

Removing the `--no-binary` guard here matches the PR goal (allow wheel downloads for metadata), but it also means this code path can now download full wheels even when the user explicitly disabled binaries. To avoid regressing the original intent of `--no-binary`, ensure there is a later, install-time enforcement that prevents *installing* from wheels while still permitting wheel downloads strictly for metadata extraction. If that enforcement is conditional, consider adding a comment here clarifying that this function is used for metadata-only and that `--no-binary` is validated elsewhere, plus add/adjust tests to cover: (1) custom index that blocks range requests, (2) `--no-binary :all:` still results in sdist build/install, not wheel install, and (3) error messages remain accurate (i.e., not "Using pre-built wheels is disabled" during metadata fetch).

Removing `Error::NoBinary` changes the public error surface of `uv-distribution`. Please ensure all call sites that previously returned/handled `uv_distribution::Error::NoBinary` are updated to use the new install-time validation error type, and that this doesn’t break downstream crates matching on this variant. If this crate is part of a public API, consider whether a deprecation period (keeping the variant but no longer emitting it) is warranted, and add/adjust tests to assert the new error message/path for `--no-binary` failures.

This check blocks *any* built distribution when `no_binary_package(name)` is true, including cases where we only need to download a wheel to extract metadata (the PR goal). If `get_wheel` is used both for metadata-only and for actual installation/unzip, consider threading an explicit intent/phase (e.g., `WheelPurpose::Metadata | WheelPurpose::Install`) or moving the `--no-binary` enforcement to the point where the wheel would actually be installed/unzipped. Otherwise, this may reintroduce the original failure mode for indexes that require downloading the wheel to read metadata.

Passing `build_options` into the prepare/build pipeline changes behavior for all installs; please double-check that this is the intended place to thread `--no-binary` semantics. In particular, ensure `build_options` is used only to defer/relax the *metadata-fetch* wheel restriction (so wheels can be downloaded for metadata extraction), while still enforcing `--no-binary` at the actual install/build step. It’d be good to add/confirm a regression test covering: custom index that disallows range requests (405) + `--no-binary :all:` where uv must download a wheel to read metadata but ultimately installs from sdist (or errors only if no sdist). Also verify that this doesn’t inadvertently allow wheel installation when `--no-binary` is set for direct URL requirements or when only wheels are available.

Adding `distribution-filename` to the lockfile is fine, but please ensure the corresponding `Cargo.toml` changes are present in this PR (and that the dependency is added only where needed). Also consider whether this should be a workspace dependency to avoid duplicating the same crate across multiple packages (it appears again later in this lockfile).

Extension matching here is case-sensitive (e.g., `Foo.WHL` or `sdist.TAR.GZ` will be rejected), while `is_tar()` uses `eq_ignore_ascii_case("tar")`. Consider normalizing `extension` (e.g., `to_ascii_lowercase()`) or using `eq_ignore_ascii_case` in the match arms for consistency and better UX across platforms/filesystems.

Swallowing the `DistExtension::from_path` error here (`Err(_) => {}`) loses the main benefit of this change: surfacing an invalid/unsupported extension at parse time. Consider either (a) returning a `Result<Option<Self>, ExtensionError>` (or a crate-specific error) so callers can emit a clearer message, or (b) at least logging/tracing the error / mapping it to a higher-level parse error. As-is, this still degrades to `None` with no context, which may keep the confusing UX depending on how `None` is handled upstream.

This slices the filename based on `extension.to_string().len() + 1`, but it never verifies that `filename` actually ends with `.{extension}`. If a caller passes a mismatched `extension` (e.g., `Zip` for `foo.tar.gz`), this will silently truncate the wrong suffix and then produce confusing downstream errors. Consider validating the suffix explicitly (e.g., `let suffix = format!(".{}", extension); let stem = filename.strip_suffix(&suffix).ok_or(Extension)?;`) to ensure correctness and keep the error localized to extension handling.

Renaming `kind: FileKind` to `ext: SourceDistExtension` is a semantic change: `FileKind` likely covered more than just sdist extensions (e.g., wheels vs sdists), while `SourceDistExtension` implies this struct is *only* for source distributions. Please double-check that `DirectSourceUrl` is never constructed/used for wheel URLs or other non-sdist artifacts; otherwise this will either reject valid inputs or force callers to misclassify. If this type is intended to be sdist-only, consider renaming the struct (or adding a doc comment) to make that constraint explicit and prevent future misuse.

Removing `UnknownFileKind(Url)` changes the error surface for unsupported/unknown distribution extensions. Please ensure all call sites that previously returned `Error::UnknownFileKind` are updated to return a new, more specific parse-time error (ideally including the offending URL/filename and the unsupported extension) so users don’t regress to a generic "Failed to extract archive" message. Also verify this is a non-public API or that downstream crates won’t break due to the enum variant removal; if it is public, consider a deprecation path or a replacement variant to preserve compatibility.

Bumping the size limit from 256 to 264 makes the test pass, but it also weakens the guardrail without explaining why the extra bytes are acceptable. Since this PR adds an `ext` field (and likely changes enum layout), consider either (a) adding a short comment here explaining the size increase (e.g., due to `SourceDistExtension`/`DistExtension` storage), or (b) reducing the size impact by boxing/indirecting the new field or reordering fields/variants to minimize padding. Otherwise this test will keep drifting upward over time without a clear rationale.

This wraps `sdist.ext` in `DistExtension::Source(...)`, but it’s not obvious whether `sdist.ext` is already a `DistExtension` (or a `SourceDistExtension`/similar) and whether this conversion is lossless/correct for all source types (e.g., `.tar.gz` vs `.zip`). If `sdist.ext` can ever be `None`/unknown, consider making that explicit here (e.g., `Option<...>` handling) so we don’t reintroduce late failures. Also double-check that `RequirementSource::Url` expects `ext` to reflect the actual archive type used for extraction, not just “source vs wheel”, otherwise downstream extraction/diagnostics may still be confusing.

This changes the heuristic from "can be parsed as a URL" to `looks_like_unnamed_requirement`, which now returns true for any string containing `/` or `\\`. That will likely flag valid *named* requirements that include environment markers with paths/URLs (e.g., `pkg; sys_platform == "win32" and os_name == "nt"` is fine, but `pkg; extra == "foo/bar"` or other marker values containing slashes could be misclassified depending on what `expanded` contains). Consider tightening this check to only apply when the cursor is at the start of the requirement (i.e., before a name is parsed) and/or only when the token being inspected is the leading requirement token, not marker strings. Also, since the comment says `foo/bar`, it might be worth explicitly documenting why path separators imply an unnamed requirement and adding a regression test for the original `.git` URL case plus a case where a slash appears later (markers/extras) to ensure we don't reject valid requirements.

Adding `distribution-filename` as a dependency makes sense for centralizing extension parsing/validation, but please double-check this crate boundary: `pypi-types` is a low-level types crate, so pulling in filename/extension logic can increase coupling and potentially introduce heavier transitive deps. If `distribution-filename` depends on higher-level crates (or brings in extra features), consider keeping the dependency optional or ensuring it stays lightweight and purely parsing-focused. Also ensure the workspace dependency is declared in the root `Cargo.toml` `[workspace.dependencies]` to avoid resolution issues.

Removing the `file_type` re-export/module is fine if the extension parsing logic has been fully moved behind the new `DistExtension` types, but please double-check for downstream/public API breakage: `pypi-types` consumers may have been importing `pypi_types::file_type::*` (or relying on `FileType`/helpers) via this re-export. If this is an intentional breaking change, consider documenting it in the changelog/release notes, or keep a compatibility re-export (possibly deprecated) to avoid surprising external users.

`MissingExtensionUrl`/`MissingExtensionPath` now carry an `ExtensionError`, but the variant name/message still reads like the extension is missing. `DistExtension::from_path` can also fail for reasons other than “missing” (e.g., unsupported/unknown extension). Consider renaming these variants (e.g., `InvalidExtensionUrl` / `InvalidExtensionPath`) and/or adjusting the error text to cover both missing and unsupported cases to avoid misleading diagnostics.

This maps any `DistExtension::from_path` failure into `ParsedUrlError::MissingExtensionUrl`, but `from_path` can fail for reasons other than a missing extension (e.g., unsupported/unknown extension like `.git`). Using a "MissingExtension" variant for all parse errors will keep the error confusing and is semantically incorrect. Consider introducing/using a more general error variant (e.g., `InvalidExtensionUrl` / `UnsupportedExtensionUrl`) and only using `MissingExtensionUrl` when the path truly has no extension. Also, passing `url.to_string()` may lose structured context; if possible, keep the `Url` in the error type (or include both) to avoid stringly-typed errors and preserve formatting.

This snapshot hard-codes the full list of supported extensions in the error message. That makes the test brittle if the supported set changes (or ordering/formatting changes) elsewhere. Consider asserting on a more stable substring (e.g., "end in a supported file extension") or generating the expected list from the same source used by the parser (if available) so the test stays in sync with the implementation.

This snapshot now expects the verbatim URL scheme to be rewritten to `git+https` and the path to include `.git`. That’s a behavior change in how requirements.txt preserves the original input: if the user wrote `https://github.com/...` (without `git+` and without `.git`), we probably shouldn’t mutate the verbatim representation, only the parsed interpretation. Consider keeping `verbatim.url`/`given` exactly as provided and storing the inferred VCS type separately, otherwise downstream formatting/round-tripping and user-facing output may unexpectedly change.

This snapshot now expects the verbatim URL scheme to be rewritten from `https` to `git+https` and the path to include `.git`. Please double-check that this normalization is intentional for requirements.txt parsing: (1) are we allowed to mutate the user-provided URL in the `verbatim` representation, or should `verbatim.given` preserve the original input exactly (e.g., `https://.../pandas-dev/pandas`)? (2) If the input was plain `https://...` without an explicit `git+` prefix, consider whether the parser should classify it as Git while still keeping `verbatim.given` unchanged, to avoid surprising round-trips and to match user expectations.

This test change switches the URL to a VCS requirement (`git+... .git`). That aligns with the issue, but it also changes the semantics of the fixture from a plain direct URL to a VCS URL. Please ensure the parser/test harness for `requirements-txt` explicitly supports `git+https://...` in this context; otherwise this fixture may no longer be exercising whitespace handling for direct references. If the intent is to keep testing direct URL parsing/whitespace, consider adding a separate fixture line for VCS URLs (or keep the original URL and add a new VCS case) so both behaviors are covered.

Bumping the cache bucket version is fine, but this will invalidate existing caches. Please ensure the corresponding test mentioned above (`crates/uv/tests/cache_clean.rs`) is updated in the same PR, and consider whether any other references (docs/changelog or other cache-version assertions) need to be kept in sync. Also double-check that the version bump is necessary for this change set (i.e., the on-disk layout/keys for the `Simple` bucket actually changed) to avoid unnecessary cache churn.

This warning loses the actual parse failure reason from `DistFilename::try_from_filename(...)`, which can make debugging harder (and may reintroduce “confusing error” style messages, just earlier). Consider capturing/logging the error (e.g., `match` on the result and `warn!("Skipping file for {package_name}: {err}")`) and/or include both the filename and the error. If the intent is to avoid noisy logs, at least add a short reason like "unrecognized distribution filename" so users know why it was skipped.

This maps a `DistExtension::from_path` failure into `ParsedUrlError::MissingExtensionUrl`, but the error case here is broader than “missing extension” (e.g., unsupported/unknown extension like `.git`). Consider using a more general `ParsedUrlError` variant (or renaming/adding one) that covers both missing and unsupported extensions, so the resulting message matches the actual failure and avoids reintroducing confusing diagnostics for `.git` URLs.

This changes the cache key from the parsed/normalized `url` (previously produced by `ParsedArchiveUrl::try_from(...)`) to `dist.url` directly. Please double-check that `dist.url` is already normalized in the same way (e.g., fragment stripped, query canonicalization, percent-encoding normalization) so we don't accidentally create multiple cache entries for semantically identical direct URLs. If `dist.url` can include `#subdirectory=` or other fragments, consider explicitly normalizing/stripping those for the cache key (or add a regression test ensuring cache hits remain stable across equivalent URLs).

Adding `distribution-filename` as a dependency makes sense for validating extensions earlier, but please ensure this crate actually needs it (i.e., the new parsing/validation logic lives in `uv-extract` rather than a higher-level crate). If it’s only used for a small enum/parse helper, consider whether the dependency should be optional or whether the logic belongs in an existing shared crate to avoid pulling extra transitive deps into `uv-extract`. Also verify the workspace dependency is declared in the root `Cargo.toml` `[workspace.dependencies]` to prevent CI failures.

Switching `archive` to accept `SourceDistExtension` is a good step for earlier validation, but note that this function previously handled `FileKind::Wheel` by unzipping. With the new signature, wheels can no longer be extracted via this path. If `archive` is still used for wheel downloads anywhere, this will become a compile-time or behavioral regression. Consider either (a) renaming this function to clarify it is *sdist-only* (e.g., `extract_sdist_archive`) and ensuring all call sites are updated accordingly, or (b) widening the enum/type to include wheel/zip-as-wheel cases if this function is intended to remain generic for all distribution archives.

This match arm now accepts any `DistExtension::Source(_)`, which is good for centralizing validation, but it also removes the explicit list of supported sdist archive types that previously lived here. Please double-check that `DistExtension::Source` cannot represent unsupported/unknown extensions (e.g., `.git`, `.zip` without being a valid sdist, etc.) and that the parse-time error message is user-friendly and specific (ideally mentioning the URL and the supported extensions). Otherwise, we may still end up with confusing behavior if an invalid extension slips through or if the error loses context at this layer.

This match arm now ignores `ext` instead of `kind`. If `ext` is intended to be the validated distribution extension, consider whether satisfaction logic should explicitly use it (e.g., to distinguish VCS URLs vs archive URLs, or to ensure the installed dist type matches the requested extension). If it’s truly irrelevant here, it may be worth adding a brief comment explaining why `ext` is intentionally ignored to avoid future confusion, especially since this change is part of “enforce extension validity at parse time”.

Same as above: `RequirementSource::Path` now carries `ext`, but this arm discards it. If `ext` is used elsewhere to validate/normalize path-based requirements, ensure this function doesn’t need to re-check compatibility between the requested path’s extension and the installed distribution. Otherwise, a short comment clarifying that `ext` is validated earlier and not needed for satisfaction would improve maintainability.

Adding `distribution-filename` as a dependency makes sense for upfront extension validation, but please double-check that `uv-python` actually uses it in this crate (or will in this PR). If it’s only needed transitively elsewhere, consider keeping it scoped to the crate that performs parsing to avoid unnecessary dependency bloat. Also ensure the workspace dependency is declared in the root `[workspace.dependencies]` with a compatible version/features so this doesn’t break workspace resolution.

This assumes the URL always has at least one path segment; `path_segments()` can be `None` (e.g., opaque URLs) and `last()` can be `None` for URLs ending with `/`. Since this code now errors specifically on missing/unsupported extensions, consider replacing the `unwrap()` chain with a fallible extraction that returns `Error::MissingExtension` (or a more specific error) when the filename segment is absent, to avoid panics and to produce the intended user-facing message.

Renaming `kind` to `ext` improves clarity, but this match arm now assumes the URL parser always populated a valid `DistExtension`. Please ensure `RequirementSource::Url` cannot be constructed with `ext` missing/unknown (e.g., for `.git` URLs) and that the parse-time error message is user-facing and actionable ("unsupported distribution extension" vs later "failed to extract archive"). Also consider whether `ext` should be passed by value (Copy) rather than `*ext` if the type is small and Copy, to avoid unnecessary indirection.

Relying on `unreachable!()` here makes the resolver panic if parsing ever misclassifies a path URL as `ParsedUrl::Path` while still carrying a `DistExtension::Wheel` (e.g., due to future refactors or edge cases like local `.whl` paths). Since this is user-facing input, it’s safer to either (a) make `ParsedUrl::Path` carry a `SourceDistExtension` type instead of `DistExtension`, or (b) return a structured error when `Wheel` is encountered (or route it to the appropriate `Direct`/wheel handling). This avoids turning a parse/validation bug into a hard crash.

Now that `RegistrySourceDist` stores the extension directly, make sure the type of `filename.extension` matches the field type exactly (e.g., `DistExtension` vs `SourceDistExtension`) and that it’s `Copy`/`Clone` as needed. If `filename.extension` is still a `SourceDistExtension`, consider converting here (or earlier) to the unified extension type to avoid leaking source-dist-specific enums into resolver types. Also verify downstream code that previously relied on `kind: FileKind` (e.g., download/extract paths) has been updated to use `ext` consistently, otherwise this can become a silent behavior change.

The error message says "expected one of: {0}", but `ExtensionError` may represent more cases than just a missing/unknown extension (e.g., unsupported extension, no filename in URL, etc.). Consider either (a) renaming this variant to something broader like `InvalidExtension`/`UnsupportedExtension`, or (b) adjusting the display message to match the actual semantics of `ExtensionError` (and possibly include the offending path/URL for context). As-is, users may still get a confusing message if the failure isn't strictly "missing".

Renaming `kind` to `ext` improves clarity, but this line still dereferences a pointer (`*ext`). If `DistExtension` is `Copy`, consider passing it by value earlier (or cloning explicitly) to avoid implicit deref/copy and make ownership clearer. Also, double-check that `ParsedArchiveUrl::from_source` now expects a validated `DistExtension` (not a broader "kind") so we don't accidentally lose information needed to distinguish archives vs VCS URLs; otherwise `.git` URLs may still be routed through the archive path and produce confusing errors.

Now that `RegistrySourceDist` stores `ext` instead of a derived `FileKind`, make sure all downstream consumers that previously relied on `kind` (e.g., archive extraction / content-type selection / error messages) are updated to use `ext` consistently. In particular, verify that `ext` is guaranteed to be validated at parse time (as per the PR goal) so this field can’t carry an unsupported extension and reintroduce the old “unsupported archive type” failure later. If any code still expects `FileKind`, consider adding a small conversion helper (e.g., `impl From<DistExtension> for FileKind`) to keep call sites simple and avoid duplicated matching elsewhere.

This hard-codes the cache schema/version (`simple-v12`) in the test. If the intent is to track a cache format bump, consider centralizing the version in a shared constant/helper used by both production code and tests (or deriving it from the code under test) to avoid future churn when the cache version increments again. Otherwise, this test will fail on the next schema bump even if behavior is correct.

This test data change makes the URL look like a valid sdist, which avoids the new “validate extension at parse time” error path. However, `https://example.org/does/not/exist.tar.gz` will still fail later if the test ever tries to fetch it (networking, 404, etc.). If the intent is to keep this dependency in a `dont_install_me` extra and ensure it never gets resolved/fetched, consider adding an assertion (or structuring the test) that guarantees the extra is not selected, so the test doesn’t become flaky if resolution behavior changes. Alternatively, use a clearly local/fixture URL (file://) if the code path ever needs to touch it.

This change makes the test input look like a valid sdist URL, which avoids exercising the original “missing extension” parse-time validation. If the intent is to keep coverage for the no-extension error case (and prevent regressions for the reported confusing `.git`/URL handling), consider keeping the original `.../does/not/exist` entry and asserting the new parse-time error message, or add a separate test that specifically covers a direct URL without an extension in `tool.uv.sources` (distinct from the new `no_extension` test which uses `pip_install`). Otherwise this test now only verifies the happy path and may no longer protect the behavior that motivated the change.

Potential schema/semantics ambiguity: the key name `supported-markers` is pretty generic and could be confused with existing `environment-markers` (fork markers) or other marker concepts. Consider a more explicit name like `supported-environment-markers` (or `supported-lock-markers`) to make it clear these are the user-specified supported environments used to constrain universal resolution. Also, since you’re serializing via `filter_map(MarkerTree::contents)`, any `MarkerTree` without `contents` will be silently dropped; if that can happen, it may be better to either serialize the full `MarkerTree` (like `fork_markers` does) or at least assert/log when `contents` is `None` to avoid losing data unexpectedly.

Using `expect("A non-forking resolution exists in forking mode")` makes this path panic on inconsistent state. If this can be triggered by user input (e.g., supported environments filtering out all forks or producing mixed fork/non-fork resolutions), consider returning a structured error instead of panicking, or at least include more context (e.g., number of resolutions / marker mode) in the message. Also, the message reads inverted: you’re expecting a *forking* resolution to exist, not a non-forking one.

This snapshot now expects `supported_environments: []`. If an empty list is meant to represent “no constraint / all environments supported”, consider whether serializing it explicitly is desirable for snapshot stability and backward compatibility. If the field is optional in the lock format, omitting it when empty (or using `Option` with `skip_serializing_if`) can reduce churn across snapshots and avoid implying a semantic difference between “unset” vs “set to empty”. If empty has distinct meaning, it may be worth adding a brief comment in the lock schema/docs and ensuring older lockfiles (without the field) deserialize to the same behavior.

This snapshot now asserts `supported_environments: []`. If `supported_environments` is intended to be an optional/omitted field when not configured, consider whether serializing it as an explicit empty list is desirable. Emitting `[]` can create noisy diffs and may lock in a representation that complicates backward/forward compatibility (e.g., older lockfile readers, or future changes where `None` vs `[]` have different semantics). If the field is semantically "unspecified" here, prefer omitting it from the serialized form and updating snapshots accordingly; if `[]` is the canonical default meaning "no constraints", add a brief comment in the lock schema/docs and ensure deserialization treats missing and empty equivalently.

This snapshot now expects `supported_environments: []`. If an empty list is meant to represent the default "no constraint" behavior, consider whether the serialized form should omit the field (or use `None`) to preserve backward compatibility and avoid churn in snapshots/lockfiles. This snapshot now includes `supported_environments: []`. Please confirm the intended semantics of an empty list: does it mean "no constraints" (i.e., all environments supported) or "supports nothing"? If it's the former, consider making that explicit in the serialized form (or docs) to avoid ambiguity, and ensure the resolver treats `[]` equivalently to `None`/omitted for backwards compatibility with older lockfiles. If it's the latter, this could accidentally make existing locks unusable when the field is introduced.

This snapshot now includes `supported_environments: []`. Please confirm the intended semantics of an empty list: does it mean "no constraints / all environments supported" (likely), or "supports nothing"? If it's the former, consider documenting/encoding that explicitly (e.g., `None` vs `[]` in the serialized form) to avoid ambiguity and future regressions where an empty list could be misinterpreted. Also ensure other snapshots/tests cover the non-empty case so this field’s behavior is exercised beyond the default.

This snapshot now includes `supported_environments: []`. If it's the former, consider documenting that in the lock format and/or ensuring serialization uses `#[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")]` (or equivalent) to avoid churn in snapshots/lockfiles when the field is unset. If it's the latter, this would be a breaking change and should be validated/guarded in parsing and tests.

This snapshot now expects `supported_environments: []`. If an empty list is meant to represent "no constraint" (i.e., all environments supported), consider whether serializing it explicitly is desirable vs omitting the field (or using `None`) to preserve backward compatibility and reduce snapshot churn. If `[]` instead means "supports nothing", that would be a functional bug—please confirm the semantics and ensure the default is unambiguous in both the lock format and resolver logic.

This snapshot now expects `supported_environments: []`. If an empty list is intended to mean "no constraint" (i.e., resolve for all environments), consider whether serializing it explicitly is desirable vs omitting the field (or using `Option`) to keep older lockfiles/snapshots stable and reduce churn. If `[]` instead means "supports nothing", that would be a behavioral footgun—ensure the semantics are clearly documented and covered by tests (e.g., `None`/missing => unconstrained; non-empty => constrained).

This snapshot now expects `supported_environments: []`.
