This snapshot now asserts `supported_environments: []`. If `[]` is intended to mean "no constraint / all environments supported", consider whether the serialized form should omit the field (or use `None`) to preserve backward compatibility and avoid churn in snapshots/lockfiles. If `[]` instead means "supports nothing", this would be a behavioral footgun—please ensure the semantics are unambiguous and covered by tests (e.g., round-trip serialization and resolution behavior when the user does not specify supported environments).

This adds an `environments` field that is silently ignored during deserialization (`IgnoredAny`) and never serialized. Given the PR goal (“Allow user to constrain supported lock environments”), this looks like a placeholder to accept the key without error, but it also means user-provided `environments` will have no effect unless it’s parsed elsewhere. If this is intentional for backward/forward compatibility, consider adding a comment explaining why it’s ignored here and where it’s actually handled. Otherwise, you likely want a typed field (e.g., `Vec<String>` / marker expression type) so the setting can be consumed, and ensure it’s included in the schemars schema (right now it’s skipped) so users can discover it.

Serialization currently skips markers whose `contents()` is `None`, which can silently drop user-provided supported environments and change semantics (e.g., an "empty"/"any" marker might be intended to mean "no constraint"). Consider serializing a stable representation for all `MarkerTree`s (e.g., serialize an empty string or a canonical form), or explicitly error if a marker cannot be represented as a string so the user doesn't lose configuration on round-trip.

Re-exporting `SupportedEnvironments` from the crate root is fine, but please ensure this is part of the intended public API surface. If it’s only needed internally by other crates in the workspace, consider using `pub(crate)` re-exports or exposing it through a more specific module path to avoid committing to a long-term stable API. Also verify `SupportedEnvironments` has appropriate docs since it’s now publicly reachable from `uv_workspace::SupportedEnvironments`.

`environments` is declared as `Option<SupportedEnvironments>` but the `#[option]` metadata sets `default = r#"[]"#`. If the default is an empty list, this will likely deserialize to `Some(...)` (or fail) rather than `None`, which can change semantics (e.g., treating "no config provided" vs "user explicitly provided empty"). Consider either (a) making this field non-optional (`SupportedEnvironments`) if you always want a default, or (b) removing the default and letting it be `None` when unspecified, and handling the defaulting logic in code. Also ensure `SupportedEnvironments` supports both `str` and `list[str]` as advertised by `value_type = "str | list[str]"`.

This method finds the workspace package by scanning `self.packages.values()` and matching `root() == self.install_path()`. That’s potentially O(n) per call and also relies on path equality semantics (normalization/symlinks) being consistent between `root()` and `install_path()`. If this accessor is called frequently during resolution, consider caching the workspace package id at construction time (or storing a direct reference/index), and/or using a more robust comparison (e.g., normalized/absolutized paths) to avoid surprising `None` when paths differ only by normalization.

This message is printed via `writeln!(printer.stderr(), ...)` but the result is intentionally ignored (`let _ =`). That can silently drop the warning if stderr is unavailable, and it also bypasses the existing warning infrastructure used elsewhere in this file (`warn_user` / `warn_user_once`). Consider using `warn_user` (or at least `writeln!(...)?` / `.ok()` with a comment) so the user reliably sees why the lockfile was ignored, and so output formatting/verbosity is consistent across commands.

Consider using a structured type instead of `String` for the supported-environments/marker expression (e.g., a `MarkerTree`/`MarkerExpression`/`SupportedEnvironments` type, or at least `Box<str>`/`Cow<'static, str>`). A raw `String` makes it easy to lose context (what exactly was incompatible, which environment was evaluated, etc.) and can lead to inconsistent formatting across call sites. If you keep `String`, it may be worth including more actionable detail in the error (e.g., the current platform triple/markers and the configured supported environments) so users can diagnose why the lock is rejected.

Potential marker-evaluation mismatch: `env.evaluate(platform, &[])` assumes the marker environment is fully represented by `venv.interpreter().markers()`. If `supported_environments` can include markers that depend on extras (or other context beyond interpreter markers), passing an empty extras slice may incorrectly reject a valid environment. Consider either (a) documenting/enforcing that `supported_environments` must not reference `extra`, or (b) evaluating with the relevant extras context (if any) or using a dedicated evaluation helper that matches how markers are interpreted elsewhere in sync/lock install paths.

This uses `unwrap()` in a test, which will panic without context if the lockfile isn't written (or path changes). Prefer propagating the error with `?` (since the test returns `Result<()>`) or at least `expect("failed to read uv.lock")` to make failures actionable. Using `?` also keeps the test consistent with the rest of the function's error handling.

This test hard-codes an environment marker that assumes the test runner is on Python >= 3.11 (since `TestContext::new("3.12")`), but the failure message later asserts `python_full_version < '3.11'`. Two concerns: (1) the marker in `pyproject.toml` uses `python_version` while the expected error prints `python_full_version`, which may be an implementation detail and could make the test brittle if formatting/normalization changes; consider aligning the input marker with the expected output (use `python_full_version` in the config) or relax the assertion to match the semantic constraint rather than the exact string. (2) If the test suite is ever run under a different interpreter (or `TestContext` doesn’t fully control the interpreter used by `uv sync`), this could become flaky; consider explicitly ensuring the sync uses the intended Python or choosing a marker that is guaranteed to be incompatible with the test environment.

The documented default for `environments` is `[]`, but the text says "By default, uv will resolve for all possible environments". An empty list can be interpreted as "no environments" rather than "all" depending on implementation. Consider clarifying the semantics explicitly (e.g., "Unset or empty means all environments") and/or documenting the actual default as `null`/omitted if that's what triggers universal resolution.

The description/examples here are a bit misleading given the issue/PR intent (constraining *lock environments* / supported platforms). `python_version >= '3.6'` is a valid marker, but users will likely want examples like `platform_system != 'Windows'` or `sys_platform == 'linux'`. Consider updating the description to clarify these are PEP 508 environment marker expressions used to constrain universal resolution, and (optionally) document whether multiple entries are OR'ed vs AND'ed. Also consider adding `minItems: 1` (if empty is invalid) and/or `uniqueItems: true` to avoid duplicates.

Typo/grammar in the error message: "Workspace does contain any buildable packages" reads incorrectly (and is confusing for users). It should likely be "Workspace does not contain any buildable packages" (or "Workspace contains no buildable packages"). Also consider whether this should say "workspace" (lowercase) for consistency with other messages, and whether the example should mention the actual backend detected/expected rather than hard-coding setuptools.

Typo/grammar in the expected error message: "Workspace does contain any buildable packages" reads incorrectly (it implies the opposite). This should likely be "Workspace does not contain any buildable packages" (or "Workspace doesn't contain..."). If the implementation intentionally uses this wording, consider rephrasing for clarity; otherwise fix the message and update the snapshot accordingly.

This heredoc is passed to the guest shell, but the string uses single quotes around `x#{...}` so Ruby interpolation will not occur and the guest will literally see `x#{self.class...}`. That will make the ID comparison always fail. Consider interpolating on the Ruby side (e.g., `id = self.class::GUEST_DETECTION_NAME` and then `test "x#{id}" = "x$ID"`) or avoid Ruby interpolation entirely by hardcoding `openwrt` here. Also note `source` may not exist in BusyBox `ash` (use `. /etc/os-release` for POSIX compatibility).

This snapshot hard-codes the combined condition string "development|production". That value is a bit opaque and may be brittle if Vite changes how it represents mode-dependent conditions (e.g., splitting into separate entries or using a different delimiter). Consider asserting the presence of the key conditions (e.g., contains "module" and "browser" for client, "node" for ssr) and separately asserting that a mode-related condition exists, rather than matching the exact serialized form. This will keep the test focused on the regression (resolve.conditions matching the corresponding environment) while reducing churn from unrelated formatting/representation changes.

This change makes the top-level `resolve` defaults behave like the client environment when `consumer` is `undefined`, which matches the PR intent. However, please double-check that `consumer === undefined` is only used for the *top-level* resolve options and not for other call sites where `consumer` might be accidentally omitted—otherwise server-like contexts could silently start using client `mainFields`/`conditions` (notably including `browser`). If there are multiple call paths, consider making the top-level case explicit (e.g. a separate boolean/enum like `isTopLevelResolve`) or ensure all non-top-level invocations always pass `'client' | 'server'` to avoid regressions.

This test name is a bit too generic and doesn’t clearly tie to the reported failure mode (import scan / optimizeDeps pre-bundling failing due to duplicate declarations across multiple `<script type="module">` blocks in HTML). Consider renaming to something like `optimizeDeps scan tolerates duplicate top-level bindings across multiple html module scripts` and/or add a brief comment asserting the intent (dev server should start and page should render). Also ensure the corresponding fixture HTML actually contains multiple module scripts reusing the same identifier; otherwise this test may pass without covering the regression.

This test case is meant to validate that separate `<script type="module">` blocks don’t share scope during the import scan, but using a top-level `const` without any side effects can be tree-shaken/ignored by some transforms and may not reliably exercise the bug. Consider making the first module script have an observable side effect (e.g. `window.__reusedName = 1` or `console.log(reusedName)`), or at least reference `reusedName` so the scanner/bundler must parse and keep the declaration. That will make the playground assertion more robust and less dependent on implementation details of the scan/build pipeline.

Using a global incrementing `scriptId` to build the virtual module key can be non-deterministic across rebuilds/watch mode and across concurrent scans, which may lead to unstable module IDs (hurting caching/HMR consistency) and potential collisions if the plugin instance is reused. Consider deriving a stable key from the script’s position (e.g., index in the extracted script list / start offset) and the file path, or resetting `scriptId` per `path` (e.g., `const key = `${path}?script=${i}`) so the same HTML produces the same virtual module IDs every time.

Skipping this test reduces coverage for the regression this PR is addressing (avoiding `import.meta.url` in non-ESM outputs). If the inline-worker path is currently broken, consider either (a) updating the assertion to match the new expected behavior for inline workers, or (b) splitting into two tests: keep a non-inline case that asserts no `import.meta.url` appears in legacy/non-ESM output, and add a separate TODO/expected-fail test for the inline-assets behavior. At minimum, add a link to an issue/PR tracking the inline-assets gap so this skip doesn’t become permanent.

This comment is helpful but a bit ambiguous about where the `n_head_kv` logic is actually applied. In the code below, the condition only checks `multi_query` and `not new_decoder_architecture`, but it doesn’t show the corresponding branch that uses `n_head_kv` when `new_decoder_architecture` is True. Consider either (a) updating the comment to explicitly reference the exact branch/return path where `n_head_kv` is used, or (b) adding/adjusting the conditional logic so the behavior described here is enforced in this function. Otherwise, future readers may assume `n_head_kv` is handled here when it might be handled elsewhere (or not at all).

This changes the ALiBi bias construction from HF’s repeated row vector to a pairwise difference matrix. Even if results match for your tested models, this is a behavioral change that could affect other architectures/attention backends (e.g., sign convention, broadcasting expectations, dtype). Consider (1) adding a brief rationale + reference (ALiBi paper equation) and clarifying the sign/direction (i-j vs j-i), (2) ensuring the resulting bias dtype matches what downstream expects (often float) to avoid implicit casts, and (3) adding/keeping a small unit test comparing against HF for a few prompt lengths to prevent regressions.

Gating the reduce on `self.world_size > 1` is a good optimization, but please ensure `self.world_size` is always defined for this module (including any alternative init paths / subclasses). Also consider whether `get_tensor_model_parallel_world_size()` can change at runtime (e.g., re-init of parallel state); caching it in `__init__` could become stale. If dynamic changes are possible, prefer querying the world size in `forward()` or add an assertion that parallel state is initialized and stable. Finally, if `reduce_results` is used to enforce a specific collective semantics even in single-rank runs (e.g., for consistency/testing), this change alters behavior; add a brief comment or test to document the intended semantics.

These inline comments are helpful, but they hardcode specific model names and may become stale as HF config/model_type mappings evolve. Consider either (a) making the comment more general (e.g., "Falcon RW configs") or (b) adding a short note/link to the upstream Transformers config/model_type values that require these keys. Also verify that both keys are still needed across Falcon variants; if only one is required, keeping both could mask misconfigurations.

Same concern as above: tying this mapping to specific repos/variants can drift over time. If the intent is to support Falcon configs where `model_type` is `RefinedWebModel`, it may be clearer to mention the actual HF `model_type`/class name relationship rather than specific checkpoints. If there are known differences between 7B and 40B that require separate keys, a brief explanation would help future maintainers.

The comment says newer Falcon models use the official HF `FalconConfig`, but this module only imports/exports `RWConfig`. If vLLM relies on this package to register/resolve supported config classes, consider also importing and adding `FalconConfig` (guarded by a try/except for older `transformers` versions) or clarify in the comment where `FalconConfig` is handled. Otherwise this can be misleading and may cause missing support depending on how config dispatch is implemented.

Calling `/sbin/ldconfig` unconditionally is brittle: it may not exist in minimal containers, may not be executable, and can fail on some distros where `ldconfig` lives elsewhere (e.g., `/usr/sbin/ldconfig`) or where the user lacks permission. Consider using `shutil.which("ldconfig")` (and/or checking common paths), and handle `FileNotFoundError`/`CalledProcessError` by falling back to `ctypes.util.find_library`, `ldconfig -p` parsing only when available, and/or searching `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`/known library dirs. This will avoid raising during import/init on systems without ldconfig.

Switching from FlagInitialModulesAsUsedPlugin to FlagAllModulesAsUsedPlugin changes the semantics significantly (now *all* modules in the compilation are marked as used). That likely fixes the tree-shaking/removal issue for DLL builds, but it can also increase DLL size and potentially affect side-effect pruning. Please ensure this is intentionally gated only by `!entryOnly` (as here) and consider adding/confirming a regression test that demonstrates the original runtime failure (e.g., a module only referenced via DLL manifest/exports getting dropped in production). Also double-check that the plugin is applied early enough relative to optimization passes that rely on `usedExports`/`sideEffects` so the marking actually prevents elimination.

Directly mutating `module.used` is relying on internal/legacy module state and may not be sufficient/compatible across module types (e.g. Harmony/ConcatenatedModule) or future webpack internals. Prefer using the module graph APIs to mark usage (e.g. `compilation.moduleGraph` / `setUsedExports`-style mechanisms) or at least guard this with feature checks. Also consider whether setting `usedExports = true` is correct vs. indicating "all exports used" (often `true` means "unknown"), and ensure this doesn’t interfere with tree-shaking semantics outside of DLL builds.

This condition is a bit too broad: `!module.used` will also be true when `module.used` is `0` (or any other falsy value), which could unintentionally overwrite a meaningful state. If the intent is to only initialize when the property is unset, consider checking explicitly for `undefined` (or `null`) instead, e.g. `if (module.used === undefined) module.used = false;` (or `if (module.used == null) ...`). That better preserves any pre-existing `used` state set by other compilation steps/plugins.

The added file appears to include line-number prefixes ("1:", "2:", "3:") as literal source text. If this is meant to be executed by webpack in the test case, those prefixes will make the file invalid JavaScript and could cause the test to fail for the wrong reason. Please remove the prefixes so the file contains only valid JS (e.g., `export default function createB() { return "b"; }`). Also consider matching the repo's formatting (spaces vs tabs) to avoid lint/style noise in snapshots.

This file appears to include line-number prefixes ("1:", "2:", "3:") as literal text, which would make the JS invalid and cause the test to fail to parse. Please remove the prefixes and keep only the actual code. Also align indentation with the rest of the test fixtures (no leading spaces, consistent tabs/spaces).

The file content appears to include prefixed line numbers/indentation (e.g. `           1:`) as literal code. If the prefixes are intentional for a harness, consider using a different fixture format or add a comment explaining why this file is not parsed as JS.

This re-export is fine, but for a regression test around DllPlugin + side effects/tree-shaking it may be too “pure” unless `createB` has observable side effects or is actually consumed by the test entry. Consider ensuring the test asserts that this export is retained/usable at runtime (e.g., by calling `b()` from the consuming bundle) or add a comment/fixture behavior that makes it clear why this re-export matters for the bug (modules being removed before being flagged as used). Otherwise the test could pass even if the underlying issue regresses because the export is never referenced.

This line appears to have an unintended leading indentation and a stray `1:` label, which makes the file invalid JS (or at least very confusing) and likely not what the test runner expects for `test.config.js`. If the intent is to disable tests for this config case, it should just be `exports.noTests = true;` (no label, no extra whitespace). Please remove the `1:` prefix and normalize formatting so the config is parsed correctly.

The `test` regex uses an unescaped `.` before `(module|dependency)`, so it will match any character (e.g. `0-create-dllXmodule`) rather than a literal dot. If the intent is to target files like `0-create-dll.module` / `0-create-dll.dependency`, escape the dot and consider anchoring to avoid accidental matches (e.g. `/0-create-dll\.(module|dependency)$/`). This makes the test more precise and reduces the chance of the rule applying to unintended modules in the fixture.

The file content appears to include diff/annotation prefixes (e.g., `1:`) and leading indentation before `it(...)`. In webpack's test fixtures this will be executed as JavaScript, so these prefixes will cause a syntax error. Please remove the `N:` markers and normalize indentation so the file is valid JS (e.g., start with `it("should include...") { ... }`).

The `name` option in `DllReferencePlugin` should match the `output.library` (or `DllPlugin` `name`) used when creating the DLL, not a file path. Using a relative path like `"../0-create-dll/dll.js"` is brittle and can break when the test is executed from a different CWD or when the DLL config changes. Consider setting this to the actual library name exported by the DLL (e.g. `"dll"` / `"dll_[hash]"` depending on the fixture) and rely on the manifest for mapping, or add a brief comment explaining why a path is required here and how it stays in sync with the DLL build.

Enabling `usedExports` here changes the test setup to explicitly exercise tree-shaking in the DLL build, which matches the reported production-only failure. However, this option is mode-dependent in real configs (typically enabled in production). To keep the test intention clear and avoid accidental behavior changes if the test mode/config defaults change, consider either (a) adding a short comment explaining why `usedExports` must be forced on for this case, or (b) setting `mode: "production"` for this config (if not already) and relying on defaults. Also ensure the corresponding assertions cover the regression (modules not being dropped before being marked used), otherwise this could become a no-op config tweak.

The added line appears to include prefixed line-number text (`1:`) and leading indentation, which would make this file invalid JavaScript and likely break the test case parsing/execution. Test fixtures should contain plain JS source without embedded line numbers. Please change this to `import { constants } from "test-package";` (no prefix) and ensure formatting matches other configCases fixtures.

This line appears to include a prefixed `1:` (likely copied from a numbered diff/view) which would make the file invalid JavaScript and break the test fixture. The file should contain only the ES module export statement without any line-number prefix/indentation. Please remove the `1:` (and similarly on the next line) so the fixture parses correctly.

This re-exports the entire namespace object as a named export. That’s fine, but for a regression test targeting DllPlugin/tree-shaking it may be too “used” because line 5 logs `constants`, which forces the binding to be referenced and can mask the original issue (modules being removed before being flagged as used). Consider removing the `console.log(constants)` side effect (or moving usage into the actual test entry) so the test specifically validates that the re-exported namespace survives even when it’s only re-exported (not directly referenced) in the package entry.

This line appears to include the diff viewer’s line-number prefix (`1:`) as literal source code, and it’s also indented with leading whitespace. This line (and the rest of the file) appears to include prefixed line numbers/indentation (e.g. `           1:`) as literal source code. This line looks like it accidentally includes a leading "1:" (likely copied from a numbered diff/context) and excessive indentation, which would make the file invalid JS and break the test harness. This test config sets `libraryTarget: "commonjs2"` but doesn’t specify an explicit `output.library` name. For `DllPlugin`/`DllReferencePlugin` scenarios, the manifest typically needs a stable library identifier to reference at runtime; relying on defaults can make the test less representative and potentially brittle across changes. Consider adding `output.library` (and, if needed, `DllPlugin`'s `name` option) to ensure the manifest and runtime reference are deterministic for this case.

This test currently only asserts that requiring the DLL entry doesn’t throw, but it doesn’t actually verify the reported regression (“all modules are flagged as used” / no missing exports at runtime). Consider adding an explicit assertion that a known module from the DLL (ideally one that was previously tree-shaken/removed) is present/usable (e.g., require a specific export and assert its type/value). Also, the indentation here looks inconsistent with the rest of the test suite (leading tab/spaces); please align formatting to the project’s standard to avoid lint/style noise.

Using a relative path string for `name` is a bit brittle in tests because it depends on the current working directory and path separator behavior. Consider using an absolute path (e.g., `path.resolve(__dirname, "../0-issue-10475/dll.js")`) or at least `path.join` to make the test more robust across environments. Also double-check that this value matches the `output.library` used when generating the referenced DLL manifest; otherwise the reference can silently mismatch.

Adding /*#__PURE__*/ here is a good hint for tree-shaking, but please double-check that `mixin2(Y)` is actually side-effect free in this test case. If `mixin2` can have side effects (e.g., mutating `Y`, registering globals, etc.), marking it pure could allow optimizers/minifiers to drop or reorder it incorrectly. If the intent is specifically to assert webpack emits the pure annotation for extends expressions, consider adding/adjusting an assertion in the test to cover this behavior explicitly so the annotation doesn’t become a silent semantic change.

The new JSDoc type was changed to `/** @type {TODO[]} */`, which looks like a placeholder and removes useful type information. This should be a concrete union type matching `expr.body.body` for class bodies (e.g. `(MethodDefinition | PropertyDefinition | StaticBlock)[]`), consistent with the hook typings above. Leaving `TODO` here will degrade editor/TS checking and can hide real type errors (e.g. accessing `item.value` on `StaticBlock`).

This assertion expects the selection to move to the *directory row* ("dir2") after deleting the last file in dir1. That’s a reasonable UX choice, but it’s also a bit brittle: depending on how the panel computes the “next selectable entry”, it might instead select the next *leaf* entry (e.g. the first child under dir2 if it’s expanded) or even the parent (dir1/root) if collapsing occurs. Consider tightening the test to assert the intended selection policy explicitly (e.g. “next visible entry in the flattened list, skipping deleted subtree”) and/or add a comment explaining why selecting the directory header (not its first child) is the desired behavior. Otherwise this test may fail with harmless UI changes (like auto-expanding/collapsing or different selection rules for expanded dirs).

This `#endif` appears to close the previous conditional block (likely `#if XCHAL_HAVE_LOOPS`), but the indentation/placement makes it easy to misread as part of the restore sequence. Please double-check that the preprocessor nesting is correct after this change (i.e., that the loop-register restore remains guarded by the intended `#if`, and that the new `#if XCHAL_HAVE_S32C1I` block is not accidentally nested/paired incorrectly). Consider adding a short comment like `/* end XCHAL_HAVE_LOOPS */` on the `#endif` to prevent future mismatches.

This offset math is hard to reason about and easy to break when the base save area layout changes. Since SCOMPARE1 is conceptually an optional “tail” field, consider defining it more directly/robustly, e.g. `#define BSA_SCOMPARE1_OFF (BASE_SAVE_AREA_SIZE - 60)` (with a compile-time check that `BASE_SAVE_AREA_SIZE` matches the documented layout), or compute it as `BASE_SAVE_AREA_SIZE - BASE_SAVE_AREA_SIZE_SCOMPARE` (if it’s always last). Also, this macro is currently defined unconditionally; if `XCHAL_HAVE_S32C1I` is false, `BASE_SAVE_AREA_SIZE_SCOMPARE` is 0 and this offset still resolves to a valid-looking value even though the slot doesn’t exist—guard the definition with `#if XCHAL_HAVE_S32C1I` (or ensure all uses are similarly guarded) to avoid accidental save/restore into the wrong stack location on cores without S32C1I.

Saving SCOMPARE1 here makes sense, but please double-check the feature guard: SCOMPARE1 is used by the S32C1I atomic sequence, so gating on XCHAL_HAVE_S32C1I is reasonable, but some Xtensa configs may expose SCOMPARE1 even when S32C1I is absent/present under a different macro. If there is a more direct capability macro for SCOMPARE1 (or for atomic CAS support) in this codebase/toolchain headers, prefer that to avoid silently skipping save/restore on affected cores. Also ensure BSA_SCOMPARE1_OFF is defined/allocated in the stack frame layout under the same condition so offsets don’t diverge between save/restore paths.

This relies on comparing the current PSP against `priv_stack_start` to infer which stack is active. Two concerns: (1) the comment says the priv stack is in a higher memory area than the default stack, but the code only checks `PSP >= priv_stack_start` and does not verify an upper bound (e.g., within priv stack range). If PSP is temporarily 0/invalid during transitions (e.g., userspace enter sequence) or if stacks are not strictly ordered in some configurations, this heuristic could mis-detect and set PSPLIM to the wrong region. Consider using explicit stack bounds (start/end) for the privileged stack and check `PSP` is within that range, or track the active stack via a thread/arch flag updated atomically during stack transitions. Also, ensure `__get_PSP()` is safe to call here w.r.t. preemption/interrupts; if PSP can change concurrently, you may need to guard this with a lock/irq disable to avoid racing and programming PSPLIM based on a stale PSP value.

Returning 0 on a received WebSocket CLOSE frame is ambiguous: in many read APIs, 0 means EOF/peer closed, but in MQTT this may be interpreted as a clean transport shutdown rather than an error, potentially skipping proper disconnect/error handling. Consider returning a distinct error (e.g., -ENOTCONN / -ECONNRESET) or explicitly triggering the MQTT disconnect path so the client state machine can react deterministically. Also, if the close code/reason is available, it may be useful to propagate/log it for diagnostics.

This hard-codes the response path to the *UART backend* returned by `shell_backend_uart_get_ptr()`, which may not be the shell instance that received the SMP request (e.g., multiple shells/backends, non-UART backends like RTT/USB CDC via different backend, or a shell running on a different UART). It also ignores the `arg` parameter that could carry the correct shell/transport context. Consider using the shell API to write to the originating shell (e.g., pass `const struct shell *` via `arg` and use `shell_fprintf`/`shell_write`/`shell_print_stream`), or otherwise select the backend/device from `arg` rather than a global getter. Also, `uart_poll_out()` is a busy-wait per byte; for larger SMP responses this can block the system—prefer `uart_tx()`/async or buffered write if available, or at least document/limit usage. Finally, add null checks for `su`, `su->ctrl_blk`, and `scb->dev` to avoid potential NULL deref during early init or if UART backend is not enabled.

CMake `if()` expects a variable name/expression, not a dereferenced value. Using `if (${CONFIG_TLS_CREDENTIALS})` can break when the symbol is unset/empty (it expands to `if ()`) or if it expands to a non-boolean string. Prefer `if(CONFIG_TLS_CREDENTIALS)` (or `if(DEFINED CONFIG_TLS_CREDENTIALS AND CONFIG_TLS_CREDENTIALS)`) to make the condition robust across configurations.

Enabling newlib for this test may unnecessarily restrict the set of boards/platforms that can run it (many Zephyr tests use minimal libc). If the test doesn’t require newlib-specific behavior, consider dropping this or switching to CONFIG_MINIMAL_LIBC=y to improve CI coverage and reduce footprint.

The client certificate is being added with the wrong credential type. `TLS_CREDENTIAL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE` should be `TLS_CREDENTIAL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE` for the client cert; otherwise the TLS stack may not present the cert during mutual auth and can lead to handshake failures (especially for TLS_PEER_VERIFY_REQUIRED). Please change the type here to the client-specific one to match the intent and the server-side verification modes being tested.

The YAML indentation/structure here looks incorrect: the top-level key should not be indented, and line-number prefixes ("1:", "2:", etc.) should not be part of the file content. This will likely break Twister parsing. Please format as standard Zephyr testcase.yaml, e.g.:

common:
  tags: net socket tls
  filter: TOOLCHAIN_HAS_NEWLIB == 1
  platform_exclude: ...

tests:
  net.socket.tls:
    min_ram: 65536

Also consider whether the Newlib filter is strictly required for this test (mbedTLS typically works with other C libraries too), or document why it’s needed.

The help text says this option enforces returning "Command Disallowed" when enabling/disabling scanning that is already enabled/disabled, but the PR description/BT spec behavior indicates the opposite (re-enabling should be allowed and apply Filter_Duplicates changes; disabling when already disabled should be a no-op). Please rename the symbol and prompt (e.g., "BT_CTLR_SCAN_ENABLE_LENIENT" / "Allow redundant scan enable/disable") or fix the help text to match the intended behavior. As written, this is likely to confuse users and may lead to selecting the wrong behavior.

This change maps `BT_HCI_ERR_CMD_DISALLOWED` to `SUCCESS` when `CONFIG_BT_CTLR_SCAN_ENABLE_STRICT` is disabled. That matches the PR description for “disable when already disabled”, but it may be too broad: `CMD_DISALLOWED` can also be returned for other reasons (e.g., invalid state due to ongoing procedures), and silently converting those to success could mask real failures and make debugging harder. Consider narrowing the condition to only the idempotent cases mandated by the spec (enable when already enabled / disable when already disabled), e.g., by checking current scan state (or having `ll_scan_enable()` return a more specific error) before overriding the status. Also, please add a short comment referencing the spec behavior being implemented so future readers understand why this override is safe.

Storing keepalive as a plain `bool` in `net_context->options` may be insufficient for TCP keepalive support: keepalive typically needs per-connection parameters (idle, interval, count) and defaults. Consider either (a) adding a dedicated TCP options struct (e.g., `struct { bool keepalive; uint32_t keepidle; ... } tcp;`) or (b) clearly documenting where these TCP-specific values are stored (likely in the TCP connection object) and how they are initialized/reset on context reuse. Also, if this is meant to mirror socket semantics, ensure the default is explicitly initialized (false) when contexts are allocated/cleared to avoid stale state across reuses.

Please verify the numeric values for these sockopt constants match Zephyr's existing ABI expectations and common POSIX/Linux values. In Linux, SO_KEEPALIVE is 9 (SOL_SOCKET level), and TCP_KEEPIDLE/TCP_KEEPINTVL/TCP_KEEPCNT are 4/5/6 (IPPROTO_TCP level). Introducing different values can break source/binary compatibility for applications that assume standard values, especially when porting code. If Zephyr intentionally uses its own numbering, consider adding a clear comment explaining the divergence and ensure there are no collisions with other SO_* options in this header.

Enabling keepalive by default can add periodic traffic and wakeups (power impact) and may be unexpected for constrained devices. Consider defaulting this to `n` (or gating with a more specific default like `default y if !NET_L2_IEEE802154` etc.), and ensure the feature is only active when `SO_KEEPALIVE` is set at runtime. At minimum, document the power/traffic implications in the help text.

set_context_keepalive() ignores the provided len and unconditionally dereferences value as an int. This can lead to out-of-bounds reads or unaligned access if callers pass a different size (e.g., uint32_t/bool) or len==0. Please validate `value != NULL` and `len == sizeof(uint32_t)` (or `sizeof(int)` if that’s the API contract) similar to the other setters, and consider using a fixed-width type (uint32_t) for consistency with get_context_keepalive().

Keepalive probes per RFC1122 should use SEG.SEQ = SND.NXT-1 (or SND.UNA-1) and ACK = RCV.NXT, but this code uses `conn->seq - 1` without any guard. If `conn->seq` can be 0 (e.g., just after initialization/wrap), this underflows and will send 0xFFFFFFFF, which is likely wrong. Please ensure the sequence number used for the probe is derived from the correct TCP state variable (e.g., SND.NXT) and handle wrap/zero safely (or use helper macros for seq arithmetic). Also consider whether `conn->seq` is the right field here vs. `conn->seq` meaning next-to-send in this implementation.

Minor style nit: the closing comment should match Zephyr's usual spacing and the opening directive. Consider `#endif /* CONFIG_NET_TCP_KEEPALIVE */` (space after `/*` and before `*/`) for consistency and readability.

Indentation/formatting looks off here (the `if (ret < 0)` block is indented more than the surrounding `case` body). This will likely fail Zephyr's checkpatch/style checks and makes the control flow harder to read. Please align the `if` with `ret = ...` and drop the unreachable `break` after `return 0` (or keep `break` and remove the early return).

The doc comment formatting/indentation is inconsistent and contains tab characters (e.g., the leading whitespace before "This function..." and the tabbed @param alignment). Zephyr generally prefers spaces and consistent Doxygen style. Please reflow this comment to match surrounding blocks (one space after `*`, no tabs), and consider using `@brief` + a short description line without extra indentation. Also, it would help to document expected behavior when suspend/resume are called multiple times (nesting/refcounting) and whether these APIs are thread-safe, since callers may invoke them from different contexts during mcumgr transfers.

Resuming the shell log backend here (on the first non-mcumgr byte) looks risky: it assumes the backend was previously suspended and that `sh`/`sh->log_backend` are valid. If `shell_backend_uart_get_ptr()` can return NULL (or the backend isn't initialized yet), this will dereference NULL. Also, resuming on any non-mcumgr byte may re-enable logging while an SMP transfer is still in progress (e.g., interleaved console traffic), potentially reintroducing the corruption/stall. Consider (1) guarding `sh && sh->log_backend` before calling resume, and (2) tying suspend/resume to explicit SMP session state (e.g., suspend when entering MCUMGR state and resume when leaving it / after TX completes) or using a refcount/flag to avoid unbalanced resume calls.

This directly forces the backend state to DISABLED, duplicating `z_shell_log_backend_disable()` and bypassing any existing disable semantics (e.g., refcounting, synchronization, flushing, or future side effects). If the intent is a temporary suspend during MCUMGR, consider reusing the existing API (call `z_shell_log_backend_disable()` / `z_shell_log_backend_enable()`), or implement suspend/resume with proper nesting (counter) so concurrent users don’t accidentally re-enable a backend that another subsystem still expects disabled. Also, this new function is non-`static` and not `z_`-prefixed like the other exported symbol; ensure it’s declared in the appropriate header and follows the subsystem’s naming/export conventions.

By removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable set, extended advertising reports will no longer be marked discardable at the H4 transport layer. This matches the PR note that dropping "No more to come" fragments can cause unrelated events to be concatenated, but it also changes buffering/backpressure behavior: extended adv reports can be frequent/large and may now contribute to RX queue pressure or latency. Please ensure this is intentional for all controllers and document the rationale here (e.g., comment referencing reassembly requirement), and consider whether periodic advertising reports (and/or other fragmented LE meta subevents) should also be treated similarly to avoid the same class of corruption when reassembly is enabled.

Removing the discard of BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT is necessary for host-side reassembly, but please double-check that this function is only used for events that are safe to drop under buffer pressure. If the ESP32 transport still drops other LE meta events, we may still risk corrupting reassembly (e.g., if periodic adv reports are also used) or starving the RX queue. Consider adding a brief comment here (or near the call site) explaining why extended adv reports must never be discarded (data_status fragmentation/reassembly), and verify whether BT_HCI_EVT_LE_PERIODIC_ADVERTISING_REPORT should also be treated as non-discardable for the same reason.

This change stops treating LE Extended Advertising Report events as discardable/zero-timeout. That aligns with the PR note that dropping extended reports can break host-side reassembly, but it also changes behavior for this transport: extended reports will now be handled like other events (potentially blocking longer / increasing queue pressure). Please confirm this is intentional for STM32WB and that the host reassembly depends on *never* dropping BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT. If so, consider adding a brief comment here explaining why ext adv reports must not be marked discardable, and verify whether periodic advertising reports (BT_HCI_EVT_LE_PERIODIC_ADVERTISING_REPORT) need similar treatment to avoid future regressions when periodic reassembly is added.

Removing the discard of BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT makes sense for host-side reassembly, but please ensure this function’s contract is still correct for all users: if rpmsg transport previously relied on dropping these events for throughput/latency, this change may increase RX load and queue pressure. Consider adding a short comment here (or in the caller) explaining that extended adv reports must not be discarded because the host needs all fragments (data_status) to reassemble, and verify that the RX path has sufficient buffering/backpressure to avoid overruns when extended reports are frequent/large.

This change stops treating LE Extended Advertising Report events as discardable/low-latency (K_NO_WAIT). Given the PR goal (host-side reassembly of extended adv reports), dropping or deprioritizing these events can break reassembly by losing the final fragment and concatenating unrelated fragments. However, please double-check the intended behavior for this transport: if extended adv reports are no longer discardable here, could this increase RX thread blocking/latency under heavy scan traffic? If so, consider adding a comment explaining why ext adv reports must not be discardable, or gating the behavior on whether host reassembly is enabled (e.g., CONFIG_BT_EXT_ADV / CONFIG_BT_EXT_SCAN_BUF_SIZE) to avoid regressions for builds that don’t use reassembly.

Dropping `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable/`K_NO_WAIT` path changes behavior for extended advertising reports: they will now be treated like non-discardable events and may block/queue differently under load. Given the PR note that extended adv fragments must not be dropped (or unrelated fragments get concatenated), this makes sense, but please ensure the reassembly logic in the host can still make progress if the user channel is slow (e.g., avoid unbounded backlog). Also consider whether other fragmentable events (e.g., periodic advertising report) need the same treatment, and add a brief comment here explaining why ext adv reports are intentionally not discardable to prevent future regressions.

Nit: comment grammar/wording. "The reported data is a truncated." reads incorrect/unfinished. Consider "The reported data is truncated." or "The reported data was truncated." Also, since this is a new public API bit, please ensure the doxygen comment clearly states when this flag is set (controller vs host truncation) and whether it can be combined with other properties (e.g., EXT_ADV/SCAN_RESPONSE).

The hard-coded upper range (1650) matches the maximum extended advertising data length, but it would be better to tie this to a named constant/symbol (if available) to avoid spec drift and to document why 1650 is the limit. Also consider whether the lower bound should be 0 or a more realistic minimum (e.g., 255) since values like 1 will lead to almost all reports being truncated and may create confusing behavior. Finally, the prompt/help text says "Maximum advertisement report size"—clarify that this is the host reassembly buffer for *extended* advertising reports (and whether it applies to periodic reports too), and mention memory impact since this is a potentially large per-host allocation.

The truncation math looks wrong here: you compare `evt->length + ext_scan_buf->len` against `net_buf_max_len(ext_scan_buf)`, but then compute `size_to_copy` as `net_buf_max_len(ext_scan_buf) - evt->length`. This ignores the bytes already accumulated in `ext_scan_buf->len` and can underflow (wrap) when `evt->length` is larger than the max, or simply copy too much/too little. It should be based on remaining capacity, e.g. `size_to_copy = net_buf_max_len(ext_scan_buf) - ext_scan_buf->len` (and clamp to 0 if already full). Also consider guarding against `net_buf_add_mem(..., 0)` and ensure `size_to_copy` is wide enough (use `size_t`/`uint16_t`) since extended adv data can exceed 255 bytes across fragments.

This change stops marking LE Extended Advertising Reports as discardable. That may be intentional for reassembly (to avoid dropping fragments), but it also changes memory/backpressure behavior for all extended adv reports on H4: previously they could be dropped under low-memory conditions, now they may be treated as non-discardable and potentially increase RX queue pressure/latency. Please confirm the new intended policy and, if extended adv reports are now handled elsewhere, add a brief comment or adjust the condition to match the new reassembly path (e.g., only mark as non-discardable when `data_status` indicates incomplete/continuation, otherwise keep them discardable). Also consider periodic advertising reports if they are affected by the same rationale.

By removing the discard of BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT, the ESP32 HCI driver will now forward LE Extended Advertising Reports to the host. That’s likely required for the new reassembly logic, but please double-check that the ESP32 controller/transport actually supports and emits this subevent (and that the host is configured to handle it). If ESP32 firmware can still produce extended adv reports in cases the host can’t parse (e.g., missing feature gating), consider guarding this behavior behind the relevant Kconfig/feature check or controller capability detection to avoid unexpected event handling or log spam.

This change stops treating LE Extended Advertising Report events as discardable/low-latency (K_NO_WAIT). With the PR reassembling extended advertising data in the host, dropping these events here can break reassembly (missing fragments) or increase latency/backpressure if the RX thread blocks. Please confirm STM32WB controller/transport never emits BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT, or update the logic to keep extended reports handled appropriately (either still mark them discardable, or ensure they are not dropped elsewhere and that reassembly can tolerate any queuing delays). If the intent is to avoid discarding fragments, consider explicitly documenting that here and ensuring the RX path guarantees delivery/order for extended reports.

By removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable list, extended advertising reports will now be forwarded over rpmsg instead of being dropped. That aligns with the reassembly feature, but please double-check the original reason this was discardable (often to reduce host traffic/CPU). If the intent is to always support extended adv reassembly, consider gating this behavior behind a Kconfig (e.g., only forward when host-side reassembly/extended scanning is enabled) and/or ensure downstream consumers can handle the increased event volume without overruns.

This change stops treating LE Extended Advertising Report events as discardable/low-latency (K_NO_WAIT) in the SPI RX thread. Given the PR goal (reassembling fragmented extended advertising reports), this may be intentional to avoid dropping fragments, but it also changes behavior for controllers that only use extended reports (or use them heavily), potentially increasing RX thread blocking/latency and risking buffer pressure elsewhere. Please confirm the new reassembly path guarantees timely consumption of BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT; if not, consider keeping EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT in this discardable fast-path (or gating it behind a Kconfig/feature flag) and/or add a comment explaining why extended reports must not be discardable here.

This change stops treating LE Extended Advertising Report events as discardable/low-latency (K_NO_WAIT). With the new host-side reassembly, that may be intentional, but it also changes behavior for userchan consumers: extended adv reports may now be queued/back-pressured like other events, potentially increasing latency or causing buffer pressure elsewhere. Please confirm that extended advertising reports are no longer forwarded via userchan (or are always reassembled into a different event) and add a brief comment here explaining why EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT is excluded. If extended reports can still reach this path, consider keeping BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT discardable (or making it conditional on the reassembly Kconfig) to avoid regressions for existing userchan users.

The docstring is a bit unclear/grammatically off ("a truncated") and could be more precise about what this flag means for consumers. Consider rewording to "The reported data is truncated." and explicitly stating whether this indicates truncation after host-side reassembly (i.e., final delivered payload is incomplete) vs. truncation of a single HCI fragment. Also, since this is a new public API bit, please ensure it’s documented how it maps to HCI Data_Status values (e.g., "incomplete"/"truncated"), and whether it can be set for both legacy and extended reports.

The `range 1 1650` hard-codes the maximum to 1650 bytes, but the controller’s supported maximum extended advertising data length can vary (and may be less than 1650). Consider either (a) documenting that 1650 is the spec maximum and the effective limit is additionally constrained by controller capabilities, or (b) deriving/validating this against a controller capability/config (if available) to avoid giving users a false expectation that setting 1650 will always work. Also, if this buffer is used for reassembly, it might be worth clarifying in the prompt/help that this is the *reassembled* report size (not per-fragment) and what happens on overflow (truncate vs drop whole report).

Potential buffer over-read: `size_to_copy` is computed from `evt->length` and `ext_scan_buf` capacity, but it is not bounded by the actual remaining bytes in the HCI event buffer (`buf->len`). If the controller/event is malformed or truncated, `net_buf_add_mem(ext_scan_buf, buf->data, size_to_copy)` may read past `buf->data`. Consider clamping `size_to_copy = MIN(size_to_copy, buf->len)` (and/or validating `buf->len >= evt->length` earlier) before copying, and handle the error path (discard/reset state) if the buffer is shorter than expected.

Zephyr test apps typically use the repository-wide minimum CMake version (often 3.20.0 today, but it can change). Consider using the standard pattern from other tests (or omitting this line if the top-level tooling already enforces it) to avoid unnecessarily bumping requirements for this test when Zephyr’s minimum changes.

Hard-coding `CONFIG_BT_EXT_SCAN_BUF_SIZE=91` makes the test fragile and potentially inconsistent with the stated goal of supporting very long advertising data (e.g., 1650 bytes). If this value is meant to be the *per-fragment* HCI payload size (e.g., 255 minus headers) or some minimum to force fragmentation, please add an inline comment explaining why 91 is the correct/required value for this test. Otherwise consider setting it to a value derived from the test vectors (or using a larger value that matches the intended reassembly buffer size) so the test remains valid across controller/host changes and is easier to understand/maintain.

There’s a commented-out early return left in the command handler. This looks like debug scaffolding and is risky to keep in-tree because it can be accidentally re-enabled and bypass all HCI command handling (breaking bt_enable() behavior and test validity). Please remove the commented line, or replace it with a clear conditional (e.g., behind a test-specific Kconfig) if it’s intentionally used for troubleshooting.

The YAML indentation/structure looks incorrect: the file starts with leading spaces and a key `1:tests:` which appears to be an accidental line-number prefix rather than valid Zephyr testcase schema. It should likely start at column 0 with `tests:` (no numeric prefix), otherwise Twister may fail to parse/collect this test. Please remove the `1:`/`2:`/`3:`/`4:` prefixes and fix indentation to match other `testcase.yaml` files.

This unconditionally enqueues ISO TX contexts into `free_tx`, but `free_tx` is part of the bt_conn TX callback infrastructure and may not be defined/initialized when `CONFIG_BT_CONN=n` (the reported failure mode). Please ensure `free_tx` exists and is initialized in the same build configurations where this code is compiled, or gate this on the same Kconfig symbol that provides `free_tx` (or introduce an ISO-specific FIFO instead of reusing `free_tx`). Also consider guarding against double-initialization (e.g., if `bt_conn_iso_init()` can be called more than once) to avoid enqueuing the same elements multiple times.

Since this is an internal header, adding a new init entry point is fine, but please ensure the name and placement match existing init patterns (e.g., bt_conn_init/bt_iso_init) and that it’s only declared when ISO is enabled. Consider guarding the prototype with the relevant Kconfig option (e.g., CONFIG_BT_ISO) to avoid unused/undefined symbol issues in builds where ISO is off, and add a brief comment describing when/where it must be called (init order matters for FIFO/tx context setup).

Calling `bt_conn_iso_init()` from `bt_init()` is a good fix for CONFIG_BT_CONN=n builds, but please ensure the init ordering is correct and the symbol is always available when CONFIG_BT_ISO=y. In particular: (1) verify `bt_conn_iso_init()` does not depend on `bt_conn_init()`/ACL connection state being initialized first (otherwise this should be moved after the relevant init or guarded by additional Kconfig checks), and (2) consider whether this should be `IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_BT_ISO) && IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_BT_CONN)` or the opposite (i.e., explicitly support CONFIG_BT_CONN=n) depending on where `bt_conn_iso_init()` is compiled. If the intent is to make ISO broadcast independent of CONFIG_BT_CONN, confirm the function is built under CONFIG_BT_ISO alone and add a brief comment here about why ISO needs a separate init path.

Casting a pointer return value to `uint32_t` is potentially unsafe on 64-bit builds and can truncate the address. If `get_acpi_shm()` returns an address/ptr, consider changing `data` to `uintptr_t` (or `uint64_t` if the protocol requires) and cast via `uintptr_t` (e.g., `*data = (uint32_t)(uintptr_t)api->get_acpi_shm(emul->target);`) or, better, update the API/command semantics to return a 32-bit value explicitly. Also ensure `emul->target` is guaranteed non-NULL here (assert or validate) since the call site changed from `emul` to `emul->target`.

This changes the callback to receive `emul->target` (a `struct emul *`) instead of the bus-specific `struct i2c_emul *`. Please double-check the `i2c_emul_api::transfer` function pointer signature and all implementations to ensure they were updated accordingly; otherwise this will compile but break at runtime/ABI level (or fail to compile depending on typedefs). Also consider adding an `__ASSERT_NO_MSG(emul->target)` here (and/or validating `emul->target->data/cfg` as needed) to avoid NULL deref if an emulator instance wasn’t properly linked to a target device via DT/phandle.

This changes the callback to receive `emul->target` (a `struct device *`) instead of the emulator instance. Please double-check the SPI emulator API contract: if `api->io` is now defined to take the emulated *target device* as its first argument, all implementations and function pointer typedefs must be updated consistently. Otherwise this will compile but break at runtime/ABI level (mismatched function signatures) or lose access to emulator state that previously came via `struct spi_emul *`/`struct emul *`.

Also consider adding an `__ASSERT_NO_MSG(emul->target);` before dereferencing/using it, since this is a new required field and failures will be harder to diagnose without an explicit assert.

This assigns DT_REG_ADDR(node_id) into different fields depending on bus type, but it assumes the same devicetree node has a meaningful `reg` for all supported buses. For SPI, `reg` is typically the chip select index, while many SPI devices use `cs-gpios` (and `reg` may be absent or not represent CS). For eSPI, `reg` may not map to `chipsel` at all depending on binding. Consider (a) validating that `reg` exists for the node (e.g., DT_NODE_HAS_PROP(node_id, reg)) and producing a clearer build-time error, and/or (b) using bus-specific DT properties/macros (e.g., DT_SPI_DEV_CS_GPIOS_* or a dedicated binding property) instead of overloading `reg` into `chipsel`. Otherwise this can silently misconfigure emulators or fail to compile on valid DTS configurations.

The rename from `parent` to `target` changes the semantic meaning of this pointer (it’s no longer “parent emulator” but “the owning/target emulator”). Please ensure this is consistently updated across the eSPI emulation implementation (registration, lookup, and any CONTAINER_OF usage) and that the naming matches the rest of the emulator framework (e.g., other bus emul headers using `target` vs `emul`). Also consider clarifying in the comment what “target” refers to (the `struct emul` representing the device emulator instance) and whether it can ever be NULL; if it’s truly required, it may be worth adding an assertion/validation at registration time rather than only documenting it here.

Changing the callback signature to take `const struct emul *emulator` improves access to emulator cfg/data, but it also removes direct access to the bus-node context (`struct i2c_emul`) that may be needed by some implementations (e.g., per-bus-node state, list linkage, or retrieving the I2C controller/registration context). If the intent is that all state lives in the parent/target `struct emul`, consider documenting how an implementation should access I2C-specific context (addr, controller, etc.) and/or provide a helper to get the `struct i2c_emul` from the `struct emul` when needed. Also ensure all existing implementations and registration code are updated accordingly, since this is an ABI/API break for out-of-tree emulators.

Changing the callback signature to take `const struct emul *emulator` improves access to common emulator data, but it also removes direct access to SPI-bus-specific context (`struct spi_emul`) that may still be needed for registration/list linkage (`node`) or SPI-emul-specific state. Please ensure there is a clear/official way for implementations to recover the `struct spi_emul` (or equivalent bus context) from `struct emul` (e.g., via `emulator->bus.spi` or a helper accessor) and update the API docs accordingly. Also consider whether `const struct emul *` is sufficient: if emulators need to mutate per-emulator runtime state, they should do so via `emulator->data` (documented) rather than requiring a non-const pointer.

This dereferences `emul->bus.i2c` unconditionally based on `bus_type`, but the pointer may be NULL (e.g., misconfigured EMUL_DEFINE, or bus type set but bus union not populated). Consider adding an `__ASSERT(emul->bus.i2c != NULL, ...)` (and similarly for other bus types) before writing `->target`, or handle NULL gracefully to avoid a hard fault during init.

The `switch (emul->bus_type)` has no `default:` case here. If `bus_type` is invalid/uninitialized, the `target` back-pointer won't be set, but init will still run. Consider adding a `default` that logs/returns `-EINVAL` early to keep behavior consistent with the later registration switch (which does have a default).

Registration is attempted even if `emul->init()` failed (rc != 0). That can leave partially initialized emulators registered on the bus. Consider `continue;`/`goto next` after a failed init, or only register when init succeeds, unless there's a strong reason to register regardless.

This warning message is a bit misleading: the `default` case can also be hit when the bus type is known but the corresponding CONFIG_*_EMUL is disabled (because the `case` is compiled out). Consider logging the `bus_type` value and/or clarifying the message (e.g., "bus type not supported or not enabled") to aid debugging.

This function is now declared to return `int`, but it still has a `void`-style name/signature in the comment history and (in this hunk) doesn’t use `parent` at all. Please ensure this matches the expected `emul_init_t` prototype after the API change (i.e., return type and parameters), and either use `parent` (e.g., for any device-specific init that still depends on the emulated device) or explicitly mark it unused to avoid warnings (`ARG_UNUSED(parent)`). Also consider returning meaningful error codes if init can fail (e.g., null cfg/data/reg), rather than always `0`.

This change looks incorrect: `espi_dev` is the real eSPI device, not the emulator instance. Using `espi_dev->data` will return the driver's runtime data, not `struct espi_host_emul_data`. Previously you correctly derived the emulator data from the `struct espi_emul *` returned by `api->find_emul()`. If the new API intends to avoid `CONTAINER_OF`, you likely need to obtain the owning `struct emul *` (or `struct espi_emul`'s `parent`) from `emul_espi` and then use `parent->data`, or update `find_emul()` to return `const struct emul *` directly. As-is, this will cause type confusion/UB and likely crash or corrupt memory.

This init function used to call `i2c_emul_register(...)` and return its status, but now it unconditionally returns 0. Unless registration is guaranteed to be performed elsewhere (e.g., common bus init code) and cannot fail, this risks leaving the emulator unregistered while still reporting success, which will make tests silently fail/hang. If registration has moved to a framework-level path, consider either removing the now-dead `parent`/`dev_label` usage here (or documenting why they’re still needed), or add an assertion/verification that the emulator was registered. Otherwise, restore the registration call and propagate errors.

Good fix to avoid overrunning the source AD field when the fragment includes the 2-byte AD header. However, this assumes `set_data->len >= 2`. If `set_data->len` can ever be 0 or 1 (e.g., due to fragmentation/remaining length calculations), `set_data->len - 2` will underflow (likely to a large `size_t`) and reintroduce an OOB read/write. Consider guarding with `if (set_data->len < 2) return -EINVAL;` (or assert) and/or compute a bounded copy length like `size_t copy_len = MIN((size_t)(set_data->len - 2), (size_t)(len - offset));` to ensure both destination capacity and remaining source bytes are respected.

Typo in the documentation: "callling" should be "calling". Also consider clarifying the NULL-conn behavior more explicitly (e.g., "conn is NULL when invoked to build PACS notifications triggered by capability register/unregister") to avoid ambiguity for callback implementers.

This call ignores the return value from `get_pac_records()`. If `get_pac_records()` can fail (e.g., no PACs, buffer too small, invalid `type`), `read_buf.len/data` may be stale or inconsistent and you may end up notifying garbage or an empty payload. Please capture and handle the return code (and consider clearing `read_buf.len` on failure) before calling `bt_gatt_notify_uuid()`. Also ensure `type` is always initialized for all build combinations/paths; if neither `if` matches, `type`/`uuid` would be uninitialized here.

The overflow/upper-bound check uses INT_MAX, but this function returns int32_t and the rest of the code historically used MAX_WAIT as the sentinel. Consider using INT32_MAX (or a dedicated K_TICKS_FOREVER/timeout max constant) instead of INT_MAX to avoid width mismatches on non-ILP32 targets and to make the intent explicit. Also, the cast currently happens after the subtraction in `to->dticks - ticks_elapsed`; if `dticks` is wider than int32_t, consider casting operands before subtracting to avoid intermediate overflow/truncation (e.g., `(int64_t)to->dticks - (int64_t)ticks_elapsed`).

Typo in the enum constant name: `BT_AUDIO_LOCATION_TOP_BECK_RIGHT` should likely be `BT_AUDIO_LOCATION_TOP_BACK_RIGHT` ("BECK" -> "BACK"). As this is a public API symbol, the typo will leak into applications and be hard to change later. Please fix before merge (or, if already released, consider adding the correctly spelled alias and deprecating the misspelled one).

The API name and signature are a bit ambiguous: it’s unclear whether this sets a global PAC location, a per-capability location, or a per-connection value, and what the expected behavior is when the value changes (e.g., does it trigger PACS notifications immediately, and what error is returned if PACS isn’t initialized/registered). Consider expanding the doxygen to document scope (sink/source, global vs per endpoint instance), side effects (notification semantics), and return values (e.g., -EINVAL for invalid type/location, -EALREADY if unchanged, -EACCES if not supported). Also, `location` is typically a bitmask; if `enum bt_audio_location` is used as a flags type, it may be better expressed as `uint32_t` (or a dedicated typedef) to avoid implying a single enumerator value.

This changes the Kconfig symbol from `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITIES` to `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITY`. Please double-check the actual symbol name in Kconfig and ensure it matches everywhere (other CMakeLists, source `#ifdef`s, and documentation). If the intended symbol is still plural, this will silently stop compiling `capabilities.c` when the feature is enabled, or break existing configs. If this is a deliberate rename, consider adding a Kconfig compatibility alias (deprecated old symbol selecting the new one) to avoid breaking existing applications.

This changes the dependency symbol from `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITIES` to `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITY`. Please double-check that `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITY` is the correct (and defined) Kconfig symbol and that all other references across the tree were updated accordingly. If the symbol was renamed, consider adding a compatibility/alias option (or select/imply) to avoid breaking existing defconfigs and out-of-tree projects that still set `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITIES`.

Renaming the hidden/virtual option from `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITIES` to `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITY` can be a breaking change for any Kconfig fragments/defconfigs that reference the old symbol. Ensure the old symbol is either removed everywhere or kept as a deprecated alias that selects/implies the new one, and verify that any `depends on`/`select` relationships elsewhere now point to `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITY` so the feature gating still works as intended.

Removing the BT_PAC_*_LOC Kconfig options makes the location no longer configurable at build time. That’s fine if the new design fully moves location storage/initialization into the server (capabilities layer), but please ensure there is a clear replacement for setting an initial/default location (e.g., via a new Kconfig, devicetree, or an explicit API call during init). Otherwise existing boards/apps that relied on BT_PAC_SNK_LOC/BT_PAC_SRC_LOC will silently lose their configured location and default to 0 (mono/unspecified). Also consider adding a Kconfig migration note in release notes or a deprecated alias to avoid breaking downstream configs.

This callback unconditionally calls bt_audio_unicast_server_location_changed(type), but this file only compiles the unicast server callbacks when CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_UNICAST_SERVER && CONFIG_BT_ASCS. If bt_audio_unicast_server_location_changed() is not available under that exact config (e.g., it’s only built when ASCS is enabled, or has different guards), this can become a link/config mismatch. Consider guarding the call with IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_UNICAST_SERVER) (and/or CONFIG_BT_ASCS if required), or ensure the symbol is always available when this callback is compiled. Also, since this is a write path, consider returning the error (or mapping it) if notification fails, rather than always returning 0, unless the spec/stack expects write to succeed even if notify cannot be sent.

This takes the address of a temporary returned by sys_cpu_to_le32(location). In C this is not an lvalue, so it’s either a compile error or (if it compiles via macro tricks) a pointer to a short-lived object. Use a local variable (e.g., uint32_t location_le = sys_cpu_to_le32(location);) and pass &location_le, and ensure the size matches the pointed-to type (sizeof(location_le)). Also note that get_pac_loc() expects enum bt_audio_location*, but here `location` is uint32_t; consider using enum bt_audio_location for consistency and to avoid type mismatches.

Defining a location mask here may be redundant or risky if the Bluetooth Audio API already defines a canonical mask/width for `bt_audio_location` (and it’s easy for these to diverge). Consider reusing an existing constant from `bluetooth/audio/audio.h` (or a shared header) instead of introducing a new one in an internal PACS header. Also, `BIT_MASK(28)` assumes locations are limited to 28 bits; please ensure this matches the spec and the actual underlying type width (e.g., `uint32_t`) and add a brief comment or static assert if needed to prevent future mismatches.

This wrapper just forwards to an internal PACS API. Consider whether this should be part of the public unicast server API at all, or if the notification should be triggered directly from the PACS/capabilities layer when the location value is updated. If you keep this function, please add a brief doxygen comment clarifying when it must be called (e.g., after server-side location update) and what errors it can return, and ensure the header declaration is added/updated so callers don’t need to include internal headers. Also verify that calling bt_pacs_location_changed() is safe in all contexts (e.g., from GATT write callbacks vs. other threads) and that it handles NULL/disabled PACS cases gracefully.

This looks like a copy/paste bug: in the CONFIG_BT_PAC_SRC branch you call bt_audio_capability_set_location() with BT_AUDIO_SINK. That will update the sink location twice and never set the source location. Use BT_AUDIO_SOURCE here (or the correct enum for the source direction) so the test actually exercises source location updates/notifications.

This change drops the `const` qualifier from `source`, which weakens the API contract and may force callers to cast away const or prevent calling this function with a const pointer. If `bt_audio_broadcast_source_get_base()` does not modify the source object, keep the parameter `const struct bt_audio_broadcast_source *source`. If mutation is required (e.g., lazy BASE generation/caching), consider documenting that side effect explicitly in the doxygen and/or providing a separate `*_get_base_const()` accessor to preserve const-correctness for read-only use cases.

Setting a fixed main stack size in the sample config may be unnecessary and can mask stack-usage regressions across boards. Consider only adding this if the sample actually overflows the default stack after the subgroup changes, and if so, add a brief comment explaining why 2048 is required (or prefer adjusting via Kconfig defaults/board overlays if this is board-specific).

Please verify that CONFIG_BT_ISO_MAX_CHAN=2 is sufficient for the new configuration. With 2 subgroups and 2 streams, the number of ISO channels may exceed 2 depending on how the controller/host maps BIS/streams to ISO channels. If the intent is to support 2 BIS total, add a comment clarifying the relationship; otherwise consider increasing this to match the worst-case channel usage for the sample.

Bumping ISO TX buffers to 4 makes sense with more streams, but it would be good to ensure this scales with CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_STREAM_COUNT (e.g., 2x streams) and add a short comment/rationale. Otherwise future changes to stream count may silently under-provision buffers and cause runtime ISO underruns.

Potential divide-by-zero / invalid configuration risk: `streams_per_subgroup` is computed by dividing by `ARRAY_SIZE(subgroup_param)`, which is `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT`. If that Kconfig is ever set to 0, this will be a compile-time error or undefined behavior depending on how it’s used. Also, if `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_STREAM_COUNT` is not an exact multiple of subgroup count, the remainder streams are silently ignored and `params` will point past the end for the last subgroup if `streams_per_subgroup` rounds down to 0. Consider adding BUILD_ASSERTs to enforce `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT > 0` and `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_STREAM_COUNT % CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT == 0` (and maybe `streams_per_subgroup > 0`) to make the sample robust and avoid subtle misconfiguration bugs.

This changes the minimum from 0 to 1. If 0 was previously used to effectively disable broadcast sources while keeping BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SOURCE enabled (e.g., for size-constrained builds or to satisfy dependencies), this becomes a breaking Kconfig change. Consider whether 0 should remain allowed (and handle it in code), or add a clear rationale in help text / commit message explaining why 0 is no longer valid. Also double-check that all code paths assume at least one source when the feature is enabled.

`streams_encoded` is a `uint8_t *`, so `streams_encoded++` increments the pointer, not the encoded-stream counter. This will corrupt the pointer passed from `encode_base()` and likely lead to out-of-bounds access when indexing `source->stream_data[streams_encoded]` for subsequent subgroups. Use `(*streams_encoded)++` (or `++(*streams_encoded)`) to increment the counter value, and consider making the counter a `size_t` to avoid overflow when `BROADCAST_STREAM_CNT` can exceed 255.

This allocates per-stream codec-specific data for *all* BROADCAST_STREAM_CNT streams inside every bt_audio_broadcast_source instance, which can significantly increase RAM usage (especially if CONFIG_BT_CODEC_MAX_DATA_COUNT is non-trivial). Consider making this storage conditional (e.g., only when CONFIG_BT_CODEC_MAX_DATA_COUNT is enabled), or allocating per-subgroup/per-stream data dynamically or via pointers sized to the actual stream_count/subgroup configuration. Also ensure the indexing contract is clear: if stream_data is keyed by BIS index/stream index, document how it maps when multiple subgroups are used (and how unused entries are handled).

Consider adding basic argument validation here (e.g., return early if `stream` is NULL) to avoid a potential NULL dereference. Also, `ops` could be `const struct bt_audio_stream_ops *` since the function only stores the pointer and does not modify the ops table; this better communicates intent and allows placing ops in read-only memory. If changing the type is too invasive, at least document whether `ops == NULL` is allowed to unregister callbacks.

This hard-codes the broadcast source to a single subgroup. Given the stated goal of adding subgroup support, consider either (a) wiring this up to accept multiple subgroups from the shell (e.g., parse subgroup count/stream mapping/metadata) or (b) at least add a TODO/comment explaining why the shell command still creates only one subgroup. Also ensure `create_param` is fully initialized (e.g., `memset(&create_param, 0, sizeof(create_param))`) before setting fields, in case the struct gains new members (like packing/metadata) that must be zeroed to avoid undefined behavior.

This test config hard-codes the subgroup count to 1, which may mask regressions in the new multi-subgroup API. Consider either (a) adding a separate test configuration that sets `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT` > 1 to exercise the new functionality, or (b) adding a comment here explaining why the test remains at 1 (e.g., controller limitations) and ensuring the test suite includes coverage for multiple subgroups elsewhere.

This indexes `stream_params` by subgroup index, but `stream_params` is sized by `ARRAY_SIZE(broadcast_source_streams)` (total streams), while `subgroup_params` is sized by `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT` (subgroups). If the subgroup count exceeds the number of streams, this becomes an out-of-bounds access; if the intent is multiple streams per subgroup, this also only assigns one stream per subgroup. Consider explicitly mapping streams to subgroups (e.g., compute an offset and `params_count` per subgroup) and add a build-time check like `BUILD_ASSERT(CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT <= ARRAY_SIZE(stream_params))` (or adjust array sizing) to prevent OOB.

This change drops the `const` qualifier from `source`, which weakens the API contract and may force callers to cast away const or prevent passing a const pointer. `bt_audio_broadcast_source_get_base()` appears like a read-only accessor that serializes BASE into `base_buf`, so it should likely keep `const struct bt_audio_broadcast_source *source` unless the implementation truly mutates `source` (e.g., caching/allocating). If mutation is required, consider documenting it explicitly or using internal mutable state instead of changing the public signature.
