This line appears to include diff/annotation artifacts (leading spaces and a `1:` prefix) rather than a valid Kconfig assignment. `prj.conf` should contain plain `CONFIG_FOO=...` lines. Please remove the line-number prefix/indentation so it is parsed correctly (e.g., `CONFIG_MAIN_STACK_SIZE=2048`). Also consider whether the sample really needs to override the main stack size, and if so, add a brief comment explaining why.

Potential divide-by-zero and silent truncation here. If CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT is 0, ARRAY_SIZE(subgroup_param) becomes 0 and this will crash at runtime. Also, if STREAM_COUNT is not an exact multiple of SUBGROUP_COUNT, the integer division will drop the remainder and some streams in stream_params will never be assigned to any subgroup (and the print will report fewer streams than configured). Consider adding BUILD_ASSERTs to enforce SUBGROUP_COUNT > 0 and STREAM_COUNT % SUBGROUP_COUNT == 0 (or handle the remainder explicitly).

Changing the minimum from 0 to 1 is a behavior change: it removes the ability to build with broadcast source support enabled but with zero sources allocated (often used to compile in APIs while saving RAM). If that use-case is still valid, consider keeping `range 0 ...` and enforcing non-zero only where required (e.g., via `depends on`/`select` or runtime checks), or add a brief note in help text / commit message explaining why 0 is no longer supported.

`streams_encoded` is a `uint8_t *`, but this increments the pointer rather than the value it points to. That will corrupt the pointer and break subsequent indexing into `source->stream_data` (and can lead to out-of-bounds reads/writes). Use `(*streams_encoded)++` (or `*streams_encoded += 1`) instead. Also consider making `streams_encoded` a `size_t` to match counts and avoid overflow when `BROADCAST_STREAM_CNT` can exceed 255.

This embeds per-stream codec-specific data for *all* possible streams (`BROADCAST_STREAM_CNT`) directly in `bt_audio_broadcast_source`, which can significantly increase RAM usage (especially if `CONFIG_BT_CODEC_MAX_DATA_COUNT` is non-trivial). Consider storing this per-stream data alongside the BIS/stream objects (or allocating per active stream/subgroup) rather than a fixed-size array in the source object. Also, the comment says "for each stream in the subgroup" but the field is on the source and sized by total stream count; please clarify whether indexing is global stream index vs per-subgroup, and ensure the mapping is unambiguous in the API/implementation.

Consider adding a NULL check for `qos` before dereferencing its fields. This helper is now compiled for both unicast and broadcast source, and callers may pass NULL to indicate "no QoS" or due to error paths; without a guard this will crash. If NULL is not allowed, add an `__ASSERT_NO_MSG(qos != NULL)` (or return false with a BT_DBG) to make the contract explicit.

Setting `subgroup_param.params_count` to `ARRAY_SIZE(stream_params)` assumes all entries in `stream_params` are valid/used streams. If the shell command supports creating fewer BIS/streams (or if some `broadcast_source_streams[]` entries are unused), this will pass uninitialized/invalid stream params to the API. Consider tracking the actual number of streams requested/initialized (e.g., `size_t stream_count`) and set `params_count` accordingly, and/or explicitly initialize per-stream fields (codec, data, metadata) for only the active streams.

This test config still documents that only 1 stream is supported by the controller, but now also pins subgroup count to 1. If the intent of this PR is to validate multi-subgroup / per-BIS codec config support, consider adding (or updating) a dedicated test configuration that sets *_SUBGROUP_COUNT > 1 (and, if possible, stream count > 1) to actually exercise the new API paths. Otherwise this may mask regressions by only testing the single-subgroup case.

This indexes `stream_params` by subgroup index, but `stream_params` is sized to `ARRAY_SIZE(broadcast_source_streams)` (i.e., number of streams), while `subgroup_params` is sized to `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT` (number of subgroups). If subgroup count can exceed stream count, this becomes an out-of-bounds access. Even when counts match, this hard-wires 1 stream per subgroup and ignores any remaining streams. Consider explicitly mapping streams to subgroups (e.g., maintain a running `stream_idx` and assign `params_count`/`params` accordingly), and add a build-time/runtime check that total streams >= sum(params_count) and that indices stay within `stream_params`.

Using CONFIG_SYS_CLOCK_HW_CYCLES_PER_SEC as the condition can be misleading here because it’s a Kconfig value and may not reflect the actual SYSCLK being configured from DT (e.g., if the clock tree changes or if SYSCLK is derived differently). Consider basing the decision on the computed target SYSCLK/new_flash_freq (or DT clock-frequency) used for flash latency calculation, so overdrive is enabled exactly when required by the real configured frequency. Also, this assumes Power Scale 1; if other power scaling is supported, the threshold may differ and should be derived from SoC/LL constraints rather than a hard-coded 180 MHz check.

This line changes the referenced Kconfig symbol from `CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC` to `CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS`. Please double-check that the new symbol actually gates the availability of `pthread_rwlock_*` APIs (and that it exists in Kconfig) rather than being purely a documentation rename. If `CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC` is still the real dependency (or if both are required), the conformance table will become misleading. Also ensure the indentation/formatting is consistent with surrounding entries (the previous line had an extra leading space before `69`).

This change switches the feature-test macro gate from `CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC` to `CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS`. Please double-check that `CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS` is always selected/enabled when Zephyr actually provides `pthread_rwlock_*` (or equivalent) support, otherwise `_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS` may be advertised without implementation (or vice versa). If `CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC` is being deprecated/renamed, consider keeping backward compatibility here (e.g., `#if defined(CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS) || defined(CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC)`) during the deprecation window to avoid breaking out-of-tree configs that still set the old symbol.

This change renames a standard POSIX macro (`PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER`) to a non-standard name (`POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER`). That will break existing code and also diverges from IEEE 1003.1 expectations (the normative name is `PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER`). If the intent is to standardize Kconfig options, it shouldn’t require renaming public API macros. Consider keeping `PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` as the primary definition and (if needed) add `POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` as an alias for compatibility, or gate the alias behind a deprecation period. Also ensure any internal uses and documentation are updated consistently if you keep both.

This switches the feature test from CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC to CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS. Please double-check that CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS is the correct new normative symbol for enabling pthread rwlocks (and that it is selected whenever rwlocks are actually available). If the implementation is still gated by CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC (or another pthread option) elsewhere, sysconf() may start reporting _POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS even when the API isn’t built/linked, or vice versa. Consider aligning the Kconfig dependencies (or using a composite condition) and adding a brief comment mapping old->new to avoid mismatches during the deprecation window.

Switching the build guard from CONFIG_PTHREAD_RWLOCK to CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS is fine for the new naming, but please ensure backward compatibility: if CONFIG_PTHREAD_RWLOCK is now deprecated (not removed), this change will stop compiling rwlock.c for existing configs and can break users (and potentially newlib POSIX expectations). Consider supporting both symbols during the deprecation window (e.g., `zephyr_library_sources_ifdef(CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS rwlock.c)` plus an additional `...ifdef(CONFIG_PTHREAD_RWLOCK rwlock.c)` or a Kconfig `select`/alias so either option enables the source). Also verify that the new symbol is enabled by the relevant POSIX API umbrella option so rwlocks are still built when expected.

The prompt string is incorrect for this symbol: `config PTHREAD_RWLOCK` is described as "pthread_spinlock_t support". This looks like a copy/paste from `PTHREAD_SPINLOCK` and will confuse users and migration tooling/docs. Please change the prompt to something like "pthread_rwlock_t support [DEPRECATED]" (or "pthread_rwlock support") to match the symbol and the selected replacement (`POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS`).

This file previously used the Kconfig template mechanism (TYPE/type/type-function + rsource) to generate the pooled IPC type config and related glue. Replacing it with `module = PTHREAD_RWLOCK` likely changes semantics: `module` is used for logging config templates, but it does not recreate the pooled object/type configuration that `Kconfig.template.pooled_ipc_type` provided. Please verify that the rwlock pool sizing/enablement symbols and any generated dependencies (e.g., the symbol that actually enables pthread rwlock support and the pool count wiring) are still defined elsewhere; otherwise this can silently drop required Kconfig options and break builds/feature completeness. If the pooled template is still needed, consider keeping the `rsource "Kconfig.template.pooled_ipc_type"` (or reintroducing equivalent config entries) in addition to the logging module block.

Switching to `POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` is fine, but please ensure this macro is always defined/visible in this TU for all configurations that build `rwlock.c` (including when legacy `PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` is still used by out-of-tree code). If `POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` is introduced as a replacement, consider providing a compatibility alias (e.g., `#define PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` under a deprecated Kconfig or header guard) to avoid breaking existing code and to keep the initializer semantics identical (type/width, constant expression, and sentinel value used by `to_posix_rwlock`). Also double-check that comparing `*rwlock` against the initializer remains valid if the underlying type changes (e.g., pointer vs integer).

When assigning the initializer, confirm `POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` is a valid constant expression for `pthread_rwlock_t` and matches the expected sentinel checked in `to_posix_rwlock()`. If the goal is Kconfig standardization, it may be worth keeping `PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` as a deprecated alias in the public headers so applications that call `pthread_rwlock_init()` and also use static initializers don’t break at compile time.

This is a header conformance test, and the line is currently commented out because rwlocks are not implemented. Switching to `POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS_INITIALIZER` may be misleading/incorrect: IEEE/POSIX defines `PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` (and `pthread_rwlock_t`), not a `POSIX_*` initializer macro. If the intent is to validate the new Kconfig naming, that should be done via config symbols, not by inventing/using a non-standard C macro in a pthread header test. Consider either keeping `PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` in the comment, or (better) adding a compile-time check guarded by `#ifdef PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` / `#ifdef CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS` once implemented, rather than referencing a potentially undefined macro here.

Removing the `mp->fs = NULL` assignment avoids the immediate NULL-deref in concurrent I/O, but it also changes the post-unmount state: callers may still see a non-NULL `mp->fs` and attempt operations on an unmounted (and potentially deinitialized) backend. This can mask the unmounted state and risks use-after-unmount depending on how the FS driver tears down in `unmount()`. Consider instead (a) adding proper synchronization/refcounting around mount usage (e.g., a per-mount lock or in-flight op counter that `fs_unmount()` waits on), and/or (b) keeping `mp->fs = NULL` but only after ensuring no active file operations can reference it, plus adding NULL checks in fs_* paths. At minimum, document the intended invariant for `mp->fs` after unmount and ensure other code paths don’t rely on it being NULL to detect unmounted state.

Using memcpy here is unnecessary and slightly obscures intent since both objects are the same type. Prefer direct assignment (`match->discrete = tmp;`) which is clearer, type-safe, and avoids any potential issues if the struct ever gains padding/attributes that make raw byte copies undesirable. If you keep memcpy, consider adding a brief rationale (e.g., avoiding -Wmaybe-uninitialized or similar), but assignment should work fine for plain structs.

Potential divide-by-zero and overflow risk: `frmival->denominator` is not validated before division, and the multiplication by `NSEC_PER_SEC` happens before the division which can overflow for large numerators. Consider guarding against `denominator == 0` (e.g., return 0 or UINT64_MAX, or assert) and/or reordering to reduce overflow risk (e.g., divide first when possible, or use a helper like `uint64_t_mul_div_u64()` if available). Also, the docstring says "microseconds" but the function name and computation are nanoseconds; please align the comment (and/or rename if the intent is usec).

The project name `integration` is quite generic and may be confusing when browsing build logs or IDE project lists, especially since this lives under `tests/drivers/video/api`. Consider renaming it to something more specific (e.g., `video_api` or `drivers_video_api`) to better reflect the test suite and avoid potential naming collisions across tests.

Setting a global max pool size to 16KB may be too tight for some formats/resolutions and could make the API test brittle if the test later expands (e.g., larger frames/metadata). Consider either (a) sizing this based on the test’s actual buffer requirements with a comment explaining the chosen value, or (b) using a more conservative size to avoid future failures when the skeleton/test evolves.

This assertion is duplicated on the next line: you call `zassert_not_ok(ret);` and immediately after `zassert_not_ok(ret, "expecting wrong format to mismatch");`. The first one is redundant and also makes the test output less clear (it can fail without the helpful message). Consider removing the bare assertion and keeping only the message-bearing one.

Consider whether `platform_allow: native_sim` is sufficient for CI coverage. If this test is intended to validate the API broadly (and especially if it’s pure emulation), it may be better to use `platform_exclude` for known-incompatible targets or add `integration_platforms: [native_sim]` to ensure it runs in standard CI. Also verify the chosen platform name matches Zephyr’s canonical platform identifier used in Twister for your tree/version.

Please keep the formatting consistent with the surrounding entries (tabs/spacing). The existing lines align the source filename column; this new line uses a single tab and will likely misalign in editors. Consider matching the pattern used above (e.g., `zephyr_library_sources_ifdef(CONFIG_VIDEO_ESP32\t\tvideo_esp32_dvp.c)`), or run the same formatting as the rest of the file.

Please keep Kconfig `source` entries in a consistent, discoverable order (typically alphabetical by filename or grouped by driver class). Adding `Kconfig.emul_imager` here may break that convention depending on the rest of the file. Consider placing both emulation sources together in the appropriate section (e.g., near other emulation/test drivers if any) and/or adding a short comment header to clarify these are virtual/emulation drivers used for API tests.

Defaulting this to `y` means the emulated imager driver will be enabled whenever the DT compatible is present, which can be surprising for users and may pull in extra code in builds that include the node but don’t intend to use the emulation. Consider making this `default n` (or gating it behind an explicit `depends on VIDEO`/test option) and enabling it from the relevant test/samples via prj.conf instead.

Defaulting this to `y` can unintentionally enable the emulated RX driver whenever the DT symbol is present, which may not be desirable for production builds or when multiple RX backends exist. Consider using `default n` (or a more conditional default, e.g. `default y if ...`) and letting boards/tests explicitly enable it via Kconfig or the test scenario. This matches the usual pattern for emulation/skeleton drivers to avoid surprising enablement.

Bug: `value` is a pointer to the control value, but this casts the pointer itself to `int` and writes that as the register value. This will produce nonsense (and is UB on 64-bit). Dereference/cast the pointed-to type instead (e.g. `*(int *)value` or `*(int32_t *)value` depending on the control ABI), and consider validating range/width before truncating to `uint8_t` for the register write.

This is a self-referential initialization and leaves `vbuf` uninitialized (and will typically fail to compile with `-Werror=uninitialized`/`-Winit-self`). It should be initialized to `NULL` (or just declared without an initializer) before the `while ((vbuf = k_fifo_get(...)) != NULL)` loop. E.g. `struct video_buffer *vbuf = NULL;`.

The binding defines `child-binding` nested inside another `child-binding`, which is likely a YAML/schema mistake. In Zephyr DT bindings, `child-binding` should typically be a single mapping describing the child node schema. If the intent is to apply `video-interfaces.yaml` to children, this should probably be:

```yaml
child-binding:
  include: video-interfaces.yaml
```

As written, the extra nesting may be ignored or cause schema validation issues, and it’s unclear what the intermediate level represents.

This looks like an accidental double nesting: `child-binding:` contains another `child-binding:`. In Zephyr DT bindings, you typically want a single `child-binding` block describing the child node schema. The extra level may cause the binding to be ignored/misinterpreted by dt-schema. Consider removing one level so it becomes:

child-binding:
  include: video-interfaces.yaml
  properties: ...

If you intended to validate grandchildren, please add a clarifying comment and ensure this matches dt-schema expectations (usually `child-binding` is not recursively nested like this).

Changing the unit-address from a sentinel (deadbeef) to a concrete address is fine, but please ensure this address does not collide with any other nodes in this overlay (or common test overlays) and that it matches the `reg` base (which you also changed). If the goal is simply to avoid “funny” addresses, consider adding a short comment explaining the chosen address range (e.g., reserved test MMIO window) to prevent future overlaps.

Adding `video_emul.c` to the test build is fine, but ensure it is only compiled when the corresponding Kconfig/DT dependencies for the emulated video driver are enabled. Otherwise, this can break builds for platforms/scenarios that don’t select the emulation driver (e.g., missing headers/symbols). Consider guarding this source with a Kconfig option (e.g., `CONFIG_VIDEO_EMUL`/test-specific config) via `target_sources_ifdef()` or similar, or ensure `prj.conf` for this test always enables the required symbols.

Using `remote-endpoint-label` is not a standard DT graph property in Zephyr; the common pattern is `remote-endpoint = <&phandle>;` (or `remote-endpoint = <&label>;` where the label resolves to a phandle). Unless this PR also adds tooling/bindings support for `remote-endpoint-label`, this will likely be ignored by DT parsing and the graph connection won’t be established. Consider switching to `remote-endpoint = <&test_video_emul_rx_ep_in>;` here (and similarly for the other endpoints).

Passing an integer literal as a `void *` is undefined/implementation-defined and will break on 64-bit hosts (e.g. native_sim) and with stricter compiler warnings. The video control API expects `void *` to point to the value storage (or a typed object), not to carry the value in the pointer. Use an actual variable (e.g. `int exposure = 30; video_set_ctrl(..., &exposure);`) or the correct control-specific type if the API defines one. Same issue applies to the gain control below.

Defining `unsigned` as `signed` to coerce `__SIZE_TYPE__` into a signed type is very brittle and can have surprising side effects (even if scoped) with some toolchains, static analyzers, and macro-expanded code in this header. It also relies on `__SIZE_TYPE__` being an unsigned type, which is typical but not guaranteed by the C standard. Consider using a more explicit/portable approach: prefer `__SSIZE_TYPE__` when available, or derive a signed type via `__typeof__(sizeof(0))` tricks / compiler builtins, or fall back to `intptr_t`/`ptrdiff_t`-based sizing. At minimum, add a compile-time assertion that `k_ssize_t` is signed and same width as `size_t` (or document the intended relationship).

Typo in help text: "flexibiltity" should be "flexibility". Also consider whether this option belongs under a more specific menu (e.g., filesystem/posix/vfs) rather than kernel-wide, since it affects ABI/typedefs and may have broad impact; if it stays here, it may need clearer wording about ABI compatibility and rebuild requirements when toggled.

Switching to `k_ssize_t` addresses the layering violation, but please ensure this file includes the header that defines `k_ssize_t` (typically `<zephyr/sys/types.h>` or an equivalent Zephyr type header) and does not rely on indirect includes. Also double-check any subsequent comparisons/assignments involving `len` (e.g., against `size_t`/`int` or negative error returns) to avoid signed/unsigned conversion warnings or behavior changes now that the type is Zephyr-specific.

Casting `offset` to `long` for `%lx` is not portable and can truncate on LLP64 (e.g., Windows) or if `k_off_t` becomes 64-bit while `long` is 32-bit. Prefer using Zephyr's inttypes helpers and a format macro that matches `k_off_t` (e.g., `LOG_ERR("... offset %" PRI... , (k_off_t)offset)`), or cast to `int64_t/uint64_t` and use `PRId64/PRIu64` as appropriate. Also consider whether the offset should be printed in hex at all; if so, use an unsigned type for the cast/format.

Switching to k_ssize_t helps avoid pulling POSIX types below the POSIX layer, but please ensure this file includes the header that defines k_ssize_t (typically <zephyr/sys/types.h>) and that the return value semantics remain correct. Also verify that all callers/related APIs (e.g., MIPI DSI write helpers) expect a Zephyr-native signed size type; if they still use ssize_t, you may need an explicit cast at the boundary or adjust the interface consistently to avoid signed/width mismatches and warnings on some toolchains.

Switching the offset type to k_off_t is consistent with removing POSIX types below the POSIX layer, but please double-check the arithmetic in this function: `const size_t remainder = BIT(config->addr_width) - offset;` now mixes `size_t` and `k_off_t` (which may be signed and/or 64-bit). If `offset` is negative or larger than `BIT(addr_width)`, this will underflow/overflow and produce a huge `remainder`, potentially bypassing bounds checks. Consider validating `offset >= 0` and `offset < BIT(addr_width)` (or clamping) before the subtraction, and/or casting explicitly to a known unsigned type after validation to avoid surprising implicit conversions.

Be careful using a signed offset type for bitmask alignment. `k_off_t` is typically signed, so `address & (sizeof(buf) - 1)` can yield surprising results if `address` is negative (sign-extension / implementation-defined behavior). Even if negative addresses are currently rejected elsewhere, it’s safer and clearer to compute the alignment using an unsigned type (e.g., `size_t addr_jmp = (size_t)address & (sizeof(buf) - 1);`) after validating `address >= 0`, or explicitly cast to `uintptr_t`/`size_t` before masking. Also consider adding/ensuring a range check in the caller path so `address` can’t be negative before this point.

Switching the offset parameter from POSIX `off_t` to Zephyr `k_off_t` is consistent with the goal of removing POSIX-layer types below the POSIX API. However, please ensure this file includes the header that defines `k_off_t` (typically a Zephyr kernel/base type header) and does not rely on transitive includes; otherwise this may break builds when include order changes. Also verify the fake function signature still matches the real EEPROM driver API (i.e., the corresponding `eeprom_read/eeprom_write` prototypes) so the mock remains type-compatible across all configurations (notably any Kconfig that changes `k_off_t` width).

Switching the syscall verifier signature to `k_off_t` is consistent with removing POSIX types below the POSIX layer, but please ensure this file (or an included header) actually defines `k_off_t` for all build configurations. If `k_off_t` comes from a specific kernel/types header, include it explicitly here to avoid relying on transitive includes. Also verify that the corresponding driver API (`struct eeprom_driver_api::read/write`) and the generated marshalling code (`eeprom_read_mrsh.c`) were updated to the same type; otherwise you may introduce ABI/type mismatches between the syscall wrapper and the implementation.

Switching to k_off_t helps remove the POSIX layering dependency, but please ensure this matches the EEPROM driver API signature expected by the subsystem. If the public eeprom_driver_api still uses off_t (or another type), this will either fail to compile (incompatible function pointer type) or silently mismatch on some toolchains. Consider updating the corresponding API typedef/prototype (or adding a thin wrapper) so the implementation signature exactly matches, and verify the required header providing k_off_t is included (typically <zephyr/types.h> or the appropriate kernel type header) to avoid relying on indirect includes.

Including <zephyr/types.h> just to get k_off_t is fine for the layering goal, but please double-check this driver doesn’t already get k_off_t via another common header (to avoid redundant includes) and that this include is the preferred one per Zephyr style for k_* typedefs. Also ensure no remaining uses of POSIX off_t/ssize_t remain in this file after the signature changes, otherwise you may end up needing both type systems or implicit declarations depending on include order.

Switching to k_off_t addresses the POSIX layering issue, but please ensure this file includes the header that defines k_off_t (typically <zephyr/sys/types.h>) and does not rely on transitive includes. Also consider validating/casting before passing `offset` into `eeprom_xec_execute_reg_set(...)` if that API still expects a narrower/unsigned type, to avoid truncation or sign issues when k_off_t is 64-bit.

Switching from POSIX `off_t` to Zephyr `k_off_t` aligns with the layering goal, but please ensure the rest of this function (and any called EEPROM APIs) consistently use `k_off_t` to avoid implicit conversions/truncation on 32-bit targets. Switching the callback return type to `k_ssize_t` is consistent with removing POSIX layering violations, but this is a public API type change for the settings subsystem and may break out-of-tree users and any in-tree code that still declares callbacks returning `ssize_t`. Changing the public API from `off_t` to `k_off_t` is a source-level (and potentially ABI) breaking change for any out-of-tree users and in-tree code that includes this header. If the intent is to remove POSIX layering, consider providing a compatibility path (e.g., typedef/alias when `CONFIG_POSIX_API` is enabled, or an inline wrapper overload-style pair of functions) or at least document the migration in the header/Kconfig and ensure all call sites are updated. Switching from POSIX `off_t` to `k_off_t` addresses the layering issue, but please ensure this header actually includes the definition of `k_off_t` (typically via a Zephyr base type header). API change: `stream_flash_erase_page()` now takes `k_off_t` instead of `off_t`. Defining ZVFS_O_CREAT via COND_CODE_1(CONFIG_PICOLIBC, ...) hard-codes libc-specific flag values into a base-OS header. This risks ABI/behavior mismatches when using other libcs (newlib, minimal libc) or when the underlying fcntl values differ, and it reintroduces a subtle layering/portability problem (now from libc config rather than POSIX headers). Consider instead: (a) keeping these constants in the POSIX/VFS layer (e.g., a zvfs fcntl header included only above the line), (b) deriving them from a single Zephyr-owned definition (not libc-dependent), or (c) providing a translation layer from ZVFS_* to native/posix flags in the POSIX shim. At minimum, please document why these exact numeric values are correct across supported libcs and add build-time assertions/tests to catch divergence.

Tying minimal libc internal typedefs to Zephyr kernel types introduces a new dependency from libc headers to Zephyr headers. That may be acceptable for Zephyr’s minimal libc, but it’s worth double-checking this doesn’t reintroduce a layering/cycle problem in the opposite direction (e.g., if <zephyr/types.h> pulls in headers that depend on libc/POSIX types). If the goal is to keep libc headers self-contained, consider defining _OFF_T_/_SSIZE_T_ in terms of fixed-width C types here (or include a very small, dependency-free Zephyr type header) and ensure k_off_t/k_ssize_t are guaranteed to be available and stable at this include point.

Using `_SSIZE_T_` here assumes it is always defined by the toolchain headers. That’s true for some libc/compiler combinations, but not guaranteed across all Zephyr-supported toolchains (and may differ between newlib/picolibc/clang). Please ensure `_SSIZE_T_` is defined before use (e.g., via including the appropriate compiler/system header that provides it, or add a fallback typedef based on `__SIZE_TYPE__`/`__PTRDIFF_TYPE__` with a static assert about signedness/width). Otherwise this can break minimal libc builds with “unknown type name” errors or silently change ABI if `_SSIZE_T_` maps unexpectedly.

Removing <sys/types.h> is consistent with the goal of avoiding POSIX headers in lower layers, but please ensure this file no longer relies on POSIX typedefs (e.g., ssize_t, off_t, size_t via sys/types.h on some platforms). Since qsort() typically only needs size_t (from <stddef.h>/<stdlib.h>) and integer types, this is likely fine, but it would be good to confirm there are no implicit dependencies and that the build still succeeds across all toolchains/architectures. If any non-ISO types are still needed, prefer Zephyr-native types or include the specific ISO header that provides them.

Switching the alignment mask from `sizeof(mem_word_t)` to `sizeof(uintptr_t)` changes the intended copy granularity and may silently regress performance or correctness if `mem_word_t` was chosen to match the architecture’s optimal/allowed unaligned access width (or if it was defined differently under some toolchains). If the goal is to remove the `mem_word_t` dependency, consider either (a) keeping `mem_word_t` but defining it locally in this file using only ISO/Zephyr headers (e.g., `typedef uintptr_t mem_word_t;` with a comment), or (b) add a static assert / comment explaining why `uintptr_t` is the correct word type here across all supported targets (including 64-bit and any strict-alignment MCUs). Prefer computing the offset from the flash area base (e.g., `off_t off = (uint8_t *)&prepared_mark - (uint8_t *)FLASH_AREA_BASE;`) or use the appropriate API/helper that takes an absolute address if that’s what is intended. Also, if the goal is to remove POSIX `off_t` usage, consider using Zephyr’s native offset type (`k_off_t`) or whatever type `flash_write()` actually expects, rather than `uintptr_t`.

This takes the global spinlock before iterating the list, but `disk_access_get_di()` is also called from `disk_access_register()`/`unregister()`. In the new code those functions call `disk_access_get_di()` *before* taking the lock, which reintroduces a race (concurrent register/unregister can mutate `disk_access_list` while this function is traversing it). Consider moving the lock acquisition to the start of `disk_access_register()`/`unregister()` and either (a) add a private `disk_access_get_di_locked()` helper that assumes the lock is held, or (b) have `disk_access_get_di()` optionally skip locking when already locked. Also ensure the lock is always released on all exit paths (including early returns) if you refactor.

Adding a new mandatory function pointer to `struct spi_driver_api` is an ABI/source-compatibility hazard: every existing SPI controller driver that defines `spi_driver_api` will now fail to build unless it initializes this new member. Consider making this field conditional (e.g., `#ifdef CONFIG_PINCTRL` or a new Kconfig like `CONFIG_SPI_APPLY_DEFAULT_PIN_STATE`) and/or placing it at the end with a default initializer pattern used across Zephyr. Also ensure all in-tree SPI drivers are updated to set `.apply_default_pin_state = NULL` (or an implementation) to avoid breakage.

Setting TZ for native libc makes the test deterministic, but `setenv()` can fail and it also mutates global process state for the rest of the test binary. Consider asserting the return value (e.g., `zassert_ok(setenv(...))`) and/or restoring the previous TZ value after the check to avoid cross-test interference. Also verify that `CONFIG_NATIVE_LIBC` is the intended Kconfig symbol for native_posix/host-libc builds (some platforms use `CONFIG_EXTERNAL_LIBC`/`CONFIG_LIBC_IMPLEMENTATION_*`), otherwise this guard may not trigger where needed.
